query: What is the main reason for Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein's meeting with President Trump on Thursday?

documents.text:
Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein's tenure is uncertain as he is scheduled to meet with President Donald Trump on Thursday, White House press secretary Sarah Sanders announced Monday. Trump stated he seeks "transparency" and "openness" regarding the situation and confirmed he spoke with Rosenstein earlier that day.

Rosenstein's fate, initially expected to be decided Monday, was postponed due to Trump's attendance at the United Nations General Assembly in New York. Rosenstein departed the White House midday Monday still holding his position.

Reports from NBC News' Pete Williams indicate that Rosenstein discussed his potential departure with White House Counsel Don McGahn on Saturday, though the initiator of the conversation remains unclear. Sources familiar with the discussion suggest Rosenstein would not accept termination by Chief of Staff John Kelly, insisting any decision must come directly from the President.

However, multiple sources informed NBC News that Trump had decided over the weekend, after consulting with staff and allies, not to fire Rosenstein. Among those advising Trump were Fox News host Sean Hannity and Rep. Mark Meadows (R-N.C.). Meadows has called for Rosenstein to testify under oath regarding whether he considered wearing a wire to record Trump or sought to invoke the 25th Amendment to remove the president.

A source close to the White House described the mood among the President's allies as "chaos." Another individual close to the President suggested that Rosenstein's departure, regardless of the circumstances, would place the burden on Trump to ensure Special Counsel Robert Mueller can complete his investigation without interference.

Sources close to Rosenstein report he considered resigning over the weekend following a story in The Times. One source stated Rosenstein felt "personally betrayed" by the story's sources, calling it a "knife in the back." Another indicated Rosenstein defended himself, labeling allegations of suggesting the 25th Amendment as "absurd."

Rosenstein's position became precarious after reports surfaced that he had discussed wearing a wire to record Trump and explored initiating a 25th Amendment removal process. Sanders confirmed in a statement that Rosenstein and Trump would have an "extended conversation" on Thursday to address the recent news.

Thursday also marks the scheduled testimony of Supreme Court nominee Brett Kavanaugh and Christine Blasey Ford before the Senate Judiciary Committee. Kavanaugh denies Ford's accusation of sexual assault.

Rosenstein's departure could jeopardize the future of Special Counsel Robert Mueller's Russia investigation, including the delivery and public release of his final report. Trump has expressed frustration with Rosenstein and the Justice Department's handling of investigations into officials Trump believes conspired against him.

Jay Sekulow, one of Trump's lawyers, stated on his radio show Monday that Rosenstein's ouster would necessitate a thorough review of the special counsel's probe. Sekulow suggested this review should encompass allegations surrounding the inquiry, including the Christopher Steele dossier and the appointment of the special counsel.

The discussions of a wire and the 25th Amendment, first reported by The New York Times, are seen as the primary reasons for Trump considering Rosenstein's removal. Rosenstein stated Friday that there is "no basis" to deem the president unfit and that he never attempted to record Trump.

Two administration officials told NBC News on Friday that Rosenstein's wire discussion was sarcastic. However, individuals who spoke with then-Acting FBI Director Andrew McCabe, who was present at the meeting, recall Rosenstein being serious about surreptitiously recording conversations with Trump.

In a weekend interview with Geraldo Rivera, Trump acknowledged considering Rosenstein's removal, stating, "I’ll make a determination sometime later but I don’t have the facts."

Democrats urged congressional intervention to protect Mueller's investigation. Rep. Val Demings (D-Fla.) stated Congress must act to "shield the Mueller investigation against President Trump’s obstruction."

Andrew McCabe, who was fired by Attorney General Jeff Sessions, echoed these concerns, emphasizing the importance of protecting Mueller's investigation for the integrity of law enforcement and the rule of law. He expressed deep concern that Rosenstein's departure would put the investigation at risk.

Secretary of State Mike Pompeo and Mueller's office declined to comment on the reports.

**Correction: Previous Breaking News Alert Deemed a False Alarm**

This is a correction to a recently issued alert. Initial reports indicated significant breaking news, but subsequent verification has confirmed that the alert was a false alarm.

Please disregard all information contained in the previous alert. Sources have since clarified that the situation does not warrant the level of attention initially suggested. We sincerely apologize for any undue concern this premature alert may have caused.

In essence, there have been no substantial developments, and all prior information should be considered inaccurate. There is no further need to monitor this story at this time. We regret any confusion and remain dedicated to delivering accurate and timely information.

Deputy AG Rod Rosenstein to meet with President Trump Thursday over Times article 4:41 PM ET Mon, 24 Sept 2018 | 01:25 President Donald Trump will meet with Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein on Thursday amid swirling reports that the No. 2 Justice Department official's departure is imminent. "At the request of Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein, he and President Trump had an extended conversation to discuss the recent news stories," White House press secretary Sarah Huckabee Sanders said in a statement. "Because the President is at the United Nations General Assembly and has a full schedule with leaders from around the world, they will meet on Thursday when the President returns to Washington, D.C." Axios published a bombshell report Monday that Rosenstein is resigning, citing sources with knowledge. But the report was contradicted by other news outlets, including from NBC News' Pete Williams, who reported that Rosenstein would not resign of his own accord after his off-the-cuff comments about possibly recording and removing Trump were revealed last week. He will only depart if the White House fired him and will refuse to resign if asked to do so, Williams reported. News of Rosenstein's potential departure was a "huge shock" to the Justice Department, Williams added. Although Trump has sharply criticized Rosenstein over the Russia probe, his departure could create a big problem for the White House: filling the position six weeks before the crucial midterm elections. A Justice Department official told The New York Times that if Rosenstein is out, then Solicitor General Noel Francisco would oversee the Russia investigation. Shortly after the resignation reports, Rosenstein was at the White House for a previously-scheduled principals meeting, a Justice Department official told NBC. Trump arrived in New York City on Sunday for the United Nations General Assembly. He was scheduled to have dinner with Japanese Prime Minister Shinzo Abe that evening.

FILE - In this July 13, 2018 file photo, Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein speaks during a news conference at the Department of Justice in Washington. Rosenstein is expecting to be fired, heading... (Associated Press) FILE - In this July 13, 2018 file photo, Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein speaks during a news conference at the Department of Justice in Washington. Rosenstein is expecting to be fired, heading to White House Monday morning.(AP Photo/Evan Vucci) (Associated Press) NEW YORK (AP) — The Latest on Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein (all times local): 1 p.m. The White House says President Donald Trump and his embattled Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein spoke on Monday and will meet Thursday at the White House amid uncertainty about Rosenstein's fate. Thursday is the same day that Trump's Supreme Court nominee, Brett Kavanaugh, and a woman who has accused him of sexually assaulting her when they were teenagers, are set to appear before the Senate Judiciary Committee. White House spokeswoman Sarah Huckabee Sanders says Trump and Rosenstein had "an extended conversation" Monday "to discuss the recent news stories" at Rosenstein's request. Rosenstein, who oversees special counsel Robert Mueller's investigation into Russia election meddling, had been expecting to be fired Monday following after critical comments he made about Trump. Trump is currently in New York for the United Nations General Assembly, so the two will meet Thursday "when the President returns to Washington, D.C." __ 12:01 p.m. Former Acting FBI Director Andrew McCabe says that if Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein leaves his post, it puts the special counsel's Russia investigation "at risk." The Associated Press has confirmed that Rosenstein is heading to the White House with the expectation that he will be fired. Rosenstein oversees special counsel Robert Mueller's investigation into Russia election meddling and has been the probe's chief public defender. The development comes just days after reports that in the days after the firing of FBI Director James Comey, Rosenstein had raised the idea of secretly recording President Donald Trump and of invoking the 25th Amendment to have the Cabinet remove the president from office. McCabe says in a statement that he had "no role" in providing information of "any kind" for those news reports. __ 10:50 a.m. Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein is expecting to be fired Monday at the White House, after critical comments about President Donald Trump. That's according to a source familiar with his thinking who wasn't authorized to speak on the record and spoke to The Associated Press on the condition of anonymity. It follows reports Friday that Rosenstein floated the idea of secretly recording Trump last year and that he raised the idea of using the 25th Amendment to remove Trump as unfit for office. Rosenstein has denied the reports. ___ 10:40 a.m. President Donald Trump has not decided whether to fire Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein for reportedly questioning the president's fitness to serve. Trump told Geraldo Rivera in an interview aired Monday that he's looking at what, if anything, to do about Rosenstein's reported actions. The New York Times first reported that in 2017, Rosenstein had proposed secretly recording Trump and suggested his removal from office. Trump laid blame for the controversy at the feet of his attorney general. "He was hired by Jeff Sessions," Trump said in the interview, which was aired on "Fox & Friends." As for Rosenstein's future, Trump says, "We will make a determination. It's certainly a very sad story." Rosenstein issued a pair of denials, saying The Times report is inaccurate.

### Rosenstein Anticipates Firing Amidst "Deep State" Fears

Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein was reportedly heading to the White House on Monday, expecting to be fired by President Donald Trump. This development follows recent news that Rosenstein had previously considered secretly recording President Trump and exploring the possibility of using the Constitution to have his Cabinet remove him from office.

### Implications for Mueller Investigation

Any termination or resignation of Rosenstein would have significant consequences for Special Counsel Robert Mueller's investigation into potential collusion between Russia and the Trump campaign during the 2016 election. Rosenstein is the official who appointed Mueller and currently oversees the probe.

This situation is not entirely new, as President Trump had previously considered firing Rosenstein in April. That contemplation came after FBI raids on the office and home of Michael Cohen, Trump's former personal attorney, who has since pleaded guilty to multiple felonies and cooperated with Mueller's investigation.

### "Deep State" Concerns and 25th Amendment Whispers

The latest tension appears to stem from a New York Times report detailing comments made by Rosenstein in early 2017. These reports, along with an unsigned opinion piece by a senior Republican administration official, seemed to amplify President Trump's concerns about a clandestine "Deep State" working to undermine his presidency from within the government.

The anonymous official, whom Trump has called for a federal investigation to identify, wrote about a group of officials aiming to protect the country from the president's most impulsive actions. The opinion piece also mentioned "whispers" within the Cabinet about invoking the 25th Amendment, a move that was ultimately set aside due to fears of triggering a constitutional crisis.

President Donald Trump stated he anticipates meeting with Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein on Thursday to discuss his future. This comes after reports that Rosenstein informed Chief of Staff John Kelly of his intention to resign. Trump told reporters at the United Nations on Monday, "We'll be determining what's going on. We want to have transparency, we want to have openness and I'm looking forward to meeting with Rod at that time."

Rosenstein's position and future plans were unclear Monday afternoon following reports that he had suggested to colleagues the previous year that he would secretly record conversations with President Trump.

Key developments and details surrounding Rod Rosenstein's situation include:

*   **Resignation Discussions:** Kelly and Rosenstein discussed the prosecutor's resignation late last week. The White House had reportedly accepted the resignation and considered it finalized.
*   **Conflicting Reports:** Initially, one source indicated Rosenstein was expected to leave his post by the end of Monday. However, another source later confirmed Rosenstein remained deputy attorney general, with no formal resignation submitted.
*   **Presidential Meeting:** Trump confirmed he spoke with Rosenstein and a meeting was scheduled for Thursday upon his return to the White House.
*   **Russia Probe Oversight:** Rosenstein oversees the Russia investigation led by Special Counsel Robert Mueller. He visited the White House on Monday for a previously scheduled meeting.
*   **Allegations of Secret Recording:** Reports surfaced that Rosenstein had suggested secretly recording Trump. While one individual present at the alleged meeting stated Rosenstein was joking, The New York Times cited secondhand accounts suggesting seriousness. The Times also reported Rosenstein discussed identifying cabinet members willing to invoke the 25th Amendment to remove a president deemed unfit.
*   **Implications for Mueller Investigation:** Rosenstein's departure, who appointed Mueller as special counsel in May 2017, carries significant implications for the Russia investigation. A successor could potentially fire Mueller or limit the scope of his inquiry.
*   **Calls for Protection:** Democratic Senator Kirsten Gillibrand urged the Senate to protect the Mueller investigation, advocating for a bipartisan bill to safeguard its integrity. She emphasized the public's right to answers regarding Russian interference in the 2016 election.
*   **Warnings Against Dismissal:** Current and former officials, including lawmakers, had cautioned Trump against firing or pushing out Rosenstein. Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer warned against using the recording report as a pretext to replace Rosenstein with someone who would allow presidential interference in the investigation.
*   **Republican Support:** Some Republicans expressed support for Rosenstein. Senator John Cornyn acknowledged Rosenstein's performance in a difficult role but noted the challenge of Senate confirmation for a successor. Senator Orrin Hatch stated he liked Rosenstein and believed the president did as well.
*   **Mueller's Charges:** The Mueller investigation has led to charges against 25 Russian individuals and entities for election interference. It has also secured guilty pleas and cooperation agreements from individuals associated with Trump, including Paul Manafort and Michael Flynn.
*   **Trump's "Witch Hunt" Label:** President Trump has consistently characterized the Russia inquiry as a "witch hunt."
*   **Rosenstein's Appointment:** Rosenstein assumed responsibility for the Russia probe after Attorney General Jeff Sessions recused himself. Trump has publicly criticized Sessions for this recusal.
*   **Succession Plan:** Trump has the option to appoint a temporary replacement for deputy attorney general while nominating a Senate-confirmed successor. The Justice Department's line of succession could allow Solicitor General Noel Francisco to take control, though his past association with a law firm that represented Trump has raised questions. Francisco has defended Trump administration policies and previously made a statement in court that he later clarified.
*   **Rosenstein's Background:** Rosenstein, 53, was appointed by Trump and previously served as U.S. attorney for Maryland for 12 years under Presidents George W. Bush and Barack Obama. He joined the Justice Department in 1990 and is recognized for his work in reshaping departmental priorities.
*   **Defiance Against Impeachment Efforts:** In May, Rosenstein opposed Republican lawmakers seeking his impeachment for withholding Justice Department documents related to the Russia probe. He stated the department would not be "extorted" and would adhere to the rule of law.
*   **Criticism from House Republicans:** Representative Jim Jordan, a vocal critic of Rosenstein and the Russia probe, insisted Rosenstein must testify before the House Judiciary Committee, regardless of his job status, to answer questions about the alleged recording discussions.
*   **Comey Firing:** In 2017, Rosenstein's letter outlining concerns about then-FBI Director James Comey's handling of the Hillary Clinton email investigation was cited by Trump in Comey's firing. Trump later indicated the Russia investigation was also a factor.
*   **Presidential Frustration:** Trump reportedly discussed dismissing Rosenstein with aides in April due to increasing anger over the Mueller investigation and Rosenstein's supervision of it. Trump and some Republicans have argued the probe is tainted and should be ended.

Senator Susan Collins, a Republican from Maine, expressed concern on Twitter about reports of Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein's potential departure. She emphasized his "critical role" in overseeing the Russia inquiry.

Jay Sekulow, President Trump's personal lawyer, admitted on his radio show that he was uncertain if Mr. Rosenstein would be forced out. Sekulow used this uncertainty to argue for halting the Russia investigation. He stated that if Mr. Rosenstein resigned, it would be "necessary and appropriate" to "step back" and implement a "time out on this inquiry."

Key developments regarding Mr. Rosenstein's status included:

*   **White House Meeting:** Reports emerged that Mr. Rosenstein attended a pre-scheduled meeting with top administration officials in the West Wing. This indicated he had neither resigned nor been fired.
*   **Attorney General's Return:** Attorney General Jeff Sessions returned to the Justice Department around the same time, coinciding with growing clarity that Mr. Rosenstein was not being dismissed.

Speculation about Mr. Rosenstein's position continued until 12:48 p.m. At that time, White House Press Secretary Sarah Huckabee Sanders tweeted a statement. She announced that Mr. Rosenstein had requested a meeting with the President. Ms. Sanders explained that due to the President's attendance at the United Nations General Assembly and his busy schedule with world leaders, the meeting would occur on Thursday after the President's return to Washington.

Within an hour of the press secretary's tweet, Mr. Rosenstein departed the White House. News cameras captured him being escorted to his black SUV by White House Chief of Staff John Kelly. His motorcade then quickly returned him to the Justice Department. There, the deputy attorney general resumed his scheduled meetings, including one focused on white-collar crime.

Meanwhile, other law enforcement officials redirected their attention to preparations for a Tuesday meeting between Attorney General Sessions and state attorneys general concerning tech companies.

A prepared statement, drafted by Ms. Flores, was ultimately not released. However, the fact that Mr. Rosenstein might remain in his position for at least another 72 hours is unlikely to resolve the situation. A departure by Mr. Rosenstein this week would have plunged the administration into further turmoil, especially with the midterm elections just weeks away.

White House officials said Monday that Deputy Attorney General Rod J. Rosenstein will stay in his job for now, following a chaotic morning of speculation that he was about to resign — a development that would have further destabilized a Justice Department already under siege because of the Russia investigation. Job insecurity has been a regular feature of Rosenstein’s life for more than a year, as the president and his allies have publicly attacked him over special counsel Robert S. Mueller III’s probe into Russian interference in the 2016 campaign and whether any Trump associates conspired with those efforts. But his hold on his job never seemed as tenuous as on Monday morning, after reports last week that Rosenstein, the No. 2 official at the Justice Department, had suggested secretly recording the president and mounting an effort within the Cabinet to remove him from office. Rosenstein has disputed the accuracy of those accounts, but his denials have left plenty of room for interpretation. Now, President Trump plans to meet with Rosenstein on Thursday to discuss the situation — and the deputy attorney general’s fate remains uncertain. [A chaotic opening to what could be a fateful week] Multiple officials said that during weekend conversations between and among White House and Justice Department officials, Rosenstein indicated that he was considering resigning. During some of those conversations, Rosenstein said his resignation might be warranted to end the controversy, according to people familiar with the discussions, who spoke on the condition of anonymity to talk about internal deliberations. Some Trump aides had counseled the president against quickly firing Rosenstein, arguing that such a move could be exploited politically by the Democrats and would be better done after the midterm elections. Friday night, Rosenstein spoke with White House counsel Donald McGahn, according to a person familiar with the discussions. In that conversation, Rosenstein said he was willing to resign, but McGahn urged him to wait until they could talk further Monday morning, this person said. By Sunday, some senior Justice Department officials had concluded that Rosenstein was about to be out of his job and began planning for Solicitor General Noel Francisco to take over supervision of the Russia probe. As part of that contingency plan, Matthew Whitaker, chief of staff to Attorney General Jeff Sessions, was slated to assume the other responsibilities of the deputy attorney general, according to the people familiar with the discussions. When Rosenstein went to the White House on Monday morning, senior Trump advisers expected him to resign, according to several advisers. A Justice Department official, however, said Rosenstein had no intention of resigning but went there expecting to be fired. Before the meeting, one news outlet reported that Rosenstein had “verbally resigned.” That led Justice Department officials to believe he was going to be forced out, and they drafted a statement about his expected departure. As often happens in government, the two sides heading for a high-stakes confrontation decided instead to hold another meeting. “At the request of Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein, he and President Trump had an extended conversation to discuss the recent news stories,” White House press secretary Sarah Huckabee Sanders said in a statement. “Because the President is at the United Nations General Assembly and has a full schedule with leaders from around the world, they will meet on Thursday when the President returns to Washington, D.C.” After Rosenstein met with White House Chief of Staff John F. Kelly, he went to a meeting of senior administration officials, the first sign that, at least for now, he was staying on the job. [McCabe memos say Rosenstein considered secretly recording Trump] Rosenstein appointed and has been overseeing Mueller, and ousting the deputy attorney general would probably raise concerns that the president or his allies were kneecapping the Russia investigation. One Trump adviser said the president has not been pressuring Rosenstein to leave. The person said Rosenstein had expressed to others that he should resign because he “felt very compromised” and the controversy hurt his ability to oversee the Russia inquiry, said a person close to Trump. Trump’s attorney, Rudolph W. Giuliani, said Monday that if Rosenstein is replaced, “they should put a brief hold on the investigation and review it from beginning to end.” Rosenstein has been a target of Trump’s public ire and private threats for months, but uncertainty about his future deepened after it was revealed Friday that memos written by Andrew McCabe, then deputy FBI director, said that in May 2017, Rosenstein suggested secretly recording the president and invoking the 25th Amendment to replace him. McCabe memorialized discussions he had with Rosenstein and other senior officials in the stress-packed days immediately after James B. Comey’s firing as FBI director. At that moment, the bureau was deeply suspicious of Rosenstein’s role in the decision, and the Justice Department was worried that it had lost credibility with Congress for giving Trump a memo that said the FBI needed new leadership. Others involved in those May 2017 discussions said Rosenstein’s comments about secretly recording Trump were sarcastic and came as McCabe was pressing the Justice Department to investigate the president’s firing of Comey as possible obstruction of justice. [How removal under the 25th Amendment works: A beginner’s guide] In statements Friday, Rosenstein denied that he ever seriously contemplated secretly recording Trump or pursuing the 25th Amendment to replace him, as was first reported by the New York Times. “The New York Times’s story is inaccurate and factually incorrect,” Rosenstein said. “I will not further comment on a story based on anonymous sources who are obviously biased against the department and are advancing their own personal agenda. But let me be clear about this: Based on my personal dealings with the president, there is no basis to invoke the 25th Amendment.” In a second statement hours later, Rosenstein said: “I never pursued or authorized recording the president and any suggestion that I have ever advocated for the removal of the President is absolutely false.” That second statement was issued after a tense meeting at the White House between Rosenstein and Kelly, according to people familiar with the encounter. Those people said Kelly had urged Rosenstein to issue a more forceful denial, but officials in the White House and the Justice Department said they thought the second statement was in some ways weaker than the first — putting Rosenstein on even thinner ice. For more than a year, Trump’s comments about the Russia investigation have fueled demands on the right that Rosenstein be forced out of his job — countered by alarm on the left that his removal would signal a collapse of the traditional independence of the Justice Department. Trump congressional allies who have been hammering Rosenstein over access to Justice Department documents, including McCabe’s memos, said reports that he might resign or be fired highlight their need to see such documents. “The latest reports on Rod Rosenstein underscore the desperate need for transparency at the DOJ,” Rep. Mark Meadows (R-N.C.) wrote in a tweet Monday. “Release the documents. Declassify everything. Stop the games and show Americans the truth about this Russia investigation. If they have nothing to hide, they should act like it.” Meadows and Rep. Jim Jordan (R-Ohio) have also called for Rosenstein to come back to Capitol Hill to testify this week, as the House is set to break for the campaign season soon. “We’ve got to have Rod Rosenstein in front of the Judiciary Committee answering questions,” Jordan said in an interview. There are no indications that leading Republicans have agreed to call Rosenstein to testify, and House Judiciary Committee Democrats said Monday that they had received no notice from Chairman Bob Goodlatte (R-Va.) about an official summons. That makes it unlikely that Rosenstein will head to Capitol Hill before his expected meeting with Trump on Thursday to discuss his fate. Democrats said Rosenstein should hang tough. “Under no circumstances should Rod Rosenstein resign,” Adam B. Schiff (Calif.), the ranking Democrat on the House Intelligence Committee, wrote in a tweet. “This would place the Mueller investigation in even greater jeopardy. Rosenstein should continue to do his job, protect the independence of the DOJ, and if the President intends to obstruct justice, force Trump to fire him.” Rosenstein, a Republican and career Justice Department official who served under Presidents George W. Bush and Barack Obama, came into office on a wave of bipartisan support, but Comey was fired soon afterward, and Rosenstein was immediately drawn into fierce partisan battles surrounding the Russia inquiry. He became deputy attorney general in April 2017 and assumed oversight of Mueller’s investigation after Attorney General Jeff Sessions — who did not disclose to Congress that he had met during the 2016 campaign with Russia’s ambassador to the United States — recused himself from the inquiry. Just days into his job as the No. 2 official at the Justice Department, Rosenstein wrote a memo criticizing Comey’s handling of the earlier investigation of Hillary Clinton’s use of a private email server for government work when she was secretary of state. The White House used Rosenstein’s memo to justify Comey’s firing. Days later, Rosenstein appointed Mueller, and the special counsel has since been examining the firing of Comey and whether it was part of a pattern of behavior that amounts to obstruction of justice by the president. Karoun Demirjian, Josh Dawsey, Robert Costa, Sari Horwitz, Matt Zapotosky and Robert Barnes contributed to this report.

### Rod Rosenstein's Reported Resignation Offer and His History of Controversy

Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein has reportedly offered to resign from his post, a move that emerged during conversations with White House Chief of Staff John Kelly, according to a source close to Rosenstein. The formal acceptance of this resignation offer remains uncertain at this time.

This reported offer follows significant allegations published by *The New York Times* journalists Adam Goldman and Michael S. Schmidt. Their reporting last week claimed that Rosenstein had considered invoking the 25th Amendment and had discussed wearing a wire during meetings with President Trump. Rosenstein has since publicly denied both of these allegations.

**Update:** This article and its headline have been revised to accurately reflect the current uncertainty surrounding the acceptance of Rod Rosenstein's resignation offer.

query: What was the significance of Earl Spencer's eulogy during Princess Diana's funeral?

documents.text: Earl Spencer, Princess Diana's brother, her two sons Prince Harry and Prince William, and her former husband Prince Charles arrive into Westminster Abbey for her funeral, Sept. 6 1997. (Photo: Associated Press) It may be the most memorable funeral oration of all time: Heartbroken, loving and defiant, it was by turns elegiac and fiery, elegant and over-the-top. When Princess Diana's younger brother Charles, the ninth Earl Spencer, delivered his eulogy at her funeral in Westminster Abbey on Sept. 6, 1997, no one among the 1,900 sitting inside, nor the million listening outside in parks and on The Mall, expected what poured forth. Least of all the royal family sitting right by him. And when he finished, voice trembling with emotion, no one expected the surging applause that erupted outside and soon spread inside the abbey. It was just one more unforgettable moment when the world said goodbye to the Princess of Wales. Here is the text of Spencer's eulogy: "I stand before you today the representative of a family in grief, in a country in mourning before a world in shock. "We are all united not only in our desire to pay our respects to Diana but rather in our need to do so. For such was her extraordinary appeal that the tens of millions of people taking part in this service all over the world via television and radio who never actually met her, feel that they too lost someone close to them in the early hours of Sunday morning. It is a more remarkable tribute to Diana than I can ever hope to offer her today. No Need for Royal Title "Diana was the very essence of compassion, of duty, of style, of beauty. All over the world she was a symbol of selfless humanity. All over the world, a standard bearer for the rights of the truly downtrodden, a very British girl who transcended nationality. Someone with a natural nobility who was classless and who proved in the last year that she needed no royal title to continue to generate her particular brand of magic. "Today is our chance to say thank you for the way you brightened our lives, even though God granted you but half a life. We will all feel cheated always that you were taken from us so young and yet we must learn to be grateful that you came along at all. Only now that you are gone do we truly appreciate what we are now without and we want you to know that life without you is very, very difficult. "We have all despaired at our loss over the past week and only the strength of the message you gave us through your years of giving has afforded us the strength to move forward. Earl Spencer, Princess Diana's brother addresses the congregation in London's Westminster Abbey, Saturday Sept. 6 1997 during the funeral service for his sister, Diana, Princess of Wales. Earl Spencer, scathingly condemned the newspapers and the paparazzi which had followed Diana's every step and pledged himself to protect Diana's son's, Princes' William and Harry from such intrusion. Princess Diana was killed in a car crash in Paris on Aug. 31. (Photo: John Stillwell, pool photo) Great Gift of Intuition "There is a temptation to rush to canonize your memory, there is no need to do so. You stand tall enough as a human being of unique qualities not to need to be seen as a saint. Indeed to sanctify your memory would be to miss out on the very core of your being, your wonderfully mischievous sense of humor with a laugh that bent you double. "Your joy for life transmitted where ever you took your smile and the sparkle in those unforgettable eyes. Your boundless energy which you could barely contain. "But your greatest gift was your intuition and it was a gift you used wisely. This is what underpinned all your other wonderful attributes and if we look to analyze what it was about you that had such a wide appeal we find it in your instinctive feel for what was really important in all our lives. "Without your God-given sensitivity we would be immersed in greater ignorance at the anguish of AIDS and HIV sufferers, the plight of the homeless, the isolation of lepers, the random destruction of landmines. Prince William, right, and Earl Spencer at Westminster Abbey for funeral ceremony of Princess Diana, Sept. 6, 1997. (Photo: JOEL ROBINE, Associated Press) Innermost Feelings of Suffering "Diana explained to me once that it was her innermost feelings of suffering that made it possible for her to connect with her constituency of the rejected. "And here we come to another truth about her. For all the status, the glamour, the applause, Diana remained throughout a very insecure person at heart, almost childlike in her desire to do good for others so she could release herself from deep feelings of unworthiness of which her eating disorders were merely a symptom. "The world sensed this part of her character and cherished her for her vulnerability whilst admiring her for her honesty. "The last time I saw Diana was on July 1, her birthday in London, when typically she was not taking time to celebrate her special day with friends but was guest of honor at a special charity fundraising evening. She sparkled of course, but I would rather cherish the days I spent with her in March when she came to visit me and my children in our home in South Africa. I am proud of the fact apart from when she was on display meeting President Mandela we managed to contrive to stop the ever-present paparazzi from getting a single picture of her — that meant a lot to her. "These were days I will always treasure. It was as if we had been transported back to our childhood when we spent such an enormous amount of time together — the two youngest in the family. "Fundamentally she had not changed at all from the big sister who mothered me as a baby, fought with me at school and endured those long train journeys between our parents' homes with me at weekends. "It is a tribute to her level-headedness and strength that despite the most bizarre-like life imaginable after her childhood, she remained intact, true to herself. Charles, Earl Spencer, the brother of Princess Diana, arrives by car at St James's Palace Sept. 3, 1997. (Photo: JOHN STILLWELL, Associated Press) Diana's Goodness Threatened the Media "There is no doubt that she was looking for a new direction in her life at this time. She talked endlessly of getting away from England, mainly because of the treatment that she received at the hands of the newspapers. I don't think she ever understood why her genuinely good intentions were sneered at by the media, why there appeared to be a permanent quest on their behalf to bring her down. It is baffling. "My own and only explanation is that genuine goodness is threatening to those at the opposite end of the moral spectrum. It is a point to remember that of all the ironies about Diana, perhaps the greatest was this — a girl given the name of the ancient goddess of hunting was, in the end, the most hunted person of the modern age. "She would want us today to pledge ourselves to protecting her beloved boys William and Harry from a similar fate and I do this here Diana on your behalf. We will not allow them to suffer the anguish that used regularly to drive you to tearful despair. Blood Family Will Protect Sons "And beyond that, on behalf of your mother and sisters, I pledge that we, your blood family, will do all we can to continue the imaginative way in which you were steering these two exceptional young men so that their souls are not simply immersed by duty and tradition but can sing openly as you planned. "We fully respect the heritage into which they have both been born and will always respect and encourage them in their royal role but we, like you, recognize the need for them to experience as many different aspects of life as possible to arm them spiritually and emotionally for the years ahead. I know you would have expected nothing less from us. More Timeline of Princess Diana's life: Timeline: Princess Diana's life and the events that made her who she was "William and Harry, we all cared desperately for you today. We are all chewed up with the sadness at the loss of a woman who was not even our mother. How great your suffering is, we cannot even imagine. "I would like to end by thanking God for the small mercies he has shown us at this dreadful time. For taking Diana at her most beautiful and radiant and when she had joy in her private life. Above all we give thanks for the life of a woman I am so proud to be able to call my sister, the unique, the complex, the extraordinary and irreplaceable Diana whose beauty, both internal and external, will never be extinguished from our minds." Source: The BBC Read or Share this story: https://usat.ly/2wTSLTd

The Internet Archive is dedicated to preserving the web through extensive web crawling. This process goes beyond simply capturing the pages you visit; it also meticulously archives all the pages those pages link to. This proactive approach ensures that if a linked page is later changed or deleted from the live internet, a preserved copy of its original state, as it existed when the initial page was created, remains available.

This initiative, often called "No More 404," aims to fix broken links across the internet. By directing users from defunct links to the archived versions, the Internet Archive seeks to restore the original user experience and maintain the context of authorial intent. According to the Internet Archive's own reports, this effort has already seen significant success in identifying and rectifying millions of broken links on supported websites. Their ultimate goal, as stated by Brewster Kahle, the founder of the Internet Archive, is to build a "universal library" that ensures information remains discoverable and accessible for future generations, even as the digital world constantly changes. This comprehensive archiving strategy, supported by organizations like the Sloan Foundation and the National Science Foundation, contributes to a more stable and enduring online information ecosystem.

Twenty years after her tragic death, the world continues to remember Princess Diana, "the People's Princess," a beloved humanitarian who died at just 36 in a Paris car crash. Her sons, Prince William and Prince Harry, were 15 and 12 respectively at the time of her passing.

Today, the princes, now 35 and 32, marked the anniversary privately. They visited the transformed Kensington Palace sunken garden, now filled with Diana's favorite white flowers, including roses, tulips, hyacinths, and forget-me-nots. Representatives from charities Diana championed, such as Great Ormond Street Hospital, the English National Ballet, the Leprosy Mission, Centrepoint, the Royal Marsden Hospital, and the National Aids Trust, joined them.

Princes William and Harry also made an impromptu visit to Kensington Palace to speak with well-wishers and view the tributes left at the gates. Kensington Palace shared on Twitter, "The Duke and Prince Harry are grateful for the many flowers, letters, and messages they have received about their mother. They wanted to say thank you to those who made the journey to Kensington Palace."

In a documentary, Harry shared for the first time publicly about his mother's sudden death, stating, "There's not a day that William and I don't wish that she was still around, and we wonder what kind of a mother she would be now, and what kind of a public role she would have, and what a difference she would be making."

William expressed his sadness that Diana would never meet his wife, Princess Kate, or their children, Prince George and Princess Charlotte. He told GQ magazine, "I would like to have had her advice. I would love her to have met Catherine and to have seen the children grow up. It makes me sad that she won’t, that they will never know her."

During Diana's 1997 funeral, close friend Elton John sang, "Your candle's burned out long before your legend ever will."

Diana's style, glamour, and humanitarian work continue to captivate the public. Her legacy is largely defined by her tireless efforts for those affected by HIV/AIDS and leprosy, who were often marginalized. She is credited with changing public perception, notably by publicly embracing a young boy with AIDS. Her walk through an Angolan minefield shortly before her death brought global attention to the devastating impact of landmines, contributing to the eventual signing of the Ottawa Treaty by Britain and 120 other countries.

Harry recounted at an awards ceremony, "One of the things our mother taught William and I was the value of doing good when no one is watching. She visited hospitals late at night to comfort patients. She spent hours writing letters to privately support the work of others. She achieved a lot by shining a spotlight, but she worked just as hard when the cameras were gone."

Diana's sons have recreated her study at Kensington Palace in the music room at Buckingham Palace, featuring her desk where she organized much of her charitable work. They have also commissioned a statue to honor her on the Kensington Palace grounds. Harry stated in a BBC documentary, "Now all I want to do is try and fill the holes that my mother has left, and that's what it's about for us, is trying to make a difference and in making a difference, making her proud. She was the Princess of Wales, and she stood for so many things, but deep down inside for us, she was a mother. And we will miss our mother, and I wonder every single day what it would be like having her around."

Kensington Palace has hosted several commemorative events, including the memorial garden. An exhibition, "Diana: Her Fashion Story," showcasing her evolving style, opened in February.

Numerous documentaries have explored Diana's final days and her impact. Her brother, Lord Charles Spencer, noted in a recent documentary, "One of the reasons I want to talk now is because I think that after 20 years someone shifts from becoming a contemporary person, to one of history. And Diana deserves a place in history." He described her as "a special person, and not just a beautiful one."

Ken Wharfe, Diana's former protection officer, told ABC News, "What I liked about her was that there was always there was only the one side. What you saw in public was actually how she behaved in private. And I think you know what the public got was the real Diana. This wasn't, you know, a person that dressed up for the occasion and spoke for that occasion."

A significant challenge for William and Harry is keeping Diana's memory alive for George and Charlotte. On what would have been Diana's 56th birthday, they held a re-dedication service at her grave at Althorp, the Spencer family home, which George and Charlotte attended.

William shared in an ITV documentary how he keeps Diana's memory alive at home: "I think constantly talking about Granny Diana, so we've got more photos up around the house now of her and we talk about her a bit and stuff." He added, "It's hard because obviously Catherine didn't know her, so she cannot really provide that level of detail. So I do regularly, putting George or Charlotte to bed, talk about her and just try and remind them that there are two grandmothers, there were two grandmothers in in their lives. It's important that they know who she was and that she existed."

Princess Diana's enduring global appeal was deeply rooted in her authentic connection with people, a bond frequently strengthened through her extensive charitable endeavors. This document offers personal accounts from individuals who encountered the 'people's princess,' providing intimate glimpses beyond the public persona.

### A Royal Wedding and a Moving Service

Susan Philcox of Oxford was the sole female server at the wedding of the Prince of Wales and Lady Diana Spencer, a role she secured via a ballot ticket. The magnificent ceremony, held at St. Paul's Cathedral, was a significant family occasion, with her father and brother also serving as ushers. The day commenced exceptionally early, with the trio granted special passes to travel along the royal procession route. "We were driving on the route at 5 am, waving to the crowds who were already up and cheering everything and everyone that passed by," Mrs. Philcox recalls.

Inside the cathedral, Mrs. Philcox observed Diana's entrance. "I saw Diana enter through the west door of the cathedral, having her dress adjusted, and then begin the slow walk up the aisle," she recounts. "She seemed nervous when she arrived – I smiled when she fumbled Prince Charles' names." Following the ceremony, the crowd's roar was palpable as Charles and Diana exited. Mrs. Philcox later encountered the princess again at St. Paul's in 1982 for an event commemorating the conclusion of the Falklands War. "We were standing by the clergy at the west end at the service's conclusion, and she looked very pretty, if a little flushed. She told the dean, chapter, and the Archbishop how moving the service had been."

### The Red Cross and a Chat About Beans on Toast

In 1983, Princess Diana assumed the patronage of the Red Cross Youth. Edith Conn, the British Red Cross president for Greater Manchester, met her at a youth orchestra performance in Manchester in the mid-1980s. "I had quite a chat with Diana," Mrs. Conn remembers. "We were introduced, and she asked me about my role with the Red Cross, how long I'd been involved, and what brought me to the organization. Then we just chatted about everyday things." Mrs. Conn fondly recalls Diana's candid response when asked about her evenings: "'Oh no, I'm going home to have beans on toast and I'm going to watch EastEnders.' That has always stayed with me." Mrs. Conn felt the princess was "definitely a people person." "What struck me was her natural beauty. When she spoke to you, she looked directly at you, not over your shoulder. You felt as though she was genuinely interested in what you did and what you had to say. She was lovely. I feel very fortunate to have met her. It was a true privilege, and this anniversary should be a time to celebrate her life."

### An Officer's Lasting Memory

In 1989, Princess Diana represented the Queen at the passing out parade at Britannia Naval College in Dartmouth. Jennifer Warnock, then a young naval officer, met the princess aboard a ship. "Diana knew what to ask and had clearly spent time researching her conversation topics. She came across as confident yet shy. She was important yet down to earth – she told me she was wearing layers of thermals beneath her coat dress to stay warm, which was hard to believe given how trim she appeared." Mrs. Warnock's most enduring memory is of a woman who differed from the media's portrayal. "I remember excitedly calling my parents afterward. I was surprised by my own reaction. I've told many people that when they talk about 'the X factor,' Diana was the only person I've ever met who possessed it."

### A Normal Mum Having Fun

Hamish Goddard, who now resides in Majorca, was working at the F1 Chelsea Raceway near Chelsea Harbour in the mid-1990s when Princess Diana would bring Princes William and Harry for go-karting. "The boys were talented drivers, and Prince William even became the track's outright lap record holder," he states. "His name and lap time were permanently displayed on the large electronic scoreboard above the start/finish line, though it took some time before any journalist realized who William Wales was!" He added that management attempted to ensure the family's privacy, though this was not always achievable. "I recall a time when Harry was admiring and sitting on one of the staff's Ducati motorbikes in the private car park outside the clubroom," says Mr. Goddard. "He noticed a row of cameras along the top of the wall of the adjacent company's car park. No heads were clearly visible – just the cameras. Harry came inside quickly, visibly unhappy and unsurprised." However, Mr. Goddard's primary recollections are of an adoring mother with her "lovely and polite" sons. "Diana used to chat with us while the boys raced around – drinking a coffee, happy and relaxed – a normal mum having fun, out and about with her children."
