## Preserving the Web: Archiving Pages and Combating Broken Links

The Internet Archive is actively working to preserve the web by archiving pages as they are created, along with the pages they reference. This proactive approach ensures that even if a referenced page is later changed or removed from the live web, a link to its archived version will remain accessible. This preserved snapshot allows users to see the content as it existed when the original page was written.

### The "No More 404" Initiative

The ultimate aim of this archiving effort is to address the pervasive issue of broken links across the internet. By creating and maintaining these archived versions, the Internet Archive hopes to facilitate the replacement of broken links with functional references to archived content. This initiative also aims to provide companion links, offering readers insight into the original intent of a page's authors. The project specifically targets supported "No More 404" sites for these comprehensive crawls.

Astronaut Scott Kelly is set to return to Earth on March 1, 2016, concluding an extraordinary 520-day mission aboard the International Space Station (ISS). Throughout his extended stay, Kelly has captivated audiences by sharing a wealth of breathtaking images from orbit via Twitter, offering a unique perspective on our planet.

Kelly's year-long mission, which began approximately one year prior to his return, has provided an unprecedented opportunity to document life and Earth from space. He is scheduled to land late Tuesday night. On Saturday morning, Kelly shared a particularly spectacular sunrise, noting that the ISS's rapid orbit means a sunrise or sunset occurs every 92 minutes. Reflecting on his experience in September, he tweeted, "Of all the sunrises I've seen on my #YearInSpace, this was one of the best! One of the last too. Headed home soon."

Kelly's diligent documentation of his time in space has been a highlight of his mission. He has frequently posted on social media, often multiple times a day, showcasing remarkable sights. For instance, two months into his mission, he captured an approaching storm over Texas. Shortly after, to mark the 25th anniversary of the Hubble Space Telescope, he photographed the Milky Way in a stunning display.

His posts have highlighted the planet's diverse beauty. Chicago was visible with the rare phenomenon of the Aurora Borealis in the background. The Sahara Desert, from Kelly's vantage point, displayed an array of unbelievable colors, which he likened to watercolors in some of his posts. On November 22, 2015, he tweeted, "Pictures like this make me really regret that my watercolors didn't make it up here. #EarthArt #YearInSpace."

Further examples of his visual diary include a beautiful depiction of the French Riviera and an image of a frozen lake in the Himalayas, captured just weeks before his scheduled return. Central America was also presented in striking detail during the early morning light.

Through his "Colors of Earth" series, Kelly consistently illustrates the planet's vibrant and intricate beauty. The sands of Africa, with their vivid hues and complex patterns, were particularly awe-inspiring. On February 22, 2016, he shared, "Will be missing these sands too, but looking forward to sandy beaches up close. #EarthArt #Africa #YearInSpace."

### Scott Kelly Concludes Historic Year in Space, Passing ISS Command

Astronaut Scott Kelly has successfully completed his nearly year-long mission aboard the International Space Station (ISS), setting a new American record for the longest single spaceflight duration. As his mission concludes, Kelly has officially handed over command of the ISS to fellow NASA astronaut Tim Kopra. This significant milestone comes just before Kelly's return to Earth on March 1st, marking the end of an extraordinary period of human endurance and scientific research.

### The "Year in Space" Mission: A Deep Dive into Human Limits

The "Year in Space" mission, a joint endeavor by Scott Kelly and Russian cosmonaut Mikhail Kornienko, pushed the boundaries of human capability. Spending 340 consecutive days aboard the ISS, a spacecraft roughly the size of an American football field, provided invaluable data for NASA and its international partners. During this extended stay, Kelly's body was subjected to the rigors of microgravity, with scientists closely monitoring his physiological and psychological responses. For instance, the mission aimed to understand how the human body adapts to long-duration spaceflight, a critical step for future deep-space missions.

### Collaboration and Sacrifice: The Human Element of Space Exploration

The handover ceremony highlighted the profound sense of camaraderie among the astronauts. Kelly expressed his deep appreciation for his crewmates, particularly cosmonaut Mikhail Kornienko, whom he affectionately called "Misha." Kelly described goodbyes from the ISS as "bittersweet," acknowledging the unique environment they share. Alongside Kelly and Kornienko, cosmonaut Sergey Volkov, who has also been on the ISS for the past six months, is scheduled to return to Earth on March 1st. Kelly emphasized that the success of such complex missions is a testament to exceptional teamwork. "Spaceflight is the biggest team sport there is," Kelly stated, underscoring the collaborative spirit essential for achieving ambitious space exploration goals.

### Paving the Way for Future Mars Missions

Upon his return, Scott Kelly will undergo extensive medical evaluations by NASA scientists. His physical condition will be meticulously compared to that of his identical twin brother, Mark Kelly, who remained on Earth throughout the mission. This unique "twin study," a key component of the "Year in Space" initiative, will provide unprecedented insights into the effects of microgravity on the human body. The data gathered from this mission is crucial for planning future long-duration voyages, including NASA's ambitious goal of sending humans to Mars. The agency, including Mission Control, has lauded Kelly and Kornienko's contributions as "monumental" and eagerly anticipates their safe return to Houston.

NASA's groundbreaking astronaut twin experiment, designed to understand the effects of long-duration spaceflight, is nearing a significant milestone. After 340 days aboard the International Space Station (ISS), astronaut Scott Kelly is returning to Earth on March 1st. His identical twin, Mark Kelly, remained on Earth throughout the mission, serving as a crucial control subject. This ambitious study aims to provide invaluable insights into how the human body adapts to the rigors of space, particularly in preparation for future Mars missions.

The twin study's core objective is to compare physiological and psychological changes between Scott, who experienced extended time in orbit, and Mark, who lived under normal Earth conditions. This comparison allows scientists to isolate the effects of the space environment.

Key areas of investigation include:

*   **Bone and Muscle Health:** In microgravity, bones and muscles no longer bear the body's weight, leading to weakening. Astronauts experience bone thinning and muscle atrophy. To mitigate this, ISS astronauts dedicate approximately two hours daily to exercise, utilizing resistance training and treadmills. Despite these efforts, astronauts can lose an average of 1.5 percent of their bone mass per month in space.
*   **Fluid Shifts:** The absence of gravity significantly impacts the distribution of bodily fluids. Without gravity's pull, the heart works less to pump blood to the lower extremities, potentially leading to its shrinkage. This fluid shift also causes the "Charlie Brown effect," characterized by a rounder face due to fluid accumulation in the head. This can affect senses like smell and, more critically, vision, as pressure on the optic nerve can lead to decreased visual acuity. NASA employs devices like the CHABIS (a type of lower body negative pressure device) to help redistribute fluids.
*   **Radiation Exposure:** Cosmic radiation poses a significant threat to astronauts, especially on missions beyond Earth's protective magnetic field, such as a journey to Mars. The ISS benefits from some protection due to its orbital altitude, but understanding the precise dosage of space radiation that becomes dangerous is a critical research goal.

While Scott Kelly's return marks the end of the data collection phase for the twin study, the comprehensive analysis of the gathered information is just beginning. The insights gained from this year-long mission will be instrumental in ensuring astronaut safety and health for future deep-space endeavors.

To prepare astronauts for the demanding journey to Mars, NASA astronaut Scott Kelly is undergoing rigorous post-flight evaluations following his 340-day mission on the International Space Station (ISS). These tests, designed by NASA's lead flight surgeon Dr. Stevan Gilmore, are crucial for assessing an astronaut's ability to be immediately functional upon arrival on Mars, mimicking the challenges future explorers will face.

### Rigorous Post-Flight Evaluations for Mars Readiness

Scott Kelly's 340-day mission, the longest for NASA, is a pivotal step toward enabling human exploration of Mars. As Kelly himself stated, "I think we'll learn a lot about longer-duration spaceflight and how that will take us to Mars someday." Upon his return to Earth, Kelly, alongside cosmonaut Mikhail Kornienko, will participate in a series of field tests. These assessments, overseen by Dr. Gilmore, will evaluate an astronaut's immediate post-landing capabilities, including:

*   **Immediate Mobility:** Can astronauts quickly stand and maintain balance?
*   **Navigational Skills:** Can they navigate a course and walk a straight line?
*   **Operational Readiness:** Could they perform critical actions such as opening a hatch or executing an emergency spacewalk?

Beyond these immediate physical challenges, extensive medical examinations will be conducted over several weeks. These include detailed blood draws and continuous heart monitoring, as documented by NASA's medical teams.

### The Twin Study: Unlocking Long-Duration Spaceflight Secrets

A groundbreaking aspect of this mission is the "twin study," comparing Scott Kelly's year in space with his identical twin brother, Mark Kelly, who remained on Earth. This study, a collaboration involving researchers from institutions like the National Space Biomedical Research Institute (NSBRI), provides invaluable data on the physiological and psychological effects of prolonged spaceflight. While Russian cosmonauts have previously spent longer durations in space, data collection from those earlier missions, such as those by Valeri Polyakov (437 days) and Gennady Padalka (878 days cumulative), was less comprehensive than the current NASA-led initiative.

As of Day 335 of his mission, Kelly reported feeling well, and Dr. Gilmore anticipates no alarming results from the initial assessments. However, significant concerns remain regarding the long-term effects of space radiation. For Mars missions, which involve extended exposure, this is a critical factor, with estimates suggesting astronauts could receive up to 100 millisieverts of radiation dose.

Physiologist John Charles, a prominent figure in space medicine, highlights that psychological well-being, in-flight medical care, and food production are critical for Mars expeditions, which are projected to last approximately 2.5 years. The success of growing lettuce and zinnias on the ISS, as part of experiments like Veggie, demonstrates the necessity for astronauts to cultivate their own food. Kelly humorously described this need as requiring him to "channel my inner Mark Watney," referencing the fictional astronaut in "The Martian."

Charles also points out key differences between ISS stays and Mars travel, as detailed in reports from the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine:

*   **Communication:** Mars missions will have significantly limited communication, relying on email with delays of up to 22 minutes each way, rather than regular voice calls.
*   **Autonomy:** Astronauts will need to be highly autonomous, with reduced direct involvement from Mission Control, requiring them to make critical decisions independently.
*   **Living Quarters:** Tighter living spaces on Mars-bound craft will necessitate improved privacy solutions to manage stress and maintain crew cohesion.

The physical isolation from loved ones, even from the ISS, presents a considerable challenge. The prospect of traveling millions of miles to Mars, where Earth will be a distant, small blue dot, presents an even greater level of psychological isolation. NASA is planning additional one-year missions to gather more data, as Kelly acknowledged, "a year now seems longer than I thought it would be."

## Preserving the Federal Government's Digital Legacy

This project is dedicated to actively safeguarding the federal government's digital footprint, building directly on the foundational work of the 2016 End of Term project. Our core mission involves the ongoing archiving of government websites and crucial data. This vital preservation effort is achieved through regular crawling of URLs, sourced from both our esteemed partner institutions and public nominations. This systematic crawling provides an invaluable, continuous view of federal agencies' web and social media activities.

### Key Partnerships and Public Involvement

Significant collaborators in this initiative include the Environmental Data & Governance Initiative and the Data Refuge project. This collection represents a direct continuation of the 2016 End of Term web archiving efforts. To ensure efficiency and avoid duplication, all newly archived content is meticulously deduplicated against that previous collection. This process guarantees that publicly nominated websites are preserved and accessible, extending their digital life beyond the original "official" end of the End of Term project.

The public plays a crucial role in this endeavor. We strongly encourage participation from government information specialists and any interested individuals. You can contribute by submitting selected websites for inclusion in our archive. A user-friendly public nomination tool is readily available to facilitate this process. For more in-depth information regarding our partner institutions, the intricacies of the web crawling process, and details about previous End of Term projects, we invite you to visit the End of Term Archive.

U.S. astronaut Scott Kelly has retired from NASA, after 20 years of service and more than 520 days in space—more time in space than any other American. He also departs with a record as a social media maven and renowned photographer from space. Kelly made four trips to space, the most prominent being a 340-day journey that ended last month. During that flight, Kelly made more than 5,000 trips around the Earth, and drank nearly 200 gallons of drinking water made from recycled sweat and urine. From his perch 400 kilometers (249 miles) above the earth’s surface, he snapped hundreds of beautifully abstract photographs of our planet’s landforms and waterways. He spotted hurricanes ominously swirling on sapphire-blue oceans. He gazed out at the aurora’s glittering fog. He consistently turned Earth’s lands and waters into an abstract artist’s dream, with shots of beaches, deserts, forests, and everything in between. And he did it all with equipment accessible to earthlings, taking most of his images with a Nikon D4 digital camera with lenses more commonly used to photograph football games. Kelly’s photographs gained him a massive following. From Twitter to Tumblr, hundreds of thousands of social media users expressed their wonderment at the photos—and a devoted band of mapping experts tagged along, helpfully noting where Kelly snapped his pics. “It’s fun to be a part of the public’s reaction,” says David MacLean of Nova Scotia Community College’s Centre of Geographic Sciences, who’s among the most influential of Kelly’s hobbyist geotaggers. MacLean has been mapping astronauts’ photos since 2013, but says Kelly’s penchant for abstraction sets his pictures apart. “Other astronauts have more of a city and regional view: ‘Here’s Indianapolis from space,’” MacLean says. Kelly, on the other hand, “has concentrated on details of little pieces of the world.” “He’s caught the interest of quite a lot of people, [who] see these [places] in a whole new way.” And Kelly’s eye for composition hasn’t gone unnoticed at National Geographic. “Kelly’s photographs are striking and incredibly artistic," wrote Patrick Witty, National Geographic’s deputy director of photography. “[It’s] hard to believe they were made by an astronaut and not a working professional photographer.” “I’m sure every photographer on Earth is incredibly jealous of his angle.” To celebrate Kelly’s #YearInSpace, we’ve selected our favorite Scott Kelly space pictures. We hope you enjoy the photos—and check out National Geographic's photographic coverage of Kelly’s final return to Earth.

query: What is the primary goal of the United Noshes dinner project started by Jesse Friedman and Laura Hadden?

documents.text: United Noshes: Dinner Party Aims To Eat Its Way Through Global Cuisine Enlarge this image toggle caption Courtesy of Laura Hadden Courtesy of Laura Hadden The United Nations has 193 member states. And United Noshes aims to recreate meals from every last one of them, alphabetically, as a series of dinner parties. The project was started by Jesse Friedman and his wife, Laura Hadden, three years ago, as a way to explore the culinary bounty of New York City. As they cooked food from Algeria to Djibouti to Guyana, United Noshes hosted dinners that ranged from just a few friends gathered around a living room table, to dozens of guests assembled in a banquet hall. And the ingredients have ranged as well — from cashew juice to French charcuterie to fermented corn flour. These ingredients can be hard to find — especially those that haven't yet won fans in America. "One time I walked into an African market, and the person behind the counter asked me, 'So where did you do Peace Corps?' " Friedman laughs. "Because that's the only time a non-African would come into their store." Funny, enough, Friedman notes, Peace Corps cookbooks can be a surprisingly good recipe source, especially for some of the world's smaller countries. In addition to exploring global culinary diversity, Friedman has also been happy to cook for people — which doesn't always happen in New York City. The dinners started with friends and family. Then, as articles cropped up and their blog readership and mailing list grew, the meals came to include interested strangers (some from other countries themselves). From there, Friedman says, it was a natural step to turn the project into a fundraiser. "We felt we had to acknowledge the fact that many people couldn't even enjoy the sorts of foods we were celebrating from their own country," says Friedman. Enlarge this image toggle caption Courtesy of Laura Hadden Courtesy of Laura Hadden Diners contribute a small donation, and over the years the project has raised over $20,000 — first for the U.N.'s world food program, and now for Mercy Corps, an international relief and development organization based in Friedman's new home of Portland, Ore. In addition to learning about the culinary holdovers of colonialism, and several dozen very distinct ways to cook rice, the project has been a way for Friedman and Hadden to discover places with surprising foodways — like the African islands of Comoros. "There's a lot of central African countries that start with the letter c," laughs Hadden, who had grown a bit fatigued of meal after meal of fufu and collard greens. "And so we were like OK, another one of these meals. And then [we] discovered this really great cuisine." Comoros, with its Portuguese, French and Arabic influences, has a rich culinary tradition quite different from its African neighbors. That includes the dish United Noshes featured, a lobster in vanilla crème fraiche sauce, which became an all-time favorite. Of course, there have been meals that were less successful: the attempted yak butter and chiles of Bhutan, or the sheeps head of Iceland, or the red palm oil fry disaster of Cameroon. But United Noshes' impact isn't just the money that's being raised. Friedman and Hadden say it's the connections that happen when you share a meal with the world — both at the table, and beyond. "I had a cab driver in D.C. from Burkina Faso," Remembers Hadden. "And I said, 'Oh, I've had food from Burkina Faso.' And he was like, 'No, you haven't.' And I was like, 'Yes, I have.' And I told him the name of the little doughnuts. And he said, 'Oh, my god! I used to sell them in the market with my mom when I was a kid!' " Friedman and Hadden should reach the halfway point of their project in a few weeks — with a meal from Libya. They expect to conclude the entire project in about four more years.

We're hosting a series of 194 dinner parties for the members of the United Nations, in alphabetical order. We invite guests to our home in Portland to share what they know about that country or to learn through the food. The dinner parties are also a fundraiser supporting Mercy Corps.

During emergencies, humanitarian response organizations coordinate their activities through clusters, which are groups arranged by sector and led by one or more agencies. The World Food Programme leads the Logistics and Emergency Telecommunications clusters and co-leads the Food Security cluster. WFP also provides the United Nations Humanitarian Air Service and the United Nations Humanitarian Response Depots for use by the humanitarian community. Learn more about humanitarian support and services

United Noshes is a culinary project dedicated to exploring global cuisine by hosting 194 dinner parties, one for each member of the United Nations, in alphabetical order. This ambitious undertaking began in July 2011 in Brooklyn, initially in a modest 500-square-foot apartment, and later relocated to Portland, Oregon, in August 2014. The core mission is to recreate authentic meals, meticulously researching recipes and employing traditional preparation methods to the best of their ability and resources.

The project thrives on community involvement. They actively seek out individuals with connections to the featured countries—whether through birth, residency, family ties, or deep familiarity—to guide their culinary choices and preparation. The experience is further enriched when these individuals join the dinner parties, sharing their insights at the table and in the kitchen. The founders express gratitude for the opportunity to find joy in food and share it with friends through United Noshes.

Recognizing global food insecurity, United Noshes incorporates a charitable element. Guests are invited to bring a donation to support Mercy Corps, an international development and relief non-profit organization. These contributions are then matched, amplifying their impact.

**Key Facts & Figures:**

*   **Total Meals:** 194 dinners, covering all 193 UN member states plus the two permanently observing non-member states (Holy See and Honduras).
*   **Timeline:** The project is projected to conclude around 2019, spanning eight years. They reached the halfway point with Meal 97 (Libya) after nearly four years.
*   **Fundraising:** As of the end of 2014, over $22,800 was raised, including a dollar-for-dollar match from Google. Through their first 88 meals, they raised over $21,300 for World Food Program USA. Post-Portland move, fundraising benefits Mercy Corps.
*   **Pace:** Meals are typically held on an every-other-week schedule.
*   **Locations:** Primarily hosted at their home in Portland, Oregon, with occasional events in other locations like the Bay Area, New York, Seattle, and Washington D.C.

**Culinary Approach:**

The selection of dishes is guided by several strategies:
*   **Holiday Focus:** Utilizing significant national holidays and their associated foods, such as Day of the Dead in Haiti.
*   **Regional Diversity:** For countries with extensive culinary variations (e.g., China), efforts are made to present a cohesive and representative selection of regional dishes.
*   **Occasion Feasts:** Aiming to create a celebratory meal that highlights the distinctiveness of a country's cuisine, particularly for nations where culinary distinctions can be subtle (e.g., parts of Central Africa and the Caribbean).

The research process involves:
1.  **Cuisine Summaries:** Starting with an overview, often from Wikipedia.
2.  **Dish Identification:** Searching for distinctive and representative dishes, cross-referencing multiple recipes and descriptions to identify consistent ingredients and techniques.
3.  **Authenticity:** Prioritizing recipes that avoid Westernized adaptations and maintain traditional methods.
4.  **Expert Collaboration:** Actively working with individuals familiar with the cuisine to refine menus.

**Ingredient Sourcing:**

In New York, diverse immigrant communities provided access to specialized markets for ingredients like bitter gourds, Balkan smoked meats, frozen cassava greens, and breadfruit. While Portland offers a broader range of ingredients, sourcing can still require improvisation, especially for fish, where substitutions are sometimes necessary. When specific sauces or spice mixes are unavailable, they are often made from scratch if a recipe can be found.

**Origins of the Project:**

The idea stemmed from a desire to move beyond typical bar and restaurant meetups, reconnecting with the intimacy of home-based gatherings. Jesse missed cooking for larger groups, and they sought a way to explore the rich diversity of nations represented in New York City while acknowledging their good fortune. This spirit of global exploration through shared meals has continued in Portland, revealing the diversity present even in a city often perceived as more homogeneous.

**Future Plans:**

The United States will be the final meal, planned as a large potluck inviting a representative from each of the 50 states. While not planning a traditional cookbook due to the complexity of representing entire national cuisines from limited experience, Jesse is documenting his learning journey, exploring topics like the global variations of the word "turkey" and the diverse ways rice is prepared worldwide.

**Team:**

*   **Jesse Friedman:** Chef, researcher, shopper, and blogger. Hailing from Oakland, CA, he learned to cook from his father and bake from his mother, who ran a microwave oven store. He studied linguistics at the University of Chicago, lived abroad in France and Switzerland, and began cooking for crowds at a folk festival. Professionally, he is a product marketing manager at Google, working with the Crisis Response team and organizing online advocacy campaigns. His favorite cuisine is Eastern Mediterranean, and he has a particular fondness for fresh mozzarella.
*   **Laura Hadden:** Dishwasher, photographer, and webmaster. Originally from Tacoma, WA, by way of Vancouver, Canada, she is a documentary media artist and multimedia producer. Her work has been showcased at various film festivals and on broadcast channels. She has received awards for her short films and has been an artist-in-residence focusing on income inequality. For three years, she managed media and communications for The Moth, producing their podcast and assisting with their Peabody Award-winning radio show. She has facilitated storytelling workshops and is pursuing an MFA in Integrated Media Arts. She identifies as a reluctant foodie with a favorite cuisine of Thai.

**Press Mentions:**

*   Think Out Loud | Oregon Public Broadcasting | January 19, 2015
*   United Noshes: Dinner Party Aims To Eat Its Way Through Global Cuisine | NPR | January 17, 2015
*   The Food Show (approx. 43 minutes in) | KBOO | October 15, 2014
*   United Noshes: The Alphabet Foodies (article video) | Jewish Daily Forward | August 5, 2014
*   United Noshes: Tasting One Meal From Each United Nations Member State | Denizen | October 14, 2013
*   Solving World Hunger with Dinner Parties: Meet United Noshes | EcoSalon | January 26, 2013
*   United Noshes: 170 Dinner Parties from Afghanistan to Zimbabwe (sic) | America’s Test Kitchen Radio | October 19, 2012
*   Celebrate World Food Day With Meals From Every Country (VIDEO) | Buzz60 | October 11, 2012
*   United We Nosh | Scoutmob | October 10, 2012
*   United Noshes: Meals Around the World | Food 52 | July 13, 2012
*   From Afghanistan to Zimbabwe: Cooking through the United Nations | CNN Eatocracy | June 7, 2012
*   United Noshes Supper Club Has Served Meals From 34 U.N. Countries (and Counting) | Grub Street New York | May 15, 2012
*   Journey Through World’s Cuisine Teaches About Community | State Department IIP Digital | April 24, 2012
*   United Noshes in Washington D.C. | World Food Program USA Blog | April 24, 2012

United Noshes from Jewish Daily Forward on Vimeo. For the past three years, Jesse Friedman and Laura Hadden have been on a quest to explore world cuisine from their modest kitchen in Brooklyn. The married couple is the force behind United Noshes, a project to throw a dinner party featuring the culinary offerings of each of the 193 member states and 2 permanently observing non-members of the United Nations, from Afghanistan to Zimbabwe. Every month, Hadden and Friedman research recipes, gather ingredients, and prepare two to three feasts to represent the selected countries, going in roughly alphabetical order. Friedman does much of the cooking while Hadden documents the feast and entertains the guests. When possible, they solicit help from people familiar with a country’s culture and good. Laura Hadden Share Pinterest Email Laura Hadden It’s a project that stretches Friedman and Hadden’s taste buds and cooking prowess, but it also has another added benefit: Forming a loose community of people who love culinary experimentation. Old friends and new ones join the parties, as well as foodies who find out about the project and sign up for the newsletter. The price of admission is a donation to the World Food Program, a humanitarian organization fighting world hunger. Some 500 people have signed up for the newsletter, and Friedman and Hadden have thrown United Noshes events in Boston, D.C., Seattle, and the Bay Area, as well. At Friedman and Hadden’s 83rd party, a celebration of Israeli and Palestinian cuisine, the guests included a handful of writers, a ballet dancer, and an analytics expert, all there to sample offerings that included lamb kebabs, homemade hummus, and rugelach. “We hit upon the idea because it combined two things we’re interested in,” Friedman said. “Exploring international dishes and meeting new people. We’ve met hundreds of people who are adventurous enough to try what we’re cooking. In New York, people tend not to visit each other in their homes. We wanted to open ours up.” Some meals are more successful than others. “There are disasters of technique and some things that are just bad. Sometimes it’s like ‘All right, we tried that, never again,’” Friedman said. “We made headcheese for Iceland and it was terrible. We call those milkshake meals because even after you eat, you need to go out after for a milkshake.” Martyna Starosta Share Pinterest Email Jesse Friedman and Laura Hadden // Photo by Martyna Starosta To come up with their menus, Friedman and Hadden attempt to recreate a typical home-cooked Sunday meal from that country —something your grandmother might make for a family gathering. Some countries are more difficult to sum up than others. A place like India, for example, requires representing many different regional cooking styles. Friedman and Hadden have learned to avoid western sources for non-western dishes, and to use spices more creatively. Their search for ingredients has taken them to all corners of New York — from Harlem to Ozone Park to Astoria to Brighton Beach. “I don’t fry things any more,” Friedman said. “It gets too messy. And we try not to do things to order, anything that keeps you stuck in the kitchen and unable to interact with guests.” This summer, they are leaving New York behind for Portland. They plan to continue United Noshes from their new home. “At this rate, it’ll be another six to eight years before we’re done,” Hadden said. “But we’re not doing it for anyone else. We’ve always said that if it gets tedious than we can just stop. But it hasn’t gotten boring yet.” This story "United Noshes: The Alphabet Foodies" was written by Margaret Eby.

Week 1: Afghanistan Posted by Jesse Friedman on Monday, July 11, 2011 By virtue of the alphabet, first up in the culinary romp around the world is Afghanistan! I had it easy with the research for what to cook, since my good friend Oliver lived in Kabul for two years and is very into food. I took his advice on what to cook just about exactly, merely turning to the internet for the recipes. (Don’t think I know anyone with deep experience on Albania, so next week will require some more planning.) Afghan food turned out to be super good. It’s got a lot of spices, especially cardamom, which has a mysteriously floral aroma. But none of the dishes were at all spicy, at least none of the dishes I made. Dough | Yogurt-cucumber drink | Recipe “Say it like a Klingon and you’ll have the pronunciation,” says Oliver. And to our Westernized taste, a drink of salty, watered-down yogurt with crunchy bits of cucumber and flakes of mint may as well come from another planet. Then again, Afghans probably find it bizarre that we mix malt and yeast and let it spoil into something bitter. Anyway, everyone gave it the old college try, and nobody hated it, so that’s a good thing. (I actually like it and will probably have some of the remainder for breakfast.) Qabuli Pilau | Kabul-style lamb and rice | Recipe From all accounts, it’s Afghanistan’s national dish. A big mess of basmati rice cooked in the juices of the lamb it covers on the platter, with sauteed carrots and raisins (yes, sauteed raisins) mixed in. And of course, those spices. I wish I could have found a shoulder or shank or other sort of bone-in lamb, but Fairway let me down on this one. I think I overcooked the rice, but it was all pretty yummy. Borani Banjan | Eggplant and tomato casserole | Recipe Eggplant gets a bum rap from many people who find it rubbery and bitter, but you couldn’t accuse this dish of either. I grilled the eggplant slices first (traditionally you fry, but what the heck), and then baked it with tomato, cilantro, and a thin, garlicky, spiced tomato-y sauce. It was the first time I’d fired up the oven in this new place of ours, and I was terrified when it didn’t heat quickly, but thankfully when I cranked the dial it fired up and the dish turned out to be shockingly well balanced and flavorful. (Note to self: buy an oven thermometer, this was probably sheer luck.) Nan | Flatbread | Recipe Quoth Oliver, “When Afghan naan is fresh it’s reminiscent of a mediocre but tasty pizza dough. Think Dominos, not Delfina.” The dough, with a healthy amount of yogurt, was somewhat reminiscent of a spongy Domino crust, but it was actually super good right off the grill. I did kind of screw up the shapes: you’ll see the one on bottom is puffy (I didn’t slit the top well enough to vent it), and the one I’m holding looks more like a baguette, since I made the freshman mistake of rolling them all out and then stacking them without enough flour to keep them separated. With all the countries in the world that grilled breads, I’ll get the hang of it yet! Firni | Cardamom-scented pudding | Recipe As the gelatinous blobs of cornstarch-thickened sweetened milk flopped off the serving spoon, everyone politely requested small portions — this is not a good-looking dessert. But lo! The cardamom and saffron made for a delicate aroma, with a bit of crunch from the pistachios, and the cool milk base made for a pleasant end of meal. They asked for seconds. The weather was lovely, so we ate outside with our inaugural guests Jessica, Natasja, and Mike. Once the sun set and with the firni mostly gone, we watched Afghan Star, an excellent documentary about the equivalent of American Idol, but in a country where singing was not too long ago a grave offense, and dancing on TV a national scandal. For more shots by Laura, check out the Picasa album, including live-action grilling snaps!

This document explores a series of culinary journeys through different countries, focusing on how geography, history, and cultural exchange shape their unique food traditions. Each entry, penned by Jesse Friedman, offers a glimpse into the diverse flavors and cooking methods of nations around the globe, often tied to specific events or personal experiences.

**Peru: A Tapestry of Flavors**

Peru, a land of striking geographical contrasts, boasts everything from rugged coastlines and soaring mountains to dense jungles. This natural diversity is mirrored in its population, a rich blend of Inca, Spanish, and Asian influences. These converging cultures have profoundly shaped Peruvian cuisine, creating a culinary landscape as varied as its landscapes. Pulling together a menu that captured this breadth in a semi-coherent meal was a delightful challenge, made significantly easier with the help of our guest, Dan. His insights were invaluable in curating a selection that truly represented Peru's gastronomic richness. We also extend a heartfelt thank you to my aunt Marcia for her significant contributions.

**Paraguay: Guaraní Roots and Western Touches**

Paraguay holds the distinction of being one of only two landlocked countries in the Western Hemisphere. What truly sets it apart, however, is its linguistic heritage: the indigenous Guaraní language is understood by more people than its colonizer's language, Spanish. This linguistic dominance naturally extends to its culinary traditions. The core of Paraguayan food and cooking techniques bears a strong Guaraní legacy. Yet, like many cultures, it has also embraced certain Western ingredients, with cheese and beef becoming common additions. It's interesting to note that Paraguay is a frequent destination for Peace Corps volunteers, suggesting a strong connection and ongoing exchange.

**Papua New Guinea: A Mosaic of Cultures and Cuisine**

Situated north of Australia and east of Indonesia, Papua New Guinea is widely considered the most linguistically diverse country on Earth, home to over 800 languages and even a few uncontacted tribes. Despite sharing the island of Papua with Indonesia, a Southeast Asian nation, Papua New Guinea is culturally grouped with Melanesia, the southwestern archipelago of the Pacific. This rich cultural tapestry is vividly reflected in its ingredients and cooking methods. Our exploration revealed a fascinating interplay of indigenous practices and external influences, creating a truly unique culinary identity.

**Panama: A Street-Side Feast**

For the second consecutive year, we hosted a late-summer "Nosh" event, inviting everyone on our block to join. Laura secured a permit to close down the street, and neighbors generously brought over tables and chairs. Just as everyone settled in, the first rain of the season began to fall! Given that Panama is most widely recognized for its pivotal role in global transportation due to its famous canal, it felt fitting to be enjoying our meal in a long, communal line down the street. This setting added a unique charm to the evening, despite the unexpected shower.

**Palestine: A Distinctive Culinary Identity**

While Palestine is not a full member of the United Nations, it has held the status of a "non-member observer state" since 2012, a designation that suffices for our culinary explorations. Setting aside political considerations, Palestinian food stands out with its distinctive character. In a region where many cuisines are characterized by subtle regional variations on common themes – often leading to dishes being broadly categorized as "Middle Eastern" – the West Bank proudly claims a unique dish as the cornerstone of its culinary heritage. This signature dish is a layered creation, showcasing a distinct approach to flavor and texture.

**Palau: Island Simplicity and Culinary Ingenuity**

Palau, another of the low-population island nations scattered across the vast Pacific, presents a fascinating case study in traditional food. We've observed a general trend where traditional foods tend to be quite straightforward. Furthermore, finding precise recipes can be challenging, as cooking in Palau is often more about a way of combining ingredients and techniques rather than a rigid set of instructions. Once again, I found myself navigating Facebook groups to determine what to cook, seeking inspiration for a meal that would capture the essence of Palauan cuisine. Joining us for this culinary adventure were...

**Pakistan: A Legacy of Partition and Flavor**

Until 1947, Pakistan was an integral part of India. The intention behind its creation was to establish a new country for Muslims. However, the dividing lines were often drawn arbitrarily, based on incomplete information, or were simply destined to be problematic, given the extensive intermingling of religious communities. A prime example is the vast state of Punjab, which was cleaved in two. Subsequently, millions of people undertook arduous journeys across these newly formed borders in both directions – many perishing in the attempt – to reach the country that aligned with their religious identity. These historical factors significantly explain the profound similarities and shared culinary heritage evident in both Pakistani and Indian food, particularly within the Punjab region.

**Norway: Celebrating the Midsummer Peak**

The Midsummer festival, celebrated across Scandinavia including Norway, is perhaps a bit of a misnomer. It actually takes place around the summer solstice, the first day of summer. Regardless of the precise timing, it undeniably marks the peak of the year, characterized by exceptionally long days and gardens bursting with an abundance of fruits and vegetables. We opted to hold our celebration a few weeks earlier. This timing proved fortuitous for Portland's seasons, as it allowed us to enjoy new potatoes, strawberries, and rhubarb at their absolute best. It was certainly a departure from the traditional timing, but perfectly suited to our local harvest.

**Oman: A Geopolitical Standalone with Rich Maritime History**

Among the 193 member states of the United Nations, Oman is the sole country whose name begins with the letter "O." Geopolitically, it also occupies a somewhat unique position. Oman maintains a considerably lower profile compared to its neighbors in the Middle East. It lacks the ostentatious displays of wealth seen in some other sultanates and has avoided the internal strife that has plagued certain neighboring nations. However, Oman possesses a rich and storied history, largely due to its strategic location at the mouth of the Persian Gulf. For centuries, Omani traders navigated the Indian Ocean, establishing extensive trade routes. This maritime prowess continued until the mid-20th century, shaping the nation's economy and cultural identity.

**Niger: A Sahelian Nation and a Humanitarian Focus**

Niger, a nation situated in the Sahel region, lies to the north of its much more widely recognized neighbor, Nigeria. To avoid confusion, its English pronunciation mirrors the French: "nee-ZHEHR," and the demonym is "Nigerien," with the subtle addition of the second "E" providing the necessary distinction. As a Sahelian nation, Niger occupies a transitional zone between the vast Sahara Desert and the humid tropical coast of West Africa. Mercy Corps, our chosen fundraising beneficiary, undertakes significant work in Niger. Their efforts encompass both emergency food relief and initiatives aimed at enhancing the long-term resilience of local farmers. Joining us for this particular culinary exploration was...
