query: What specific elements of Rand Paul's op-ed appear to be plagiarized from Dan Stewart's article in The Week?

documents.text: Share On more Share On more Share On more Share On more Sections of an op-ed Kentucky Sen. Rand Paul wrote on mandatory minimums in The Washington Times in September appear nearly identical to an article by Dan Stewart of The Week that ran a week earlier. The discovery comes amid reports from BuzzFeed that Paul plagiarized in his book and in several speeches. Paul also delivered testimony before the Senate Judiciary Committee on Sept. 16, 2013, that included the copied sections. Here's how Stewart wrote his introduction to his article on "Rethinking mandatory sentencing": It's the automatic imposition of a minimum number of years in prison for specific crimes — usually related to drugs. By design, mandatory sentencing laws take discretion away from prosecutors and judges so as to impose harsh sentences, regardless of circumstances. Mandatory sentencing began in the 1970s as a response to a growing drug-and-crime epidemic, and over the decades has put hundreds of thousands of people behind bars for drug possession and sale, and other non-violent crimes. Since mandatory sentencing began, America's prison population has quadrupled, to 2.4 million. America now jails a higher percentage of its citizens than any other country, including China and Iran, at the staggering cost of $80 billion a year. Is that a good thing? Most public officials — including liberals, conservatives, and libertarians — have decided that it's not. At least 20 states, both red and blue, have reformed their mandatory sentencing laws in some way, and Congress is considering a bipartisan bill that would do the same for federal crimes. And here's how Paul wrote it a week later with the text bolded that appears copied: Mandatory-minimum sentences automatically impose a minimum number of years in prison for specific crimes — usually related to drugs. By design, mandatory-sentencing laws take discretion away from prosecutors and judges so as to impose harsh sentences, regardless of circumstances. Since mandatory sentencing began in the 1970s in response to a growing drug-and-crime epidemic, America's prison population has quadrupled, to 2.4 million. America now jails a higher percentage of its citizens than any other country, including China and Iran, at the staggering cost of $80 billion a year. Drug offenders in the United States spend more time under the criminal justice system's formal control than drug offenders anywhere else in the world. Most public officials — liberals, conservatives and libertarians — have decided that mandatory-minimum sentencing is unnecessary. At least 20 states, both red and blue, have reformed their mandatory-sentencing laws in some way, and Congress is considering a bipartisan bill that would do the same for federal crimes. Another section of the article related to John Horner, a father of three who sold some pain pills to a police informant. Here's how The Week wrote it, again with bolded text on the copied sections: When a friend asked John Horner if he could buy some painkillers, the 46-year-old father of three didn't see a problem. The Osceola County, Fla., resident had been taking prescribed painkillers for years after losing his eye in an accident, and agreed to sell his friend, "Matt," four unused bottles. After the pills exchanged hands, Horner discovered that "Matt" was in fact a police informant, and he was charged with dealing drugs. At the advice of his public defender, Horner pleaded guilty, and was later sentenced to the mandatory minimum of 25 years in jail. He will be 72 by the time he is released, and his three young children will have grown up without him. "Matt," who turned out to have a long history of drug offenses, was more fortunate — he received a reduced sentence of just 18 months after informing on Horner, and is now free. And here's how Paul wrote it: John Horner was a 46-year-old father of three when he sold some of his prescription painkillers to a friend. His friend turned out to be a police informant, and he was charged with dealing drugs. Horner pleaded guilty and was later sentenced to the mandatory minimum of 25 years in prison. John will be 72 years old by the time he is released, and his three young children will have grown up without him. The informant, who had a long history of drug offenses, was more fortunate — he received a reduced sentence of just 18 months, and is now free. "We've always known that the audience of The Week consists of smart, busy people who want to feel even smarter, including a lot of people on Capitol Hill," said Bill Falk, the editor in chief of The Week. "We'd like to thank Sen. Paul for his endorsement.'' Paul's office did not return a request for comment.

This is an update of a Street Sweeper article originally published on March 16, 2011. On March 14, 2011, a federal Indictment filed in the Southern District of Ohio charged Steve T. Kinder , 51, and Kinder Caviar Inc . with illegally harvesting paddlefish from Ohio waters and falsely reporting to the Kentucky Department of Fish & Wildlife Resources that he caught the fish in Kentucky; and , 51, and . with illegally harvesting paddlefish from Ohio waters and falsely reporting to the Kentucky Department of Fish & Wildlife Resources that he caught the fish in Kentucky; and Cornelia Joyce Kinder , 53, as well as Kinder Caviar Inc. and Black Star Caviar Company with providing false information about the paddlefish eggs to the U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service in order to obtain permits to export the paddlefish eggs to foreign customers, including the amount of paddlefish eggs to be exported, the names of the fishermen that harvested the paddlefish, and the location where the paddlefish were harvested. , 53, as well as Kinder Caviar Inc. and with providing false information about the paddlefish eggs to the U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service in order to obtain permits to export the paddlefish eggs to foreign customers, including The Kinders, husband and wife, both owned and operated Kinder Caviar and Black Star Caviar. Those companies were in the business of exporting paddlefish eggs as caviar to customers in foreign countries. The alleged violations occurred between March 2006 and December 2010. If convicted, the Kinders face a maximum penalty of five years in prison, a $250,000 fine or both on each count. The companies could be fined up to $500,000 per count. NOTE: The charges contained in the indictments are merely accusations and defendants are presumed innocent unless and until proven guilty at trial beyond a reasonable doubt. SIDE BAR: The American paddlefish (Polydon spathula), also called the Mississippi paddlefish or the spoonbill is a freshwater fish that is primarily found in the Mississippi River drainage system. Paddlefish eggs are marketed as caviar. Paddlefish were once common in waters throughout the Midwest. However, the global decline in other caviar sources, such as sturgeon, has led to an increased demand for paddlefish caviar, which has resulted in over-fishing and, as a consequence, the decline of the paddlefish population. Paddlefish are protected by both federal and Ohio law. It is illegal to harvest paddlefish in Ohio waters, but they can be harvested legally in Kentucky waters. Notwithstanding the above criminal allegations, the State of Kentucky's Department of Agriculture maintains a webpage touting Kinder Caviar. We are advised that: Caspian Sea style caviar made right here in the Bluegrass! American caviar now comparable to the caviars of the Caspian Sea! [A]s the catch and quotas of Caspian Sea caviar continue to decline, prices will steadily increase. Kinder Caviar is fast becoming the choice for the caviar savvy connoisseur. . . Also, if you visit http://Kentucky.gov, you will learn that in October 2008, Kentucky Governor Steven L. Beshear (D)appointed five members to the state's Aquaculture Task Force. The official announcement lists among those appointees: Joyce Kinder, of Perry Park, is the owner of Kinder Caviar, Inc. She represents wholesalers of aquaculture products. In the October 2008 edition of the Legislative Record the following is noted under the heading: INTERIM JOINT COMMITTEE ON AGRICULTURE AND NATURAL RESOURCES / Minutes of the 4th Meeting of the 2008 Interim /September 12, 2008: Finally, Representative McKee noted that Ms. Joyce Kinder, of Kinder Caviar, Inc.; had signed up to speak to the committee to address concerns regarding some paddlefish administrative regulations. Ms. Kinder agreed that regulations are necessary but would like to see some provisions amended. She specifically noted the revocation of a fisherman’s license or permit could occur even though water boundaries were inaccurate because of GPS positioning. She stated they feel that license revocation is extremely harsh. She also noted that they could not agree upon snagging methods. She said that under the current regulation all snagging methods must stop. Finally, Ms. Kinder noted disagreement with the size limit, noting that they do not want to hold up the process but would like the committee to take a further look at the regulation. . . Ain't it grand? The world is best by terrorism, civil war, earthquakes, tsunamis, and nuclear catastrophe but we still have time to go after alleged caviar bandits in the murky waters of Ohio and Kentucky. Where's Neil Diamond when we need him? Perhaps he could pen Kentucky Caviar as his next hit? Neil, call me. Let's chat. Do lunch. I'll bring the caviar. UPDATE On January 17, 2012, Steven and Cornelia Joyce Kinder, and their caviar companies pleaded guilty in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Ohio to trafficking in and falsely labeling illegally harvested paddlefish. According to the plea agreement Cornelia Joyce Kinder admitted to making false statements in CITES Export Registration Forms for paddlefish eggs on behalf of: Kinder Caviar : on or about March 15, 2007, she misrepresented the amount of legally-harvested paddlefish eggs that she could provide documentation for, as well as misidentified the fishermen who harvested the paddlefish and the location of harvest; and : on or about March 15, 2007, she misrepresented the amount of legally-harvested paddlefish eggs that she could provide documentation for, as well as misidentified the fishermen who harvested the paddlefish and the location of harvest; and Black Star Caviar Company: on or about Dec. 18, 2010m she completed the form using the name of a subordinate employee and forged that employee’s signature on the form in order to give the impression that she was not the applicant. According to the plea agreement, both Steve Kinder and Cornelia Joyce Kinder admitted to aiding and abetting one another in harvesting paddlefish that they should have known were harvested in Ohio waters in violation of Ohio law through the use of gill nets attached to the Ohio shoreline, on or about May 5, 2007, and, thereafter, transporting the paddlefish to Kentucky with the intent to sell them when. As part of a plea agreement, both Kinder Caviar and Black Star Caviar Company have each agreed to pay a $5,000 fine and serve a three-year term of probation, during which time those companies will be prohibited from applying for or receiving a CITES Export Permit. Additionally, Steve and Cornelia Joyce Kinder have agreed to serve a three-year term of probation, during which time they will each perform 100 hours of community service, be prohibited from fishing anywhere in the Ohio River where that river forms the border between Ohio and Kentucky, and be prohibited from applying for or receiving a CITES Export Permit, either on behalf of themselves or anyone else. In accordance with Kentucky law, the Kinders face possible suspension of their Kentucky commercial fishing licenses. Finally, as part of the plea agreement, the boat and truck that were used in furtherance of the Lacey Act crimes have been forfeited.

ANALYSIS/OPINION: [Editor’s Note: Portions of the following article should have been attributed to “Rethinking mandatory sentencing,” an article written by Dan Stewart that appeared in The Week on Sept. 14, 2013.] If I told you that in America almost 1 million black Americans were forever forbidden from voting, you might think I was talking about Jim Crow 50 years ago, but you would be wrong. According to the Sentencing Project, a staggering number of nonviolent individuals who have been released from prison, are not on probation or parole and who have committed no further crimes, are forever prohibited from voting. Many black Americans are prevented from ever voting because of the war on drugs. On Wednesday, I testified before the Senate Judiciary Committee and described the injustice and effects of mandatory-minimum sentences. These sentences are disproportionally affecting minorities and low-income communities. A recent report from the American Civil Liberties Union reports that blacks are four to five times more likely to be convicted for drug possession. Some dismiss this because they think blacks are committing more drug crimes, but in June, The New York Times reported that although black Americans were four times more likely to be arrested than whites for marijuana possession, both groups used the drug at similar rates. Why are the arrest rates so lopsided? One widely cited study by The San Jose Mercury News reviewed 700,000 criminal cases that were matched by crime and criminal history of the defendant. The analysis revealed that similarly situated whites were far more successful than blacks and Hispanics in the plea-bargaining process. Today, the United States incarcerates more people than any other country in the world, and the racial disparity in arrest rates has been absolutely devastating to the black community. Professor Michelle Alexander has even called the war on drugs “the New Jim Crow.” It’s not just black Americans. Regardless of the color of your skin, the war on drugs has ruined the lives of thousands of young people. I know a man about my age in Kentucky who grew marijuana plants in his apartment closet in college. Thirty years later, he still can’t vote, can’t own a gun, and when he looks for work, he must check the box that basically says: “I’m a convicted felon, and I guess I’ll always be one.” Getting a job is nearly impossible for him. John Horner was a 46-year-old father of three when he sold some of his prescription painkillers to a friend. His friend turned out to be a police informant, and he was charged with dealing drugs. Horner pleaded guilty and was later sentenced to the mandatory minimum of 25 years in prison. John will be 72 years old by the time he is released, and his three young children will have grown up without him. The informant, who had a long history of drug offenses, was more fortunate — he received a reduced sentence of just 18 months, and is now free. So many judges oppose mandatory-minimum sentencing precisely because such an arbitrary law does not take into account that each case is different. I want to be clear: I am not advocating for any type of get-out-of-jail-free passes for individuals who break the law. I am simply arguing that the federal government should get out of the way, and allow local and state judges to do their jobs. Mandatory-minimum sentences automatically impose a minimum number of years in prison for specific crimes — usually related to drugs. By design, mandatory-sentencing laws take discretion away from prosecutors and judges so as to impose harsh sentences, regardless of circumstances. Since mandatory sentencing began in the 1970s in response to a growing drug-and-crime epidemic, America’s prison population has quadrupled, to 2.4 million. America now jails a higher percentage of its citizens than any other country, including China and Iran, at the staggering cost of $80 billion a year. Drug offenders in the United States spend more time under the criminal justice system’s formal control than drug offenders anywhere else in the world. Most public officials — liberals, conservatives and libertarians — have decided that mandatory-minimum sentencing is unnecessary. At least 20 states, both red and blue, have reformed their mandatory-sentencing laws in some way, and Congress is considering a bipartisan bill that would do the same for federal crimes. About 1.3 million people — more than half the total prison population — are behind bars for nonviolent crimes, and federal prisons are 40 percent over capacity. “It’s a waste of tax dollars and human lives,” said Anthony Papa of the Drug Policy Alliance. It’s time for these unjust laws to end. On March 20, I introduced the Justice Safety Valve Act of 2013 with the Sen. Patrick J. Leahy, Vermont Democrat. We have been joined by Sens. Carl Levin, Michigan Democrat; Angus King, Maine independent; and Kristen Gillibrand, New York Democrat, in the Senate, and Rep. Robert C. “Bobby” Scott, Virginia Democrat, joined by 11 others, introduced similar legislation in the House. The legislation is short and simple. It amends current law to provide “authority to impose a sentence below a statutory mandatory minimum” if certain requirements are met. Mandatory-minimum sentencing has done little to address the very real problem of drug abuse, while also doing great damage by destroying so many lives. Each case should be judged on its own merits, yet mandatory minimums prevent this from happening. The Justice Safety Valve Act will be an important step in improving justice in our nation’s courtrooms. Sen. Rand Paul, Kentucky Republican, is a member of the Senate Foreign Relations and Homeland Security committees.

A significant concern has arisen regarding potential plagiarism in Senator Rand Paul's 2012 book, "Government Bullies." While previous reports from BuzzFeed highlighted instances of uncredited material from The Heritage Foundation and the Cato Institute, a closer examination reveals a striking similarity between a section of Paul's book and a Forbes article published earlier in the same year. This particular passage, detailing a legal case involving a Kentucky-based caviar company, appears to have been lifted directly from the Forbes piece without proper attribution.

The case in question involved Steven and Cornelia Joyce Kinder, owners of Kinder Caviar. They pleaded guilty to charges related to harvesting paddlefish in Ohio waters with the intent to sell them in Kentucky, a violation of the Lacey Act. The Forbes article, authored by Bill Singer, meticulously outlined the details of the plea agreement. Singer's article stated: "According to the plea agreement, both Steve Kinder and Cornelia Joyce Kinder admitted to aiding and abetting one another in harvesting paddlefish that they should have known were harvested in Ohio waters in violation of Ohio law through the use of gill nets attached to the Ohio shoreline, on or about May 5, 2007, and, thereafter, transporting the paddlefish to Kentucky with the intent to sell them."

The Forbes report further elaborated on the penalties and restrictions imposed as part of the plea agreement. It detailed that "both Kinder Caviar and Black Star Caviar Company have each agreed to pay a $5,000 fine and serve a three-year term of probation, during which time those companies will be prohibited from applying for or receiving a CITES Export Permit." The article also specified the individual consequences for the Kinders: "Additionally, Steve and Cornelia Joyce Kinder have agreed to serve a three-year term of probation, during which time they will each perform 100 hours of community service, be prohibited from fishing anywhere in the Ohio River where that river forms the border between Ohio and Kentucky, and be prohibited from applying for or receiving a CITES Export Permit, either on behalf of themselves or anyone else." The article concluded by noting that "In accordance with Kentucky law, the Kinders face possible suspension of their Kentucky commercial fishing licenses. Finally, as part of the plea agreement, the boat and truck that were used in furtherance of the Lacey Act crimes have been forfeited."

Now, let's examine how Senator Paul presented this information in "Government Bullies." The corresponding section in his book reads: "The Justice Department's plea agreement stated that both Steve Kinder and Cornelia Joyce Kinder admitted to aiding and abetting one another in harvesting the fish. The agreement stated that they should have known these paddlefish were harvested in Ohio waters in violation of state law, through the use of gill nets attached to the Ohio shoreline. This happened on or about May 5, 2007, and thereafter the Kinders had transported the paddlefish to Kentucky with intent to sell them."

Following this, Senator Paul inserted his own commentary, which also bears a striking resemblance to Singer's. Singer had written: "Ain't it grand? The world is best by terrorism, civil war, earthquakes, tsunamis, and nuclear catastrophe but we still have time to go after alleged caviar bandits in the murky waters of Ohio and Kentucky." He contrasted this with broader national issues: "We have a trillion-dollar deficit hole in our budget. We have bridges literally falling down and an infrastructure that is crumbling. We have a military that is stretched entirely too thin. We have a broken entitlement system, and a bunch of Washington 'leaders' too scared or inept to do anything about it. We have a border so porous that illegal immigrants and terrorists alike could be coming across it at any moment. We are told it cannot be fixed because we don't have the manpower. Yet somehow, some way, we have the manpower and resources to prosecute people for the following major 'crimes.'"

Senator Paul's book included this passage: "Now, if you're like me, obviously we are getting down on our hands and knees to thank our federal government for addressing this pressing matter. But it gets even more ridiculous." He then continued with a section that mirrors Singer's critique of national priorities: "We have a trillion-dollar deficit hole in our budget. We have bridges literally falling down and an infrastructure that is crumbling. We have a military that is stretched entirely too thin. We have a broken entitlement system, and a bunch of Washington 'leaders' too scared or inept to do anything about it. We are told it cannot be fixed because we don't have the manpower. Yet somehow, some way, we have the manpower and resources to prosecute people for the following major 'crimes.'"

The similarities extend to the description of the penalties. Paul's book states: "As part of a plea agreement, both Kinder Caviar and Black Star Caviar Company have each agreed to pay a $5,000 fine and serve a three-year term of probation, during which time those companies will be prohibited from applying for or receiving a CITES Export Permit. Additionally, Steve and Cornelia Joyce Kinder have agreed to serve a three-year term of probation, during which time they will each perform one hundred hours of community service, be prohibited from fishing anywhere in the Ohio River, where that river forms the border between Ohio and Kentucky, and be prohibited from applying for or receiving a CITES Export Permit, either on behalf of themselves or anyone else. In accordance with Kentucky law, the Kinders also face possible suspension of their Kentucky commercial fishing licenses. Finally, as part of the plea agreement, the boat and truck that were used in furtherance of the Lacey Act crimes have been forfeited."

While Paul's book included a link to the Forbes article in its footnotes, it did not explicitly indicate that the text itself, beyond the general source, had been directly copied. This practice, where a source is cited but the verbatim text is presented as original, is a common characteristic of plagiarism.

In response to inquiries about this specific instance, Paul's office initially stated that "there have also been occasions where quotations or typesetting indentations have been left out through errors in our approval process." They further assured that "a new approval process implemented by Sen. Paul will ensure proper citation and accountability in all collaborative works going forward."

Bill Singer, the author of the Forbes article, expressed surprise when asked if he was aware of his work being used. He noted that he had relied heavily on a press release from the Justice Department for his reporting. However, Singer observed, "It would appear whoever wrote the senator's book copied my language not the press release. Plagiarism is a very sincere form of flattery." He pointed out that even the more opinionated, sarcastic portions of his article, which critiqued the government's focus on the caviar case amidst larger national problems, were mirrored in Paul's book. This suggests a direct appropriation of Singer's phrasing and tone, rather than a mere paraphrasing of facts derived from the original press release. The parallel structure and similar sentiment in their critiques of government priorities underscore the extent of the textual overlap. This situation raises questions about the editorial oversight and the integrity of the attribution process within Senator Paul's book. The inclusion of a footnote, while a step towards acknowledging a source, does not absolve the author or publisher from the responsibility of clearly indicating when direct quotes or substantial portions of text have been used. The distinction between citing a source and properly attributing borrowed language is crucial for academic and journalistic integrity. The repeated instances of uncredited material, as reported by BuzzFeed and now further detailed with this Forbes article comparison, suggest a pattern that warrants careful consideration regarding the book's overall credibility and the author's commitment to ethical writing practices. The senator's office's acknowledgment of errors and commitment to a new approval process are noted, but the original instances remain a point of concern for readers and critics alike. The case of the Kinder caviar company, while a minor legal matter in the grand scheme of national issues, became a focal point for discussions on government overreach and resource allocation, and the way this narrative was presented in "Government Bullies" is now under scrutiny for its own narrative construction and sourcing.

Senator Rand Paul has admitted to errors in sourcing material within his published writings, including a column for The Washington Times. This acknowledgment follows recent accusations of plagiarism. Mr. Paul stated he takes personal responsibility for these oversights. He and his aides explained that staff provided him with background materials that lacked proper footnotes.

However, the Kentucky Republican, who is considered a potential 2016 presidential candidate, also suggested the news media is applying a higher standard to him than to other politicians. "The standard I’m being held to is a little different than everybody else," Mr. Paul commented on CNN's "The Situation Room." He elaborated, "They’re now going back and reading every book from cover to cover and looking for places where we footnoted correctly and don’t have quotation marks in the right places or we didn’t indent correctly."

The Washington Times confirmed on Tuesday that an independent review of Mr. Paul's columns and op-eds led to a correction for his September 20th column. In that piece, the senator failed to attribute a passage that had previously appeared in The Week. The newspaper and Senator Paul have mutually agreed to end his weekly column, which had been published every Friday since the summer.

John Solomon, Editor of The Washington Times, stated, "We expect our columnists to submit original work and to properly attribute material, and we appreciate that the senator and his staff have taken responsibility for an oversight in one column." He added, "We also appreciate the original insights he has shared with our readers over the last few months and look forward to future contributions from Sen. Paul and any other members of Congress who take the time to help educate our readers."

The website BuzzFeed reported that a section of Mr. Paul's 2012 book, "Government Bullies," seemed to borrow from an earlier Forbes article. BuzzFeed also first reported that Mr. Paul had copied parts of an opinion piece on mandatory sentencing, titled "The devastating effect of a drug-war weapon," which appeared in The Washington Times, from an article published in The Week.

Mr. Paul has been a frequent writer of op-eds in recent years, contributing hundreds of pieces to The Times and other media outlets. However, the entirety of his published work is now under scrutiny by journalists who have uncovered multiple instances of him using others' work without proper credit.

In a statement released to The Times and other media outlets on Tuesday, Doug Stafford, an advisor to Mr. Paul, asserted that the senator's ideas are his own. He explained that Mr. Paul relied on staff to furnish "supporting facts and anecdotes — some of which were clearly not sourced or vetted." Stafford further noted, "Footnotes presenting supporting facts were not always used." He assured that "Going forward, footnotes will be available on request."

Regarding the specific instance in The Times, Mr. Paul had written, "By design, mandatory sentencing laws take discretion away from prosecutors and judges so as to impose harsh sentences, regardless of circumstances." BuzzFeed discovered that this exact sentence appeared in an article by Dan Steward in The Week.

Although Mr. Stafford initially characterized the scrutiny of Mr. Paul to BuzzFeed as a "witch hunt," additional examples of sourcing issues continued to emerge. Tuesday's announcement appears to signify a strategic move by Mr. Paul to address and move past these controversies.

Mandatory sentencing laws dictate a fixed minimum prison term for specific offenses, most commonly drug-related crimes. These laws are intentionally designed to remove judicial and prosecutorial discretion, ensuring severe penalties are applied regardless of individual circumstances. The origins of mandatory sentencing can be traced back to the 1970s, a period marked by a perceived surge in drug use and associated crime. Over the subsequent decades, these policies have led to the incarceration of hundreds of thousands of individuals, often for non-violent offenses such as drug possession and sale.

The impact of mandatory sentencing on America's correctional system has been profound. Since the implementation of these laws, the nation's prison population has more than quadrupled, reaching an estimated 2.4 million individuals. This has positioned the United States as a country with the highest incarceration rate globally, surpassing even nations like China and Iran, and incurring an annual cost of approximately $80 billion.

The effectiveness and fairness of these policies are increasingly being questioned. A broad consensus among public officials, spanning liberal, conservative, and libertarian viewpoints, suggests that mandatory sentencing is not a beneficial approach. In response, at least 20 states, representing both Republican and Democratic leadership, have undertaken reforms to their mandatory sentencing laws. Furthermore, federal legislative efforts are underway, with a bipartisan bill currently being considered by Congress that aims to modify federal sentencing guidelines. Even before federal law changes, U.S. Attorney General Eric Holder issued a directive to federal prosecutors, advising them to refrain from presenting evidence that could trigger mandatory sentences for offenders with no prior significant criminal history. Holder articulated this stance by stating, "Too many Americans go to too many prisons for far too long, and for no truly good law-enforcement reason."

The genesis of mandatory sentencing in the United States can be attributed to New York State approximately 40 years ago, as a direct response to the escalating heroin epidemic. In an effort to project a strong stance against crime, Republican Governor Nelson Rockefeller enacted mandatory minimum sentences of 15 years to life for individuals found in possession of as little as 4 ounces of narcotics. These "Rockefeller Drug Laws," introduced in 1973, immediately sparked controversy. Critics, including many liberals and drug reform advocates, argued that these laws unfairly equated casual drug users with hardened drug traffickers. Nevertheless, the perceived need for stringent measures against drug-related offenses led other states to adopt similar legislation. Michigan, for instance, implemented an even more severe "650-lifer" law in 1978, mandating a life sentence for anyone apprehended with over 650 grams of narcotics.

The federal government followed suit in 1986 by enacting its own mandatory sentencing legislation. This action was largely prompted by the tragic cocaine overdose of Len Bias, a highly anticipated top draft pick for the Boston Celtics. In the wake of public grief and a desire to demonstrate that the Democratic party could also adopt a tough-on-crime posture, House Speaker Tip O'Neill swiftly guided the Anti-Drug Abuse Act through Congress. This legislation imposed a mandatory minimum five-year sentence for first-time offenders caught with 5 grams of crack cocaine, and a ten-year sentence for those found with 50 grams. Individuals convicted of participating in a "continuing criminal enterprise" involved in drug trafficking faced a mandatory 20-year sentence, regardless of their position in the drug operation, from the highest-ranking kingpin to the lowest-level courier or lookout.

The efficacy of these laws in achieving their stated goals is a subject of ongoing debate. Some law enforcement officials contend that mandatory sentencing has been instrumental in removing major drug traffickers from the streets and has contributed to the significant reduction in crime rates observed since the 1990s. However, a substantial body of critics argues that these laws have demonstrably failed to curb the flow of illicit drugs. Instead, they contend, these policies have led to the overcrowding of prisons with individuals convicted of non-violent offenses. Currently, approximately 1.3 million people, representing over half of the total prison population, are incarcerated for non-violent crimes. Federal correctional facilities are operating at 40% over capacity. Anthony Papa of the Drug Policy Alliance succinctly described the situation as "a waste of tax dollars and human lives."

The demographic profile of those incarcerated under these laws is particularly concerning. A significant majority of individuals serving time for drug offenses are African-American men. Despite comprising only 13% of the general population, Black individuals constitute over 50% of those imprisoned for drug convictions. Statistics reveal that Black individuals are four times more likely than white individuals to be arrested for marijuana possession and typically receive sentences that are 20% longer than those imposed on white offenders for similar crimes. This phenomenon of mass incarceration has had a devastating impact on Black communities across the nation. Alarmingly, one in nine Black children has a parent currently incarcerated. Ron Daniels, a lecturer at York College, City University of New York, characterized the "War on Drugs" as "ill-fated," stating that it has "disrupted families, decimated communities, and rendered America's 'dark ghettos' zones of desperation, desolation, and despair."

In response to these widespread concerns, reformers are advocating for a fundamental shift in sentencing policy. Their primary objective is to abolish mandatory sentences for non-violent offenses and to reinstate judicial discretion, allowing judges to exercise their judgment and consider individual circumstances when sentencing for minor drug crimes. Several states have already enacted reforms, including Ohio, Georgia, Pennsylvania, Hawaii, New York, and North Dakota. California voters, through a ballot initiative, repealed the state's controversial "three strikes" law, which mandated a life sentence for a third felony conviction, irrespective of the severity of the offense. Even states with traditionally conservative approaches to criminal justice are exploring alternatives to lengthy prison terms. Texas, for example, has reallocated funds originally designated for prison construction to establish drug courts, implement electronic monitoring programs, and enhance parole supervision for non-violent offenders. This strategic shift has resulted in the closure of three state prisons, and the state's crime rate has fallen to its lowest point since 1973. Senator Rand Paul (R-Ky.), a co-sponsor of federal reform legislation, has emphasized the financial and ethical drawbacks of mandatory minimums, stating, "Mandatory minimums are costly, unfair, and do not make our country safer." He further noted, "The American people are ready to make sentencing reform a priority."

The personal toll of mandatory sentencing is starkly illustrated by the case of John Horner, a 46-year-old father of three. Horner, who had been prescribed painkillers for years following an eye injury, agreed to sell four unused bottles of his medication to a friend. Unbeknownst to Horner, his "friend" was a police informant, leading to his arrest and charge of drug dealing. Following the advice of his public defender, Horner pleaded guilty and received the mandatory minimum sentence of 25 years in prison. He will be 72 years old upon his release, having missed his children growing up. In a stark contrast, the informant, who had a history of drug offenses, received a reduced sentence of just 18 months after cooperating with authorities and is now a free man. This case highlights the disproportionate impact of mandatory sentencing, where individuals with minor involvement can face severe penalties while those who facilitate investigations receive leniency.

Rand Paul's campaign is facing scrutiny over the sourcing of his published works. He recently addressed a passage in his Washington Times article, which he stated was adapted from a speech. This adaptation, he explained, contributed to a lack of thorough vetting. Paul emphasized that he takes this situation more seriously than previous disclosures.

One such disclosure occurred last week on MSNBC's "The Rachel Maddow Show." Maddow highlighted a speech from the previous Monday that incorporated portions of the Wikipedia description for the 1990s movie "Gattaca." This film depicts a science fiction future where social status is determined by DNA testing. The following day, Paul argued that this instance did not technically constitute plagiarism. He clarified, "Trying to say someone commits plagiarism, you’re saying that someone is dishonest." He added, "And, well, it would be dishonest if I tried to say, ‘Oh, I had this great idea for a movie, and this is my idea, and this is a story I wrote in college called ‘Gattaca.’ ”

Paul attributed the unattributed passages in his Washington Times article to the fast-paced environment of a new national political figure. Kevin Madden, a communications aide for Mitt Romney's presidential campaign, commented on the increasing pressure Paul faces. Madden stated, "This is what it looks like when you move from having just a Kentucky profile or just a Capitol Hill profile to building a national profile." He suggested that this pressure is unlikely to diminish.

Further complicating matters, BuzzFeed uncovered a passage in Forbes magazine that appeared in Paul's book, "Government Bullies." In some instances, the text was reproduced word-for-word. While this material was cited in the book's appendix, it was not clearly identified within the main text. Paul, without directly referencing this specific passage, explained that his book typically signaled cited material through a narrow typeset. He acknowledged that in some cases, indentations were omitted when they should have been present.

Paul indicated that more disclosures might emerge, given his extensive collection of writings and speeches. He expressed concern about the longevity of this issue, stating, "That’s why it’s like, how does this ever go away?" He concluded by saying, "The only way it goes away is that people have to accept what I’m telling them or not accept what I’m telling them — that we’ve never intended to deceive people, and we’ve made mistakes."

Last night on her show, Rachel Maddow addressed Rand Paul's comments from over the weekend when the Senator, while talking to George Stephanopoulos on ABC's This Week, expressed a desire to duel Maddow over her recent claims that Paul plagiarized large parts of his books and speeches from Wikipedia and other sources. (The accusations, by the way, are undeniably true.) "If dueling were legal in Kentucky, if they keep it up, you know, it would be a duel challenge," Paul said. "But I can’t do that, because I can’t hold office in Kentucky then.” Advertisement Now Maddow has responded to the Kentucky Republican's wimpy half-threat and, no, her reply wasn't "Pistols at dawn." Instead, she did what real adults do: She told Paul to grow up already and face the charges. "Senator Rand Paul wants to shoot at me or stab me with a sword or something for reporting something true that he has done wrong as a politician," Maddow told her audience. "Responding to the person rather than the charge is a time-tested tactic. Honestly, it's a symptom of immaturity in our political discourse." Visit NBCNews.com for breaking news, world news, and news about the economy Sponsored Worth noting: Senator Paul was charged again with plagiarism Monday night, this time for an op-ed that he wrote for the Washington Times in which he appears to have lifted lines directly from the Dan Stewart article "Rethinking Mandatory Sentencing."

query: What incidents led to the fatal stabbings on the MAX train in Portland?

documents.text:
### Strangers Intervene in Portland MAX Train Hate Crime, Sacrificing Lives

A horrific incident on a Portland MAX train saw two individuals lose their lives and another injured after they bravely intervened to protect two young girls. The bystanders stepped in when a man began shouting hateful messages at the girls. One of the targeted individuals, 16-year-old Destinee Mangum, has expressed her profound gratitude to the heroes who defended her.

#### The Attack Unfolds

Mangum recounted that she and her 17-year-old friend were on the train when a man, later identified as Jeremy Joseph Christian, 35, approached them. He began yelling what witnesses described as hate speech and bias language. Mangum, whose friend is Muslim, stated that the man told them to "go back to Saudi Arabia" and that they "shouldn't be here, to get out of his country." He also made demeaning remarks, suggesting they were "nothing" and should "kill ourselves."

#### Bystanders Step In

Feeling scared, Mangum and her friend moved to the back of the train. It was then that a stranger intervened. According to Mangum, the man said, "he’s talking to you guys? You guys can’t disrespect these young ladies like that." This led to an argument.

#### Tragic Consequences

The situation escalated tragically. As Mangum and her friend prepared to exit the train, they turned to see the fight. The assailant then began stabbing people, creating a scene of chaos and bloodshed. Mangum described it as "blood everywhere" and recalled them "just started running for our lives."

#### Gratitude and Remembrance

Mangum's mother, Dyjuana Hudson, expressed her deep debt to the two men who were killed and offered condolences to their grieving families. "I want to say thank you so much," Hudson said. "I couldn’t imagine what you’re going through right now as far as losing someone and I’m sorry it had to be at the hands of my children."

Destinee Mangum herself conveyed a heartfelt message to the strangers who became heroes. "I just want to say thank you to the people who put their life on the line for me, because they didn’t even know me and they lost their lives because of me and my friend and the way we look," she stated. "And I just want to say thank you to them and their family and that I appreciate them because without them, we probably would be dead right now."
