The Internet Archive's "No More 404" initiative actively crawls and archives web pages, along with the content they link to. This proactive approach ensures that even if a referenced page is later altered or removed from the live web, a preserved version remains accessible. The Archive aims to replace broken links with these archived snapshots, thereby restoring lost content and allowing users to view the original intent of web page authors. This ambitious project seeks to mend the pervasive issue of broken links across the internet.

Journalist Dan Rather has penned an emotional open letter to President Trump, urging him to publicly address the tragic stabbing attack in Portland. This horrific incident occurred on a MAX train, the city's light-rail system, on Friday. Two men, Ricky John Best, 53, and Taliesin Namkai-Meche, 23, were fatally stabbed. They were attempting to intervene against a white supremacist who was yelling hate speech. The victims were trying to stop the man from harassing two young women who appeared to be Muslim. Police stated that another man, Micah David-Cole Fletcher, sustained injuries during the knife attack. Jeremy Joseph Christian, a 35-year-old man identified as a known white supremacist, was subsequently arrested. He is currently facing charges, including aggravated murder.

Based on the available facts, Rather, the former anchor of CBS Evening News, suggested that this attack could be classified as terrorism. He wrote of the victims, "They were brave Americans who died at the hands of someone who, when all the facts are collected, we may have every right to call a terrorist." He specifically mentioned Best and Namkai-Meche. However, two days after the attack, President Trump had yet to address the incident. The White House had not issued any statements, and the president had not posted any tweets regarding the event.

Rather expressed his belief that this particular story might not align with narratives Trump promoted during his campaign and has continued to echo in the White House. He pointed out, "They were not killed by an undocumented immigrant or a 'radical Islamic terrorist.' They were killed in an act of civic love, facing down a man allegedly spewing hate speech directed at two teenage girls, one of whom was wearing a hijab."

The Portland attack sent shockwaves across the nation due to the extreme violence displayed in a public setting. It was particularly disturbing that the victims were attacked for standing up against hate speech. Reports of hate crimes targeting Muslims have reportedly been increasing, especially since the commencement of Trump's presidential campaign. Rather acknowledged that many experts in this field do not believe this rise is coincidental. He clarified that he does not directly blame the President for this specific incident, nor does he believe others should. However, he emphasized the significant influence a President's words, associates, and the overall tone they set can have on the nation.

Rather implored Trump to acknowledge Best and Namkai-Meche by name, or at the very least, to tweet about their deaths. President Trump returned from the G7 conference in Italy on Saturday night. Despite the gravity of the Portland attack, he reportedly found time to send more than a dozen tweets on various other subjects since the incident. One of his tweets questioned the media's focus on the Montana Congressional race after a Republican victory, stating, "Does anyone notice how the Montana Congressional race was such a big deal to Dems & Fake News until the Republican won? V was poorly covered." The White House did not immediately provide a response to a request for comment. In contrast, Hillary Clinton, Trump's opponent in the 2016 presidential election, did tweet about the attack on Saturday. She expressed her heartbreak, stating, "No one should have to endure this racist abuse. No one should have to give their life to stop it."

... rudence and that standard must apply to the President. But that doesn't mean that those who have not yet been convicted cannot provoke strong suspicions of guilt. That's what drives law enforcement to investigate, when there's a lot of smoke, there's usually a fire. News of the Roger Stone indictment raises the number of close associates, very close associates, to the President who have either been indicted or pled guilty to a staggering number. No one can let themselves become inured to this. There can be no outrage fatigue. Our only historical measuring stick is Watergate. But even that stain on American history did not have the specter of foreign interference from a hostile power like this one. As the walls tighten around President Trump, his reaction in Tweet and tone resembles a mob boss. But with the President, his threats and talk of "rats" are there in the open. Many seasoned prosecutors say these actions clearly pass the bar of witness tampering. For many people, the credibility of President Trump claiming he didn't know or do anything wrong has long since been shattered. This latest news makes any such assertions even harder to take. This isn't a witch hunt of course. But it is a "which hunt", as in which domino will be the next to fall in what is already one of the most sordid chapters in the American story. Innocent until proven guilty. That is the bedrock of American jurisp

The sole survivor of a brutal attack on a crowded MAX train, Micah David-Cole Fletcher, 21, is reportedly on the path to recovery. His survival is attributed in part to the quick actions of a compassionate bystander. Fletcher is continuing to heal from severe knife wounds to his neck, sustained during the May 26th incident. The alleged assailant, Jeremy Joseph Christian, 35, is accused of verbally assaulting a teenage girl wearing a hijab and her friend before turning his violent rage on Fletcher and two other men who intervened.

Christian allegedly attacked Fletcher, Army veteran Rick Best, 53, and recent Reed College graduate Taliesin Myrddin Namkai-Meche, 23, with a knife, inflicting neck wounds on all three. Tragically, Best and Namkai-Meche succumbed to their injuries. Fletcher, an aspiring poet, is showing signs of improvement at an area hospital, according to his girlfriend, Miranda Helm. "He's getting a little bit stronger," Helm shared on Sunday. "He's getting good rest. He's finally able to eat now."

Fletcher's recovery has been aided by several factors:

*   **Medical Care:** Fletcher is receiving treatment at an area hospital for severe knife wounds to his neck.
*   **Bystander Intervention:** A compassionate bystander, described as a veteran with first-aid knowledge, immediately provided aid.
*   **Family Support:** His girlfriend, Miranda Helm, has provided updates on his progress, noting his increasing strength and ability to eat.

Helm also revealed that Fletcher expressed a desire to meet the man he credits with potentially saving his life. This unidentified individual immediately rushed to Fletcher's side after the attack. His actions included:

*   Applying pressure to Fletcher's wound with a cloth.
*   Locating Fletcher's cell phone.
*   Contacting Fletcher's mother and reassuring her of his ability to provide aid.
*   Downplaying the severity of Fletcher's injuries to spare his mother immediate distress, stating he had been stabbed in the arm. This thoughtful deception allowed Fletcher's family to arrive at the hospital with a greater sense of composure, as medical staff had already indicated he would survive.

Fletcher's friends have shared updates on his recovery, including photos on a "Tri Met Hero Recovery" GoFundMe page.

*   One image shows Fletcher giving a "thumbs up" from his hospital bed.
*   Another reveals the extensive nature of his neck wound, stretching from below his chin to under his left ear.

The GoFundMe aims to cover his medical and recovery expenses, with a message expressing Fletcher's gratitude for the overwhelming support.

Meanwhile, new information has emerged regarding Christian's alleged prior behavior:

*   **Previous Confrontation:** Officials reported that Christian reportedly initiated a confrontation at a MAX station the day before the fatal attack, throwing a plastic bottle at a Black woman who then sprayed him with mace. Portland police were aware of this incident and were seeking video evidence.
*   **Criminal History:** Christian's criminal history includes an eight-year prison sentence for robbery, during which he accumulated a significant disciplinary record, including charges of assault and disobedience, according to the Oregon Department of Corrections. He served time in four different state correctional facilities before his release and subsequent post-prison supervision in Multnomah County, which concluded in January 2014.

Acquaintances have described a noticeable shift in Christian's demeanor following his release from prison. Lindsey Self, a registered nurse who knew Christian since they were 17, expressed shock and sadness. She recalled him as a "good guy -- strange at times, but funny and quirky" during their time working together at Pietro's Pizza. However, she noted a change in his behavior in recent years, citing his "rants on Facebook" as unusual. Self believes Christian was mentally ill and needed help, lamenting that friends lost touch and didn't reach out. She expressed hope that the incident would highlight the need for better mental health facilities and outreach, describing Christian as a "victim of the criminal justice system."

Tomica Carr, who has known Christian since elementary school, also expressed surprise at him being labeled a racist, noting he had many Black friends and never disrespected her. However, her daughter reported hearing Christian use the N-word at Montavilla Park in April, which shocked Carr. Carr recalled Christian planning an elementary school reunion with many Black individuals on the invitation list. She described him as the "class clown" in elementary school, prone to acting out. Echoing others, Carr stated that Christian was a changed man after prison, becoming "more nutty."

As of Sunday evening, three separate fundraising efforts for the victims and their families had collectively raised nearly $800,000. One campaign, initiated by Portland resident Nick Zukin, had garnered over $350,000 from more than 7,000 donors. Zukin stated he started the campaign to provide a channel for donations, recognizing the victims for their bravery. "These guys stood up for what was right and paid the ultimate price," he wrote on the site. "I'm glad we are helping their families as a community."

Wajdi Said, president and co-founder of the Muslim Educational Trust, speaks at a news conference after a man fatally stabbed Friday two men on a light-rail train when they tried to stop him from yelling... (Associated Press) Wajdi Said, president and co-founder of the Muslim Educational Trust, speaks at a news conference after a man fatally stabbed Friday two men on a light-rail train when they tried to stop him from yelling anti-Muslim slurs at two young women, one of whom was wearing a hijab, in Portland, Ore., Saturday,... (Associated Press) PORTLAND, Ore. (AP) — Muslims in Portland, Oregon, thanked the community for its support and said they were raising money for the families of two men who were killed when they came to the defense of two young women — one wearing a hijab — who were targeted by an anti-Muslim rant. "I am very thankful as a Muslim, I am very thankful as a Portlander ... that we stand together here as one," Muhammad A. Najieb, an imam at the Muslim Community Center, said Saturday. The two young women "could have been the victims, but three heroes jumped in and supported them," he said. A fundraising page launched by his group for the families of the dead men, a surviving victim and the two young women had raised more than $300,000 by Sunday evening. Police said they'll examine what appears to be the extremist ideology of suspect Jeremy Joseph Christian, 35, who is accused of killing the two men Friday. Christian's social media postings indicate an affinity for Nazis and political violence. Messages left at the home of Christian's mother were not immediately returned. The attack occurred on a light-rail train on the first day of Ramadan, the holiest time of the year for Muslims. Christian was being held on suspicion of aggravated murder, attempted murder, intimidation and being a felon in possession of a weapon. He was arrested a short time after the attack when he was confronted by other men. One of the victims of the hate speech is sending her thanks to those who came to her defense, according to KPTV (http://bit.ly/2qq2QEy ). Destinee Mangum, 16, told the station on Saturday that she and her 17-year-old friend were riding the train when Jeremy Christian approached them yelling what is described as hate speech. She said her friend is Muslim, but she's not. "He told us to go back to Saudi Arabia, and he told us we shouldn't be here, to get out of his country," Mangum said. "He was just telling us that we basically weren't anything and that we should kill ourselves." The girls were scared and moved to the back of the train while a stranger jumped in to help. "Me and my friend were going to get off the MAX and then we turned around while they were fighting and he just started stabbing people and it was just blood everywhere and we just started running for our lives," Mangum said. Alvin Hall said had just stepped off the train on Friday when he saw a man bleeding from the neck, KATU-TV (http://bit.ly/2rcpdwm ) reported. Hall said his instincts kicked in and he went after the suspect. "My first process was, 'What can I do? Where did he go?' and someone said, 'He ran over to the bridge,' " Hall said. "So I just took up running from the bridge up the stairs." He said he met Chase Robinson and Larry Blackwell, and the three men confronted the suspect, who turned on them with a knife. "The minute he saw me he started coming after me. He's like, 'You want some of me, you're a snitch, come on after me, you want some of this?' and started chasing me," Hall said. Soon, police arrived and took the suspect into custody. Christian will make his first court appearance in the case Tuesday, and it wasn't clear if he had an attorney. No one answered the phone at his Portland home. Police identified the men killed as Ricky John Best, 53, of Happy Valley, Oregon, and Taliesin Myrddin Namkai Meche, 23, of Portland. Mayor Ted Wheeler said Best was an Army veteran and a city employee. Meche earned a bachelor's degree in economics in 2016 from Reed College in Portland and landed a job with the Cadmus Group, a consulting firm in the area. Police say Micah David-Cole Fletcher, 21, of Portland was also stabbed and is in serious condition at a Portland hospital. Police say his injuries are not believed to be life-threatening. Fletcher is a student at Portland State University and was taking the train from classes to his job at a pizza shop when the attack occurred. In 2013, Fletcher won a 2013 poetry competition, the Verselandia poetry slam, with a poem condemning prejudices faced by Muslims, according to The Oregonian/OregonLive (http://bit.ly/2s2qPXH ). Police said one of the two young women on the train was wearing a hijab. The assailant was ranting on many topics, using "hate speech or biased language," police Sgt. Pete Simpson said. The FBI said it's too early to say whether the slayings qualify as a federal hate crime. However, Christian faces intimidation charges, the state equivalent of a hate crime. The Portland Mercury, one of the city's alternative weeklies, posted an article on its website saying Christian showed up at a free speech march in late April with a baseball bat to confront protesters and the bat was confiscated by police. The article included video clips of a man wearing a metal chain around his neck and draped in an American flag shouting "I'm a nihilist! This is my safe place!" as protesters crowd around him. The Oregonian/OregonLive also had video from the April 29 march showing Christian. Simpson confirmed the man in the videos was Christian. On what appears to be Christian's Facebook page, he showed sympathy for Nazis and Timothy McVeigh, who bombed a federal building in Oklahoma City in 1995. _____ Follow Gillian Flaccus on Twitter at http://www.twitter.com/gflaccus

**Portland MAX Train Stabbing Suspect Involved in Prior Altercation at TriMet Station**

Jeremy Christian, the suspect in the fatal MAX train stabbings that occurred on Friday, was involved in a separate physical altercation at a TriMet station the day prior to the attack. According to an anonymous source, Christian reportedly threw a bottle of Gatorade at a Black woman at the Interstate Rose Quarter Station on Thursday night around 11:25 p.m. The woman then retaliated by spraying Christian with mace.

The Portland Police Bureau has confirmed to KGW that detectives investigating the Friday stabbings are aware of this earlier incident. Christian, 35, is currently being held on suspicion of aggravated murder, attempted murder, intimidation, and being a felon in possession of a weapon. He was apprehended shortly after the Friday attack.

Authorities have stated that the Friday stabbing incident began when Christian directed racial slurs at two young women, one of whom was wearing a hijab. Several men attempted to intervene, leading to the violent confrontation. The victims of the Friday attack were identified on Saturday as 53-year-old Rick John Best of Happy Valley, 23-year-old Taliesin Myrddin Namkai Meche of Southeast Portland, and 21-year-old Micah David-Cole Fletcher of Southeast Portland. Rick John Best and Taliesin Myrddin Namkai Meche tragically lost their lives. Micah David-Cole Fletcher was released from the hospital on Monday and is currently recovering from his injuries.

© 2017 KGW-TV

The recent racially charged double murder in Portland, Oregon, has intensified existing political tensions in a city already experiencing a surge in street clashes. Following the fatal stabbing of two men who intervened when a suspected white supremacist allegedly hurled hate speech at two women, an "alt-right" organizer is refusing to cancel a planned rally. In response, antifascist activists are vowing to confront them.

Taliesin Myrddin Namkai-Meche, 23, and Rick Best, 53, were killed on a city train after confronting Jeremy Christian, 35, who was reportedly yelling racist and misogynistic slurs. Another man was injured in the attack. Christian is scheduled to appear in court soon.

Despite the tragedy and a strong condemnation from the city's mayor, "alt-right" organizer Joey Gibson, a YouTube personality and self-described libertarian behind the Patriot Prayer group, has insisted on proceeding with a "free speech rally" in downtown Portland. This has prompted self-described "anti-fascist" activists to commit to opposing the event.

Gibson, who organized a rally on April 29th where Christian was filmed making fascist salutes and shouting racial slurs, claims Christian has no affiliation with his group. Gibson stated that Christian was ejected from his rally due to his behavior and that Patriot Prayer does not endorse white supremacy. He asserted, "Jeremy Christian has nothing to do with us. He’s not even a Trump supporter. He showed up at our march out of nowhere. None of us knew who he was." Gibson also denied being racist, emphasizing his belief in equality and his opposition to Sharia law, which he believes harms women. He expressed uncertainty about the "alt-right" label, stating, "What is the ‘alt-right’? I don’t really know. I’m Joey Gibson."

However, a spokesperson for Rose City Antifa, a prominent antifascist group in Portland, dismissed Gibson's disavowals. "He definitely marched with them," the spokesperson, who identified himself only as David, stated. "When you have an event like this you can say, ‘These people are with us, these people aren’t with us,’ as much as you want. But these are the people who turned out to the event."

David highlighted Portland's history of white supremacist activity, citing the 1988 murder of an Ethiopian immigrant and the presence of neo-Nazi and militia groups. He argued that these groups have become more emboldened recently. Rose City Antifa provided evidence, including an article on their website, identifying several white supremacists who attended the April 29th rally. "He may have been the most brazen and visible white supremacist, but he was certainly not the only one," David said, noting the presence of KKK members, Identity Europa members, and others with swastika symbols.

The spokesperson contended that the presence of such individuals and the escalating violence at these rallies undermine Gibson's claims of promoting free speech. "It doesn’t matter what the purpose of a rally is," he stated, "it matters what happens at that rally. How do the organizers of that rally conduct themselves? Who comes to join that rally and what’s the effect of them coming together?"

Gibson anticipates significant opposition at his upcoming rally, acknowledging, "There will be more hatred, opposition, anger. Because here are people running around irresponsibly saying Jeremy Christian was part of our group." He also mentioned pressure on antifascist leaders following a Department of Homeland Security report labeling anti-Trump protests involving "black bloc" anarchists as "domestic terrorist violence." Gibson predicted, "I guarantee right now, there is going to be at least one kid arrested in downtown Portland who gets arrested because he believed the lie."

Rose City Antifa is organizing a counter-demonstration, distributing flyers that express the sentiment, "The thing that they’re going to discover is that Portlanders are tired of this. They’re tired of this circus. They’re tired of the excuses [Gibson] gives for throwing these rallies. People are tired of seeing white supremacists together in their streets." The group anticipates a large turnout, suggesting that the city's political confrontations, which have escalated since the election of President Donald Trump, may continue.

query: What evidence is authorities focusing on in their investigation of the Brussels attacks?

documents.text: Brussels, Belgium (CNN) In grainy images from surveillance footage, a man wearing light-colored clothes and a hat pushes a baggage cart through the airport. It's one key piece of evidence authorities are looking at as they search for suspects after two explosions at the Brussels airport and another at a busy subway station in the Belgian capital Tuesday killed at least 30 people and wounded 230 others. ISIS claimed responsibility for the coordinated attacks, but authorities said it's too soon to say for sure whether the terror group was behind the blasts. So far, police have released photos of three men they say are suspects tied to the airport attack, standing side-by-side. Police say they are looking for the man on the right in connection with the Brussels attack. Two of the men, wearing black in surveillance images, are believed to be suicide bombers who died in the explosions in the airport's departure lounge. But investigators believe the one in light-colored clothing planted a bomb at the airport, then left. Authorities called him a wanted man and asked for the public's help tracking him down. "The third man left a bomb in the airport, but it didn't explode. ... And we are now looking for this guy," Belgium's Interior Minister Jan Jambon said. A photograph released by investigators shows the three suspects side-by-side. Federal Prosecutor Frederic Van Leeuw said the two men wearing black in the photograph were likely the suicide attackers. Video shows the men exiting a taxi and moving through the airport, according to two U.S. officials. The man dressed in white left the airport after accompanying the other two, they said -- a move the officials said appeared to be planned. Taxi driver tip sparks raid A break in the investigation may have come from a taxi driver who took the suspects to the airport. The driver contacted authorities after seeing surveillance footage and gave them the address where he picked the men up, according to two U.S. officials briefed on the investigation. That information prompted authorities to raid a residence after the attacks, the officials said. Investigators found a nail bomb, chemical products and an ISIS flag during a house search in the northeast Brussels neighborhood of Schaerbeek, Belgium's federal prosecutor said in a statement. Hours later, they were still combing through the building for evidence. Security was high. At one point, a helicopter hovered overhead, carrying a sniper with a weapon trained on the building. As masked, armed officers stood guard outside the building, the burst of camera flashes inside could be seen from the street below. Officers left the building carrying bags of evidence they loaded onto vehicles. Ties to Paris attacks? A Belgian government representative told CNN that 10 people were killed and 100 wounded at Brussels' international airport. At least 20 people died and 130 were wounded at the Maelbeek metro station, officials said. The blasts sent wounded people fleeing into the streets, spurred evacuations of nuclear plants and transit hubs and led to raids in some areas as authorities searched for suspects and evidence. Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels An injured woman leaves the airport in Brussels, Belgium, after two explosions rocked the facility on Tuesday, March 22. There was also an explosion at a subway station in the city. Hide Caption 1 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels Windows are blown out after the deadly attack at the airport. Hide Caption 2 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels A police officer directs passengers in a smoke-filled airport terminal. Hide Caption 3 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels Two wounded women are seen in the airport. Hide Caption 4 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels This photo from inside the airport was shared by Jef Versele on Facebook. Hide Caption 5 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels Subway passengers walk along the tracks following a blast at the Maelbeek metro station. Hide Caption 6 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels A private security guard helps a wounded woman outside the Maelbeek metro station. Hide Caption 7 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels Wounded people are treated outside the subway station. Hide Caption 8 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels Rescue teams evacuate the subway station. Hide Caption 9 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels Police officers guard the area around the subway station. Hide Caption 10 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels People react as they walk away from the Brussels airport. Hide Caption 11 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels A man with blood stains on his sweater leaves the airport. Hide Caption 12 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels Passengers leave the airport after the attack. Hide Caption 13 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels A young girl looks out of the window of a bus after airport evacuations. Hide Caption 14 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels People stand near the airport after evacuations. Hide Caption 15 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels Passengers gather outside the airport. Hide Caption 16 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels Police officers stand guard near the Maelbeek metro station. Hide Caption 17 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels A police helicopter flies above the area near the subway station. Hide Caption 18 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels People embrace outside the Brussels airport. Hide Caption 19 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels People are led away from the airport after the attacks. Hide Caption 20 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels A victim receives first aid near the Maelbeek metro station. Hide Caption 21 of 22 Photos: Terror attacks in Brussels Ambulances arrive at the airport. Hide Caption 22 of 22 Authorities in Belgium have been trying to crack down on terror threats for months as they raided homes in the area in search of suspects. Tuesday's violence came just days after investigators closed in on Europe's most wanted man, Paris attacks suspect Salah Abdeslam, who was hiding out in a Brussels suburb. The "working assumption" is that the attackers came from the network behind November's massacres in Paris , which left 130 dead, Belgian security sources said, while cautioning it is very early in the latest investigation. ISIS also claimed responsibility for those attacks. As doctors treat the wounded and authorities search for suspects, a key question remains unanswered: Could the attacks have anything to do with Abdeslam's arrest On Tuesday, Belgian Prime Minister Charles Michel deflected a question about whether there is any link between the attacks and the Belgium-born French citizen's capture, saying it is too early to tell. Michel said Tuesday he had "no information" about who was responsible for the attack, adding that authorities will find that out, but now their focus is on caring for the victims. Two senior U.S. officials told CNN they believe the Belgium attack is tied to the same network as Abdeslam. Fears realized One of the two airport explosions happened outside security checkpoints for ticketed passengers and near the airline check-in counters, an airline official briefed on the situation said. The subway station blast happened about an hour later in the Brussels district of Maelbeek, near the European Quarter, where European Union institutions are based. "We were fearing terrorist attacks," Michel told reporters Tuesday. "And that has now happened." Belgium is no stranger to terror . "The Belgians have been sitting on a ticking time bomb," a U.S. counterterrorism official said, given all those who have traveled from the small European nation to Syria and Iraq to join ISIS , then possibly come back home. But for survivors of Tuesday's blasts, the repeated warnings from officials in recent months didn't dull the shock of seeing the carnage. "You cannot believe it; you cannot believe it," said Jef Versele, who was in the airport's departure hall when bombs exploded there. "It was so insane. Not in my backyard." The second blast inside the airport blew out windows, created a lot of smoke and caused parts of the ceiling to fall, he added. "People were on the floor," Versele said, estimating he saw 50 to 60 who were thrown to the ground and didn't seem to be able to walk. Anthony Barrett saw the wounded carried out on stretchers and luggage carts as he watched from his hotel across from the terminal building. "I could see people fleeing," he said. 'We remain united' After the attacks in Brussels, the home of NATO and the capital of the European Union, leaders inside Belgium and beyond vowed not to back down in their fight against terror. In Belgium, where officials declared three days of national mourning, Michel offered a resolute message to those who supported and cheered the attackers. "To those who have chosen to be barbarous enemies of freedom, democracy and fundamental values ... we remain united as one," Michel said. "We are determined to defend our freedoms and to protect our liberties." In its message claiming responsibility, ISIS noted that Belgium is one of the nations "participating in the international coalition against the Islamic State." A Twitter post widely circulated by prominent ISIS backers Tuesday featured the words, "What will be coming is worse."

BRUSSELS — Bombs packed with nails terrorized Brussels on Tuesday in the deadliest assault on the European heartland since the Islamic State’s attacks on Paris four months ago, hitting the airport and subway system in coordinated strikes that were also claimed by the militant extremist group. The bombings paralyzed Brussels, the headquarters of the European Union and NATO, prompted international travel warnings to avoid Belgium and reverberated across the Atlantic to the United States, where New York and other major cities raised terrorism threat levels. Anxieties intensified about the inability to prevent mass killings at relatively unprotected places. At least 30 people were killed by two blasts at the Brussels airport departure area around 8 a.m. and one in a subway station shortly after 9. The police found at least one other unexploded bomb in a search of a Brussels house hours later. And Europe’s most wanted person suddenly became an unidentified man in a white coat and dark hat seen pushing a luggage cart in an airport surveillance photo taken just before the bombings. Two other men in the photo, each wearing a black glove on his left hand, were identified by Belgian prosecutors as suspected suicide bombers who appeared to have died in the explosions. “To those who have chosen to be the barbaric enemies of liberty, of democracy, of fundamental values, I want to say with the greatest strength that we will remain assembled and united,” the Belgium prime minister, Charles Michel, said at a news conference Tuesday evening, declaring a three-day mourning period.

On March 22, 2016, Brussels was struck by coordinated explosions at its international airport and within its subway system, resulting in at least 13 fatalities and numerous injuries. These attacks occurred mere days after the apprehension of a key suspect in the November Paris attacks within the city.

### Airport Devastation

At Brussels Airport, two explosions ripped through the departure hall, causing ceilings to collapse and widespread chaos. Witnesses described scenes of panic and destruction, with blood splattering the area. In the immediate aftermath, all flights were canceled, and arriving planes and trains were rerouted. Belgium's national terror alert level was raised to its highest setting, and residents were instructed to stay put, effectively paralyzing the city. Security was also significantly heightened at Paris airports.

### Subway Tragedy

The subway system also became a target. Near the Maelbeek station, close to the European Union headquarters, a powerful explosion occurred within a stopped train car. Rescue workers set up a temporary medical station in a nearby pub as dazed commuters emerged from the metro. One survivor described the scene as a "war scene," with debris, ruptured pipes, and the grim reality of the casualties.

### Preceding Concerns and Suspects

European security officials had been on high alert for weeks, with intelligence suggesting the Islamic State was planning an attack. The recent arrest of Salah Abdeslam, a central figure in the November 13 Paris attacks that killed 130 people, intensified these concerns. While Abdeslam had reportedly formed a new network and had access to weapons, there was no immediate evidence linking him or the Islamic State to the Brussels attacks at the time.

### Eyewitness Accounts

Survivors recounted harrowing experiences. Zach Mouzoun, who had just disembarked from a flight, described the second, more powerful explosion at the airport as causing ceilings to fall and pipes to burst, mixing water with the blood of victims. Denise Brandt, an American woman, recognized the visceral feeling of an explosion and instinctively fled, guiding others away from the danger.

### Airport's Significance

Brussels Airport, a crucial European hub connecting the capital to 226 global destinations, handled nearly 23.5 million passengers in 2015. The attacks disrupted its operations, with passengers being escorted onto the tarmac and the public urged to avoid the area.

*Associated Press writers Raf Casert in Brussels and Angela Charlton and Lori Hinnant in Paris contributed to this report.*

**Brussels Attacks: Airport and Metro Bombings Kill Over 30**

More than 30 people are confirmed dead and dozens more injured following coordinated attacks at Brussels International Airport and a city metro station. The twin blasts at Zaventem airport, occurring around 7:00 AM GMT, claimed 11 lives. An hour later, an explosion at Maelbeek metro station, near the European Union headquarters, resulted in approximately 20 fatalities.

Brussels police are actively searching for a man captured on CCTV pushing a luggage trolley through the airport. He was seen with two other individuals believed to have perished in the explosions. The Islamic State (IS) group has claimed responsibility for the attacks via its affiliated Amaq news agency. In response, Belgium has elevated its terrorism alert to the highest level, and three days of national mourning have been declared. Prime Minister Charles Michel described the attacks as "blind, violent and cowardly," emphasizing the tragedy and urging for "calmness and solidarity."

**Airport Devastation**

The departures area of Zaventem airport was struck by two blasts shortly after 8:00 AM local time. Belgian prosecutors indicated that a suicide bomber was "probably" involved. Belgian Health Minister Maggie de Block reported that 11 people were killed and 81 wounded. Witnesses described hearing shots and shouts in Arabic before the explosions, with some caught in the second blast after fleeing the first. A third bomb was discovered and safely destroyed by security services.

Pierre Meys, a local firefighter, described the scene as "war injuries," calling it "the worst thing I've ever seen in my career." Fifteen-year-old Antoine recounted the chaos, stating, "People were running over others that had fallen, I couldn't breathe. I can't believe I'm alive." He was among hundreds escorted to a makeshift reception center at a sports hall.

**Metro Carnage**

The Maelbeek metro station attack occurred during rush hour, shortly after 8:00 AM GMT. The explosion hit the middle carriage of a three-carriage train as it was departing the platform. Alexandre Brans told AP, "The metro was leaving Maelbeek station when there was a really loud explosion. It was panic everywhere. There were a lot of people in the metro." Brussels Mayor Yvan Mayeur confirmed that "about 20" people died and over 100 were injured, with 17 in severe condition. The station's proximity to EU institutions led the European Commission to instruct employees to remain indoors or at home, and all EU institutional meetings were canceled. Ryan McGhee, a catering worker, described the city as being in "lockdown" with a "tense" atmosphere.

**Heightened Security and International Condemnation**

Local and international travel faced significant disruption, with all flights canceled and security measures intensified across Europe. Eurostar and Thalys train services to and from Brussels were suspended. In the UK, security was increased at Gatwick and Heathrow airports, and British nationals were advised to avoid crowded areas in Belgium. UK Prime Minister David Cameron convened a Cobra response committee meeting, while France's cabinet held an emergency session. Additional security was also deployed at Dutch airports.

US President Barack Obama condemned the blasts as "outrageous attacks against innocent people." The 28 EU leaders issued a joint statement calling the bombings "an attack on our open democratic society." French President Francois Hollande stated, "The terrorists have struck Belgium but it is Europe that was targeted," while Russian President Vladimir Putin labeled the attacks "barbaric."

Belgian Interior Minister Jan Jambon had previously warned of potential revenge attacks following the arrest of Salah Abdeslam, a key suspect in the November Paris attacks. He noted that "stopping one cell can... push others into action."

**Intelligence Gaps and Future Concerns**

European security experts had anticipated further attacks, but the speed and impact of the Brussels bombings were a shock. If linked to Abdeslam's arrest, the incident highlights the alarming efficiency of a functioning terrorist network. The sophisticated planning involved in logistics, explosives, weaponry, and the willingness of individuals to carry out such attacks on civilian targets is a significant concern. The immediate priority is apprehending any individuals posing an imminent threat. However, the attacks underscore substantial intelligence deficiencies, with Brussels perceived as a vulnerable target. French President Francois Hollande's call for a "calm, lucid and determined" response from Europe, sustained over time, remains crucial.

This document outlines our cookie policy, emphasizing our commitment to your privacy. You can find detailed information about the cookies we use, their purposes, and how to manage your preferences via the "More information" link.

Like most websites, we use cookies to enhance your experience. These cookies help us gather data about your visits and interests. This allows us to continue offering our content for free, personalize your website experience, and display relevant advertisements, ensuring our site remains engaging for you.

**Understanding Cookies at VTM Nieuws**

Websites employ technologies like cookies to improve usability and engagement. Cookies can be used by website owners or third parties, such as advertisers. Since cookies do not reveal personal contact details like your address or phone number, they are not used for direct marketing through these channels.

We believe it's crucial for you to understand the cookies we propose to use and their purposes. This transparency aims to:

*   Guarantee your privacy.
*   Increase our website's user-friendliness.
*   Keep our website free by showing you advertisements.
*   Maintain and improve the quality of our content.

To personalize your cookie settings, click "Settings." By clicking "Continue to website," you trust us to provide you with the best possible website experience.

**Types of Cookies We Use:**

**1. Essential Cookies for Website Functionality**

These cookies ensure our website operates correctly. They include:

*   Storing your preferences (language, location, search result display).
*   Optimizing video playback (buffer size, screen resolution).
*   Saving login details for convenience.
*   Adapting website display based on your browser settings.
*   Detecting and preventing website misuse (e.g., failed login attempts).
*   Ensuring website accessibility through load balancing.
*   Enabling interaction features like commenting.
*   Remembering items in your shopping cart.
*   Storing information entered during checkout to avoid re-entry.
*   Transferring information between pages for longer forms or orders.

**Specific Essential Cookies:**

*   **Adblock detect (own):** Detects adblocking tools.
*   **Adobe Typekit:** Functional.
*   **DFP (Doubleclick):** Advertising.
*   **Drupal (after login):** Functional.
*   **Facebook connect:** Login via Facebook.
*   **Freewheel:** Advertising.
*   **Gigya socialize:** Registration & Login.
*   **Google IMA Ads:** Advertising.
*   **Google Maps GEO:** Location data.
*   **Google Publisher tags:** Advertising.
*   **Google Recaptcha:** Security.
*   **Google Save Frame:** Advertising.
*   **Hello Bar Smartbar:** Functional.
*   **Myfonts Counter:** Functional.
*   **Sourcepoint:** Adblocking tool.
*   **SSO Sign on:** Single Sign-On.
*   **SUMO Smartbar:** Functional.
*   **Usersnap:** Bug Tracking Software.

**2. Cookies for Measuring Website Usage**

We use these cookies to understand which parts of our website are most interesting to visitors. This helps us improve the site's structure, navigation, and content. We use third-party software for this measurement, generating statistics on visitor numbers and popular content. These statistics are not linked to individuals.

We use cookies for:

*   Tracking visitor numbers.
*   Monitoring time spent on pages.
*   Analyzing page visit sequences.
*   Identifying areas needing improvement.
*   Optimizing the website.

**Specific Usage Measurement Cookies:**

*   **Boomerang:** Statistical.
*   **Chartbeat (+ mab / video):** Statistical / optimization.
*   **CIM / Gemius:** Statistical - viewing figures.
*   **CO2stats:** Statistical.

*   **Stats service:** Statistical.

*   **Visual Measures:** Statistical.
*   **Visual Website Optimizer (VWO):** A-B statistical.

For cookies placed by third parties and their data collection, please refer to their respective privacy statements, as these may change without our influence.

**3. Cookies for Behaviorally Targeted Content**

Our goal is to provide you with the most relevant information. We adapt our website content and advertisements to your interests. If these cookies are not used, you will still see advertisements, but they will not be personalized to your interests. These are similar to TV ads that relate to the program you're watching.

These cookies enable us to:

*   Recommend interesting articles based on your reading history.
*   Suggest relevant videos based on your viewing habits.
*   Propose topics aligned with your interests.

**Specific Behaviorally Targeted Content Cookies:**

*   **Froomle Recommendation:** Recommendation.
*   **Gravity Recommandation:** Recommendation.
*   **Trackuity iframes:** Advertising.

For cookies placed by third parties, please consult their privacy statements.

**4. Cookies for Personalized Advertising**

Much of our content is free, supported by advertising. Some advertisements use cookies, placed by us or third parties. To deliver personalized ads, we analyze your browsing behavior across the internet to infer your interests. This allows us to tailor content for different customer groups. For example, a user interested in football might see different ads than someone interested in travel. Third parties may also collect this information and combine it with data from previous website visits.

These cookies allow us to:

*   Record your visits to estimate your interests.
*   Share browsing behavior information with other websites.
*   Utilize third-party services for ad delivery.

## Australian PM Criticizes European Security Following Brussels Attacks

Australian Prime Minister Malcolm Turnbull has voiced criticism of European security measures, suggesting they had been allowed to weaken prior to the recent attacks in Belgium. When questioned about the possibility of a similar terrorist incident occurring in Australia, Turnbull stated, "You cannot guarantee that there will be no terrorist incident." However, he assured Australians that the nation's security systems, border protection, and domestic arrangements are significantly more robust than those in Europe, where he believes security has regrettably been allowed to decline.

Turnbull linked these European security vulnerabilities to ongoing challenges faced by the continent. In response, the federal opposition leader, Bill Shorten, commented that it was too soon after the attacks for the Prime Minister to be critiquing Belgium's actions. Shorten emphasized that the current focus should be on acknowledging the loss of innocent lives, with critical questions to be addressed in the days ahead. Australia's current official threat level designates a terrorist attack as "probable."

Here is a revised timeline of events in Turkey on Friday, focusing on clarity, direct relevance to the query, and increased factual detail:

**Timeline of Key Events During Turkey's 2016 Coup Attempt**

This timeline details significant events as they unfolded in Turkey on Friday, July 15, 2016, based on Reuters reports. All times are in GMT.

**Late Evening (GMT)**

*   **2352:** The Turkish Prime Minister declared the situation was under control, attributing the events to the Gulen movement and announcing a no-fly zone over Ankara. A U.S.-based organization linked to Gulen had previously denied any involvement.
*   **2339:** The state-run Anadolu Agency reported that a bomb struck the Turkish parliament in Ankara. A Reuters witness also heard a blast in Istanbul.
*   **2320:** U.S. Secretary of State John Kerry stated in a phone call with the Turkish foreign minister that he emphasized "absolute support for Turkey's democratically elected, civilian government and democratic institutions."
*   **2313:** A group associated with U.S.-based cleric Fethullah Gulen described accusations of involvement in the coup attempt as "highly irresponsible." They condemned military intervention in Turkish politics and expressed concern for citizen safety.
*   **2305:** The White House released a statement indicating President Obama and Secretary of State Kerry agreed that all parties in Turkey should support the elected government. They urged restraint and the avoidance of bloodshed.
*   **2304:** Anadolu Agency reported that 17 police officers were killed at the Ankara special forces headquarters. This claim lacked independent confirmation.
*   **2259:** Turkish broadcaster NTV reported that a Turkish fighter jet shot down a military helicopter used by coup plotters over Ankara.
*   **2251:** The commander of the special forces stated that a group had engaged in treason but would not succeed. He affirmed that the military did not condone the coup.
*   **2247:** The Prime Minister asserted that gangs and illegal formations were behind the coup attempt, labeling it a terrorist act. He confirmed the government remained in charge and urged citizens to take to the streets.
*   **2237:** The commander of Turkey's First Army, responsible for Istanbul and western regions, characterized the coup plotters as a small faction and stated there was "nothing to worry about."
*   **2226:** Two loud explosions were heard in the center of the Turkish capital.
*   **2208:** Tanks surrounded the Turkish parliament building and opened fire. Gunfire was also heard at Istanbul airport.
*   **2203:** The Turkish justice minister identified members of a movement loyal to U.S.-based cleric Fethullah Gulen within the army as being behind the attempted coup.
*   **2151:** Witnesses reported that a military helicopter opened fire over Ankara, and an explosion was heard in the capital.
*   **2135:** Turkish state broadcaster TRT went off the air but later resumed broadcasting from London.
*   **2126:** President Tayyip Erdogan urged people to protest the coup attempt by a minority military faction. Speaking via a cellphone video link to CNN Turk, he stated the attempt would receive a "necessary response" and announced his return to Ankara.
*   **2122:** The Turkish Prime Minister tweeted that all measures would be taken to quell the coup attempt, even if it resulted in fatalities. He mentioned sieges at unspecified important buildings and urged citizens to remain calm.
*   **2118:** A presidential source confirmed that the president and the government remained in power.
*   **2105:** The Turkish state broadcaster read a statement on behalf of the military. This statement announced plans for a new constitution, accused the government of undermining democratic and secular rule, declared the country was run by a "peace council," and imposed martial law and a nationwide curfew.
*   **2102:** The head of the Istanbul branch of Turkey's ruling AK Party reported that soldiers entered the party building and instructed them to leave.
*   **2058:** A TRT correspondent told Reuters that soldiers had entered the buildings of the Turkish state broadcaster in Ankara.

*   **2038:** CNN Turk reported that Turkish President Tayyip Erdogan was safe.
*   **2025:** The Turkish military issued a statement via email and reported on Turkish TV channels, claiming it had taken power to protect the democratic order. The statement also asserted that all existing foreign relations would be maintained.
*   **2002:** Prime Minister Binali Yildirim announced an attempted coup was underway and called for calm. He stated a group within the military had tried to overthrow the government and that security forces had been mobilized to "do what is necessary."
*   **1950:** Gunshots were heard in Ankara, with military jets and helicopters observed flying overhead. Helicopters were also seen over Istanbul.
*   **1929:** Istanbul's Bosphorus Bridge and Fatih Sultan Mehmet Bridge were closed. Footage from Dogan News Agency showed cars and buses being diverted.

The coordinated terrorist attacks on March 22, 2016, in Brussels, Belgium, plunged the European capital into a state of emergency. Bombings at Brussels Airport and a metro station resulted in at least 31 deaths and over 180 injuries, starkly illustrating the severe security threats confronting Europe.

The initial explosions occurred in the Brussels Airport departure lounge during the busy morning rush. Eyewitnesses described a scene of devastation, with collapsed ceilings, shattered windows, and widespread debris. One of these blasts was attributed to a suicide bomber. Approximately an hour later, a second explosion struck a subway train near the European Union headquarters, forcing terrified passengers to evacuate through darkened tunnels.

In response, Belgian Prime Minister Charles Michel declared, "What we feared has happened," urging calm and solidarity during this "black moment for our country." Belgium raised its terror alert to the highest level, causing significant disruptions, including flight and train diversions, and directives for people to shelter in place. Airports across Europe immediately enhanced their security measures. French Prime Minister Manuel Valls, speaking after a crisis meeting, stated, "We are at war," characterizing the events as "acts of war." European security officials, including those from Europol, had been anticipating a major attack, with intelligence pointing to the Islamic State group. These concerns were amplified by the recent arrest of Salah Abdeslam, a key suspect in the November Paris attacks, which revealed a broader network with individuals still at large, according to a report by the Belgian Federal Police.

While no group immediately claimed responsibility, Prime Minister Michel noted no immediate evidence linked Salah Abdeslam, the Paris suspect arrested days prior, to the Brussels attacks. Abdeslam had reportedly informed Belgian authorities after his arrest that he had established a new network and was planning further attacks, as detailed in court documents.

The attacks at Brussels Airport (Zaventem) involved two explosions in the departures area, resulting in 11 fatalities and 81 injuries. Witnesses described the scene as "atrocious" and a "war scene," with at least one Kalashnikov rifle discovered in the departure lounge, according to the Belgian Federal Police. The Brussels Metro attack saw an explosion on a rush-hour subway train near Maelbeek station, killing 20 people and injuring over 100. Passengers described widespread panic, and rescue workers set up a makeshift medical center in a nearby pub. Following the attacks, the metro system and airport were shut down, leading to over 200 flight diversions or cancellations. Survivors recounted harrowing experiences, with one individual narrowly avoiding the airport blast while shopping, as reported by local news outlets.

query: What sparked Amazon Studios to investigate allegations against Jeffrey Tambor?

documents.text: (UPDATED with Jill Soloway statement) EXCLUSIVE: Three weeks after Amazon Studios boss Roy Price resigned over sexual harassment claims, the company is conducting an investigation into allegations against Transparent star Jeffrey Tambor. An Amazon spokesperson confirmed to Deadline that the company has initiated an investigation. In a statement, Tambor himself calls the claims “baseless.” In its early stages, the probe primarily stems from allegations by Tambor’s former assistant Van Barnes of implied inappropriate behavior on the part of the Emmy, Golden Globe and SAG Awards winner. The investigation into the claims by the transgender Barnes, which she made online in a private Facebook post, began earlier this week, we hear. In the post, she claims that her former boss, whom she does not identify by name, had repeatedly propositioned her, made lewd comments, groped her and threatened to sue her if she spoke up. “I am aware that a former disgruntled assistant of mine has made a private post implying that I had acted in an improper manner toward her,” Tambor told Deadline today. “I adamantly and vehemently reject and deny any and all implication and allegation that I have ever engaged in any improper behavior toward this person or any other person I have ever worked with. I am appalled and distressed by this baseless allegation.” Amazon’s stance on such allegations is to investigate promptly and thoroughly, a source says. The investigation is in the process of speaking to members of the Transparent production and Tambor personally. The Jill Soloway-created series, Amazon’s flagship comedy, is currently not in production. “Anything that would diminish the level of respect, safety and inclusion so fundamental to our workplace is completely antithetical to our principles,” Soloway said in a statement today. “We are cooperating with the investigation into this matter.” As well as working for Tambor on Transparent, Barnes has also appeared on E! now-shuttered I Am Cait with Caitlyn Jenner and the Amazon documentary anthology miniseries This Is Me back in 2015. This investigation into Tambor comes as the Jeff Bezos-founded studio has seen a near exodus of executives in recent weeks, led by studio head Price, who was suspended and subsequently resigned after details became public about how he had lewdly propositioned The Man in the High Castle executive producer Isa Dick Hackett during San Diego Comic-Con in 2015. Price had been the subject of an investigation by Amazon after Hackett relayed the Comic-Con incidents to company executives. The studio later said that “we looked closely at this specific concern and addressed it directly with those involved.” Price stayed in his job for over two years until the spotlight on disgraced producer Harvey Weinstein’s alleged decades long behavior of sexual harassment and sexual assault also turned toward Price and his conduct. Tambor is repped by the Gersh Agency and the Burstein Company.

Former actress Aurélie Wynn has accused Gossip Girl star Ed Westwick of raping her, days after he denied allegations of sexual assaulting actress Kristina Cohen. “I have never forced myself in any manner, on any woman. I certainly have never committed rape,” Westwick, 30, wrote on Twitter and Instagram on Tuesday. A rep for Westwick did not immediately respond to a request for comment from PEOPLE. Wynn, whose stage name was Aurelie Marie Cao, claimed Westwick sexually assaulted her in July 2014. Facebook; FeatureflashSHM/REX/Shutterstock “I said no and he pushed me face down and was powerless under his weight,” she wrote in a post shared on Facebook Wednesday. “I was wearing a one piece bathing suit that he ripped, I was in complete shock,” Wynn wrote of the alleged incident at Westwick’s rental home. Wynn also said she told her then-boyfriend Glee star Mark Salling – who recently plead guilty to child pornography possession – about the alleged incident. However, he blamed her and broke up with her, the actress said in her post. A rep for Salling did not immediately respond to a request for comment from PEOPLE. In addition, Wynn says that when she told her friends, they advised her to not tell anyone as she would be labeled “that girl” or seen as someone “trying to get my 10 seconds of fame.” However, years later, Wynn felt inspired to come forward because of Cohen. “I am so incredibly thankful all of this is finally coming to light and that there is justice in the world. I believe you Kristina Cohen and thank you for speaking up so eloquently and really encompassing what young female actresses have to go through at the hands of men like Ed Westwick. Without you I would not have had the strength to speak up publicly about my ordeal,” she concluded her post. Cohen — who has played minor roles in several movies and TV series — alleged she was raped in 2014 while she was visiting Westwick with her then-boyfriend, who is not named but referred to as a producer who was friends with the actor.

**Terry Crews to Pursue Full Legal Action Against WME Agent Adam Venit for Alleged Sexual Assault**

Actor Terry Crews is vowing to take comprehensive legal action against a prominent Hollywood agent, Adam Venit, following an alleged sexual assault. Crews was seen departing the LAPD's Hollywood Division, signaling his firm resolve to seek justice. He has publicly stated his intention to file both a civil lawsuit and pursue criminal charges against Venit, who leads the motion picture group at the William Morris Endeavor (WME) agency.

In the wake of these serious allegations, WME has placed Venit on administrative leave as the agency conducts an internal investigation into the matter. According to reports, the alleged incident took place last year at a Hollywood event. It is suggested that Venit may have been involved in a prank at the party, which reportedly included "wedgie-ing" attendees. Representatives for Adam Venit have been approached for comment but have not yet issued a statement.

### Terry Crews Files Police Report Over Alleged Hollywood Executive Assault

Actor Terry Crews, 49, has officially filed a police report with the Los Angeles Police Department (LAPD) regarding a sexual assault he alleges was perpetrated by a "high level Hollywood executive." This formal report was submitted to officers at the LAPD Hollywood Station approximately one month after Crews first publicly disclosed the incident.

Confirming the report to TMZ, Crews articulated his clear motivation: "People have to be held accountable." He also stated his firm intention to pursue legal action against the individual, vowing, "We're going to go all the way."

### Recalling the 2016 Incident

In early October, the *Brooklyn Nine-Nine* star shared details of the alleged groping incident on Twitter. He cited the widespread Harvey Weinstein scandal as a significant catalyst for his decision to come forward, tweeting, "This whole thing with Harvey Weinstein is giving me PTSD. Why? Because this kind of thing happened to ME."

Crews detailed that the alleged assault occurred at a "Hollywood function" in 2016. He claims the executive approached him and "groped my privates."

### The Aftermath and Decision to Speak Out

Crews recounted his immediate reaction, stating, "Jumping back I said What are you doing?! My wife saw everything n we looked at him like he was crazy. He just grinned like a jerk." He admitted he considered retaliating physically but ultimately reconsidered the potential repercussions.

"‘240 lbs. Black Man stomps out Hollywood Honcho’ would be the headline the next day," Crews explained, adding that he feared he would have been jailed. Consequently, he and his wife departed the event.

### Seeking Accountability and Understanding

Following the incident, Crews discussed the event with numerous contacts who worked with the executive. He mentioned that the man behind the alleged assault did contact him and offer an apology, but "never really explained why he did what he did."

Crews ultimately decided not to pursue the matter further at the time, fearing ostracization due to the predator's significant power and influence within the industry. He expressed understanding for why many victims remain silent in similar situations, noting that the executive's influence was reportedly substantial. The actor has declined to name the executive and conveyed empathy for those who have chosen to stay silent, acknowledging the immense pressure they may face.

An advertising executive, Tiffany Bacon Scourby, has come forward with allegations of sexual assault against former *Entourage* star Jeremy Piven, joining a growing number of accusers. Scourby shared her experience with PEOPLE, stating she felt compelled to speak out after reality TV personality and *Playboy* Playmate Ariane Bellamar publicly accused Piven of similar misconduct on October 30th. Piven's representatives have issued strong denials of Bellamar's claims.

Scourby expressed her belief in Bellamar's account, telling PEOPLE, "I 100 percent believe she’s being honest." Bellamar's tweets detailed alleged instances of Piven groping her on the *Entourage* set and at the Playboy Mansion. Following Bellamar's accusations, actress Cassidy Freeman of *Longmire* also shared her experience via an Instagram post, stating, "I know what you did and attempted to do to me when I was far too young. THAT I know. And you know it too. Unless there were so many of us, that you can’t remember." Freeman concluded by urging Piven to cease such behavior.

In response to the escalating allegations, a pre-taped appearance by Jeremy Piven on *The Late Show with Stephen Colbert*, scheduled for November 3rd, was canceled. CBS confirmed to *Entertainment Weekly* that the interview, recorded on Monday, October 30th, could not adequately address the recent developments, leading to a replacement guest.

Scourby, now 39 and residing in Washington, D.C., recounted her interactions with the 52-year-old actor over several phone conversations. She claims to have first met Piven in October 2003 at a New York City club during a work-related event. "He was very nice to me," she told PEOPLE. Piven mentioned an upcoming taping of *Late Night with Conan O’Brien* and invited her as his guest, providing his phone number.

The following morning, after her work meeting, Scourby met Piven around 11 a.m. at the Trump International Hotel & Tower. She recalled the scene, stating, "I remember I was wearing this white ribbed turtle neck and brown slacks — business attire — and I was sitting right next to him on the couch." Their conversation, lasting approximately five minutes, was interrupted when Piven informed her his publicist was en route.

Suddenly, Scourby alleges, "he jumped on top of me. I tried to push him off and he forced me to the ground." She described Piven exposing himself and rubbing his genitals against her body. During the roughly 15 minutes she was in his suite, Scourby remained clothed. She stated Piven "held down my hands" and ejaculated "all over my white turtleneck."

Following the alleged assault, Scourby recounted pushing Piven off, running to the door, and rubbing her chest against the hallway wall to remove his semen from her sweater. She immediately left the hotel, hailed a cab, and "just burst into tears," crying for the entire ride back to her hotel. From the taxi, Scourby called a close friend, who has since corroborated her account to PEOPLE. Upon returning to her hotel, she showered before boarding a train back to Washington, D.C., where she "just cried on the train the whole way back."

In the 14 years since the alleged incident, Scourby has married and become a mother. She explained she "never thought about saying anything" until witnessing Piven's strong denial of Bellamar's allegations. "It couldn’t have just happened to me and couldn’t have just happened to her," she reasoned, feeling a connection to other potential victims.

In a statement to PEOPLE, Piven categorically denied Scourby's account: "Let me be absolutely clear, this simply did not happen. I would never force myself on a woman, ever. I cannot speak as to why a person would create a story like this." A representative for Piven further stated the actor would be willing to take a polygraph test, asserting it "would have clearly proven this allegation to be entirely fabricated, a complete lie." The representative added that Piven was "now looking at legal options."

On Thursday, Piven reiterated his stance via Twitter, calling all accusations against him "completely fabricated." The 52-year-old actor stated, "Let me begin by saying that the accusations against me are absolutely false and completely fabricated. I would never force myself on a woman. Period. I have offered to take a polygraph to support my innocence. I keep asking myself, ‘How does one prove something didn’t happen?’"

Piven expressed his inability to speculate on accusers' motivations. "What I am not able to do is speculate as to the motivations of these women. As a human being I feel compassionate for the victims of such acts, but I am perplexed as to the misdirection of anger with false accusations against me and hope they do not detract from the stories that should be heard." He further wrote, "We seem to be entering dark times — allegations are being printed as facts and lives are being put in jeopardy without a hearing, due process or evidence. I hope we can give people the benefit of a doubt before we rush to judgment." Piven concluded by emphasizing the unproductive nature of such accusations: "Continuing to tear each other down and destroy careers based on mere allegations is not productive on any level. I hope we can use this moment to begin a constructive dialogue on these issues, which are real and need to be addressed."

query: What steps are Congressional Republicans taking to dismantle the Affordable Care Act?

documents.text: (Sarah Parnass/The Washington Post) Congressional Republicans on Wednesday launched their long-promised effort to dismantle the Affordable Care Act, even as they acknowledged that they may need several months to develop a replacement along conservative lines. Signifying how enormous a priority the issue is for the incoming administration, Vice President-elect Mike Pence met privately to discuss it with House and Senate Republicans. He offered no details afterward about what a new health-care law might look like but vowed to unwind the existing one through a mixture of executive actions and legislation. Meanwhile, President Obama made a rare Capitol Hill appearance, meeting behind closed doors with Democrats from both chambers. He urged members of his party not to help the GOP devise a new health-care law. The dueling high-level visits, on the same day that the Senate opened debate on a budget resolution that would begin rolling back the law, highlighted the sharp political fault lines that surround the future of the government’s health policies. The president, who was accompanied by Senate Minority Leader Charles E. Schumer (D-N.Y.), House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi (D-Calif.) and Rep. Frederica S. Wilson (D-Fla.) as he entered the Capitol, took no questions from reporters before or after the nearly two-hour meeting. But participants said he told members of his party that they did not have to “rescue” Republicans and that they should “stay strong” as the GOP strives to replace the law. (Reuters) Pence told reporters that he and Trump would pursue a “two-track approach” to chip away at the ACA through executive powers and legislation. Trump is “working on a series of executive orders that will enable that orderly transition to take place,” Pence said, and is eyeing other policies that can be reversed. [With Obamacare, GOP faces the ‘Pottery Barn rule’: You break it, you own it ] While Pence did not identify other policy targets, Republicans from Western states urged him Wednesday to undo some of the public land protections Obama has created through the 1906 Antiquities Act. Other executive actions, including those providing new safeguards for LGBT Americans and curbing greenhouse gas emissions linked to climate change, also could come under fire. According to a lobbyist in touch with congressional aides on the ACA issue, the Trump transition team has been considering ways to strip down the health benefits that insurers must provide in plans that they sell to individuals and small businesses. This list of “essential health benefits” was envisioned by the law but was defined in a regulation written by the Department of Health and Human Services. As a result, the incoming administration could alter it without help from Congress. [Why Obamacare is unlikely to die a swift death] Trump took to Twitter on Wednesday morning to urge Republicans to “be careful in that the Dems own the failed Obamacare disaster.” In a dig at Schumer and his allies, Trump added: “Don’t let the Schumer clowns out of this web.” Less than half an hour after Trump’s social media messages, Schumer tweeted: “Republicans should stop clowning around with America’s health care. Don’t #MakeAmericaSickAgain.” Schumer and other Democrats echoed Obama, saying they did not feel any responsibility to craft a substitute health-care bill. “If you are repealing, show us what you’ll replace it with. Then we’ll look at what you have and see what you can do,” said Schumer, who met briefly with Pence on Wednesday. (Reuters) White House press secretary Josh Earnest said that Obama told Democrats that they are well positioned to defend the law, which has extended insurance to more than 20 million Americans. However, Obama also told lawmakers that he recognized that he had not succeeded in selling the law to the public. “There was an acknowledgment that so many features were so popular, but there was a failure to communicate that,” Sen. Jeff Merkley (D-Ore.) said. [Donald Trump, meet your toughest hurdle: The U.S. Senate] Pence, who was greeted by applause as he entered the House GOP meeting with Reince Priebus, Kelly Anne Conway and several other future White House aides, made it clear that Trump, starting on the first day of his presidency, intended to embark on an aggressive campaign to reverse executive actions made by Obama. “They didn’t enumerate a list, just that which came by the pen can die by the pen,” said Rep. Mark Sanford (R-S.C.). The first bill Republicans introduced in the new Senate that convened Tuesday was budget legislation with instructions for House and Senate committees to begin repealing the Affordable Care Act. The bare-bones spending outline requires members of four committees — Ways and Means and Energy and Commerce in the House; and Finance, as well as Health, Education, Labor and Pensions, in the Senate — to produce bills just seven days after Trump’s inauguration that each would save $1 billion over a decade by slashing elements of the heath-care law. [Democrats: Trump will make America sick again] House Republicans tasked with writing the repeal legislation said that no final decisions have been made on what it will include. Rep. Patrick J. Tiberi (R-Ohio) who chairs a House subcommittee that oversees health care, called the incoming administration’s Jan. 27 deadline “a challenging goal.” Among the unanswered questions is whether Republicans will immediately end health-care taxes, such as an additional 0.9 percent Medicare payroll tax and a 3.8 percent investment income tax, that were created to help pay for the Affordable Care Act. Some Republicans have insisted that the taxes should be eliminated immediately, even if lawmakers decide to delay the repeal of other parts of the law. Others worry that repealing the taxes will make it impossible to pay to keep Obamacare afloat while a replacement is finished. “We’re not sure yet,” Tiberi said. “There’s just not consensus on this.” Rep. Chris Collins (R-N.Y.), a member the Trump transition team, said in an interview that “we have six months to work out the replacement plan” for the sweeping health-care law. Senate rules allow budget resolutions to pass by a simple majority — a maneuver that guarantees that the chamber’s Democratic minority will not have enough votes for a filibuster to block repeal. Only changes related to taxes, spending or the long-term federal budget are eligible for the simple-majority treatment, however. Other parts of the law, such as the structure of the insurance marketplaces, probably would require a filibuster-proof margin of 60 votes in the Senate, a trickier task since the new Senate has 52 Republicans. (The Washington Post) Since its passage by Congress in the spring of 2010 — entirely with Democratic votes — the ACA has spurred the most significant changes to U.S. health policy since the creation of Medicare and Medicaid during the Great Society legislation of the 1960s. It also has faced sustained opposition from Republicans, leading to two Supreme Court cases and a lawsuit over cost-sharing subsidies that is now before the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit. The ACA is best known for having expanded insurance coverage, starting in 2014, in two ways: new marketplaces selling private health plans to Americans who do not have access to affordable health benefits through a job, and an expansion of Medicaid in about three-fifths of the states. The marketplaces got off to a shaky start because of computer dysfunction with HealthCare.gov, the federal enrollment website. The president’s visit to the Hill was part of a broader, last-ditch effort by his administration to preserve a central element of his domestic legacy. On Wednesday morning, New York Gov. Andrew M. Cuomo (D) drew attention to the stakes for his state by issuing a county-by-county breakdown of the estimated 2.7 million New Yorkers who would lose coverage if the law were repealed. He pointed out that New York would lose $595 million this year in federal money that has helped the state expand its Medicaid program. HHS officials have been trying to use to their advantage the fact that the change in administrations will take place while the fourth year’s enrollment period for ACA health plans is underway. On Wednesday, HHS released HealthCare.gov enrollment figures showing that 8.8 million Americans chose or were automatically re-enrolled in ACA health plans between Nov. 1 and Dec. 31 in the 39 states that rely on the federal exchange website. That is about 200,000 more than at the same time a year ago, although the proportion of new customers was lower. The new drive to unwind the health-care law will take time.
