Mike DeBonis, Ed O’Keefe, Sean Sullivan and David Weigel contributed to this report. Read more at PowerPost

Democrats are rallying under the slogan "Make America Sick Again" as they push back against Republican efforts to repeal and replace the Affordable Care Act (ACA), commonly known as Obamacare. Senate minority leader Chuck Schumer and his progressive colleagues fear a national health care catastrophe if the law is dismantled without a viable alternative. Republicans, however, are eager to repeal the ACA, making it a top priority for the 115th Congress. Crafting a replacement policy that avoids leaving millions uninsured presents a significant challenge, leading some to speculate about a "repeal in name only" scenario.

Here's what you need to know about the ACA:

*   **What is Obamacare?**
    *   The Affordable Care Act is a federally run insurance exchange.
    *   It established marketplaces where individuals without health insurance can shop for government-regulated health care plans.
    *   These plans cover standard and essential health benefits.
    *   Uninsured citizens can also check their eligibility for government subsidies.
    *   Americans are required to have health insurance or face a fine, unless they qualify for an exemption. The annual penalty for uninsured Americans in 2016 was $695 per adult.
    *   The ACA was signed into law by President Obama in 2010 to expand health insurance coverage.
    *   This expansion included broadening Medicaid eligibility and increasing funding for the program, which serves low-income Americans.
    *   Individuals satisfied with their current private health insurance plans could continue with them, renew them, or switch to plans available through the marketplace.

*   **Coverage Statistics:**
    *   As of February 2016, approximately 20 million people gained health insurance through the ACA. This includes enrollment in the marketplace, Medicaid expansion, staying on parents' plans, and other provisions.
    *   The Kaiser Family Foundation estimated that at the end of 2015, 28.5 million non-elderly Americans remained uninsured.

*   **Republican Opposition:**
    *   **Costs:** Conservatives object to tax increases associated with Medicaid expansion and subsidies for lower-income individuals.
    *   **Free Market Principles:** Some argue that the ACA's uniformity and regulation stifle medical innovation.
    *   **Job Growth Concerns:** Republicans have blamed the ACA for hindering job growth by imposing costs on larger businesses.
    *   **Complexity:** The law's extensive nature (2,700 pages) and the confusing rollout of Healthcare.gov in October 2013 contributed to public dissatisfaction.
    *   **Premium Increases:** In late October 2016, the White House announced an average premium increase of 25% across 39 states served by the federally run online market.

*   **Repeal Prospects:**
    *   With Republicans controlling both the White House and Congress, repeal is a strong possibility.
    *   Previous attempts to declare the ACA unconstitutional failed in 2015 when the Supreme Court upheld it in a 6-3 decision.
    *   Republicans have made over 60 attempts to repeal the law since 2010, all unsuccessful until now.
    *   President-elect Donald Trump, who campaigned on repealing and replacing Obamacare, has indicated a willingness to preserve key provisions, such as allowing young adults up to age 26 to stay on their parents' insurance and prohibiting insurers from denying coverage for pre-existing conditions.

*   **Potential Replacements:**
    *   While a definitive alternative is not yet established, Tom Price, nominated as Secretary of Health and Human Services, has proposed the Empowering Patients First Act.
    *   This act would offer tax credits to individuals without insurance to help cover the cost of private insurance, with credits based on age rather than income. Democrats argue this could disadvantage lower-income individuals.
    *   Price's plan would also eliminate the Medicaid expansion, which provided coverage to approximately 14 million low-income Americans under the ACA.

*   **Timeline for Change:**
    *   Any repeal and replacement of the ACA is expected to be gradual, spanning two to four years, to avoid leaving millions uninsured.
    *   A "repeal and delay" approach is also a possibility, allowing Republicans time to develop a more comprehensive health care law. The Congressional Budget Office estimates that 22 million Americans could become uninsured if the ACA is repealed without an immediate replacement.

Breaking News Emails Get breaking news alerts and special reports. The news and stories that matter, delivered weekday mornings. / Updated By Jane C. Timm Donald Trump spent nearly his entire 2016 campaign promising to repeal and replace Obamacare. On Wednesday, weeks away from inauguration, he expressed trepidation about doing just that and urged his fellow Republicans to be cautious in their efforts. As GOP lawmakers begin the process of repealing President Obama’s landmark legislation, it’s worth taking a look at the eight times Trump has changed his position on Obamacare since announcing his bid for president more than a year and a half ago. These recalculations and shifts aren’t unusual for Trump – he took 141 positions on 23 major issues during his run, and has taken 15 new stances on nine issues since being elected – but it is a particularly illustrative shift in the differences between the rhetoric of a candidate and a leader faced with the realities of governance. 1. Repeal Obamacare. Look to Canada for inspiration. Two months into his White House bid in August 2015, Trump was asked repeatedly if he still supported the single-payer health care he'd touted in the past. He said America should have a private system but repeatedly praised Canada and Scotland's socialized system. "As far as single-payer, it works in Canada. It works incredibly well in Scotland. It could have worked in a different age, which is the age you're talking about here," Trump said. "What I'd like to see is a private system without the artificial lines around every state...Get rid of the artificial lines, and you will have yourself great plans. And then we have to take care of the people that can't take care of themselves. And I will do that through a different system." 2. Repeal Obamacare. Cover everybody. "I am going to take care of everybody," Trump told CBS News a month later in September. "I don't care if it costs me votes or not. Everybody's going to be taken care of much better than they're taken care of now." 3. Repeal Obamacare, but 'I like the mandate' During a CNN town hall on February 18, 2016, Trump started to answer a question about how he'd replace the Affordable Care Act with health savings accounts, "which are great," but interrupted himself to talk at length about how he's "a self-funder." When pressed by interviewer Anderson Cooper about what would happen when Obamacare is repealed and the mandate disappeared, therefore allowing insurance companies to deny coverage to those with pre-existing conditions, Trump said: "Well, I like the mandate. OK. So here's where I'm a little bit different. I don't want people dying on the streets and I say this all the time." 4. Repeal Obamacare. Replace it with something. Trump was mocked in the February 25 debate for being vague about how he would replace Obamacare. "You'll have many different plans. You'll have competition, you'll have so many different plans," he said, still declining to offer more specificity. 5. Repeal Obamacare. Not everyone will be covered. His health care plan, released online in March, had far more in common with the kind of boilerplate health care proposals the rest of the Republican party touts than his earlier praise for Canada suggested it might. It would likely cause 21 million people to lose their health insurance and cost about $270 billion over 10 years, according to the nonpartisan budget advocacy group Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget (CRFB). It offers up unspecified amounts of grants to states to replace Medicaid, but it's not clear how or what those would look like, or how they would cover the millions of people that Trump's plan lets fall through the cracks. CRFB noted that block grants "could generate a wide range of savings" to the federal budget, but without details on them, it is "impossible to score any savings" from his plan. 6. I do want to keep parts of it, we might just amend it. In an interview with the Wall Street Journal, Trump said he would be OK with amending Obamacare instead of fully repealing it. He also indicated that he wanted to keep the protections for patients with pre-existing conditions, and the measure that allows parents to keep their children insured until age 26. “Either Obamacare will be amended, or repealed and replaced,” Trump said in November in one of his first interviews as the president-elect. If they do repeal it, he said there would be no gap between the repeal of Obamacare and the replacement of it with something else. 7. Begin to repeal on day one. Vice President-elect Mike Pence told Congressional Republicans that Trump’s efforts to repeal Obamacare would begin day one. It's unclear, however, what if any plan there is for a replacement bill. 8. 'Be careful' -- don't take the blame! Shortly after Pence indicated that repeal efforts were in the works, Trump warned Republicans to "be careful" about taking on the bill -- and its liabilities -- arguing that it would "fall on its own weight."

## Marsha Blackburn's Obamacare Repeal Poll: A Twitter Firestorm and Shifting Political Winds

On January 3, 2017, as Representative Marsha Blackburn of Tennessee participated in a mock swearing-in ceremony on Capitol Hill, administered by House Speaker Paul Ryan, a significant online event unfolded. This moment, captured by AP Photo/Zach Gibson, preceded a highly publicized Twitter poll launched by Blackburn, a vocal critic of the Affordable Care Act (ACA), commonly known as Obamacare. The poll aimed to gauge public sentiment on repealing the landmark health-care law.

### The Poll: A Direct Challenge to Obamacare

Representative Blackburn's Twitter poll posed a direct question to her followers: "Do you support the repeal of Obamacare? RT if you do, and share what you want to see as the replacement." She actively encouraged supporters of repeal to retweet the poll and share their ideas for alternative health-care solutions. This initiative was part of a broader Republican effort to dismantle the ACA, a law championed by President Barack Obama.

### Backlash and White House Intervention

The poll's results quickly defied expectations, revealing a significant surge in opposition to repealing Obamacare, far outweighing expressions of support. This unexpected outcome presented a considerable hurdle for Blackburn's repeal advocacy. Adding another layer to the unfolding narrative, reports from The Hill.com indicated that actions from the White House may have amplified the poll's reception.

On the very day Blackburn posted her poll, White House Deputy Press Secretary Eric Schultz retweeted it to his substantial following. This move occurred as President Obama himself was actively engaged in defending the ACA, his signature legislative achievement. President Obama visited Capitol Hill that Wednesday, January 4, 2017, to rally Democratic support against the Republican-led efforts to repeal and replace the law. According to a report by The Hill, Schultz's retweet was seen by some as a strategic move to highlight the public's potential opposition to repeal.

### President-Elect Trump's Cautious Counsel

Concurrently, President-elect Donald Trump, who had made repealing the ACA a cornerstone of his campaign, weighed in on the developing situation. On Wednesday, January 4, 2017, Trump issued a series of tweets advising his fellow Republicans to proceed with caution regarding ACA repeal and replacement. He suggested that Democrats bore responsibility for the ACA's perceived shortcomings. Furthermore, Trump cautioned that Republicans could face significant political backlash if their repeal and replace efforts resulted in increased numbers of uninsured Americans or higher insurance premiums, a concern echoed by some policy analysts at the time.

**Fact Check: Examining Lawmakers' Claims About the Affordable Care Act (ACA)**

As the future of the Affordable Care Act (ACA), commonly known as Obamacare, is debated, competing claims about its impact are being made. Republicans have pledged to repeal the law, while Democrats are working to preserve it, each framing themselves as champions of Americans' healthcare. This analysis examines key assertions surrounding the ACA.

**Claim: Obamacare has caused "massive premium increases."**

**Fact Check:** While some individuals purchasing insurance on the ACA's marketplaces have experienced significant premium hikes – with the average benchmark plan increasing by 25% nationwide, according to the Kaiser Family Foundation – the overall picture is more complex and varies by state. For instance, Arizona saw an average increase of 116%, while premiums in Indiana and Massachusetts actually decreased. Crucially, most exchange enrollees receive government subsidies that substantially reduce their out-of-pocket costs. A study by the Urban Institute indicated that, even without subsidies, exchange plans were approximately 10% cheaper than typical employer-sponsored plans. The perception of higher costs can also be influenced by how employer plans are subsidized by businesses, making them appear less expensive to employees.

**Claim: Under Obamacare, people are "stuck with one option."**

**Fact Check:** This is not the majority experience, although competition has declined. The number of insurance companies participating in the ACA marketplaces has shrunk, leading to less choice for some. This year, one in five exchange customers had only a single insurer to choose from, a notable increase from 2% in 2016, as reported by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS). However, nearly 60% of customers still have a choice of three or more insurers. This lack of competition, which can drive up prices, is more pronounced in rural areas and the South. Insurers have faced challenges partly due to fewer young, healthy individuals enrolling than anticipated. Potential solutions suggested by supporters include more generous subsidies or stronger enrollment penalties. President Obama also revived the idea of a public insurance option to complement private plans.

**Claim: "The health care system has been ruined, dismantled under Obamacare."**

**Fact Check:** Healthcare costs were rising at a faster pace before the ACA. The majority of Americans under 65 still receive health insurance through their employers, though this percentage has been gradually declining. While the cost of employer-sponsored coverage has increased since the ACA's passage, the annual rate of increase was substantially higher in the decade preceding the law. Some of the savings from slower premium growth have been offset by higher deductibles.

Democrats counter that repealing the ACA would be far more damaging. Senate Democratic leader Chuck Schumer stated, "Instead of working to further ensure affordable care for all Americans, [Republicans] seek to rip health care away from millions of Americans, creating chaos in our entire economy." He warned that repealing Obamacare would severely impact rural hospitals.

**Claim: ACA repeal would "rip health care away from millions."**

**Fact Check:** This is true if protections or replacements are not put in place. The ACA has extended health coverage to approximately 20 million Americans through subsidized individual policies, expanded Medicaid, and allowing young adults to remain on their parents' plans. The uninsured rate has fallen to a historic low of around 10%, according to the National Center for Health Statistics. This figure would be even lower if 19 states had expanded Medicaid. Repealing the ACA without a substitute could jeopardize coverage for many of these individuals. Furthermore, millions more who purchase insurance on the exchanges could lose their coverage if the market becomes unstable. The Urban Institute estimates that up to 30 million people could lose health coverage, doubling the uninsured rate. Republicans have pledged an "orderly transition" with a replacement plan, and the effective date of repeal could be delayed. However, insurers might hesitate to participate if the individual market is clearly being phased out.

**Claim: Rural hospitals are going to suffer.**

**Fact Check:** This is a valid concern if the ACA is repealed outright, and it's also linked to the ACA's original structure. The potential impact on hospitals stems from a trade-off made when the ACA was enacted. The government reduced payments to hospitals for treating Medicare patients and the uninsured, anticipating that increased insurance coverage would offset these reductions. Hospitals fear that if repeal reduces insurance coverage without restoring these other payments, they could face a significant increase in unpaid bills. Both the American Hospital Association and the Federation of American Hospitals have urged lawmakers to either preserve insurance coverage or reinstate hospital payment structures.

WASHINGTON — Republicans hope to repeal major parts of the Affordable Care Act using an expedited procedure known as budget reconciliation. The process is sometimes called arcane, but it has been used often in the past 35 years to write some of the nation’s most important laws. “Reconciliation is probably the most potent budget enforcement tool available to Congress for a large portion of the budget,” the Congressional Research Service, a nonpartisan arm of Congress, has said. Here is a primer. Q. What is the budget reconciliation process? A. It is a way for Congress to speed action on legislation that changes taxes or spending, especially spending for entitlement programs like Medicare and Medicaid. Although conceived primarily as a way to reduce federal budget deficits, it has also been used to cut taxes and to create programs that increase spending — changes that can raise deficits. In the Senate, a reconciliation bill can ordinarily be passed with a simple majority. For other bills, a 60-vote majority is often needed to limit debate and move to a final vote.

Almost 8.8 million people signed up as of last Saturday for insurance plans sold by the federal Obamacare health plan marketplace for 2017 coverage, officials announced Wednesday. That tally compares to about 8.6 million plan selections on that HealthCare.gov exchange during the same time period for the prior open-enrollment season. HealthCare.gov serves customers in 39 states; the remaining are served by their own, state-run marketplaces. "With 8.8 million Americans signed up for coverage through HealthCare.gov, more than last year at this time, it is clear that Americans want and need this vital coverage," said U.S. Department of Health and Human Services Secretary Sylvia Burwell. "As we enter the New Year, Americans who are still uninsured should sign up by January 15th to have coverage starting February 1." About 6.55 million of the plan selections were from returning customers. The remaining 2.2 million were from new HealthCare.gov customers.

Florida is the largest swing state and is seen as a must-win for Mr Trump. The Republican candidate later flew to North Carolina and will then head west to Nevada. Opinion polls in recent days have suggested Mr Trump is gaining support but he still remains behind Mrs Clinton in most surveys. In Florida, the contest appears to be tight. Real Clear Politics' poll average puts the Democratic candidate ahead, but poll analysis website FiveThirtyEight says Mr Trump has a 52.6% chance of winning the state. US President Barack Obama won Florida in 2012 by a margin of just 0.9% over Republican Mitt Romney. Candidates need 270 electoral college votes to win the presidency. Florida is worth 29. Some 37 million early voters have already cast their ballots. Reports suggest many more Latino voters are turning out early in key states including Florida, Arizona and Nevada compared to past elections. Media playback is unsupported on your device Media caption Beyonce and Jay Z turn out for Hillary Clinton Florida has a significant Latino population, including many Cubans. Mr Trump told a rally in Tampa: "I say to the Hispanic community living in the inner city, to the African-American community, I say: what the hell do you have to lose?" He said Cubans supported him. He said he would provide jobs and solve crime for inner-city communities. Mrs Clinton courted voters in a Haitian-American district of Miami before holding a rally in Pembroke Pines. Her short address there ended in a downpour of rain. She will later make an appearance in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, alongside pop star Katy Perry. Florida's Latino vote Image copyright Reuters Image caption Some Latinos support Trump, despite his harsh rhetoric targeting immigrants Hispanics make up 16% of registered voters in Florida this election, the Associated Press reports, up from 13.5% in 2012. American Hispanics mostly vote Democrat but Florida's large Cuban population - which make up 31% of the state's Hispanic vote - have traditionally voted Republican. Donald Trump recently began to speak out against the US lifting its economic embargo on Cuba in a play for older voters, analysts say. But they also point out that as more younger Cubans have settled in Miami, support for warming US-Cuba relations - which Mrs Clinton backs - is growing among the community. Polls now suggest that more Miami Cubans are against the embargo than for it. Mr Trump's divisive and insulting comments about immigrants from Mexico and elsewhere have also angered many Latinos. Both candidates held rallies in Ohio and Pennsylvania on Friday. In Cleveland, Mrs Clinton ended the day's campaigning at a concert, where she was joined by the singer Beyonce and her husband, rapper Jay Z. The Clinton campaign is putting on several events with high-profile figures from the entertainment world as it tries to energise young and minority voters. Rocker Jon Bon Jovi will later appear with Democratic vice-presidential candidate Tim Kaine in St Petersburg, Florida. On Friday night, Mr Trump told supporters in Hershey, Pennsylvania, that he "didn't have to bring J-Lo or Jay Z" to draw crowds. "I am here all by myself. Just me. No guitar, no piano, no nothing," he said. In other campaign developments: Documents alleging to show that Mr Trump's wife, Melania, worked illegally as a model in the US for a brief period two decades ago before she obtained the legal right to work have been obtained by the Associated Press. The National Enquirer, a US tabloid, paid $150,000 (£120,000) to a former Playboy model for exclusive rights to the story of an alleged affair she had with Donald Trump from 2006-2007, the Wall Street Journal reports. But the Enquirer, which supports Mr Trump, then killed the story, it says. The National Enquirer has denied paying to suppress the story and the Trump campaign says the alleged affair did not occur. Democratic campaign chief John Podesta said Mr Trump should ask the governor of New Jersey, Chris Christie, to step down as the head of his transition team after two of his aides were found guilty of orchestrating traffic jams in New Jersey as an act of political retribution against a local Democratic mayor. Mr Christie has denied any involvement. Who is ahead in the polls? See full poll tracker Mrs Clinton has faced a torrent of negative headlines after the FBI said last week it was looking into emails that may be connected to her use of a private email server while she was secretary of state. The Clinton camp have questioned the timing of the announcement. Two senior Democrats have now called for an investigation into the role of former New York mayor Rudy Giuliani, a Trump surrogate, after he appeared to suggest he knew about the inquiry before it was announced. Separately, US authorities have said they are assessing the credibility of information on a possible al-Qaeda extremist attack before election day. New York City, Texas and Virginia were said to be possible targets but a police spokesman said the information "lacks specificity". In the small Macedonian town of Veles, a surprising digital phenomenon has emerged: over 100 pro-Trump websites are being operated by locals, primarily young men, seeking to capitalize on the lucrative U.S. online advertising market. BuzzFeed News identified this network of sites, which use American-sounding domain names like WorldPoliticus.com and TrumpVision365.com, all publishing aggressively pro-Trump content.

These Macedonian operators are driven by economic incentives, not political allegiance. As Facebook's earnings reports indicate, a U.S. user is worth approximately four times more than a user outside the U.S. The fraction-of-a-penny-per-click revenue from U.S. display advertising, a market in decline for American publishers, translates into significant earnings in Veles. According to BuzzFeed News interviews, the most effective strategy for these site operators is to create sensationalist and often false content that appeals to Trump supporters, thereby maximizing shares and engagement on Facebook.

This has led to Veles becoming a significant source of misinformation, contributing to the spread of false and misleading content identified in a recent BuzzFeed News analysis of hyperpartisan Facebook pages. These sites highlight the economic motivations behind producing misinformation tailored for wealthy advertising markets and platforms like Facebook and Google AdSense. A university student in Veles, who requested anonymity, explained to BuzzFeed News, "Yes, the info in the blogs is bad, false, and misleading but the rationale is that 'if it gets the people to click on it and engage, then use it.'"

BuzzFeed News' investigation, using domain registration records and online searches, uncovered over 100 active U.S. politics websites originating from Veles, with some boasting hundreds of thousands of Facebook followers. An additional 40 domains registered in Veles are no longer active. While a report by Meta.mk identified six such sites, and The Guardian reported 150, BuzzFeed News' findings point to a larger operation.

The primary motivation for launching these sites is financial. A 17-year-old operator, who runs a site with four others, stated, "I started the site for a easy way to make money. In Macedonia the economy is very weak and teenagers are not allowed to work, so we need to find creative ways to make some money." He added that revenue from a small site can be enough to afford many things, unlike in his current situation where he cannot afford music gear despite being a musician.

Content on these sites is often aggregated or plagiarized from fringe and right-wing U.S. sources. Macedonian operators find stories, create sensationalized headlines, and post them to their sites, then promote them on Facebook to drive traffic and ad revenue. While some experimented with left-leaning content, Trump-related stories consistently performed better on Facebook. A 16-year-old operating BVANews.com told BuzzFeed News, "People in America prefer to read news about Trump."

BuzzFeed News' research indicates that the most successful posts from these Macedonian sites are frequently false or misleading. For instance, a post on ConservativeState.com, claiming Hillary Clinton endorsed Trump in 2013, garnered an astonishing 480,000 shares, reactions, and comments on Facebook. This significantly outperformed a New York Times exclusive on Donald Trump's tax returns, which generated over 175,000 Facebook interactions in a month. The Clinton story was sourced from TheRightists.com, a site that admits to publishing both real and fake content. While WikiLeaks emails show Clinton mentioning a desire for successful business people in politics in a private speech to Goldman Sachs, she did not mention Donald Trump, making the headline quote false.

Four of the five most successful posts identified by BuzzFeed News from Macedonian sites were false, including claims that the Pope endorsed Trump and that Mike Pence called Michelle Obama the "most vulgar first lady we've ever had." These four posts alone generated over 1 million Facebook interactions, resulting in substantial traffic and ad revenue, while misinforming many.

However, the Macedonian operators report that the market has become saturated, making it harder to earn money. Those who started in early 2016 are reportedly earning the most. One student mentioned a friend earning $5,000 per month, or "even $3,000 per day" during peak engagement. The 16-year-old behind BVANews.com, along with a partner, also runs health websites and averages 1 million page views per month on their politics site, though they declined to share revenue figures. The 17-year-old and his partners are still awaiting Google AdSense approval, currently earning no revenue with about 800 daily views.

The university student, who launched his U.S. politics site in August but stopped updating it to focus on a more successful health and well-being site, estimated "thousands" of health-related sites are run from Veles. He noted that U.S. politics became a lucrative opportunity in 2016 due to Trump and Facebook, but added, "I stopped because I didn't really enjoy doing it and we didn't actually make any money from it since there are so many people posting already. The people who started early are the ones reaping the rewards."

A recent Washington Post-ABC News tracking poll reveals a tight race between Donald Trump and Hillary Clinton nationally. Hillary Clinton holds a slim four-point lead over Donald Trump as the campaign enters its final weekend. According to the poll, 30% of likely voters have already cast their ballots.

Interviews conducted from Monday through Thursday show Clinton with 47% support and Trump with 43%. This lead is within the margin of sampling error. It is similar to Clinton's 47-44 margin from the previous four-day period. However, it represents the largest numerical gap between the candidates in the past week of daily tracking.

Libertarian Gary Johnson's support has increased from 3% to 4% in the latest poll. Jill Stein remains at 2%, unchanged from the past week.

Clinton is showing weaknesses in the campaign's final days. On the question of honesty and trustworthiness, she trails Trump by five points. She also no longer holds a significant lead among voters who have already voted nationwide. Among those who have not yet voted, Trump supporters are more likely to say they are following the contest "very closely." This suggests they may have higher turnout rates.

Clinton's current 4-point edge is identical to a mid-October Post-ABC poll. It also matches President Obama's 4-point winning margin in 2012. Clinton benefits from substantial advantages with groups that supported Obama in his two elections.

African American likely voters support Clinton by 86% to 6% over Trump. This is slightly lower than Obama's 93-6 margin in the 2012 exit poll. Latinos favor Clinton by 71% to 23%. This is similar to a 67-19% margin in a Washington Post-Univision News Poll of Latino voters released Thursday. It also mirrors Obama's 71-27 margin in 2012.

While the overall gender gap in support for Clinton and Trump is similar to past elections, the divide is particularly pronounced among younger voters in the latest Post-ABC Tracking Poll. Among likely female voters under 40, Clinton leads Trump 59% to 31%. In contrast, among younger men, the split is 41% for Clinton and 38% for Trump. This is an area where Clinton may see stronger turnout, as younger women have historically voted at higher rates than their male counterparts.

Party loyalists continue to support their party's nominees at similar levels. 88% of Democrats support Clinton, and 85% of Republicans support Trump. These figures are slightly lower than in previous years. This is partly due to the significant support for third-party candidates. Typically, candidates receive over 90% support from their own party members.

Independents favor Trump by 45% to 39%. Both Clinton and Trump receive support from over 80% of independents who lean toward one party. However, those who lean toward neither party favor Trump by 43% to 34%.

**Early Voting Trends**

Early voting is increasing rapidly. 30% of likely voters report having already voted. This is higher than the 21% who had voted by the end of the previous weekend. The current percentage of likely voters who have cast ballots early is higher than at the same point in the Post-ABC Tracking Poll four years ago. Five days before the 2012 election, 22% reported voting early.

Clinton holds only a narrow lead over Trump among early voters, 49% to 44%. This is similar to her overall lead among likely voters but smaller than her 54-41 advantage earlier in the week, according to the Post-ABC Tracking Poll. While the preferences of early voters who have already cast ballots cannot change, the growing number of early voters can influence overall preferences. Polls can only estimate these shifts roughly.

With turnout being crucial in the final days, Clinton supporters who have not yet voted are slightly less likely to report following the race closely. The poll indicates that 73% of Trump supporters planning to vote in the coming days say they are following the race "very closely." This compares to 64% of Clinton supporters.

Voters also perceive Trump as more honest and trustworthy than Clinton by 44% to 39%. This is a slight narrowing from Trump's eight-point lead over the past weekend. Clinton trails on this measure due to weaker faith in her honesty among Democrats compared to the faith Republicans have in Trump. Independents also state by 18 points that Trump is more trustworthy than Clinton. 20% of independents and 12% of Democrats say "neither" candidate is honest.

This Washington Post-ABC News poll was conducted via cellular and landline phones from October 31 to November 3, 2016. It surveyed a random national sample of 1,419 likely voters. The margin of sampling error is plus or minus three percentage points. Sampling, data collection, and tabulation were performed by Abt-SRBI of New York.

**Tightening Presidential Race: Trump Gains Momentum as Election Day Nears**

With Election Day just three days away, the presidential race has tightened considerably, with Republican nominee Donald Trump showing renewed momentum. The key question is whether Trump has enough time to close the gap with Democratic opponent Hillary Clinton. While current opinion polls suggest Clinton still holds an advantage, her lead has narrowed significantly, indicating a potentially close election.

**Key Factors Influencing the Election Outcome:**

*   **Trump's Rising Support:** Late-breaking voters appear to be shifting towards Trump in both national and state polls. Despite negative publicity surrounding FBI Director James Comey's announcement about newly discovered emails, Clinton's support has not significantly eroded. As of late Friday afternoon, Clinton held 45.1% support in the RealClearPolitics (RCP) national polling average, a slight decrease from her recent high. At that time, Trump trailed by six points, but his support has since increased to 42.7%.

*   **Shifts in Voter Demographics:** Libertarian Party nominee Gary Johnson has seen a slight decrease in support, with Trump potentially benefiting more from this shift than Clinton. Polling data from sources like the Pew Research Center indicates that previously hesitant Republican voters are now supporting Trump. The central question remains whether Trump can overtake Clinton, as he has not held a sustained lead during the campaign, according to FiveThirtyEight's analysis.

*   **Clinton's "Firewall" States Under Pressure:** Clinton's strategy relied on winning states previously secured by John Kerry in 2004, including Pennsylvania, Michigan, and Wisconsin. This assumption is facing increasing uncertainty. In New Hampshire, Trump has led or tied Clinton in the four most recent major polls, according to an ABC News/Washington Post tracking poll. Clinton's lead in Pennsylvania has narrowed to less than three points, a significant drop from her earlier double-digit advantage. Her advantage in Michigan has decreased to four points from seven points a week prior, according to a Detroit Free Press poll. Wisconsin is currently considered a safer state for Clinton, though recent Marquette University Law School polls show a tightening race there as well. A loss in either Pennsylvania (20 Electoral College votes) or Michigan (16 Electoral College votes) could significantly complicate Clinton's path to the presidency, as outlined by election forecasters at The Cook Political Report.

*   **Candidate Travel and Mobilization Efforts:** Clinton's decision to campaign in Detroit suggests concerns about Michigan's outcome and reports of decreased enthusiasm among black voters, a key demographic for the Democratic party, according to analysis by The New York Times. Her closing campaign event in Philadelphia will feature former President Bill Clinton, President Obama, and First Lady Michelle Obama, aiming to bolster support in Pennsylvania. Trump's schedule is more varied, with planned events in Florida, North Carolina, Nevada, Colorado, Iowa, Wisconsin, and Pennsylvania over the weekend and into Monday. Democrats have historically leveraged celebrity endorsements, a trend evident in this campaign's final days. Concerts featuring artists like Jay-Z and Beyoncé in Cleveland aimed to encourage voter turnout for Clinton, with organizers citing an estimated 10,000 attendees. Stevie Wonder performed at a similar event in Philadelphia, drawing a crowd of over 5,000. Jon Bon Jovi is scheduled for events in St. Petersburg and Charlotte. The actual impact of these appearances on voting remains to be seen, though studies by the University of Pennsylvania have shown a correlation between celebrity engagement and voter turnout in past elections.

*   **Potential for Further Surprises:** The current election cycle has been marked by significant drama and unusual events. The possibility of further unexpected news, perhaps from sources like WikiLeaks, which has released numerous documents throughout the campaign, cannot be ruled out. Experts at the Brennan Center for Justice have noted the unprecedented nature of the information landscape in this election.

Beyoncé and Jay-Z headlined a powerful campaign rally for Hillary Clinton in Cleveland on November 4, 2016, aiming to energize crucial voter groups. The event at the Wolstein Center featured a star-studded lineup of hip-hop artists, all rallying behind the Democratic presidential candidate.

The concert was a direct appeal to young and minority voters, demographics the Clinton campaign actively sought to mobilize. The artists delivered strong endorsements for Clinton, emphasizing her historic potential to become the nation's first female president, drawing a parallel to the precedent set by the country's first Black president.

Key moments from the rally highlighted the campaign's message:

*   **Beyoncé's Call to Vote:** The iconic singer stressed the historical importance of the election, reminding the audience that women had only recently gained the right to vote. She urged everyone to exercise this right, declaring, "But we have to vote."
*   **Inspiring Future Generations:** Beyoncé shared her personal vision, expressing her hope that her daughter would see a woman leading the country, thereby broadening her perceived horizons. She stated, "That's why I'm with her."
*   **Hip-Hop's Endorsement:** Alongside Beyoncé and Jay-Z, artists like Big Sean, J. Cole, and Chance the Rapper took the stage to encourage votes for Clinton.
*   **Critique of the Opposition:** Jay-Z directly addressed concerns about Donald Trump, stating, "His conversation is divisive. He cannot be our president."
*   **A Nod to Past Support:** Big Sean drew a connection to a similar free event Jay-Z hosted for Barack Obama's campaign in 2008, reinforcing the collective support with the phrase, "we with her."

The rally also incorporated visual elements and quotes that reinforced campaign themes. Clinton's own enthusiasm was evident as she commented on the concert's energy and the distinctive blue pantsuits worn by Beyoncé's backup dancers. The event underscored the message that "bad officials are elected by good citizens who do not vote."

Hillary Clinton's recent campaign visit to Detroit underscores Michigan's crucial role in the upcoming presidential election. With 16 electoral votes, Michigan has historically been a Democratic stronghold, voting for the party's nominee since 1988. While President Obama won the state by 9.5 points in 2012, recent polling indicates a tightening race, with Clinton's lead over Donald Trump narrowing to just four points. This shift has made Michigan a key target for both Trump's campaign and the Republican National Committee, particularly given the state's significant white, non-college-educated voter demographic, which has shown support for Trump elsewhere.

### Campaign Strategies and Confidence

Clinton's campaign attributes the narrowing polls to national political trends and the lingering impact of the FBI's renewed review of her email investigation. Michigan's senior adviser, Steve Neumann, acknowledged the tightening race nationwide and emphasized the importance of a strong ground game. The campaign has maintained a significant presence with 35 field offices in Michigan and recently launched a six-figure advertising campaign in the state, alongside other key swing states.

During a rally in Detroit, Clinton presented a stark contrast with her opponent, urging voters to reject a candidate who "demeans women and mocks the disabled, who insults African-Americans and Latinos." Despite the increased pressure, campaign officials express confidence in their standing, with chairman John Podesta stating Michigan remains in their "likely win" column and that they feel good about the state's demographics.

This visit marked Clinton's third trip to Michigan since the Democratic National Convention, a frequency lower than her engagement in other battleground states like Ohio and Pennsylvania. Her campaign has also deployed prominent surrogates, including Vice Presidential nominee Tim Kaine, former President Bill Clinton, Chelsea Clinton, and celebrities like Uzo Aduba and Cher. Aides suggest Michigan's limited early voting options contribute to this more concentrated campaign schedule.

### Republican Optimism and Past Surprises

Conversely, Republican strategists are optimistic about their chances in Michigan, with the RNC spokesperson calling the race "dead even." The memory of Bernie Sanders' unexpected primary victory over Clinton in March, where polls predicted a five-point win for Clinton, serves as a reminder of the state's unpredictable political landscape. While Clinton's campaign distinguishes between primary and general election dynamics, the Sanders upset remains a significant cautionary tale.

A loss in Michigan would significantly complicate Clinton's path to victory, requiring wins in states like Nevada, Florida, and Pennsylvania. Despite the heightened stakes, Clinton appeared composed during her campaign stops. The rally concluded with the song "Don't Stop Believin'," a nod to "South Detroit," a location the songwriter has clarified is not a specific geographical place.

President Obama has adopted a new catchphrase, "C'mon, man," during his campaign efforts for Hillary Clinton. This expression serves as a way for Obama to voice his frustration with Donald Trump and Republican lawmakers, much like "Yes, We Can" was a rallying cry during his own presidential runs.

Obama has used "C'mon, man" in various contexts to highlight what he sees as unacceptable behavior or reasoning from Trump and his supporters. For instance, in Fayetteville, North Carolina, he addressed excuses made for Trump's statements, such as "He didn't really mean it" or "It's locker-room talk." Obama countered these justifications with "C'mon man," emphasizing that policy disagreements shouldn't excuse disqualifying actions. Similarly, in Miami, he questioned Trump's suitability for the presidency by pointing to his sensitivity to criticism, like his reaction to a "Saturday Night Live" skit. Obama's use of "C'mon, man" in this instance drew applause from the audience.

The phrase has resonated with audiences, with one attendee even shouting "C'mon, man!" during a rally in response to Obama's discussion of Republican obstructionism. White House spokesman Eric Schultz acknowledged the phrase's popularity, stating it "sums up the frustration that not only the president has, but that a lot of voters have." He cited Republican senators' pledges to block any Supreme Court nominee Clinton might propose as an example of this frustration, referencing their earlier blocking of Obama's nominee. The phrase "c'mon, man" is also known as the title of an ESPN segment and, according to Urbandictionary.com, is typically used when someone says or does something foolish.

**Election Polls: A Shifting Landscape and Key State Dynamics**

Recent election polling data reveals a dynamic and evolving race, with national trends suggesting a potential stabilization for Hillary Clinton while state-level polls indicate heightened competition. This comes after a period where national polls had shown a downward trend for Clinton.

**National Poll Trends and Expert Analysis**

Over the past 24 hours, national tracking polls from reputable sources such as ABC News and The Washington Post, The New Orleans Times-Picayune, and the Los Angeles Times have indicated gains for Clinton. While the LA Times poll, for instance, still favors Donald Trump, most national tracking polls are no longer showing significant movement towards him. Some, like the IBD/TIPP tracking poll, suggest a steady race.

Major, one-off national polls from established organizations like CNN and NBC News are anticipated this weekend. These are expected to significantly influence the overall electoral forecast. Currently, the national polling sentiment, as analyzed by FiveThirtyEight, suggests Clinton may have reached a low point a few days prior, possibly following the FBI's announcement on July 22nd, and is now showing signs of recovery.

**State-Level Polls: Areas of Concern for Clinton**

Despite the positive national trends for Clinton, state polls present a less encouraging outlook. Several polls indicate a tied race in New Hampshire. In Pennsylvania, Clinton's lead has narrowed to approximately 3 percentage points in our forecast, a notable shift from previous indicators. Michigan's polls are also tightening, with a significant percentage of undecided voters, reportedly around 5-7% in some surveys.

New Mexico's polling has become so close that it is now considered a "state to watch" by analysts at RealClearPolitics, though this may be a precautionary measure. Clinton's numbers have remained stronger in Wisconsin and Virginia, with polls consistently showing her with leads of 5-7 percentage points in Wisconsin and 4-6 percentage points in Virginia. Colorado, however, shows very mixed polling data, with some polls showing a slight Clinton lead and others indicating a near tie.

**Swing State Dynamics and Polling Methodologies**

We have incorporated all newly added swing state polls since our last update, including the latest state-by-state tracking from SurveyMonkey, Ipsos, and Pulse Opinion Research. It is worth noting that many of these recent polls were not conducted by traditional live callers. For those who distrust online or automated polling, this might offer some reassurance, as traditional methods are still being employed by firms like Marist College and Quinnipiac University.

We are also observing a number of automated polls from firms with Republican leanings and middling pollster ratings, such as those from Automated Insights and Opinion Research Corporation. Our model accounts for this by weighting polls based on their ratings from organizations like FiveThirtyEight and the American Association for Public Opinion Research (AAPOR), and adjusting for potential partisan biases.

However, the overall electoral map is becoming more uncertain for Clinton. States that once seemed like a secure "firewall," such as Ohio and Iowa, are now showing increased competition. Nevada, for example, could become a more critical state than New Hampshire, especially considering early voting statistics which show a higher turnout among Democratic-leaning voters in some areas. It's also possible that Florida or North Carolina could become the decisive states if Clinton experiences a late decline in Michigan or Pennsylvania, with polls in Florida showing a margin of error of +/- 3 percentage points.

**Overall Forecast and Statistical Significance**

Clinton's Electoral College chances remain at 65 percent in both our polls-only and polls-plus forecasts, showing little change from the previous day. She also maintains an approximate 3-percentage-point lead over Trump in the national popular vote forecast, a margin that has fluctuated between 2.5 and 3.5 percentage points over the past week. While the situation doesn't appear to be worsening for Clinton, it's also not definitively improving. At this stage, even a small swing of 1 percentage point in either direction could be significant, as a 4-point lead would offer considerably more security than a 2-point one, representing a difference of millions of votes nationally.

query: What are some of the core company values that new employees at Uber are asked to subscribe to?

documents.text: SAN FRANCISCO — When new employees join Uber, they are asked to subscribe to 14 core company values, including making bold bets, being “obsessed” with the customer, and “always be hustlin’.” The ride-hailing service particularly emphasizes “meritocracy,” the idea that the best and brightest will rise to the top based on their efforts, even if it means stepping on toes to get there. Those values have helped propel Uber to one of Silicon Valley’s biggest success stories. The company is valued at close to $70 billion by private investors and now operates in more than 70 countries. Yet the focus on pushing for the best result has also fueled what current and former Uber employees describe as a Hobbesian environment at the company, in which workers are sometimes pitted against one another and where a blind eye is turned to infractions from top performers. Interviews with more than 30 current and former Uber employees, as well as reviews of internal emails, chat logs and tape-recorded meetings, paint a picture of an often unrestrained workplace culture. Among the most egregious accusations from employees, who either witnessed or were subject to incidents and who asked to remain anonymous because of confidentiality agreements and fear of retaliation: One Uber manager groped female co-workers’ breasts at a company retreat in Las Vegas. A director shouted a homophobic slur at a subordinate during a heated confrontation in a meeting. Another manager threatened to beat an underperforming employee’s head in with a baseball bat.

Some Uber users trying to delete the app received a notification from the company specifically mentioning the allegations of sexual harassment detailed over the weekend in a blog post by former employee Susan Fowler Rigetti. A screenshot of one such interaction, confirmed to Mashable as authentic by an Uber spokesperson, demonstrates the lengths to which the company is going to assure fleeing customers that "what [Fowler] describes is abhorrent and against everything Uber stands for and believes in." A spokesperson said the above language was only used in response to customers who specifically referenced the latest allegations, and that the number of account-deletion requests following the publication of Fowler's blog post have been low. "Everyone at Uber is deeply hurting after reading Susan Fowler's blog post," the message reads. It goes on to say that a law firm with former U.S. Attorney General Eric Holder as partner will lead an independent investigation into the matter. "We believe in creating a workplace where a deep sense of justice underpins everything we do and it's everyone's number one priority to create change in the coming months and years." Some, shall we say, remain skeptical. Still, look forward to the independent investigation by paid Uber lobbyist Eric Holder and Uber board member Arianna Huffington — Paul Bradley Carr (@paulbradleycarr) February 23, 2017 In the meantime, it's clear that Uber can no longer ignore Susan Fowler. UPDATE: Feb. 23, 2017, 10:41 a.m. PST This post has been updated to reflect that, according to a spokesperson, Uber has stopped sending out the above message in response to account-deletion requests.

Dear Mr. Kalanick,

Susan J. Fowler, a former Uber employee, recently published a blog post detailing serious accusations against members of your management team. These allegations include sexual harassment, gender discrimination, intentional deception, career sabotage, and illegal threats of termination. The widespread reaction to Ms. Fowler's post has been one of sadness and disgust.

While the full truth of internal company matters may remain unknown to outsiders, events of this magnitude can profoundly impact an organization. This situation presents an opportunity for Uber to critically examine its current culture and future direction.

### Independent Investigation

You have stated that your CHRO, Liane Hornsey, is conducting an urgent investigation, with board member Arianna Huffington ensuring its independence. However, if any part of Ms. Fowler's allegations holds true, it is crucial to engage a completely independent organization for a thorough and unbiased inquiry. This will ensure the full scope of the issues is revealed and the investigation's impact is not diminished.

### Leadership Reevaluation

The claims suggest a deeply problematic company culture. For instance, Ms. Fowler reported that her engineering team's female representation dropped from over 25% to under 6% in less than 1.5 years. This raises serious questions about why female employees are leaving at such a rapid rate.

Employee behavior often mirrors that of leadership. If management and HR personnel are accused of such unethical conduct, your response as CEO must be decisive. To genuinely address the company's health, consider significant leadership changes, potentially including your own departure.

### The Case for Stepping Down

Uber has a history of being perceived as disregarding rules and mistreating drivers, issues that have persisted despite rebranding efforts. Stepping down could be a selfless act that benefits the company, demonstrating your commitment to its mission.

This would allow you to cooperate fully with an independent investigation, gaining crucial insights into what went wrong. Such a period of reflection and learning could better equip you to lead in the future, perhaps even returning to Uber after sufficient time has passed.

### Listening to Your People

While hasty decisions are ill-advised, delays are also detrimental. It is strongly recommended that you speak directly with your employees to understand their experiences. Even more importantly, reach out to former employees, including Ms. Fowler, and listen to their perspectives. They are likely to offer the candid feedback needed at this critical juncture.

Ultimately, true leadership is not defined by a title or position, but by actions and a genuine commitment to what is right. It is time for Uber to move forward.

Sincerely,

Justin Bariso

## Addressing Uber's Crisis: Beyond the #DeleteUber Movement

The recent wave of users deleting Uber from their smartphones, prompted by serious allegations of sexual harassment and employee mismanagement detailed by a former engineer, highlights a significant public outcry. While this protest offers a sense of immediate action, its impact on Uber's global revenue is likely to be minimal. Many participating in the #DeleteUber movement are U.S.-based users, and past campaigns, like the one following CEO Travis Kalanick's ties to President Donald Trump, saw only a small fraction of users depart. Furthermore, such boycotts disproportionately affect individual drivers who rely on the platform for their livelihood, rather than the company's leadership.

### The Need for Leadership Change

The pervasive "bro culture" that allowed for serious misconduct to go unaddressed directly stems from the top, making Travis Kalanick's termination a necessary step. Kalanick's history of indifference, his dismissive remarks about taxi drivers and current Uber drivers, and his alleged passive response to a subordinate's unethical suggestion regarding a journalist, all point to a leadership style that has fostered a toxic environment. Susan Fowler Rigetti's impactful blog post detailing Uber's systemic sexism underscores that the company prioritizes high performers over basic human decency, an attitude undeniably shaped by Kalanick. Removing him could allow the organization to begin functioning more effectively.

### Reimagining Uber's Leadership and Structure

Uber requires a new CEO, and the company's core mission, despite its operational flaws, remains valuable. A leader who embodies diversity, ideally a woman, should take the helm. Qualified candidates like Ursula Burns, former CEO of Xerox, or Susan Wojcicki, CEO of YouTube, should be considered. While Arianna Huffington's digital engagement is noted, her position on Uber's board since April 2016 makes her complicit in the current crisis. Her recent prominence in promising accountability rings hollow given her past inaction. The board must prioritize appointing a new CEO.

Beyond leadership, offending managers must be removed. The alleged propositioning of Fowler Rigetti and the described "shenanigans" by mid-level managers necessitate significant restructuring. Individuals hired directly by Kalanick, who likely share his perspectives, should be scrutinized.

Uber's entire Human Resources department should be disbanded. An HR department that prioritizes protecting management and the company's bottom line over its employees is fundamentally flawed. Fowler Rigetti's account of HR dismissing complaints about management and sexual harassment demonstrates a department that became a barrier to resolution, not a solution.

Finally, Uber's sexual harassment policies must be rewritten and published online. Transparency is paramount. While the specifics of past policies are unclear, they were evidently ignored. A new, externally drafted policy, publicly accessible, could foster adherence and enforcement. While a complete overhaul is improbable, and superficial apologies and minor adjustments are more likely, Uber's board must seriously consider these recommendations for the company's future.

I suppose I shouldn’t focus on the leather jacket, but to me that was the swag that broke the millennial’s back in this whole sorry saga taking place at Uber right now. What leather jacket, you may ask? Why, the leather jacket that some boneheaded manager at the car-hailing phenom apparently could not seem to buy for female engineers in his unit. It is perhaps the most absurd part of a beautifully written and deeply disturbing blog post by Susan Fowler. Let’s be clear, her chronicle of appalling allegations of sexism and sexual harassment after working at Uber for a year are the real and true issues here, deep-seated problems that the company must totally eradicate, and quickly. Simply put, Fowler’s indictment of Uber — which has raised $16 billion, is valued at over $65 billion and has attracted top investors from across Silicon Valley and the world — was devastating in its knife-sharp detail of a bro culture gone very wrong. It’s also more than depressing, because it echoes so many stories I’ve heard over the years in tech from women at all levels and in all kinds of careers and at all kinds of companies. The too-frequent anecdotes of unrelenting aggression of all kinds that never seems to improve, even after critical events like the Ellen Pao trial, the yucky mess at Tinder, the debacle at GitHub or, well, this. After seeing little change, women often decline to make these kinds of experiences public and often just move on to a better company where the culture is not as toxic. Poisonous is more like it at Uber, from Fowler’s account of her short time there, which must have felt like an eternity. Along with unwelcome and cloddish sexual advances from a manager from whom there was no easy escape, she wrote of relentless infighting for positioning among executives and their minions and a corporate ethos that sounded akin to a geek “Game of Thrones,” except not even remotely entertaining. Throughout it all, there was perhaps the most incompetent of human resources staffs, which seemed to pick the very worst choice every time to muck up a bad situation even further. But Fowler’s side story of the leather jackets is one that drives it all home to a surreal level. To get you up to speed, here’s her account of the battle royale over swag, which you need to read all the way through: Things were beginning to get even more comically absurd with each passing day. Every time something ridiculous happened, every time a sexist email was sent, I’d send a short report to HR just to keep a record going. Things came to a head with one particular email chain from the director of our engineering organization concerning leather jackets that had been ordered for all of the SREs. See, earlier in the year, the organization had promised leather jackets for everyone in the organization, and had taken all of our sizes; we all tried them on and found our sizes, and placed our orders. One day, all of the women (there were, I believe, six of us left in the org) received an email saying that no leather jackets were being ordered for the women because there were not enough women in the organization to justify placing an order. I replied and said that I was sure Uber SRE could find room in their budget to buy leather jackets for the, what, six women if it could afford to buy them for over a hundred and twenty men. The director replied back, saying that if we women really wanted equality, then we should realize we were getting equality by not getting the leather jackets. He said that because there were so many men in the org, they had gotten a significant discount on the men’s jackets but not on the women’s jackets, and it wouldn’t be equal or fair, he argued, to give the women leather jackets that cost a little more than the men’s jackets. We were told that if we wanted leather jackets, we women needed to find jackets that were the same price as the bulk-order price of the men’s jackets. What? What? WTF? I had so many questions: Who was this idiotic director? Why would he email back something so stupid? Did anyone ever teach him what fairness and equality actually was? Who raised him? Wolves? Fonzie? And why was I so irked over $120 apparel? I’ll tell you why: Because it suggested that things were so badly run at Uber with regard to treating women techies fairly that one manager could not even pretend to make an effort on even the most basic of gestures to allow his female employees to feel included. And being made to feel powerless and excluded on things both little and big is what this is all about, isn’t it? Not being able to do anything about what is a patently wrong situation, because there is no real choice but to stay and endure or leave and lose a chance to be part of what is obviously a really huge opportunity from both a work and financial point of view. The ugly message: Hey honey, don’t let the door hit you — hey, nice ass, by the way — on the way out if you don’t like it. Obviously, this is not representative of the whole company, as Fowler noted, too. There are many, many good people working at Uber and they don’t deserve to be painted by the same brush as those who are not as good. Or, more precisely, the not-good who are too good. Fowler said Uber managers called these people “high performers” — those able to misbehave with no consequences because of their mad skills at engineering. Or, as Uber board member Arianna Huffington more accurately dubbed them at an all-hands meeting earlier this week: “Brilliant jerks.” While that sounds to me like far too many people in Silicon Valley, it’s actually a very good way to describe the problem Uber has. Which is to say, brilliant jerks and the executives who tolerate them, some of whom are also brilliant jerks. So now what does Uber do to rid itself of them, as Huffington has promised she and a team investigating the mess would? From the looks of it, it has begun in a very fast and very necessary public mea culpa, which is likely being driven by what appears to be new and more experienced executives who have gotten there over the last six months. The obvious face of that we-are-so-so-so-sorry has been — and has to be — CEO Travis Kalanick, who has also served — and quite effectively — as the chief tough guy at the company in the past. In a profile of him that I did for Vanity Fair in 2014, I began thus: Every now and then, when he’s spoiling for a fight, Travis Kalanick has a face like a fist. At these times, his eyes crinkle, his nose flares, and his mouth purses just like a clenched hand readying a punch. Even his Marine-style, salt-and-pepper hair seems to stand on end and bristle, as it were, at whatever the 38-year-old entrepreneur happens to be facing down ... He has directed barbs — in speeches and videos, and on Twitter — especially fervently toward the taxi industry, but also toward city and local regulators across the country (and now the world), his rivals, and sometimes even his own customers when they dare to question his company’s practices. But is it real? Sort of and yet not so much, as it turns out. As one venture capitalist who has worked with Kalanick says of him: “It’s douche as a tactic, not a strategy.” Yes, Kalanick has done douche as a tactic very well indeed, which is one big reason the company grew so aggressively. As with all startups, the company’s DNA is usually set by its founders, and his swaggering tone seeped into the culture, where it then was magnified and went viral. It also curdled. And that’s why we find ourselves where we are now, where that unchecked pugnaciousness and a now-warped version of not-giving-any-fucks is no longer working. How can I tell? Well, after a longish period of no really truly awful stories about some incident of bad behavior at Uber — which has plagued the company since its 2009 founding, as it has skipped from one avoidable controversy after another — this is precisely why the #deleteuber meme caught on so quickly just before this latest crisis. While it is pretty clear that Uber didn’t mean to take advantage of the taxi strike in New York that was the reaction to President Donald Trump’s inane immigration ban, everyone quickly believed that it did. And that then caused real damage to Uber’s business, with hundreds of thousands of deletions over what was essentially a misunderstanding. This was followed by Kalanick joining and then leaving Trump’s business advisory council, which he did after the ban was announced — and was the right thing to do — with intense pressure from employees and customers. But did Kalanick catch a break on either side of those moves? No, he certainly did not. He was seen as an opportunist joining, by kissing up to Trump, and he was considered an opportunist leaving, for trying to look principled by dissing Trump. Pro tip: You can’t look like the good guy when you have played up the bad-guy role for so long. Ask Darth Vader, who had to die to get redemption. Cultivating a dark image has consequences, and lurking always is the belief that Uber must be up to no good. More to the point, that is why no one is going to give the company a break here — see this piece by the New York Times (Cocaine! Groping! Beyoncé-hiring!) — and which is only the first of more to surely come. And already many are trashing its investigation into the Fowler allegations as too insular and cooked, calling it a sham even before it has really started. So, will this mess impact Uber’s current business and its reputation with consumers? Certainly. Will it make it harder to attract talent and hold onto talent it already has? Definitely. Will it make it more difficult to get the breathing room when Uber makes its next inevitable mistake? Oh, yes, it will. Will it make Kalanick’s command a little weaker? Uh-huh, especially if he does not act decisively here. There is one silver lining, though. Even Uber’s critics do not imagine this situation will put its expected initial public offering in 2018 in danger as yet. “If Uber shows everyone the money, a lot will be forgiven,” said one investor who is appalled by what he read from Fowler. “They will get a pass.” Yes, the money. Always the money, which always seems to render silent those complicit. But is a pass what Uber wants if it truly wants to build a company that lasts well into the future? Kalanick was apparently raw and emotional at the all-hands meeting, where he promised “better” and some thought him genuinely contrite. Let’s hope so. There are always moments in a startup’s journey of great consequence and this is one of those times for Uber, to finally grow up and stop being the jackass we all think it is. In other words, will Uber take those brilliant jerks and tell them not to let the door hit them — hey, nice leather jackets, by the way — on the way out?

Susan Fowler's recent exposé on her experiences at Uber has sparked critical conversations throughout Silicon Valley. As a female engineer at Uber, and one deeply involved in diversity and inclusion initiatives, I've found myself at the center of these discussions. In the past 24 hours alone, over 30 individuals have contacted me for my perspective, advice, or simply to offer support. This post serves as my comprehensive response to those inquiries.

Fowler's account, alongside similar narratives, highlights the uncomfortable realities within the tech industry. While awareness of sexism in technology is widespread, its true severity is often underestimated until it directly impacts our immediate work environment. Companies can feel like insulated entities, but Fowler's experience is a stark reminder that no organization is immune. The fact that her article is currently the top post on Hacker News underscores that people are finally paying attention.

I find the situation appalling and deeply disturbing, yet not entirely surprising. My primary astonishment stems from the apparent surprise of others. For years, many of us have been vocal about these pervasive issues. When incidents like this surface, they are not isolated events but symptoms of systemic sexism, a problem that extends far beyond the dismissal of a few HR representatives. This is not solely about Susan Fowler, SREs, or even Uber; it is a collective challenge. If this serves as a wake-up call for HR, SREs, and Uber, that is a positive development, but it must be a wake-up call for everyone.

To address whether this reflects the daily reality for women in tech: the short answer is no, but the long answer is a resounding yes. My day-to-day experience at Uber has been largely positive. I have supportive managers who allocate 20% of my time to diversity and inclusion initiatives. My colleagues are engaged and actively seek ways to assist when I discuss workplace sexism. Many at Uber have made significant strides in understanding concepts like unconscious bias and privilege. I have never been propositioned for sex at Uber, particularly not by anyone in my management chain.

However, I am part of the 60% of women who have experienced sexual harassment in Silicon Valley. During my first internship at Google at age 20, a manager, approximately 30 years old, attended a party I was hosting. He brought absinthe and later followed me into my bedroom, suggesting we "play Go." I managed to redirect our conversation back to the common room, where he made inappropriate comments and began drinking heavily. As he became increasingly intoxicated, he followed me and made bizarre remarks. At the time, we found it humorous and joked about it for weeks. Eventually, the head of our program learned of the jokes and reported the incident to HR. After a week of recounting the events, the individual was informed he could no longer serve as an intern manager. I no longer find this amusing; I find it disgusting and horrifying, especially considering it occurred at a highly respected company like Google. I share this not to single out Google, but to emphasize that such incidents are widespread.

My continued presence at Uber is driven by a belief that the company needs my contributions. Uber is poised to become a significant industry player, and I am committed to contributing to its success with a team of intelligent, empathetic, and diverse individuals at the helm, rather than abandoning ship. It is infuriating that incidents like Susan's can go unnoticed for so long, and it is unsettling to realize this is likely just the tip of the iceberg. There were moments this morning when I felt disinclined to come to work. I left early to process these events and compose this post. However, it is precisely when I feel most inclined to quit that Uber needs me most. While Uber may not always recognize it, I have made a tangible difference. A senior product manager recently confided that he had not encountered the concept of unconscious bias until he met me. I mentor women on navigating management, pursuing promotions, and articulating their concerns. I lead diversity and inclusion programs, am involved in outreach for LadyEng, and am scheduled to speak at an all-female technology conference. None of these impactful contributions would be possible if I had left.

I am also encouraged by Travis Kalanick's swift response. Uber is now better equipped to address such issues than ever before, and I have high hopes for our new heads of diversity and HR. Today's announcement that Uber will release its diversity statistics, while regrettable that it took such an event to prompt it, is a welcome development. Thank you, Susan, for finally breaking through in a way that many of us have been striving to achieve for so long.

To contribute to this vital change, I urge you to:

*   **Amplify Susan's story.**
*   **Encourage colleagues and friends to share their own experiences.**
*   **Listen attentively and empathetically to those stories.**
*   **Recognize your own unconscious biases and actively seek relevant training.**
*   **Be an active ally and speak up decisively when you witness inappropriate behavior.**

Remember, while sexism is a prominent issue in Silicon Valley, it is not the only one. Crucially, do not assume this is solely an Uber problem. This situation is dire, and Uber has significant work ahead. However, this problem existed last week, and our previous efforts to highlight it went largely unheard. As you read this, perhaps feeling fortunate that your company is different, consider that the shock value of Susan's post stemmed from the fact that many Uber employees did not perceive it as a widespread problem. We acknowledge the existence of sexism, racism, ageism, homophobia, and xenophobia, but it is difficult to internalize these issues until they directly affect us. While this is a moment of pain and outrage, take time to examine the things we take for granted and consider what might surprise us tomorrow.

Uber is currently grappling with severe allegations of sexual harassment and a toxic work environment that have come to light. In response, the company and its CEO, Travis Kalanick, are undertaking damage control and internal investigations. This includes an emotional all-hands meeting for staff and the formation of a special review group. This group is notably led by former U.S. Attorney General Eric Holder and Uber board member Arianna Huffington.

Further evidence of Uber's internal issues was revealed by The New York Times. The report detailed harassment incidents at a Las Vegas party and a disturbing threat from a manager who allegedly stated they would "beat an underperforming employee’s head in with a baseball bat."

In an email to Uber employees, obtained by The Verge, Arianna Huffington outlined her initial investigation strategy. She stated that leadership is "absolutely committed to making any and all changes that need to happen." To support this, Huffington plans multiple visits to Uber offices to speak directly with employees and gather firsthand accounts. The full text of Huffington's memo is provided below.

As most of you know, I left Uber in December and joined Stripe in January. I've gotten a lot of questions over the past couple of months about why I left and what my time at Uber was like. It's a strange, fascinating, and slightly horrifying story that deserves to be told while it is still fresh in my mind, so here we go. I joined Uber as a site reliability engineer (SRE) back in November 2015, and it was a great time to join as an engineer. They were still wrangling microservices out of their monolithic API, and things were just chaotic enough that there was exciting reliability work to be done. The SRE team was still pretty new when I joined, and I had the rare opportunity to choose whichever team was working on something that I wanted to be part of. After the first couple of weeks of training, I chose to join the team that worked on my area of expertise, and this is where things started getting weird. On my first official day rotating on the team, my new manager sent me a string of messages over company chat. He was in an open relationship, he said, and his girlfriend was having an easy time finding new partners but he wasn't. He was trying to stay out of trouble at work, he said, but he couldn't help getting in trouble, because he was looking for women to have sex with. It was clear that he was trying to get me to have sex with him, and it was so clearly out of line that I immediately took screenshots of these chat messages and reported him to HR. Uber was a pretty good-sized company at that time, and I had pretty standard expectations of how they would handle situations like this. I expected that I would report him to HR, they would handle the situation appropriately, and then life would go on - unfortunately, things played out quite a bit differently. When I reported the situation, I was told by both HR and upper management that even though this was clearly sexual harassment and he was propositioning me, it was this man's first offense, and that they wouldn't feel comfortable giving him anything other than a warning and a stern talking-to. Upper management told me that he "was a high performer" (i.e. had stellar performance reviews from his superiors) and they wouldn't feel comfortable punishing him for what was probably just an innocent mistake on his part.
