Voters cast their ballots among an array of electronic voting machines in a polling station at the Noor Islamic Cultural Center, Tuesday, Aug. 7, 2018, in Dublin, Ohio. Two-term state Sen. Troy Balderson,... (Associated Press) Voters cast their ballots among an array of electronic voting machines in a polling station at the Noor Islamic Cultural Center, Tuesday, Aug. 7, 2018, in Dublin, Ohio. Two-term state Sen. Troy Balderson, is fighting off a strong challenge from Democrat Danny O'Connor, a 31-year-old county official,... (Associated Press) WESTERVILLE, Ohio (AP) — Two high-stakes elections that tested President Donald Trump's clout and cost both parties millions of dollars were too close to call early Wednesday. Trump claimed victory in one nevertheless. In battleground Ohio, the president took credit for Republican Troy Balderson's performance, calling it "a great victory," even though the contest could be headed to a recount. Democrats could also celebrate their showing in a district that has gone Republican for decades. "We're not stopping now," Democrat Danny O'Connor told cheering supporters. He'll reprise his campaign against Balderson from now through November's general election. In deep-red Kansas ' Republican gubernatorial primary, the candidate Trump backed on the eve of the election, Secretary of State Kris Kobach, was neck and neck with current Republican Gov. Jeff Colyer. The day's races in five states, like many before them, tested the persistence of Trump's fiery supporters and the momentum of the Democratic Party's anti-Trump resistance. The results were helping determine the political landscape — and Trump's standing within his own party — as the GOP defends its House and Senate majorities this fall. In Kansas, Republicans were fighting among themselves in an unusual battle for governor in which the president sided with the incumbent's challenger. Should the polarizing Kobach win the primary, some Republican operatives fear he could lose the governorship to Democrats this fall. The race could become further disrupted if Kansas City-area businessman Greg Orman makes it onto the November ballot. He submitted petitions Monday with more than 10,000 signatures for what could become the most serious independent run for Kansas governor in decades. Trump made his preference clear for Kobach. "He is a fantastic guy who loves his State and our Country - he will be a GREAT Governor and has my full & total Endorsement! Strong on Crime, Border & Military," the president tweeted on the eve of the election. Republicans were hoping for Democratic discord in Kansas' 3rd Congressional District, a suburban Kansas City district where several candidates were fighting for the chance to take on Republican Rep. Kevin Yoder in November. The five-way Democratic primary featured labor lawyer Brent Welder, who campaigned recently with self-described democratic socialists Vermont Sen. Bernie Sanders and ascending political star, Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, a New York congressional candidate. Also in the race: Native American attorney Sharice Davids and former school teacher Tom Niermann. In Ohio, the script for the special election was somewhat familiar: An experienced Trump loyalist, Balderson, was fighting a strong challenge from O'Connor, a fresh-faced Democrat, in a congressional district held by the Republican Party for more than three decades. As voters were going to the polls, Trump said Balderson would make a "great congressman." The winner takes the seat previously held by Pat Tiberi, a nine-term incumbent who resigned to take a job with an Ohio business group. Balderson and O'Connor will reprise their race in the general election in just three months. There were at least 3,367 provisional ballots left to be reviewed. That's enough for O'Connor to potentially pick up enough to force a recount. The Associated Press does not declare winners in races subject to an automatic recount. In a special election season that featured nearly a dozen congressional contests, Democrats claimed just a handful of wins, but they may have cause for optimism this fall. In virtually every special election test dating back to the spring of 2017, Democratic candidates performed significantly better than their party in those same places two years earlier. Trump won Ohio's 12th Congressional District, for example, by more than 11 points in 2016; on Tuesday night, Balderson and O'Connor were separated by less than 1 point. There are 79 House races this fall considered more competitive than the Ohio district — at least looking at Trump's 2016 performance — according to data compiled by the Democrats' national campaign committee. Despite the deadlocked race, the specific Ohio returns suggest considerably higher Democratic enthusiasm less than 100 days before the midterms. O'Connor's total of nearly 100,000 votes far exceeded what the district's former Republican congressman Pat Tiberi's Democratic opponent got in 2014. Balderson's total — just more than 101,500 votes — is barely two-thirds of Tiberi's 2014 mark of about 150,000. The two will face off again in November to see who holds the seat in 2019 and 2020. "Over the next three months, I'm going to do everything I can to keep America great again, so that when we come back here in November — get ready, we gotta come back here in November — I have earned your vote for a second time," Balderson told supporters. It's unclear how much Trump's support helped or hurt Balderson. Described by campaign operatives as a "Whole Foods" district, the largely suburban region features a more affluent and educated voter base than the typical Trump stronghold. Ohio Gov. John Kasich, a leading voice in the GOP's shrinking anti-Trump wing, once represented the district in Congress. At times, the race centered on Trump's tax cuts as much as the candidates. O'Connor and his Democratic allies railed against the tax plan, casting it as a giveaway for the rich that exacerbates federal deficits and threatens Medicare and Social Security. Balderson and his Republican allies have backed away from the tax plan in recent weeks, training their fire instead on top House Democrat Nancy Pelosi. O'Connor dominated Balderson on the local airwaves. His campaign spent $2.25 million on advertising compared to Balderson's $507,000, according to campaign tallies of ad spending. The Republican campaign arm and its allied super PAC were forced to pick up the slack, spending more than $4 million between them. In Michigan , three mainstream Democrats in suburban Detroit were among those vying for a chance at retiring Republican Rep. Dave Trott's seat in November. The field included Fayrouz Saad, who would be the first Muslim woman in Congress. And in suburban Seattle, three Democrats vied in a jungle primary for the seat held by another retiring Republican, Rep. Dave Reichert. The field was set in two Senate contests as well. In Missouri, Democratic Sen. Claire McCaskill claimed her party's nomination, while state Attorney General Josh Hawley will represent the GOP. And in Michigan, Democratic Sen. Debbie Stabenow will take on military veteran and business executive John James, who won the Republican nomination. He would join Tim Scott of South Carolina as the only black Republican senators if he wins in November. Hours before polls opened, Trump again weighed in on Twitter, casting James as "a potential Republican star." ___ Peoples reported from Washington. Associated Press writers John Hanna in Topeka, Kansas, and Angie Wang in Westerville, Ohio, contributed. ___ Sign up for "Politics in Focus," a weekly newsletter showcasing the AP's best political reporting from around the country leading up to the midterm elections: https://bit.ly/2ICEr3D

query: What was Sen. Ted Cruz's response when asked if he would support Sen. Mitch McConnell as Senate leader?

documents.text: The Senate flipped, John Boehner expanded his majority in the House and now Republicans want to show they can govern. Before the election, top-level Republican aides and lawmakers described the strategy they hoped would let them notch some early victories before the presidential election season is in full swing in the second half of 2015 — a play by Boehner and other leaders to quickly shift away from crisis governing and toward a positive agenda. Text Size - reset At the top of the list is to get a funding bill passed to keep the government open through most of next year, freeing up the calendar to take on other issues like a GOP budget and a long-term highway bill. (Senate results by state) Legislating won’t begin until after Thanksgiving. Both parties intend to spend the weeks immediately after election organizing for the new partisan makeup of Washington. Capitol Hill will burst back into action by Dec. 1, and that’s when serious lawmaking could break out. Republicans are desperate to avoid fiscal fights in the first half of 2015, which is why they will try to use the first few weeks in December to pass a long-term spending bill to keep the government open through most of the next year. Funding runs dry Dec. 11, as does the authority for President Barack Obama’s administration to train and equip moderate Syrian rebels in their fight against the Islamic State. That provision could complicate the calculus for lawmakers’ hopes for a drama-free spending bill. Congress will also try to clear a host of lapsed tailored business tax provisions and renew a government-backed program that helps insurance companies protect against terrorist attacks. And depending on the result of the Senate, Democrats may push to confirm a new attorney general in the waning months of 2014 with Eric Holder’s impending retirement. (POLITICO's 2014 race ratings) The hope is that this spate of legislating will clear the calendar for the first half of 2015, so Congress can pass a long-term highway and infrastructure bill, lift the debt limit and pass a budget. With two chambers of Congress under GOP control, the budget process could give the party its best chance to alter Obamacare, particularly if Senate Republicans try to use budget reconciliation, which requires just 51 votes. Congress will also try to fix the Sustainable Growth Rate, a pricey formula by which the government reimburses doctors who treat Medicare patients. One wild card is GOP Sens. Ted Cruz of Texas and Mike Lee of Utah, who have together vowed to stymie any legislation considered non-essential during the lame duck period. Senior aides caution that anything – or everything – can change. But it’s certain that this lame-duck session will not include action on big items like immigration reform, tax reform or a large-scale fiscal deal. (PLAYBACK: Best of 2014) Just as important as the year-end legislating is the internal meetings that both parties will hold. A Republican majority in the Senate would need to devise the structure of their committees and the ratio of Democrats to Republicans. Perhaps the most drastic change will be a new slate of GOP committee chairmen that will attack the administration’s agenda. No fan of the Obama’s energy regulatory agenda, Sen. Jim Inhofe of Oklahoma is expected to ascend to be chairman of the Environment and Public Works Committee. Sen. John McCain of Arizona is likely to end up the chairman of the Senate Armed Services Committee — a prime perch for his continued broadsides on Obama’s foreign policy. And Sen. Jeff Sessions of Alabama, one of the most conservative senators, is in line to chair the Senate Budget Committee. An expanded majority in the House means there will be a lot of housekeeping on Capitol Hill. New members will arrive in Washington Nov. 12 for their orientation and leadership elections for both the House and the Senate, including new Democratic and Republican chairmen for the Senate’s campaign arms. The House, meanwhile, will also need to elect new chairmen for key committees. (Driving the Day: Top House races to watch) The Ways and Means Committee, which is gearing up to rewrite the tax code, will get a new leader in Wisconsin Rep. Paul Ryan. Texas Rep. Mac Thornberry, a 20-year veteran of Congress, will now lead the Armed Services Committee. The Oversight and Government Reform Committee, Congress’s top investigatory panel, will get new leadership, as Rep. Darrell Issa (R-Calif.) will reach his term limit. Reps. Jason Chaffetz of Utah and Mike Turner of Ohio are seen as front-runners for that slot. There’s one race that won’t be competitive: the chairmanship of the National Republican Congressional Committee. Some House Republicans spent the fall grumbling about Walden’s leadership. But Tuesday night will quiet those concerns.

Following the Republican Senate takeover, Senator Ted Cruz of Texas declared on Tuesday night that the "era of Obama lawlessness is over." Speaking in Austin, Cruz highlighted the crucial role Republicans must play if they secure a national majority. He stated, "If and when we retake the majority nationally, it will be incumbent on Republicans to lead." Cruz articulated a vision for the party to "stand up with a bold positive, optimistic agenda to turn this country around, to bring back jobs and growth and opportunity." He further noted, "The American people have risen up and frankly, at the national level, they have given Republicans another chance."

Considered a likely 2016 presidential contender, Cruz detailed a robust conservative platform for the incoming Congress. His proposed agenda included several key objectives:

*   **Tax and Regulatory Reform:** Advocating for significant changes to the nation's tax code and regulatory environment.
*   **Immigration Policy:** Calling for an end to what he described as "amnesty" for undocumented immigrants.
*   **Protection of Liberties:** Emphasizing the importance of safeguarding religious liberty and gun rights.
*   **Repeal of the Affordable Care Act:** Making the forceful repeal of the Affordable Care Act, or ObamaCare, a paramount priority. Cruz strongly urged Republicans to "do everything humanly possible to repeal ObamaCare."

The Republican party has achieved a majority in the Senate, marking a significant shift in power. This outcome exceeded initial expectations, with the GOP securing key races and retaining crucial seats.

Notable results included Pat Roberts defending his Senate seat in Kansas against independent challenger Greg Orman. In North Carolina, Thom Tillis, the speaker of the statehouse, defeated incumbent Kay Hagan. While not widely predicted, Ed Gillespie narrowly lost to incumbent Mark Warner in Virginia, with a libertarian candidate's presence potentially impacting the final tally.

The Democratic party faced a challenging election landscape, partly due to a map that largely favored Republicans, with many former Confederate states electing Senators on November 4th. Historically, the party holding the presidency often sees weaker results in mid-term elections. Democrats also experienced losses in traditionally supportive areas, such as Maryland electing a Republican governor for the first time since 1969. In Florida, Republican Rick Scott won a closely contested gubernatorial race.

With Republicans now controlling Congress for the first time in ten years, attention turns to their legislative priorities. Mitch McConnell, set to become Senate Majority Leader, has voiced a desire for reduced partisan conflict. However, some Republican senators, like Rand Paul, have indicated plans to challenge President Obama's policies, including potentially repealing the Affordable Care Act.

Despite the new majority, McConnell's ability to advance legislation may be limited. The projected Republican seat count (53 or 54) falls short of the supermajority needed to overcome filibusters, meaning Democrats can still use procedural tactics to block bills. Additionally, some newly elected Republican senators may not always follow McConnell's lead. For example, Tom Cotton, who defeated Mark Pryor in Arkansas, previously supported a government shutdown, an action McConnell aimed to avoid. Incoming senators Joni Ernst and Thom Tillis are also expected to assert their independence.

The operational challenges for the new Senate will become apparent quickly. A key test will be the expiration of a continuing resolution funding the federal government on December 11th. McConnell will likely work to prevent a government shutdown.

Looking forward, potential areas for cooperation between Congress and the president could include trade agreements, corporate tax reform, and policies related to visas for skilled workers. However, substantial progress on broader national issues is unlikely given the divided government.

**Harry Reid Congratulates Mitch McConnell on Senate Leadership**

Following a decisive Republican victory in the Senate, Senator Harry Reid, a Democrat from Nevada, congratulated Senator Mitch McConnell, a Republican from Kentucky, on his upcoming role as the new Senate Majority Leader. This announcement came late Tuesday after Republicans secured a majority of key Senate races, marking a significant shift in the chamber's political landscape.

Senator Reid acknowledged the clear message from voters in an emailed statement. He stated, "The message from voters is clear: they want us to work together." He expressed his intention to collaborate with Senator McConnell, adding, "I look forward to working with Senator McConnell to get things done for the middle class."

The election results prompted a period of introspection for Democrats. Some within the party privately attributed the outcomes to the President and the White House. Senator Reid's strategic approach, described as a "prevent defense," did not yield the desired results.

Senator Reid also indicated his commitment to remaining in his leadership position. Despite internal dissatisfaction, there is currently no open challenge to his leadership.

The focus now shifts to the lame-duck session, the period before Senator McConnell officially assumes control of the Senate. Key issues to be addressed include:

*   Nominations
*   Passage of an omnibus spending bill

These matters represent the immediate challenges facing the Senate as it transitions to new leadership.

The recent Republican victory on Tuesday has significantly reshaped the political landscape, signaling a period of heightened gridlock and stagnation for President Obama's final two years in office. This outcome prompts a crucial question: how will the GOP effectively wield its newfound power? The Republican party successfully leveraged widespread American frustration with Washington's political climate to secure complete control of Congress for the first time since 2006. This triumph was further amplified by an expanded majority in the House of Representatives and victories in several pivotal gubernatorial races. Senator Mitch McConnell of Kentucky, a seasoned politician, is now poised to assume the role of Senate Majority Leader, having successfully defended his seat against a formidable challenge.

Despite Senator McConnell's public commitment to working with Democrats, the Republican party's track record since gaining control of the House in 2010 suggests that significant legislative achievements are unlikely. This means that pressing national issues such as immigration reform, addressing climate change, narrowing the widening wage gap, and undertaking crucial infrastructure upgrades are likely to remain unresolved. Consequently, President Obama may find himself increasingly reliant on his veto power to counter legislation passed by the Republican-controlled Congress. The fate of the Affordable Care Act, commonly known as Obamacare, remains uncertain, as it was not a central campaign issue for many Republican candidates.

The Republican takeover of the Senate could potentially embolden the more conservative factions within the party, such as the Tea Party movement, pushing the GOP further to the right. This shift was subtly indicated by Senator Ted Cruz of Texas's reluctance to publicly endorse McConnell for Majority Leader. Furthermore, House Speaker John Boehner's statement following the election did not even mention immigration as a priority for the incoming Republican Congress. Boehner acknowledged the responsibility placed upon them by the American people, emphasizing that the focus should be on delivering results and implementing solutions for the nation's challenges, particularly concerning the economy.

Beyond legislative hurdles, the Republican control of the Senate jeopardizes a key power President Obama had largely retained even after the GOP gained control of the House in 2010: the confirmation of judicial nominees. This raises the prospect of protracted political battles over any potential Supreme Court vacancies. The implications for the 2016 presidential race are multifaceted. On one hand, the Republicans' enhanced ability to obstruct Obama's agenda could further fuel voter discontent, potentially increasing their eagerness for a change in the White House. Conversely, an overreach by the GOP in an effort to appease their conservative base could lead to them being perceived as part of the problem.

The election night results mirrored the significant Republican gains seen in 2010, which President Obama himself described as a "thumping." Exit polls indicated that the president's declining approval ratings and widespread dissatisfaction with the economic recovery, despite positive statistical indicators, were key drivers of these outcomes. Many Americans reported not feeling the benefits of the economic upturn. On a more positive note, the Republican party did elect two women to the Senate, as well as one black senator and one black female representative. Democratic Senate incumbents faced defeat in Arkansas, Colorado, and North Carolina, with Republicans also securing seats in Montana, West Virginia, Iowa, and South Dakota. Many of these Senate races were anticipated to be closely contested, and there was even a possibility that control of the Senate would not be determined for weeks or months, with potential runoffs in Louisiana and Georgia, and an independent candidate in Kansas whose party affiliation remained undecided.

The Republican party's success extended to states like Kansas, Arkansas, Georgia, Kentucky, Iowa, and Colorado, which appeared to shift significantly towards the GOP in the final days of the campaign. Republican candidates strategically focused their campaigns on President Obama, a tactic that proved effective. The NBC News national exit poll revealed that the president's approval rating stood at a mere 45%, a nine-point drop from his re-election two years prior. Compounding this, a substantial 70% of voters expressed dissatisfaction with the state of the economy, with only 29% believing it was performing well.

In the gubernatorial races, Wisconsin Governor Scott Walker successfully fended off a challenge from Democrat Mary Burke, while Ohio's John Kasich secured an easy re-election. These victories position both Walker and Kasich as potential contenders for the 2016 presidential nomination. In Florida, Republican Governor Rick Scott narrowly defeated Democrat Charlie Crist, a former governor who had previously served as a moderate Republican. Texas, a traditionally red state, saw Republican Attorney General Greg Abbott win decisively against Democratic State Senator Wendy Davis, who had once been a symbol of her party's hopes for competitiveness in the state. Illinois also elected Republican Bruce Rauner, unseating incumbent Democrat Pat Quinn. In a rare victory for Democrats, businessman Tom Wolf ousted the deeply unpopular Republican Governor of Pennsylvania, Tom Corbett.

The Fix’s Chris Cillizza breaks down how close the battle for the Senate really is. (The Washington Post) The Fix’s Chris Cillizza breaks down how close the battle for the Senate really is. (The Washington Post) Sen. Ted Cruz spent the final weekend of the midterms on the far edge of the country trying to help fellow Republican Dan Sullivan win a race the GOP is counting on in its effort to retake the Senate. It’s a team-player role the tea party firebrand from Texas has filled a handful of times this fall — but one he plans to abandon if Republicans win control of both congressional chambers. In an interview at the Hotel Captain Cook here between campaign stops for Sullivan, Cruz made it clear he would push hard for a Republican-led Senate to be as conservative and confron­tational as the Republican-led House. Piggybacking on what House leaders have done, Cruz said the first order of business should be a series of hearings on President Obama, “looking at the abuse of power, the executive abuse, the regulatory abuse, the lawlessness that sadly has pervaded this administration.” (Live coverage: On the campaign trail) 1 of 13 Full Screen Autoplay Close Skip Ad × New faces to Washington? Or bigger roles? View Photos Hopes among these politicians are high that they’ll be headed to the nation’s capital – or upgrading from House digs to the Senate side of the Capitol. Caption An eclectic group is coming to Congress — or moving across the Capitol from the House to the Senate. Here are a few. Mia Love — Utah Love, a former small-town Utah mayor and fitness instructor from Brooklyn, became the nation’s first black, female, Republican member of Congress. She lost a House race in 2012 to incumbent Democrat Jim Matheson, who is retiring. Rick Bowmer/AP Buy Photo Wait 1 second to continue. Cruz also would like the Senate to be as aggressive in trying to repeal the Affordable Care Act as the House, which has voted more than 50 times to get rid of the law. Republicans should “pursue every means possible to repeal Obamacare,” Cruz said, including forcing a vote through parliamentary procedures that would get around a possible filibuster by Democrats. If that leads to a veto by Obama, Cruz said, Republicans should then vote on provisions of the health law “one at a time.” And when asked whether he would back Sen. Mitch McConnell of Kentucky for Republican leader, Cruz would not pledge his support — an indication that there are limits to how much of a partner he’s willing to be. At the heart of Cruz’s shift from the insular approach that defined his first year in office is a belief that he can use his popularity with conservatives to expand his influence in the Senate and improve his standing as he considers a 2016 presidential campaign. Cruz’s desire to turn his party further right in the coming months is one of the challenges already facing McConnell should Republicans regain the Senate, with tea party leaders inside and outside the Capitol spoiling for a number of hard-line moves. “Senator Cruz has been rather quiet over the past few months,” said Ron Bonjean, a spokesman for Trent Lott when the Mississippian was the Senate Republican leader. “That time seems to be coming to an end. I understand why he’s eager to go after Obamacare. But the reality is that it’ll take 60 votes to repeal it and Republicans will have nowhere near that amount. If Obamacare remains the focus, he will certainly get the base jazzed up about what he’s doing, but he won’t get rid of the law.” Cruz has gained some traction in terms of shaping the contours of what a Republican Senate would do, in part because McConnell and House Speaker John A. Boehner (R-Ohio) have not offered their own definitive vision of what a Republican-led Congress would look like. View Graphic Election Lab: See our current forecast for every congressional race in 2014 Two weeks ago, Cruz wrote an opinion piece in USA Today laying out 10 conservative priorities he thinks Republicans should pursue, including moving toward a flat tax and drawing a hard line on illegal immigrants. In the interview here, Cruz reiterated some of those points, such as approving the Keystone XL pipeline. McConnell has mostly been coy about what he would like to accomplish other than adding amendments to curb federal regulations to spending bills as a means of putting pressure on the president. “It’s never a good idea to tell the other side what the first play is going to be,” McConnell said at an event last month. Cruz is not interested in adding amendments that may put indirect pressure on Obama. He favors direct political combat. That way, either the president gives in, or, Cruz said, “you have clear accountability. It becomes transparent to everyone that it is the Democrats blocking meaningful progress.” Rep. Peter King (R-N.Y.), a Long Island moderate, said Republicans should be wary of Cruz’s guidance. “He is the last one we should listen to,” King said in an interview Sunday. “Don’t forget — a year ago he brought Republicans over the cliff.” Still, Cruz’s help on the trail has improved his image among some Republicans, while easing a measure of the anger aimed at him from some of the donors and colleagues he infuriated in October 2013 when he led a shutdown of the government. Cruz should be able to count on a handful of new friends, if not allies, when the Senate convenes next year. In recent weeks, he has campaigned for Senate contenders who beat Cruz-admiring insurgents in Republican primaries, from businessman David Perdue in Georgia and state Sen. Joni Ernst in Iowa to Sullivan and Sen. Pat Roberts, Kansas’s embattled incumbent. If she wins, Ernst is poised to be a powerful player in the run-up to the Iowa caucuses, the first nominating contest in the 2016 race for the GOP presidential nomination. Perdue, who has weak ties to his red state’s GOP base, could hew close to Cruz on some votes to keep conservatives in Georgia at bay. Sullivan, for similar reasons, could do the same. Cruz is not the only conservative Republican with national ambitions who is working to show skeptical Republicans that they can do more than squabble. Sen. Rand Paul (R-Ky.) has cut ads for the U.S. Chamber of Commerce, one of the groups that opposed tea party candidates in Senate primaries, and stumped for moderate former Massachusetts senator Scott Brown, who is running for Senate in New Hampshire. Cruz declined to say whether he’s going to run for president, but was dismissive of moderates in his party, particularly those who may challenge him for the 2016 Republican nomination. Of Jeb Bush, for instance, Cruz said he likes and respects him, “but I think we have seen election after election that when Republicans fail to draw a clear distinction with the Democrats, when we run to the mushy middle, we lose.” “At some point,” Cruz continued, “after Gerald Ford and Bob Dole and John McCain and Mitt Romney . . . we shouldn’t keep making the same mistakes over and over again. “One of the reasons Republicans have lost elections recently is that we have failed to engage in a meaningful way on the great issues of the day,” Cruz said. “We’ve played a prevent defense. You don’t win elections that way.”

Rand Paul (R-KY) (C) speaks while U.S. Sen. Ron Wyden (D-OR) (L), U.S. Sen. Mark Udall (D-CO) (2ndL) and U.S. Sen. Richard Blumenthal (D-CT) (R), listen during a news conference on Capitol Hill September 25, 2013 in Washington, DC. The bipartisan group of Senators announced new legislation for comprehensive surveillance reform. (Photo by Mark Wilson/Getty Images) WASHINGTON, DC - SEPTEMBER 10: U.S. Sen. Ted Cruz (R-TX) (L) and Sen. Rand Paul (R-KY) wait to speak at the 'Exempt America from Obamacare' rally, on Capitol Hill, September 10, 2013 in Washington, DC. Some conservative lawmakers are making a push to try to defund the health care law as part of the debates over the budget and funding the federal government. (Photo by Drew Angerer/Getty Images) WASHINGTON, DC - SEPTEMBER 04: U.S. Sen. Rand Paul (R-KY) (L) speaks while U.S. Sen. John Barrasso (R-WY) (C) and John McCain (R-AZ) (R) listen before the Senate Foreign Relations Committee vote on a resolution on Syria on Capitol Hill September 4, 2013 in Washington, DC. The Senate Foreign Relations Committee voted to authorize U.S. President Barack Obama to use limited force against Syria after adopting amendments from McCain (R-NV). (Photo by Mark Wilson/Getty Images) WASHINGTON, DC - SEPTEMBER 04: U.S. Sen. Rand Paul (R-KY) talks to reporters after leaving a closed door meeting about Syria at the U.S. Capitol on September 4, 2013 in Washington, DC. Later today Senate Foreign Relations Committee is expected to to vote on a resolution that would give U.S, President Barack Obama a limited window for a military operation in response to what the U.S. says was the Syrian government's use of chemical weapons against civilians. (Photo by Mark Wilson/Getty Images) WASHINGTON, DC - SEPTEMBER 03: Senate Foreign Relations Committee member Sen. Rand Paul (R-KY) talks to reporters before heading into a members-only classified briefing about Syria at the U.S. Capitol September 3, 2013 in Washington, DC. The Foreign Relations Committee will hear testimony today from Secretary of State John Kerry, Secretary of Defense Chuck Hagel and Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff Gen. Martin Dempsey. (Photo by Chip Somodevilla/Getty Images) WASHINGTON, DC - JULY 16: U.S. Sen. Rand Paul (R-KY) speaks while U.S. Sen. Kirsten Gillibrand (D-NY) listens during a news conference on sexual assault in the military, July 16, 2013 in Washington, DC. U.S. Sen. Gillibrand announced the support of 34 senators that will co-sponsor her proposal to take the decision whether to prosecute sexual assaults out of the hands of the military chain of command. (Photo by Mark Wilson/Getty Images) Sen. Rand Paul, R-Ky., takes questions Monday, July 1, 2013, from about 40 Owensboro Tea Party members at the Logsdon Community Center in Owensboro, Ky. During his remarks Paul said he supports granting more work visas if the border is secured first. (AP Photo/The Messenger-Inquirer, Gary Emord-Netzley)

query: What significant developments are expected to emerge from the Paris climate talks next month?

documents.text:
The upcoming Paris climate talks are poised to conclude next month with a landmark international accord aimed at reducing greenhouse gas emissions from both developed and developing nations. While this agreement may be hailed as a triumph for leaders like President Barack Obama, a significant part of its foundation was laid by a less expected figure: George W. Bush.

Despite his administration's past skepticism towards global warming science and its opposition to Environmental Protection Agency climate policies, Bush played a crucial role in initiating the very negotiations now culminating in Paris. This might seem surprising given the current Republican stance, which largely seeks to undermine the Paris talks and Obama's environmental agenda. However, Bush's presidency left a distinct mark, particularly at a 2007 climate conference in Bali, Indonesia.

Although President Bush himself was not present in Bali, his administration's representatives were a focal point during the two-week negotiations. Al Gore, fresh from receiving his Nobel Peace Prize, delivered a speech urging negotiators to reserve space in their future agreements for the next U.S. president. The U.S. delegation faced considerable criticism, with Bush officials being loudly booed during the closing hours.

Despite the contentious atmosphere, the Bali Action Plan, as it was known, emerged as a pivotal breakthrough in international climate talks. For the first time, countries of all economic levels committed to addressing global warming. While the plan offered only a broad framework, it was instrumental in removing a major political obstacle: the reluctance of rapidly developing nations like China, Brazil, India, and South Africa to commit to their own emission reduction plans. This commitment, first formalized in Bali, has been crucial for subsequent progress.

The path forward has not been without its challenges. High expectations surrounding President Obama, amplified by his Nobel Prize, led to disappointment at the 2009 Copenhagen summit, where a major agreement failed to materialize. It took two more years, until the 2011 Durban conference, to re-establish the momentum from the Bali framework.

The Paris talks will still grapple with complex issues, such as providing tens of billions of dollars in technical assistance to developing countries to facilitate their transition to cleaner energy sources and adapt to climate change. These foreign aid packages are also intended to encourage stronger, measurable, and verifiable emission reduction commitments. Nevertheless, the core principle established in Bali—collective global engagement in climate action—provides a strong indication of the Paris conference's potential success.

Yvo de Boer, former top U.N. climate official, described Bali as a "turning point," noting that its significance is often overlooked. Deciphering the outcomes of U.N. climate conferences can be challenging due to technical jargon and political rhetoric. These summits, held annually since 1995, often aim for tangible achievements. While outcomes can sometimes sound repetitive, each conference builds upon previous ones, supported by extensive preparatory work in side meetings and formal gatherings at U.N. climate headquarters in Bonn.

The foundational treaty for climate action was negotiated at the 1992 Rio Earth Summit, signed by George H.W. Bush. In contrast to today's political climate, the U.S. Senate ratified this treaty unanimously in October 1992. However, Bush was voted out of office the following month.

President Bill Clinton, seeking to shift climate policy from voluntary to mandatory, signed the "Berlin Mandate" in 1995. This declaration stipulated that only developed nations, including the U.S., U.K., Japan, and Canada, were legally obligated to reduce emissions, while developing nations like China and India were exempt. In 1997, under the leadership of Al Gore, the Kyoto Protocol was negotiated, codifying the Berlin language into a treaty. The U.S. secured concessions, such as broad coverage of six potent greenhouse gases and authorization for a cap-and-trade system.

However, Kyoto faced significant opposition in the U.S. The Senate had already passed a resolution, 95-0, from Senators Robert Byrd (D-W.Va.) and Chuck Hagel (R-Neb.) opposing any treaty that would harm the U.S. economy or exempt developing countries. Former Clinton negotiators acknowledged missteps in pushing developing nations for greater commitments, as countries like China and India attributed climate change to historical Western industrialization. Stuart Eizenstat, who led the U.S. delegation, described the atmosphere as intensely hostile, stating, "We completely struck out with developing countries." Daniel Bodansky, a former Clinton-era State Department climate coordinator, called the Kyoto Protocol "a false start."

Despite signing Kyoto, Clinton never submitted it to the Senate for ratification. George W. Bush subsequently withdrew the U.S. from all talks related to the 1997 agreement. International criticism intensified amid reports of the Bush White House, with its ties to the oil industry, censoring climate science and Bush backtracking on a 2000 campaign pledge to cap U.S. power plant emissions.

Bush's substantive engagement on climate issues didn't resume until 2007. By then, Democrats controlled both houses of Congress, and John McCain, a proponent of cap-and-trade, was a leading Republican presidential contender. International pressure mounted for the U.S. to adopt a new approach. Harlan Watson, a lead Bush administration climate negotiator, noted the question posed to the administration was, "What’s your alternative?"

In Bali, marathon negotiations resulted in an agreement where developed nations committed to establishing mechanisms to aid developing countries in adapting to climate change and building clean energy infrastructure. This agreement "really launched the seriousness of this idea of breaking down the firewall" between developed and developing nations, according to Watson.

This shift surprised many within the U.S. George David Banks, a former senior Bush White House environmental aide, recalled skepticism from environmentalists and Democrats who felt the administration wasn't ambitious enough and had undermined the U.N. framework. However, Banks argued that given the global increase in emissions from major economies, a different approach was necessary.

Subsequent negotiations have been complex. In Copenhagen, leaders aimed for a legally binding treaty but secured a more flexible accord where nations pledged to continue developing environmental plans, deferring the legal framework until later—a promise now being fulfilled in Paris. Banks credited the Obama administration for building on the 2007 goals, acknowledging that their success would not have been possible without Bush's groundwork. "We were able to break ground for them that they were able to exploit later," he stated.

Environmental advocates, like Kalee Kreider, a veteran of environmental groups and former Gore aide, also acknowledge the Bush administration's role in initiating progress. She described Bali as a "tipping point moment," distinguishing it from conferences focused primarily on mechanics.

The irony of Bush's foundational role is lost on many current Republicans, who largely reject climate science and oppose Obama's environmental policies. Earlier this month, they passed resolutions aimed at overturning the president's emission rules for power plants and have vowed to obstruct the Paris talks. Nigel Purvis, a Clinton-era climate diplomat, observed, "There is a lot of Republican criticism of Paris, which is ironic because the Paris architecture is the architecture that conservatives said we needed."

The images of more than 500 people are being projected onto the facade of France’s National Assembly building during the 2015 U.N. Climate Change Conference (COP21) in Paris to “remind leaders that the world is watching,” in the words of the artists behind the project. The Standing March is the collaborative effort of the French artist known as JR and the American filmmaker Darren Aronofsky. The “representation of humanity” is, the artists said, a message to the 25,000 world leaders who have gathered in Paris to negotiate a plan that will limit the rise of the global temperature to 2°C (3.6°F). “We expect decisions, we expect commitments, and we expect time frames,” Aronofsky, the controversial filmmaker behind Requiem for a Dream and Black Swan, said in a statement on the project’s website. “It’s about people, from Tuvalu to New Orleans, whose lives are threatened by climate change. The world depends on a solution.” The conference and the exhibition — which will travel around Paris for seven days after a weekend on the National Assembly building — come less than three weeks after the terrorist attacks that left 130 people dead. “For security reasons, marches are forbidden in Paris. But our art piece is a silent march,” JR said in a statement. “And we are marching, backed by the Assemblée Nationale, the heart of the French democracy. We must think about our future, the future of our environment and this is our answer to those who want to control our present.”

The 2015 Paris climate talks, officially the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change's 21st Conference of the Parties (COP21), are expected to draw approximately 22,000 official attendees. These include negotiators, delegates, diplomats, and aides from 195 countries. This figure doesn't account for the thousands of NGOs, business representatives, activists, students, and journalists who will also be present. Conservatively, total attendance is estimated at 50,000 people.

These attendees will travel from across the globe, with an average round trip distance of about 9,000 miles. They will primarily arrive by airplane. A Boeing 747, flying at full capacity, achieves approximately 16.5 miles per gallon of jet fuel. For 50,000 attendees, this translates to roughly 27 million gallons of jet fuel consumed. Burning this fuel releases about 21 pounds of carbon dioxide per gallon. Consequently, the travel for the Paris climate talks is estimated to release approximately 575 million pounds of CO2. While this number seems large, it represents only about 22 seconds of global CO2 emissions, which total around 80 quadrillion pounds annually. Including two weeks of hotel stays, local transportation, and other activities, the emissions from the event might add another half-second to global totals. These calculations, while approximate, serve as a reminder that these crucial discussions have an environmental cost.

The primary objective of the Paris talks was to limit the average global temperature rise to 2°C above pre-industrial levels. However, current national commitments fall short of this goal. If the talks were successful in reducing global emissions to under 1,000 gigatons per year by 2040, preventing a 2°C rise, the world would still face significant challenges. For context, 2015 marked the first year global average temperatures rose 1°C above the historical average, leading to documented increases in droughts, wildfires, marine life die-offs, and billions in damages from sea-level rise. These impacts would be amplified at a 2°C warming.

Despite the challenges, progress is being made. Aimee Barnes, a delegate for the California EPA, notes that achieving 156 emissions commitments from 130 countries two years prior was once considered impossible. She highlights the 2010 Cancún climate talks as an example of how groundwork laid at previous meetings can lead to significant achievements. Another key goal of the Paris talks is to mobilize financial resources for developing countries, reducing their reliance on fossil fuels. This financial support could encourage stronger commitments from major future economies like India. Furthermore, non-national actors such as cities, states, and provinces, like California with its robust economy and successful cap-and-trade program, can implement effective emissions reductions.

However, relying solely on optimism is insufficient. Past climate negotiations, like those following the Kyoto Protocol, have faltered due to a lack of enforcement mechanisms. Many experts believe a global climate agreement requires shared incentives, such as a price on carbon. Economist Steve Stoft suggests a global agreement where countries price carbon emissions at $20-$30 per ton, allowing them to implement their own programs like cap-and-trade, taxes, or regulations to meet this target while fostering economic development. While this approach has support from economists and negotiators, it is unlikely to be fully implemented at Paris. Therefore, the outcome is expected to be incremental progress, likely enough to offset the emissions generated by the event itself.

Senators are pushing to ensure Congress has a say in any climate agreement reached at the Paris summit. A "sense of the Senate resolution," introduced by Sen. Jim Inhofe (R-Okla.) and colleagues, aims to give the Senate approval power over any "protocol, amendment, extension or other agreement" from the summit. This move seeks to subject President Obama's commitments to a congressional vote, though it faces a legal challenge: proving the Paris product is effectively a treaty.

World leaders are convening in Paris to finalize a global deal on curbing greenhouse gas emissions and mitigating climate change. While a Republican-led Senate would likely reject such a deal, President Obama intends to bypass Senate ratification by not submitting it as a treaty. The administration contends that any agreement won't legally bind the U.S. and that existing legislation, like the Clean Air Act and the United Nations Framework on Climate Change (signed by President George H.W. Bush), already grants Obama the necessary authority.

However, this approach carries risks. A future president could abandon Obama's climate plan, whereas a treaty would bind all subsequent administrations. Despite not formally signing a treaty, the Obama administration is expected to pursue its policies, aiming to reduce carbon emissions by 26 to 28 percent below 2005 levels by 2025.

Nigel Purvis, founding president of Climate Advisers and a former State Department treaty lawyer, stated, "The Senate has already had its say." He explained that if an international agreement doesn't require changes to U.S. law and the president has the authority to implement it under existing law, Senate approval isn't necessary. Purvis noted that presidents have historically entered into "executive agreements" without seeking Senate consent, a practice dating back to George Washington.

Senators challenging Obama's approach, including Sens. Jim Inhofe (R-Okla.), Roy Blunt (R-Mo.), and Joe Manchin (D-WestVa.), have introduced a resolution asserting the president must seek Senate advice and consent on any Paris agreement. Inhofe stated, "Should the negotiations produce a more substantive outcome as European delegates announced, then there is no way around the Senate." He urged caution regarding diplomatic promises, suggesting the president might be making commitments he cannot fulfill.

The involvement of Inhofe, a climate change skeptic and chairman of the Senate's Environment and Public Works Committee, along with Blunt and Manchin, who support the coal industry, is unsurprising. If a Paris agreement reaches the GOP-controlled Senate, rejection is probable, potentially hindering Obama's agenda and international climate momentum. This aligns with Republican criticism of Obama's perceived executive overreach.

The core of the dispute lies in whether the Paris agreement will be "legally binding." David Sandalow, a fellow at Columbia University's Center for Global Energy Policy and former National Security Council official, clarified that the agreement would be legally binding in some areas but not for emission reduction commitments.

Secretary of State John F. Kerry indicated in a *Financial Times* interview that the Paris agreement would "definitely not be a treaty" and would lack the legally binding reduction targets seen in the Kyoto Protocol, which the Senate rejected. This stance drew criticism from European governments, which anticipate a legally binding international instrument. A spokeswoman for the EU's climate commissioner, Miguel Arias Cañete, told *The Guardian*, "The Paris agreement must be an international, legally binding agreement." French Foreign Minister Laurent Fabius suggested Kerry was "confused" on the legal point.

Administration officials argue that, unlike the Kyoto Protocol, the Paris agreement will not impose top-down, binding emission targets. Instead, it will feature "bottom-up country commitments" made voluntarily. The agreement, they explain, will "bind" countries to a process, requiring them to submit transparent climate action plans, report on progress, and consult on missed targets, rather than imposing strict outcomes. Penalties for non-compliance are also unlikely, as there is no international enforcement mechanism.

A similar model was employed in an earlier international accord on mercury emissions. Senior Obama administration officials involved in those negotiations reportedly saw the mercury pact as a precedent for Paris. Ultimately, the legal interpretation of the agreement may be less critical than national intent.

Michael Glennon, a professor of international law at the Fletcher School at Tufts University and former Senate Foreign Relations Committee counsel, stated, "Every state controls whether that state is a party to a binding agreement, and in each case, that state’s intent controls." He emphasized that if the U.S. explicitly states its intent not to enter a binding agreement, it will not be legally obligated. Glennon pointed to the Iran nuclear accord as an example where the administration bypassed Senate treaty ratification, with Congress later establishing a special review process.

In response, a group of 30 Republican senators, led by Mike Lee of Utah, introduced a resolution urging the Obama administration to submit all climate agreements to the Senate, regardless of their perceived legal binding status, especially those with targets or timetables. This resolution also threatens to withhold funding for the Green Climate Fund until such agreements are submitted. Obama proposed contributing $3 billion annually to the fund, but many Republicans oppose this. Over 100 House lawmakers signed a letter opposing the Green Climate Fund request, and Sens. Inhofe and John Barrasso (R-Wyo.) circulated a similar letter in the Senate, vowing to block U.S. taxpayer dollars for the fund until the climate agreement is submitted for Senate review.

Ultimately, compliance with any agreement will depend on national will and international pressure. Paul Bledsoe, an energy consultant and former climate policy aide, questioned the meaning of "legally binding" in an agreement without financial penalties, comparing it to the World Trade Organization, which does have penalties. He concluded, "The bottom line is it’s not going to be a treaty. The United States is not going to agree to anything that requires Senate confirmation. And that’s that."

Photo by Steffen Kugler-Pool/Getty Images On Monday, more than 140 world leaders will gather in Paris to kick off tense two-week treaty negotiations over the fate of a planet in crisis. If this were about any topic other than climate change, it might even make the news. Granted, there’s been a lot of other news out of France recently—a major climate-themed march in Paris will be canceled for security concerns. And there is going to be a lot of coverage of the Paris climate talks. But it will be nothing compared to the attention that would be paid to a last-ditch meeting to avoid a nuclear standoff—even though climate change is no less dangerous. As Climate Home previews, “a treaty at this scale has never been accomplished before, and the one under construction will affect the way the entire global economy operates.” Advertisement Maybe climate change tends to take a back seat because the talks themselves are a jargon-filled monstrosity of diplomatic protocol, which means no one—not even the diplomats themselves!—understands what’s happening half of the time. "The most experienced lawyer on the Earth will not be able to understand this text" says Oleg Shamanov, Russia envoy #ADP2 — Edward King (@edking_CH) October 23, 2015 Here we are, closing out what’s quite possibly the warmest year since the invention of agriculture 10,000 years ago, with our atmosphere’s carbon dioxide at record levels and emissions still rising. But, alas, the most interesting drama and diplomatic wrangling are buried in a sea of legalese and acronyms. Case in point: Peru’s environment minister, Manuel Pulgar Vidal—a key figure in recent years at international climate negotiations—recently tweeted a link to a document designed to provide a more-or-less official guide to the Paris talks. It’s titled: “Scenario note on the twelfth part of the second session of the Ad Hoc Working Group on the Durban Platform for Enhanced Action.” Not exactly helpful or soul-stirring. So, here is my attempt to translate diplomat-speak to commoner language, focusing on why everyone’s in Paris, what the major sticking points are, and what it all means: Advertisement “Ad Hoc Working Group on the Durban Platform,” often abbreviated ADP2, especially for hashtagging purposes, is the official title of the international climate talks. Over the last four years or so, representatives from nearly every nation on Earth have gathered about once every three months, primarily in Bonn, Germany. At these preliminary talks, ADP2 laid the framework for a draft agreement—an unwieldy 54-page document. It’s called the “Durban Platform” because back in 2011 in Durban, South Africa, world leaders agreed that the first global climate treaty would be agreed upon in 2015—which brings us to today, in Paris. This mega-gathering is officially the 21st Conference of the Parties to the Kyoto Protocol, or #COP21 for short. The text of the Durban Platform contains 1,300 square brackets that provide different options for wording. For example, here’s what the section on the global temperature target currently looks like: In Paris, it will be the delegates’ job to eliminate the square brackets in the text. Among the major sticking points: Advertisement How much and how fast should countries reduce their emissions?: The world’s first climate treaty, the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change, was signed by 165 countries in 1992. Enshrined the UNFCCC, is the idea of “common but differentiated responsibility” that although all countries should reduce their emissions, developed countries with historically high emissions—like the United States, Japan, and Germany—should make steeper cuts. Negotiations leading up to Paris produced a series of voluntary pledges, or Intended Nationally Determined Contributions (INDCs) designed to allow countries to set their own plans, and then be shamed by the international community into ramping them up. It’s worked, sort of, but since the INDCs alone are not enough, a major topic at Paris will be coming up with a method to keep increasing the rate at which countries cut emissions. Finance: One way to encourage climate-friendly development that is for rich countries to give poor countries lots of money. Developed countries have already committed to contributions of $100 billion per year by 2020, but there’s still no clear idea on where that kind of money would come from. Many poor countries, to their credit, have hard-coded their finance requirements into their INDC pledges, noting they’d be able to transition to renewable energy much more quickly with help from the international community. Loss and damage: Even with rapid emissions reductions, there’s still a lot of warming in the pipeline thanks to thermal inertia in the ocean and the inherent lag in the global climate system. Basically, if we stopped all emissions now, the amount of CO2 in the atmosphere would quickly stabilize and then slowly decline—but elevated levels would remain for centuries, barring the widespread adoption of some sort of carbon-sucking geoengineering. That means that climate-linked disasters will continue to escalate over the coming decades, and like most disasters, they’ll hit poor and vulnerable countries the hardest. Understandably, these poor and vulnerable countries want a mechanism in place to appeal for aid and help in adapting to future weather extremes. Ratchet mechanism: Since the Paris talks on their own won’t fix climate change, a key negotiating point will be how often countries should announce bolder targets. Rich countries are generally advocating a ramp-up in targets once per decade, while poor countries say new targets should be agreed upon once every five years. Advertisement Since the U.N. climate talks operate by consensus, the strategy this time around, to avoid the failures of Kyoto and Copenhagen, is to make everything voluntary. Still, the European Union—especially host country France—wants the Paris deal to be legally binding. That would mean it would need to be approved by the Republican-controlled U.S. Congress, which is about as likely as a snowball’s chance in the Oklahoma summer. So, the U.S. is forcefully opposing strict legal language in Paris. Some other key players to watch: The G77 China, which now contains 134 members (with China playing an increasingly minor role) is a major force for the interests of developing countries. China and India increasingly operate like heavyweight developed countries; they act as a sort of intermediary between the EU/U.S. and the truly threatened countries like Bangladesh, Burkina Faso, and the Maldives. Among the groups advocating for the strongest possible climate deal are the Alliance of Small Island States (AOSIS), the Vulnerable Countries Forum (V20), and the Least Developed Countries (LDC). Major fossil fuel producing countries like Russia, Venezuela, and Saudi Arabia will advocate for the weakest deal possible. If you’d like to follow along with the negotiations on Twitter, I’ve put together a list that includes the best climate journalists, activists, and diplomats from the talks.

## COP21: Paris Climate Summit - Key Challenges and Expectations

The 21st Conference of the Parties (COP21) to the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) is a pivotal global summit taking place in Le Bourget, near Paris. High-level negotiations, commencing Monday, aim to secure a legally binding international agreement to combat global warming. The primary objective is to limit the rise in global temperatures to no more than 2 degrees Celsius (3.6 Fahrenheit) above pre-industrial levels. Climate scientists, including those contributing to the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) reports, widely agree that exceeding this threshold will exacerbate severe droughts, storms, and floods worldwide.

### Global Leaders and Stakeholders Converge

COP21 brings together representatives from 196 nations. Prominent world leaders, such as U.S. President Obama and China's President Xi Jinping, are attending. These nations are the world's largest emitters of greenhouse gases, making their commitments crucial. Beyond national delegates, thousands of other stakeholders are present, including policymakers, business leaders, scientists from institutions like NASA and the Met Office, environmental activists from organizations such as Greenpeace and the WWF, and journalists. Public climate demonstrations planned for Paris and other French cities were canceled following recent terrorist attacks.

### Core Discussion Points: Emission Reductions and Equity

A central focus of COP21 is the review of greenhouse gas emission reduction pledges submitted by each country, with targets set for 2020. Notably, the United States and China have committed to transitioning their industries towards greener, low-carbon energy sources, as outlined in their respective Intended Nationally Determined Contributions (INDCs). Past climate negotiations, such as the 2009 Copenhagen Accord, faced significant hurdles due to disagreements between developed and developing nations regarding the equitable distribution of emission reduction responsibilities. The Durban Platform for Enhanced Action, established at COP21, aims to address these historical divides.

### The Human and Economic Impact of Climate Change

The human toll of climate change is substantial. According to the United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction (UNDRR), weather-related disasters over the past two decades have resulted in an estimated 606,000 deaths and 4.1 billion injuries. Furthermore, the UN estimates that approximately 90% of major disasters, including floods, storms, heatwaves, and droughts, are linked to extreme weather events. These events incur significant economic costs, with annual losses estimated to be between $250 billion and $300 billion.

### Prospects for a Binding Agreement

The likelihood of a successful, legally binding agreement remains uncertain, though optimism is present. Experts suggest that extensive preparatory work, including the submission of 178 out of 196 countries' Intended Nationally Determined Contributions (INDCs), has increased the possibility of a robust action plan. These INDCs represent public commitments outlining each nation's planned emission reductions and the methods they intend to employ, providing a foundation for negotiation. The Paris Agreement, if adopted, would be a landmark achievement in global climate governance.

The path to a global climate agreement is fraught with last-minute challenges, despite the potential for success. As Secretary of State John Kerry noted last week, "The stage is set for the possibility of getting this right, but on the other hand there are all these wrinkles and hurdles at the last minute."

**A History of Climate Negotiations**

This isn't the first attempt at a global climate treaty. Since 1992, the United Nations has convened annual summits. Two significant efforts stand out:

*   **The Kyoto Protocol (1997):** This agreement set legally binding emissions reduction targets for major economies. However, it exempted developing nations like China and India, now major polluters. President Bill Clinton did not submit the protocol to the Senate for ratification, as it was unlikely to pass, thus exempting the United States.
*   **The Copenhagen Accord (2009):** This pact aimed to replace Kyoto and included commitments from developing countries. However, it lacked the unanimous consent needed for legal enforcement, functioning primarily as a voluntary agreement.

**What's Different This Time?**

A pivotal development is the **U.S.-China agreement**. In a groundbreaking move, the world's two largest carbon emitters announced plans for joint emissions reduction policies. President Obama committed the U.S. to cutting emissions by up to 28 percent from 2005 levels by 2025, through stringent environmental regulations on power plants and vehicles. President Xi Jinping pledged that China would implement a national cap-and-trade system by 2017, limiting industrial emissions, and that China's emissions would begin to decline no later than 2030.

Since this agreement, over 170 countries, representing more than 90 percent of global carbon emissions, have submitted emissions reduction proposals. These plans are expected to form the foundation of a new agreement, marking a departure from the usual high-stakes negotiations.

**Managing Expectations: Paris vs. Past Deals**

Unlike the ambitious pronouncements surrounding the Kyoto and Copenhagen summits, the approach to the Paris talks has been more measured. No one is claiming this agreement will "save the planet." Instead, it's viewed as a crucial first step. The ultimate goal of climate policy is to limit global average temperature rise to 3.6 degrees Fahrenheit above pre-industrial levels. Scientists warn that exceeding this threshold could lead to devastating consequences.

Current research indicates the Earth has already warmed by approximately 1.7 degrees Fahrenheit since the Industrial Revolution. While the proposed national plans for Paris would still permit warming up to 6 degrees Fahrenheit, according to analyses by organizations like Climate Action Tracker, this is a significant improvement. Without these policies, scientists project a far more dangerous increase of over 8 degrees Fahrenheit.

Janos Pasztor, the United Nations assistant secretary general for climate change, stated, "The national plans will not put us on the path to lower temperatures enough, but they will bend the temperature curve." He emphasized that while it's significant progress, "it is not the endpoint. It is a turning point. It must mark the floor, not the ceiling."

**The "Rachet" Mechanism and Future Commitments**

A key concept in the Paris talks is "ratchet," implying that countries will reconvene every five to 10 years to present more ambitious emissions reduction plans. This mechanism aims to ensure continuous progress. However, it faces domestic political hurdles, particularly in countries like the United States, where implementing tougher policies may prove challenging. While some nations advocate for longer intervals between these reviews, U.S. negotiators are pushing for five-year cycles.

**Financial Commitments and Disagreements**

Funding for climate action remains a major obstacle. In Copenhagen, then-Secretary of State Hillary Rodham Clinton pledged that developed nations would provide $100 billion annually to developing countries by 2020 for clean energy transitions and climate change adaptation. This funding was intended to come from both public and private sources. A 2015 report by the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD) indicated that approximately $62 billion in climate finance was transferred from developed to developing countries in 2014. However, many developing nations in Paris are seeking legally binding commitments for significantly higher financial contributions from wealthier countries. Indian Environment Minister Prakash Javadekar argued, "The cost of action is not $100 billion. It is trillions; $100 billion is just a reparation."

**Verification and Trust**

Ensuring that governments meet their pledges without infringing on national sovereignty is another critical issue. While China's emissions reduction plans were initially welcomed, recent government data revealed that the country was burning 17 percent more coal annually than previously reported. This discrepancy has raised questions about the credibility of China's commitments and is expected to be a significant point of contention in Paris.

**Legally Binding vs. Peer Pressure**

Historically, climate agreements have sought to be legally binding treaties requiring national ratification. However, the U.S. Senate's likely opposition to such a treaty makes this approach unfeasible. Instead, the Paris agreement relies on voluntary plans, a strategy described by one negotiator as "legal jujitsu." The hope is that international peer pressure, particularly from major economies, will drive compliance, akin to a school assignment where attendance at a peer review assembly is mandatory, even if submission isn't strictly enforced.

**Potential Pitfalls**

The United Nations operates on a principle of unanimous consent, meaning a single country can derail an agreement. This was evident in Copenhagen, where a small number of nations opposed the accord. In Paris, a "wild-card" nation, such as Malaysia, Saudi Arabia, Sudan, or Venezuela, could potentially undermine the entire process.

The long-standing goal of climate negotiations has been to limit the rise in average global air temperatures to below two degrees Celsius. This target has been a cornerstone of international climate discussions for decades. However, the scientific community is increasingly divided on whether this two-degree threshold remains the most appropriate benchmark.

Here's a breakdown of the two-degree model and the reasons for the scientific debate:

*   **The Two-Degree Target:** This benchmark aims to prevent the planet's average global air temperature from rising more than two degrees Celsius above preindustrial levels.
*   **The Rationale:** Many scientists have historically argued that exceeding this two-degree limit would trigger catastrophic climate change, leading to severe and irreversible environmental impacts.
*   **Emerging Concerns:** While acknowledging the reality of global warming, a growing number of researchers question the scientific basis for the two-degree target. They suggest it may be an arbitrary figure, potentially based on less robust evidence than previously assumed.

The debate highlights the evolving understanding of climate science and the complexities of setting effective global climate policy.

query: What dating app did Eric Stonestreet admit to using during his interview on The Howard Stern Show?

documents.text: Even celebrities have resorted to using apps to get a date, including Modern Family actor Eric Stonestreet. On a recent interview on The Howard Stern Show, Stonestreet admitted he used the smartphone app Tinder to find dates. “I’ve gone out with a couple girls in LA that I met on Tinder, like on real dates,” he said. “On Tinder, there’s a decent chance of [a date] but it’s all based on immediate physical attraction. So you just swipe through people and you see pictures and you either say ‘like' or ‘nope.’” The 42-year-old actor said he has met a “decent amount” of women while using the app and has even had a few “same day” hookups with Tinder matches. Stonestreet also revealed on the show that he had a major crush on fellow comedian, Tina Fey. “Tina is hot and I’ve always found Tina extremely attractive,” Stonestreet said. “But Tina didn’t look like the Tina now. She was hot because she was so f—ing talented and talented and funny.” While Stonestreet remains single for now, he continues to try and find his dream girl by any means necessary.

Not everyone is looking for love on Tinder. Case in point: Lily Allen. The 30-year-old British hitmaker, who raises two toddlers with husband of four years Sam Cooper, reportedly uses the dating app to make new friends while out on the road. Allen's profile reads: "I'm a touring artist wanting to meet people abroad who don't want to talk about the industry formally known as entertainment," before admitting she is "married, [has] two beautiful girls, a massive mortgage and ridiculous schedule." Well, that's one way to lay it all out there up front.

## Hilary Duff's Tinder Strategy: Lessons in Authentic Online Dating

The internet buzzed when a Reddit user shared a screenshot of what appeared to be Hilary Duff on Tinder, sparking immediate skepticism. Many dismissed it as a fabrication, a common occurrence in the digital realm. However, the profile's link to Hilary's verified Instagram account added a layer of intrigue. While Duff had been separated from her husband since 2014, the notion of a celebrity of her stature using a mainstream dating app seemed improbable to many.

This speculation was definitively addressed when Hilary herself confirmed her presence on Tinder during a radio interview. She candidly revealed she was "talking to probably about nine guys," a remarkable success achieved within days of joining the platform. This rapid engagement offers valuable insights into effective online dating strategies, even for those without celebrity status. Beyond her fame and appearance, her profile likely played a crucial role. An examination of her online persona reveals several key takeaways for anyone navigating the complexities of dating apps.

### The Power of Genuine Presentation

Hilary's approach to her Tinder profile emphasizes authenticity, mirroring the natural and unvarnished image she presents on Instagram. Her profile picture features minimal makeup, projecting a genuine and relatable persona. In an online dating landscape often characterized by curated and misleading profiles, this commitment to presenting oneself as they truly are can foster trust and pave the way for more authentic connections.

Furthermore, her profile picture demonstrates a keen understanding of visual presentation, akin to the principles of "finding your light" often discussed in photography and modeling. The photograph is illuminated by flattering light, enhancing her natural beauty without resorting to artificial enhancements. This strategic use of lighting and composition creates an appealing and memorable first impression, setting her apart from more generic profiles.

### Clarity of Intent and Subtle Self-Promotion

Honesty regarding intentions is another cornerstone of Hilary's successful online dating strategy. Her profile reportedly features a simple yet effective declaration: a desire to "eat pizza." This straightforward statement, as she noted, has been a catalyst for numerous conversations. It effectively cuts through the superficiality, allowing potential matches to quickly assess compatibility based on shared, uncomplicated pleasures.

Hilary also masterfully avoids the pitfalls of "humblebragging." Her profile displays only her first name, meaning many matches likely did not immediately recognize her celebrity status. By refraining from overtly broadcasting her fame, she cultivates an environment conducive to genuine connection. This offers a valuable lesson: allow your personality and interactions to speak for themselves, rather than relying on overt hints about achievements or status.

### Leveraging Instagram for Deeper Engagement

For users comfortable with the feature, syncing Instagram photos to a Tinder profile can be a strategic move. For celebrities, it lends an immediate sense of legitimacy. For the average user, it provides potential dates with a more comprehensive and dynamic glimpse into your life, interests, and personality. This offers a richer, more nuanced portrayal than static bios and pictures alone, often sparking more meaningful conversations.

Hilary Duff's candid and strategic approach to online dating provides a compelling blueprint for success. Her strategy, built on authenticity, thoughtful presentation, clear intentions, and subtle self-promotion, underscores a crucial point: in the often superficial world of dating apps, genuine connection and relatable honesty are remarkably powerful tools.

This experience prompts broader reflections on the modern dating landscape. Have you utilized dating apps? Have you ever encountered a celebrity online? What are your personal guidelines for navigating the world of online dating? Share your thoughts and experiences in the comments below.

Hilary Duff is candidly discussing the dissolution of her four-year marriage to Mike Comrie, a decision that followed a year-long separation. In a revealing interview with Cosmopolitan, the "All About You" singer shared insights into the complexities of their relationship and her evolving perspective on love.

Duff reflected on the early days of their union, stating, "Mike and I were very in love when we met." She elaborated on the circumstances surrounding their marriage, noting, "We both really wanted to get married. I’d been working since the age of 11 or 12, so making that choice at a young age seemed right for me." While acknowledging periods of happiness, she explained the gradual realization that their partnership was no longer sustainable. "It wasn’t working well enough to stay together, but there was a lot of love involved," Duff explained. "It was just a slow set-in of us not being the match that we used to be." She expressed gratitude for their amicable approach to the separation: "I’m lucky for the person he is and I am, and how we decided to handle this."

The couple shares a two-year-old son, Luca, who is currently Duff's primary focus. The experience of her divorce has led Duff to adopt a more cautious outlook on lifelong partnerships. "I don’t want to sound bitter because I’m definitely not, but I don’t know if people are meant to be together forever," she admitted. She further elaborated on the challenges inherent in long-term relationships:

*   **Unforeseen Circumstances:** "Things happen over a long relationship that you can’t always fight."
*   **The Nature of Commitment:** While acknowledging the potential pride in a 20-year marriage, "there are also huge sacrifices."
*   **Prioritizing Well-being:** Duff emphasized her personal philosophy: "I just always want to fight for happiness."

At 27 years old, Hilary Duff is embarking on new professional ventures, including her role in the upcoming TV series *Younger* and the anticipated release of her first album since 2007.

*   Hilary Duff's message to Mike Comrie regarding moving on.
*   Photos of Hilary Duff and Mike Comrie appearing as friendly exes on Halloween.
