The enduring image of the Queen on stage and screen is more prominent than ever, especially as she approaches her 90th birthday. This raises a fascinating question: what is it truly like to embody one of the world's most recognizable faces, and what draws so many performers to the role?

For many actresses, stepping into the Queen's shoes might seem daunting. However, two performers who have taken on the challenge on stage emphasize a crucial element for embodying the character: the wig. Emma Handy, who portrayed the 1980s version of the Queen in the touring production of Moira Buffini's play *Handbagged*, found the costume transformative. The play itself imagines conversations between older and younger versions of the Queen and Margaret Thatcher during their weekly audiences. "The wig, makeup, and clothes do so much," Handy remarked. "It's extraordinary." She also noted a personal connection: "She seems to have two faces – the public and the private face. There is very little footage of her private life, but she is known to be enormously mischievous – and these are things I have in common with her."

Handy was adamant about avoiding mere impersonation, focusing instead on capturing the Queen's "essence." She found that sitting "incredibly straight-backed" helped her connect with the role. "She's probably the most photographed person in the world, so everyone has an idea of her," Handy explained. "Every year you see the Christmas broadcast, but that's not who she is. There is a massive side of her people don't know."

Marion Bailey, who played the older Queen, referred to as "Q," in the West End production, described the role as "irresistible." She recalled someone commenting that she didn't look like the Queen upon her casting, to which she confidently replied, "Oh, but I do." Bailey shared that she has often sat in makeup chairs, had her hair styled in a particular way, and looked in the mirror, thinking, "You know what? I could do the Queen."

In preparation for the London run of *Handbagged*, which initially played at the Tricycle Theatre before transferring to the Vaudeville Theatre, Bailey watched all of the Queen's Christmas messages and any other available online content. The audience's reaction exceeded her expectations. "Some nights I would walk out and there would be gasps," she said. "It was to do with the look of it and the history, and I tried to get as many of the mannerisms as I could." During the West End transfer, she overheard an American tourist whisper, "Is that the Queen?"

The Queen's presence in popular culture is undeniable. Peter Morgan's 2006 film *The Queen*, starring Helen Mirren, focused on the public reaction to Princess Diana's death. Morgan also penned *The Audience*, which saw Mirren reprise her role on stage, with Kristin Scott Thomas also playing the Queen in London. Channel 4's *The Queen* featured five actresses, including Emilia Fox and Samantha Bond, portraying the monarch at different life stages. The upcoming Netflix series *The Crown*, starring Claire Foy, will explore six decades of the Queen's life.

Beyond film and television, the Queen has inspired music, with songs like The Beatles' "Her Majesty" and the Sex Pistols' "God Save the Queen." Alan Bennett has also twice written about the monarch: in his book *The Uncommon Reader*, where a mobile library changes her life, and in his play *A Question of Attribution*, featuring a scene with the Queen and spy Anthony Blunt.

Queen lookalike Jeannette Charles built a career impersonating the monarch, even appearing in films like *The Naked Gun* and *Austin Powers in Goldmember*. Professor Philip Murphy, director of the Institute of Commonwealth Studies at the University of London, suggests that the 1969 documentary *Royal Family* offered the public their closest glimpse of the real Queen. However, he notes, "The Royal Family effectively suppressed it. They decided it reduced the mystique around them." This suppression, he argues, "creates a carte blanche for writers and satirists to make up personalities."

Professor Murphy highlights the challenge the Royal Family faces: "They want to appear human but they also want to appear somehow removed and keep this dignified distance." He points out that writing about the Queen often leads to a blurring of "fact and fiction," as the public has limited insight into her private life, and plausible fictional portrayals can influence historical perception.

Portrayals haven't always been flattering. Professor Murphy recalls *Spitting Image* as an "attack on British deference around authority figures." John O'Farrell, a lead writer for *Spitting Image*, explained their approach: "If you cast your mind back to the 1980s, there was an enormous reverential style. It was seen as poor form to laugh at the Queen's expense... But what we did was show her to be an ordinary person in an ordinary family, which is where it struck a comic nerve." He added, "The tabloids faked outrage, but people loved it... At times it was crude and disrespectful – but I think that's warranted."

Artist Alison Jackson has also courted controversy with her work, which uses lookalikes to create seemingly candid photographs of celebrities in private moments. Jackson's images depict "the Queen" performing everyday tasks like feeding her corgis, taking selfies, and doing the washing-up. "I thought it would be really good to get her doing normal things," Jackson stated. "She is a person, after all. And she does do normal things – she reads, she drives a Land Rover, she gets her hands dirty – she's quite tomboyish, really. She's not a diva and seems to come across as a normal woman, even though of course she's not." Jackson finds it interesting that her photograph of the Queen on the toilet is her least published, suggesting that "people think breaking that taboo is tasteless," though she intended it to be thought-provoking rather than controversial.

While these fictional representations may be thought-provoking, they arguably do little to reveal who the Queen truly is. The only instance of her playing herself was in a James Bond sketch for the London Olympics opening ceremony in 2012, alongside Daniel Craig. Nevertheless, this will not deter continued efforts to capture the essence of this elusive, yet ever-present, figure.

Prince William has revealed the profound support he received from Queen Elizabeth II following the tragic death of his mother, Princess Diana. In an interview with Sky News, broadcast ahead of Her Majesty's 90th birthday celebrations, the Duke of Cambridge spoke candidly about his grandmother's crucial role as a "strong female influence," particularly during his formative teenage years.

"Having lost my mother at a very young age, it has been particularly important for me that I had somebody like the Queen to look up to and who has been there and has understood some of the more complex issues when you lose a loved one," Prince William stated. He was just 15 years old when his mother died in a Paris car crash in 1997. At that difficult time, the Queen and the Duke of Edinburgh faced public criticism for remaining at Balmoral with William and his brother, Prince Harry, rather than immediately returning to Buckingham Palace. This period, marked by his relationship with his grandmother, is one he has rarely discussed publicly.

When asked about cherished childhood memories of the Queen, Prince William recounted a specific incident at Balmoral. He recalled a moment when he and his cousin were riding quad bikes and playfully chasing Zara, who was on a go-cart. Peter and William managed to steer Zara towards a lamppost, causing her to fall and nearly sustain an injury. "I remember my grandmother being the first person out and running across the lawn in her kilt," Prince William explained. "She came charging over and gave us the most almighty b*****king." This memorable experience, he shared, taught him a valuable lesson about the importance of being more careful and avoiding harm to his cousins.

Despite recent media scrutiny regarding his efforts to balance royal duties, family life, and his demanding role as an air ambulance pilot, Prince William confirmed he has his grandmother's unwavering support. "Surprisingly, she is very supportive of what I'm doing at the moment," he said. "She understands that I'm a family man and I want to be around for my children as much as I can." He described the Queen as being "very much at the helm," highlighting her incredibly active schedule. He also acknowledged the active roles of his father, Prince Charles, and the broader royal family, suggesting that a greater assumption of responsibilities will occur at the appropriate time.

The Duke of Cambridge expressed his anticipation for stepping up his royal duties in the future. However, his current focus remains firmly on his children and his "incredibly worthwhile and meaningful job" serving the community as an air ambulance pilot. He believes these commitments, alongside fulfilling his duties to the Queen when required, represent a "good balance."

The Queen and the Duke of Edinburgh are scheduled to undertake public engagements in Windsor today, prior to her actual birthday tomorrow. Her official birthday will be marked with further celebrations in June. A special program, "The Queen At 90," is scheduled to air on Sky News at 9pm tonight, with live coverage throughout Thursday.

**A Look Back at the Remarkable Life of Queen Elizabeth II**

Queen Elizabeth II, the longest-reigning monarch in British history, lived a life of unparalleled dedication and service. This retrospective explores pivotal moments from her extraordinary journey, from her early years to her historic reign.

**Key Milestones in the Life of Queen Elizabeth II:**

*   **Birth and Early Life:**
    *   Born on April 21, 1926, in London.
    *   Her mother was also named Elizabeth.
    *   Her father would later ascend to the throne as King George VI.
*   **Ascension to the Throne and Early Reign:**
    *   Became heir presumptive after her uncle, Edward VIII, abdicated in 1936, making her father King.
    *   Ascended to the throne in February 1952 upon the death of her father, King George VI, who succumbed to lung cancer.
    *   Her coronation ceremony took place on June 2, 1953, making her the sixth female monarch to reign in Britain.
*   **Family and Personal Life:**
    *   Married Prince Philip of Greece (later His Royal Highness Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh) on November 20, 1947.
    *   Her children include Prince Charles, Princess Anne, Prince Andrew, and Prince Edward.
    *   She was a devoted grandmother and great-grandmother.
*   **Historic Reign:**
    *   Became the longest-reigning British monarch in September 2015.
    *   Her reign spanned significant global events and societal changes.
    *   Celebrated her Diamond Jubilee in 2012, marking 60 years on the throne.
    *   Marked her 90th birthday in 2016 with a special portrait featuring four generations of the House of Windsor, commissioned by the Royal Mail for a series of stamps.

The Queen will undertake engagements in Windsor in the week of her 90th birthday. Wednesday 20th April The first engagement is a visit to the Royal Mail Windsor delivery office on William St, Windsor. This visit marks the 500th Anniversary of the Postal Service. The Queen and The Duke of Edinburgh will meet Mrs Moya Greene, the Chief Executive Officer of Royal Mail, and Mr Peter Long, the Chairman of the Royal Mail Group and will see the operation of the Caller's Office. This is where customers collect items that are not delivered to their home or business. Staff on duty will explain how the system works and there will be an opportunity to see how modern technology enables customers to track items. The Queen and The Duke will then have an opportunity to see how postmen and women sort mail ahead of delivery, and meet staff, including some who have given many years of service. Before they leave, Her Majesty and His Royal Highness will view an exhibition highlighting some of the key events, people and developments in the history of Royal Mail over the last half millennium and unveil a plaque marking the visit. While the visit takes place, The Royal Mail choir made up of frontline staff from Bristol will sing. They were formed as part of a BBC television series "The Choir: Sing While You Work". They regularly perform around the UK, raising funds for good causes. The second engagement is a visit to Alexandra Gardens for a ceremony to officially open the new Bandstand. The Queen, accompanied by The Duke of Edinburgh, will arrive at the Barry Avenue entrance where they will be received by Mr James Puxley, Lord Lieutenant of Berkshire. They will view an exhibition about the Bandstand and the school’s workshops which informed the design of the six commemorative plaques fixed to its railings. They will then meet school children from the six schools involved in the bandstand's decoration: Alexander First, Braywood, Dedworth Green First, Dedworth Middle, Queen Anne, Royal School as well as the designers and builders of the bandstand. A children’s choir will perform four short songs followed by a performance of Shakespeare as part of the School's Shakespeare Festival. Prior to departure The Queen will ascend the bandstand to unveil a plaque marking the day. Thursday 21st April The Queen, accompanied by The Duke of Edinburgh will undertake a walkabout in Windsor. Her Majesty and His Royal Highness will walk from the Henry VIII Gate of Windsor Castle towards the Statute of Queen Victoria at the foot of Castle Hill. Here Her Majesty will unveil a plaque marking The Queen's Walkway. The Queen’s Walkway is a 6.3km self-guided walking trail connecting 63 points of significance in the town of Windsor. The Walkway was designed by The Outdoor Trust to symbolically recognise the moment that Her Majesty became Britain’s longest reigning monarch after more than 63 years on 9 September 2015. It builds on the success of the Jubilee Walkway in London, the first urban walking trail in the world, opened by The Queen in 1977 in celebration of her Silver Jubilee. Excitingly, the bronze EIIR crested markers, embossed panel and promotional app have now also inspired a parallel network of Walkways throughout all 71 Nations and Territories of The Commonwealth in recognition of The Queen’s service as Head of The Commonwealth for more than 63 years. A dozen Walkways are already in development in various countries and Her Majesty launched the first complete route in Malta as part of the Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting in November 2015. By 2018 it is hoped that there will be 100 Commonwealth Walkways, connecting 5,000 rich and diverse points of significance, uniting the Commonwealth. In the evening, The Queen, accompanied by The Duke of Edinburgh, The Prince of Wales and The Duchess of Cornwall, will light a beacon and view two further beacons being lit along Long Walk and at Copper Horse. The Queen will light the principal beacon which will set in train a series of over 900 beacons across the country and worldwide. Beacons large and small, either specially built gas-fuelled structures or simple bonfires, will be lit across the country to mark The Queen's 90th Birthday, and individuals have been asked to attend a beacon-lighting event in their area, hosted by their local authority. June events Events are also taking place in June to mark Her Majesty's official birthday. Find out more

## A Nation Celebrates Queen Elizabeth II's 90th Birthday

On September 9th, Queen Elizabeth II became the longest-reigning monarch in British history, surpassing Queen Victoria's record with 64 years on the throne. As she celebrates her 90th birthday on Thursday, Britain is honoring a figure renowned for her "stiff upper lip" and unwavering dedication. Her biographer, former foreign minister Douglas Hurd, aptly described her as "The Steadfast." For seven decades, the Queen has maintained an enigmatic presence, often seen in her signature pastel outfits and colorful hats, a deliberate choice, according to royal experts, to ensure she is easily spotted in a crowd.

The celebrations are a quintessential British affair, featuring traditional pageantry, warm beer, extended pub hours, and equestrian displays. Actress Helen Mirren, who earned an Oscar for her portrayal of the Queen, will also be in attendance.

### A Dutiful Start to the Celebrations

True to her reputation as a diligent worker, who completed 341 engagements last year, Queen Elizabeth II kicked off her birthday festivities on Wednesday with a visit to a Royal Mail delivery center. Accompanied by her husband, the Duke of Edinburgh, she observed postal workers sorting mail and was treated to a serenade by a choir of employees.

On Thursday, the Queen will light the first of over 900 celebratory beacons across the country. The following day, President Obama is scheduled to offer his birthday wishes at Windsor Castle. The festivities will extend beyond the week, with pub opening times in England and Wales being extended by two hours, until 1 a.m., on June 10th and 11th. (Her official birthday is celebrated in June for ceremonial purposes.)

### Tributes and Reflections

In a unique tribute, a British artist has crafted a giant sculpture of the Queen's head using 800 car parts, including spark plugs for her crown, honoring her service as an Army mechanic during World War II. A new set of first-class stamps has also been released to commemorate the birthday, featuring four generations of the royal family, including young Prince George.

The occasion has also sparked commercial interest, with retailers offering a range of commemorative items. While a 22-karat gold "God Save the Queen" music box retails for $1,425, more affordable options like a souvenir waistcoat emblazoned with the Queen's image are available for $50 on eBay.

Reflecting the multicultural fabric of modern Britain, Nadiya Hussain, a Muslim baker known for her appearance on "The Great British Bake Off," will prepare an orange drizzle birthday cake for the Queen. This gesture highlights the evolving nature of the nation during her reign. Interestingly, a 68-year-old slice of the Queen's wedding cake fetched $719 at an auction in September.

### The Enigma of the Monarch

Vernon Bogdanor, a distinguished constitutional scholar at King's College London, praised the Queen's self-restraint and her ability to keep her personal views private. He argues that this enigmatic quality has been crucial to her success in symbolizing and unifying the nation. "She grew up during the war when people had to show emotional restraint," he noted, contrasting it with contemporary society.

Professor Bogdanor recalled only two notable missteps by the Queen. The first occurred in 1994 during a visit to St. Petersburg, Russia, where she reportedly commented to a Russian biologist that Manchester, England, was "not such a nice place." Buckingham Palace denied the remark at the time. More significantly, in 1997, the Queen was perceived to have misjudged the public's grief following Princess Diana's death by initially remaining at Balmoral with her grandsons, rather than returning to London to comfort the nation.

### Glimpses Behind the Palace Walls

An ITV documentary, "Our Queen at 90," offered a rare insight into the royal household. Viewers learned that her 2-year-old great-grandson, Prince George, affectionately calls her "Gan-Gan." His father, Prince William, also shared that some individuals are so overcome by meeting his grandmother that they faint.

Born on April 21, 1926, Elizabeth was initially third in line to the throne. In 1947, she married Prince Philip, defying her parents' wishes. She ascended to the throne in 1952 following the death of her father, King George VI, whose elder brother, King Edward VIII, had abdicated in 1936 to marry Wallis Simpson.

Cultural commentator Peter York suggested that the Queen's perceived "unbridled blandness" could be seen as a form of "human bondage," despite the monarchy's association with class and privilege. He noted that Britons often appreciate stories of her legendary thriftiness, such as the apocryphal tales of her turning off lights at Buckingham Palace or eating "non-banquet dinners out of Tupperware containers."

In private, the Queen is said to possess a sharp sense of humor. "She should be congratulated for living this long," Mr. York remarked. "The certainty of her life reminds Britons of the continuity of their own." He also observed moments of subtle annoyance, citing an instance in 2008 at the London School of Economics when she questioned why no one had foreseen the financial crisis. "Every so often you see a little lip-tightening that suggests, ‘What fresh hell is this?’ when she is forced to endure something hideous or idiotic," Mr. York described.

### The Future of the Monarchy

While her mother lived to 101, and the Queen shows no signs of slowing down, the word "abdication" is considered taboo within Buckingham Palace, according to Stephen Bates, author of "Royalty Inc.: Britain’s Best-Known Brand." He also expressed concerns that the presumed successors – Charles, Prince of Wales; Prince William, Duke of Cambridge; and Prince George – might not possess the same charisma as the Queen. "William has become just another middle-aged bald bloke in a suit," he commented.

Despite these potential challenges, Queen Elizabeth II appears to have fulfilled the promise she made on her 21st birthday: "I declare before you all, that my whole life, whether it be long or short, shall be devoted to your service."

query: What key points did Director of National Intelligence James Clapper make regarding the intelligence community's findings on Russian involvement in the hacking during the 2016 election?

documents.text:
U.S. intelligence chiefs publicly defended their findings on Russian interference in the 2016 election, directly countering President-elect Donald Trump's skepticism. They asserted that Russia's highest officials authorized the email theft and disclosures.

Director of National Intelligence James Clapper stated to the Senate Armed Services Committee, "There is a difference between skepticism and disparagement." He emphasized, "Public trust and confidence in the intelligence community is crucial." Clapper, CIA Director John Brennan, and FBI Director James Comey were scheduled to brief Trump on the hacking and leaks from Democratic entities supporting Hillary Clinton. Trump had previously questioned the intelligence agencies' conclusions, stating, "hacking is a very hard thing to prove" and that they have been incorrect in the past.

However, Clapper informed the Senate panel that the intelligence agencies' confidence in their findings is now "very high." He added they are "even more resolute" about Russian involvement than when they first publicly stated their assessment on October 7th. Clapper also clarified that Russian hacking did not alter the election outcome. He testified alongside Undersecretary of Defense for Intelligence Marcel Lettre and NSA Director Admiral Michael Rogers.

The hearing, initiated by Senator John McCain, highlighted a rift between Trump and some Republican foreign policy leaders. While Trump has praised Russian President Vladimir Putin, McCain and others advocate for stricter sanctions against Russia, which has denied the hacking accusations. Senator Lindsey Graham declared, "Putin is up to no good" and that it's time for decisive action, not minor responses. He advised Trump, "Mr. President-elect, when you listen to these people you can be skeptical, but understand they’re the best among us and they’re trying to protect us."

Clapper noted that while finding areas of mutual interest with Russia is beneficial, "there’s a threshold of behavior that’s unacceptable." Trump, in a series of tweets, questioned the delay in his briefing and cited WikiLeaks founder Julian Assange, who suggested a 14-year-old could be responsible for the breaches and that Russians did not provide him with information. Clapper and Rogers dismissed Assange as a credible source, citing his past endangerment of U.S. lives by publishing classified material. Clapper stated the intelligence community has "a whole lot of respect for him" and that Trump's comments "hardly help" morale.

President Barack Obama, in a television interview, expressed hope that Trump would gain confidence in the intelligence agencies after receiving his briefings. Beau Woods, deputy director of the Atlantic Council’s Cyber Statecraft Initiative, commented that while skepticism is valuable, outright dismissal of intelligence is "unhelpful for finding the truth."

McCain described Russia's hacking as an "unprecedented attack" on U.S. democracy, urging Americans to be alarmed. The intelligence officials' prepared remarks stated Moscow "poses a major threat" to U.S. networks and has developed a "highly advanced offensive cyber program." They anticipate future Russian cyber operations will target the U.S. for intelligence gathering, supporting decision-making, conducting influence operations, and preparing for future contingencies.

Clapper announced plans to brief Congress privately and release an unclassified version of their review publicly early next week, aiming to be as transparent as possible. White House Press Secretary Josh Earnest confirmed the White House has the intelligence review, ordered by Obama. Earnest called intelligence community members "patriots" dedicated to factual accuracy.

Following the hearing, Trump tweeted that the intelligence report was politicized, questioning its source and timing after NBC News reported it detailed attacks beyond the DNC, including on the White House and Joint Chiefs of Staff. He also claimed the DNC prevented the FBI from accessing its computer information, referencing a BuzzFeed story that revealed the FBI relied on a third-party analysis by CrowdStrike because the DNC denied direct server access.

In their statement, intelligence officials noted the Kremlin's increasingly "aggressive cyber posture" in recent years, targeting various organizations through phishing and masquerading as third parties. They are working to "harden" U.S. government systems, but acknowledged that over 30 nations are developing offensive cyber capabilities, and terrorist groups like ISIS continue to seek sensitive information.

Washington (CNN) The leading US intelligence official said Thursday that his foreign counterparts have expressed concern that President-elect Donald Trump is disparaging the US intelligence community, potentially damaging public confidence. "I do think public trust and confidence in the Intelligence Community is crucial" both in the US and in other countries that rely on US intelligence, James Clapper, the Director of National Intelligence, told the Senate Armed Services Committee. "I've received many expressions of concern from foreign counterparts about the disparagement of the US Intelligence Community, or I should say, what has been interpreted as disparagement of the US Intelligence Community." Clapper was speaking at a hearing on global cyberthreats that focused almost exclusively on Moscow's alleged hacking during the presidential elections. The hearing gave lawmakers and senior US intelligence officials the chance to draw a line in the sand for Trump, presenting a united front on their conclusion that Russia is a major threat to the United States and was behind election-related hacking -- a conclusion the President-elect has refused to accept. "We assess that only Russia's senior-most officials could have authorized the recent election-focused data thefts and disclosures, based on the scope and sensitivity of the targets," Clapper, Undersecretary of Defense for Intelligence Marcel Lettre and the commander of US Cyber Command, Michael Rogers, wrote in a joint statement to the Senate Armed Services Committee. Clapper said at the hearing that there was a line between critical thinking about assessment and undermining those gathering the intelligence. "I think there is an important distinction here between healthy skepticism which policymakers -- to include policymaker No. 1 -- should always have on intelligence, but I think there's a difference between skepticism and disparagement," he said. JUST WATCHED Conway defends Trump's Russian hack comments Replay More Videos ... MUST WATCH Conway defends Trump's Russian hack comments 01:20 And he noted that some intelligence agents die in service for their country. "You only need to walk into the lobby and look at the stars on the wall or the NSA and the number of intelligence people who have paid the ultimate price for their country," he said. Rogers told the committee that he was concerned about morale within the Intelligence Community and that having political leaders who are confident in intelligence agencies is "crucial to that." "I don't want to lose good, motivated people because they feel there's not room for them to contribute," Rogers said. "Without that confidence, I just don't want a situation where our workforce decides to walk because that's just not a good place for us to be." Sen. Lindsey Graham, a South Carolina Republican, said he wanted to convey to Trump that "It's okay to challenge the intel, you're right to do so, but what I don't want you to do is undermine those who are serving our nation in this arena until you're absolutely sure they need to be undermined. "And I think they need to be uplifted," Graham added, "not undermined." In his opening questions to Clapper, Committee Chairman John McCain noted the Intelligence Community's conclusion that the cyberthefts and disclosures were intended to interfere with the US election process and could only have been authorized by Russia's most senior levels. "We stand actually more resolutely on the strength of that statement that we made on the seventh of October," Clapper said. After the election, the Intelligence Community concluded that at least one Russian motive had been to help Trump win. Clapper said that the hacking did not succeed in changing any vote tallies, but that it was impossible for intelligence to assess how the information released from the breaches affected voters' attitudes. McCain emphasized the Russian role and delivered an implicit rebuke to Trump, who has urged people to "move on" from the issue of Moscow-directed hacking. "Every American should be alarmed by Russia's attacks on our nation," the Arizona Republican said. "There's no escaping the fact that this committee meets today ... in the aftermath of an unprecedented attack on our democracy." McCain added that the "goal of this review is not to question the outcome of the election nor should it be." It's crucial that the country move forward with full knowledge about what happened and that Congress take bipartrisan steps, he said. Russia reacted almost immediately, with Kremlin spokesman Dmitr Peskov telling CNN that Moscow is "sick and tired of those irresponsibly blaming everything on our country. If there is a need for an enemy, why not to try someone else?" "We have suggested cooperation on combating cyberthreats numerous times," Peskov said. "It was rejected." JUST WATCHED Is Putin the most powerful man in the world? Replay More Videos ... MUST WATCH Is Putin the most powerful man in the world? 02:00 But both Clapper and Rogers said in their testimony that the US has been trying to create international norms for cyber-related conflict. "We continue to engage with partners around the world about what is acceptable and unacceptable behavior in cyberspace, " Rogers said. "We are clearly not where we want to be in this regard." Trump has suggested that the Intelligence Community's conclusion on Russia was driven by political interests. Clapper, who enlisted in the Marine Corps in 1961, stressed that he has served every president "in the trenches" since John F. Kennedy. "I am apolitical," he said, adding that it was a priority to supply "unvarnished" and "untainted" policy recommendations to policy makers. Photos: WikiLeaks founder Julian Assange Photos: WikiLeaks founder Julian Assange Julian Assange, founder of the website WikiLeaks, has been a key figure in major leaks of classified government documents, cables and videos since his site launched in 2006. Six days later, Swedish prosecutors issued a warrant for his arrest based on allegations of sexual assault from two women. Assange refused to meet the actor, stating: "I believe you are well-intentioned, but surely you can see why it is a bad idea for me to meet with you. By meeting with you, I would validate this wretched film, and endorse the talented, but debauched, performance that the script will force you to give." Hide Caption 13 of 15 Photos: WikiLeaks founder Julian Assange Assange speaks during a panel discussion at the South By Southwest Festival in Austin, Texas, in March 2014. Hide Caption 14 of 15 Photos: WikiLeaks founder Julian Assange Assange attends a news conference inside the Ecuadorian Embassy in London in August. Hide Caption 15 of 15 On Wednesday, Trump championed WikiLeaks founder Julian Assange's claim that Russia wasn't the source of stolen documents from the Democratic National Committee that his group distributed. McCain asked Clapper whether Assange had endangered men and women serving the US with his earlier release of State Department cables. "Yes, he has," Clapper said. McCain then asked whether Assange should be accorded any credibility -- another implicit rebuke of Trump. Clapper responded, "No, he should not." JUST WATCHED Former adviser: Putin euphoric over Trump win Replay More Videos ... MUST WATCH Former adviser: Putin euphoric over Trump win 02:18 The President-elect's apparent support for Assange and his dismissal of the intelligence community's findings on Russia have alarmed fellow Republicans, but on Thursday morning, Trump tweeted that "the media lies to make it look like I am against 'Intelligence' when in fact I am a big fan!" The intelligence leaders stressed before the Senate that instead of "moving on," Russia requires vigilance. "Russia is a full-scope cyber actor that poses a major threat to US government, military, diplomatic, commercial, and critical infrastructure and key resource networks because of its highly advanced offensive cyber program and sophisticated tactics, techniques, and procedures," they said. After Thursday's public hearing, Clapper, Lettre and Rogers may brief lawmakers in a closed session to discuss classified material, Senate aides said. Clapper is due to brief Trump Friday on Russia's actions in a private meeting. President Barack Obama ordered a full review of Russian meddling into the election a month ago and received the report Thursday morning. Clapper, who will brief Trump on the findings on Friday, said the report will speak to Russia's motivations. He also told lawmakers that an unclassified version will be released to the public. Speaking to WMAQ in Chicago Thursday, Obama said it was "important" Trump and Intelligence Community work together. "My hope is that when the President-elect receives his own briefings and is able to examine the intelligence as his team has put together and they see how professional and effective these agencies are, that some of those current tensions will be reduced," Obama said. "I can speak to my own experience -- it's going to be important to make sure the President and the intelligence communities are both working on the best possible information," Obama continued. Republicans like Graham and McCain want broad investigations into Russian behavior in cyberspace and overseas. After the hearing, McCain told reporters that Russia's ability to interfere in the elections was "a threat to national security." He added that "In the broadest context, it was an act of war," but said that retaliation would have to be carefully considered. "It fits the definition of an act of war, but it doesn't mean you start shooting over it," McCain said. McCain said he didn't know why Trump refuses to accept intelligence conclusions on Russia. Asked if he had any concerns about that, McCain said he wanted to wait until Trump was briefed on Friday. Graham told CNN in December that he and McCain plan to put sanctions together that hit Russian President Vladimir Putin "as an individual and his inner circle for interfering in our election" and in other nations' elections. Graham said that there are "a hundred United States senators ... I would say that 99 of us believe the Russians did this and we're going to do something about it." Meanwhile, a broad array of Democrats are calling for a public airing of Russia's efforts to sway the election.
