query: What heroic action did Tiana Carruthers take during the Kalamazoo shooting before being shot?

documents.text: The first victim in the Kalamazoo shooting tried to protect children playing outside an apartment complex before being shot multiple times. Tiana Carruthers put herself between a group of kids and gunman Jason Dalton at the beginning of his Saturday night rampage in Michigan, when he killed six and wounded two others. JASON DALTON HAD LONG HISTORY OF DRIVING VIOLATIONS Carruthers was hit three times in the legs and arms, according toWWMT,but is expected to survive after receiving multiple surgeries. The 25-year-old and several children, including at least one of her own, were outside at the Meadows Townhomes when Dalton, a 45-year-old Uber driver, drove up right before 6 p.m. Tiana Carruthers, the first victim of the Kalamazoo shootings, reportedly put herself between gunman Jason Dalton and playing children before she was shot. (Facebook) "Have you seen Mazie?" he asked Joi Coleman, 12. As of Monday night it was unclear who Mazie is, or why Dalton asked about her before beginning the shooting. ACCUSED KALAMAZOO SHOOTER JASON DALTON CHARGED WITH MURDER Coleman said Carruthers asked him if there was anything she could do for him before he opened fire. Several children were outside the apartment complex when Dalton pulled up to them and soon began shooting. (WWMT) “He shot her once and she was still running and then he shot her again and she fell. She was asking 'Joi, help me. Joi, help me,” the young girl recounted. Kalamazoo County Sheriff Richard Fuller said that the mother "sensed trouble" before sending the kids inside as quickly as possible. KALAMZOO TEEN SQUEEZED MOM'S HAND SURVIVED MASS SHOOTING Dalton had reportedly come to the apartment complex earlier in the day. Dalton, a 45-year-old Uber driver, has admitted to the shooting, though no motive is known. (AP) The gunman would go on to shoot dead Richard Smith and his son Tyler, 17, as the pair was looking at cars around 10 p.m. Shortly after he killed Mary Lou Nye, 60, Mary Joe Nye, 62, Barbara Hawthorne, 68, and Dorothy Brown, 74, at a nearby Cracker Barrel. Fourteen-year-old Abigail Kopf was also shot at the country restaurant and was fighting for her life as of Monday night. Dalton, who had been picking up fares during the night of his attacks, was arrested early Sunday morning and now faces charges of first degree murder. Six people were killed and two, including Carruthers, were injured at three separate shooting sites on Saturday night. (Mark Bugnaski/AP) Carruthers was able to pick Dalton out of a lineup and tell investigators that he left in a silver SUV, according toMLive. Dalton, a former insurance adjuster, also reportedly confessed to the attacks and was denied bail on Monday. With News Wire Services. **Kalamazoo Shooting Spree: A Night of Terror and Unanswered Questions**

On Saturday night, a chilling series of events unfolded in Kalamazoo, leaving a community reeling and questioning the motives behind a violent spree. The terror began when 12-year-old Joi Coleman recounted a harrowing encounter in the parking lot of the Meadows Townhomes. She described how Jason Dalton, 45, approached her while she was holding a woman's hand, asking, "Have you seen Mazie?"

The woman, later identified as a victim of Dalton's violence, responded, "Is there something I can do for you? Can I help you?" Dalton's response was immediate and brutal: "then he shot her." Coleman vividly recalled the horrific sequence:

*   Dalton fired once, and the woman continued to run.
*   He shot her again, causing her to fall.
*   In her distress, she pleaded, "Joi, help me. Joi, help me."

Coleman, along with other children she was supervising, managed to escape to safety. The victim's daughter witnessed the unfolding tragedy from a window as police stated Dalton discharged 10 rounds. Tragically, three bullets struck the victim. Meghan Coleman, another child present, recounted the terror: "The little girl ran to the window and kept yelling, 'My mom is dead! My mom is dead!'" She added, "The guy kept coming back, the guy backed up and started shooting again, and then missed."

Joi Coleman's mother, who was caring for 11 children at the time, reported that Dalton returned to the scene four times. Joi described the weapon as "silver" and stated, "He was pointing the gun right at our house."

Neighbors Travis Gettys and Devin Fletter, residing next door to the Colemans, reported seeing Dalton interacting with children in the neighborhood just an hour before the shooting. Fletter expressed, "Makes me feel like he was looking for a specific person." Gettys described the surreal experience, stating, "It's such an adrenaline rush, we were just playing video games and everything happened." Coleman also recalled Dalton's initial actions: "He wasn't shooting at first, he was just looking at us and other kids sleepy and stuff." The initial victim, struck by three bullets in her legs and arm, was expected to recover.

The connection between the escalating violence was starkly identified by authorities. Travis Gettys stated, "At this point the connection between the three cases is the defendant. Is Mister Dalton."

Just hours after the first shooting, around 10 p.m., a second incident occurred at Seelye Kia on Stadium Drive. 17-year-old Tyler Smith and his father, Richard Smith, were fatally shot while looking at cars. Minutes later, a devastating attack in the parking lot of Cracker Barrel resulted in the deaths of close friends Mary L. Nye, Mary Jo Nye, Judy Brown, and Barbara Hawthorne, who were in their cars. A 14-year-old, also in one of the vehicles, remains on life support at Bronson Hospital.

Kalamazoo County Sheriff Richard Fuller described the events as "just the most awful thing I can think of having happened." West Michigan, and indeed the nation, is now grappling with the loss of six lives. As victims and their families attempt to comprehend the violence attributed to one man, Gettys reflected, "I never thought it would happen in my lifetime." When asked about her feelings on Sunday, Joi Coleman simply stated, "Scared."

The Kalamazoo County Sheriff's Office released a statement on Wednesday regarding the victim from The Meadows Apartments:

*   The family has requested that the victim's name and related information not be released.
*   This request stems from the victim and family's current inability to cope with media attention.
*   The family will inform Sheriff Richard Fuller when they are prepared to speak.
*   The Sheriff's Office urges media and concerned parties to allow the family the necessary time for recovery.
*   The victim's condition remains serious.

FILE - In this Dec. 16, 2015, file photo, a man leaves the headquarters of Uber in San Francisco. Ride-hailing company Uber received a complaint about erratic driving by Jason Dalton Saturday night, but... (Associated Press) FILE - In this Dec. 16, 2015, file photo, a man leaves the headquarters of Uber in San Francisco. Ride-hailing company Uber received a complaint about erratic driving by Jason Dalton Saturday night, but... (Associated Press) DETROIT (AP) — Uber is once again facing tough questions about safety after one of its drivers was charged with murder. The fast-growing, San Francisco-based ride-hailing company defended its screening of drivers Monday, saying it couldn't have predicted that driver Jason Dalton would engage in the random shootings that left a half-dozen people dead in Kalamazoo Saturday night. Uber said its safety procedures are robust and don't need to change. But some experts say the company needs to take a harder look at potential drivers if it wants to convince riders the service is safe, even as they agreed that Dalton wouldn't have raised red flags because he didn't have a criminal record. Dalton, who had been driving for Uber for less than a month, was charged Monday with killing six people. The shootings began Saturday evening and took place over nearly five hours. The Kalamazoo County prosecutor says Dalton picked up Uber passengers after the first shootings and probably after subsequent shootings. Uber security chief Joe Sullivan said Dalton cleared a background check and was approved to be a driver on Jan. 25. He had given slightly more than 100 rides and had a rating of 4.73 stars out of a possible five. Until Saturday, Sullivan said, Uber had no reason to believe anything was amiss. "No background check would have flagged and anticipated this situation." Still, the incident raised more questions about Uber's security. The company earlier this month agreed to pay $28.5 million to settle two lawsuits that alleged it misled customers about safety procedures and fees. It's also facing a separate a lawsuit by district attorneys in San Francisco and Los Angeles, who alleged that Uber's checks failed to prevent the company from hiring several felons. Last fall, an Uber driver in Massachusetts was sentenced to prison for raping a female passenger. Around the same time, a sixth-grade teacher moonlighting as an Uber driver was accused of raping a passenger in South Carolina. And in India in November, an Uber driver was sentenced to life in prison for raping a passenger. Uber was briefly banned from operating in New Delhi after that incident. In response to the lawsuits, Uber has stopped using terms like "safer than a taxi" and "industry-leading background checks." But it insists riders are protected, and points out that, unlike taxis, riders can rate drivers. "The system that Uber has is extremely safe," said Ed Davis, a former police commissioner from Boston who serves on Uber's Safety Advisory Board. Uber's driver background checks compare names to public court records and are far less effective than fingerprint checks that access the FBI criminal history database, says Matthew Daus, a former New York City Taxi Commissioner and a transportation lawyer who has studied the issue. Name checks can miss court records if a person uses an alias or their name is even slightly different from the records, Daus said. Uber disputes that, and says the FBI records can be flawed because they don't always make clear cases in which charges were dropped. That can discriminate against potential drivers. Sullivan says Uber checks court records back to seven years, and will send someone in person to check records that aren't online. In any case, both sides agree that Dalton wouldn't have raised red flags, since he had no prior criminal record. "A background check is just that. It does not foresee the future," Davis said. But Daus said Uber's process is still troubling, since background checks done by fingerprints by a government agency are public records that anyone can review. In this case, Uber controls the information, he said. Sullivan indicated that Uber became aware at some point Saturday of issues with Dalton's driving. He said the company received complaints about Dalton from several passengers on Saturday, including one about dangerous and erratic driving. Sullivan said Uber immediately suspends drivers who are accused of violent acts. But in the case of erratic driving, it typically contacts the driver first to make sure it hears both sides. Sullivan wouldn't say whether Uber contacted Dalton on Saturday night, referring questions to law enforcement. Uber passenger Matt Mellen said he called police to report that Dalton was driving erratically more than an hour before the shootings began. Mellen said he also tried to report Dalton to Uber. Driver behavior is a problem Uber must address with the public as it grows and adds more cars, said Allen Adamson, founder of Brand Simple Consulting. The company, which is currently chalking up 3 million rides per day worldwide, needs to show that it has tough screening procedures and doesn't just take anyone as a driver, he said. "People expect Uber to ensure that that driver is safe, secure and can be trusted. That's very hard for a rapidly growing company, and the bigger the company the harder it is to do," Adamson said. People will accept one or two fluke incidents, but "if it happens on a repeated basis, you are on thin ice with your brand," Adamson said. Customers appeared to sticking with the service, and backing the company's safety processes. Ian Leavitt, a medical research assistant at the University of Colorado in Denver, uses Uber a few times a month and he'll continue to use it despite the shootings. Leavitt says that as long as Uber makes sure drivers don't have a criminal record or links to terrorism, he's satisfied that it's doing all it can. "A background check can't predict the future. If someone with poor mental health hasn't acted out in the past, they'll make it through the check," he said.

Uber failed to act on a critical safety complaint about driver Jason Dalton, who was reported for "driving dangerously" just hours before he fatally shot six people in Kalamazoo, Michigan. The rider, Matt Mellen, described Dalton's erratic driving, including running stop signs and swerving onto lawns, after receiving a phone call. Mellen stated he "jumped out of the car and ran away" when Dalton finally stopped.

Uber confirmed that this feedback, received approximately five hours before the first victim was killed, was not reviewed. An Uber spokesperson, speaking anonymously, explained the company's prioritization system, noting they handle "3 million rides a day." The spokesperson indicated that complaints about erratic driving, as opposed to explicit threats of violence, are not immediately prioritized. "Anything that has a whiff of violence, ‘the driver assaulted me’, we suspend the person immediately," the spokesperson said, contrasting it with driving complaints. They further elaborated that deactivating a driver for poor driving without investigation could be unfair, especially given the high volume of such complaints. This specific complaint "fell into that back bucket."

Uber's policy is not to immediately deactivate drivers for erratic driving due to the sheer volume of complaints and a desire to avoid unfair consequences. "The reason we do that, just to be clear, is because with something like bad driving, we’re not going to take away someone’s ability to earn a living without talking to them first," the spokesperson stated. When asked if Uber had reached out to Dalton after receiving the complaint, the spokesperson confirmed, "We had not gotten around to doing that, no."

Jason Dalton, who passed a background check and began driving for Uber on January 25th, had completed approximately 100 rides. On Monday afternoon, Joe Sullivan, Uber's Chief Security Officer, stated that Dalton's rampage was unpredictable, citing his "overall good" rating of 4.73. Kalamazoo County Sheriff Richard Fuller confirmed Uber's cooperation with law enforcement, believing the company can "help us fill in some timeline gaps." Investigators are particularly interested in communications between Dalton and Uber, as well as with his passengers.

The victims, who had no apparent connection to the gunman or each other, were killed in a spree that began Saturday evening. The attacks occurred at the Meadows apartment complex, a car dealership, and the parking lot of a Cracker Barrel restaurant. Uber's policy prohibits passengers and drivers from possessing firearms in vehicles, with violations potentially leading to service termination.

## Kalamazoo Shootings: A Community Grapples with Unexplained Violence and a Chilling Timeline

Kalamazoo, Michigan, was plunged into shock and grief on Saturday, February 20, 2016, following a series of devastating shootings that claimed six lives and left a 14-year-old girl critically wounded. The alleged perpetrator, Jason Dalton, a local Uber driver, continued to transport passengers between the attacks, a detail that has profoundly disturbed investigators and the public. The apparent absence of any discernible connection between the victims and Dalton has amplified the mystery surrounding these horrific events, as detailed in reports by the Associated Press.

### A Day of Terror: The Unfolding Tragedy

The day's violence began outside the Meadows apartment complex. Tiana Carruthers, 25, was shot multiple times but survived, having bravely shielded children she was supervising as Dalton's vehicle approached. Prosecutor Jeff Getting confirmed that Dalton used a false name to gain Carruthers' attention before opening fire, refuting any notion that she was specifically targeted.

Approximately four hours later, and roughly 15 miles away, a father and his 17-year-old son were fatally shot while at a car dealership. Just fifteen minutes after that, five individuals were shot in the parking lot of a Cracker Barrel restaurant, resulting in four fatalities. Abigail Kopf, 14, initially believed to be among the deceased, was discovered critically injured but alive, offering a fragile glimmer of hope amidst the devastation.

### Dalton's Disturbing Commute: Uber Between Attacks

Compounding the horror, Jason Dalton allegedly continued his work as an Uber driver throughout the day's attacks. Prosecutor Jeff Getting confirmed that Dalton picked up fares even after the first shooting and likely continued to do so after the subsequent fatal incidents. Crucially, none of these passenger interactions appear to be linked to the attacks themselves, according to investigators.

Uber acknowledged receiving complaints about Dalton's erratic driving on the day of the shootings. A company spokesperson stated their policy involves contacting drivers when alerted to unsafe practices, but declined to confirm if Dalton was indeed contacted.

Adding a chilling layer to the timeline, passenger Matt Mellen reported calling the police to report Dalton's dangerous driving more than an hour before the first shooting. Mellen described Dalton's reckless behavior, including running stop signs and sideswiping vehicles, which prompted Mellen to flee the car and immediately contact authorities. Investigators are actively seeking to understand Dalton's communication with Uber and identify any passengers he transported during the critical hours. Uber confirmed Dalton had passed a background check and had completed approximately 100 rides since commencing work on January 25th.

### The Elusive Motive: A Community's Bewilderment

Jason Dalton was formally charged with six counts of murder and denied bail. He confessed to investigators, stating, "that he took people's lives." Despite this admission, the motive behind the killings remains profoundly elusive.

Law enforcement officials have been candid about the possibility that a definitive motive may never be uncovered. Prosecutor Jeff Getting articulated the profound impact on the community, stating, "This is a terrible moment in the community. We face violence like any other community does but nothing on this scale." He highlighted the loss of life, the critical condition of a young girl, and the widespread confusion, emphasizing, "And no one understands why it happened."

Sheriff Richard Fuller acknowledged that Dalton's motive and state of mind are likely the "hardest to answer for anybody." He posed a poignant question to the community, reflecting the collective bewilderment: "In the end, I ask people, because I keep hearing this question of why, 'What would be the answer that would be an acceptable answer for you?' They have to think about it for a moment, and they say, 'Probably nothing.'"

This report was compiled with contributions from Associated Press writers Tom Krisher, Dee-Ann Durbin, Ed White, and Don Babwin.

## Kalamazoo Shooting Spree: Suspect Jason Dalton Appears in Court

Jason Brian Dalton, the man accused of a devastating shooting spree across Kalamazoo County, made his first court appearance Monday to face serious charges including murder, assault, and firearms offenses. The seemingly random violence, which authorities have characterized as "your worst nightmare," has left law enforcement and the community in shock. Dalton, a married father of two, is alleged to have shot eight people at three separate locations on Saturday evening.

### The Allegations and Investigation

Prosecuting Attorney Jeffrey Getting described the killings as "very deliberate" and "intentional, deliberate and... coldly done." Disturbingly, evidence suggests Dalton continued to pick up Uber fares between the shootings, according to a source close to the investigation. Dalton faces six counts of murder, two counts of assault with intent to commit murder, and eight firearms charges. Detectives testified that Dalton admitted to "taking people's lives" after being read his rights. Appearing via video from jail, Dalton showed no outward emotion as the charges were read, and bail was denied. The only apparent connection between the victims was Dalton himself and his semi-automatic pistol, consistent with shell casings found at the scenes. "He chose people and he shot them simply because he could," Getting told CNN.

The Dalton family has issued a statement expressing their condolences and a commitment to cooperating with the investigation to understand the motive behind the violence. Sheriff Richard Fuller confirmed Dalton's cooperation but acknowledged a lack of clear answers regarding the trigger for the attacks. "My best sense is that it was somebody who was having an issue at the time and for whatever reason they decided to do what they did," Fuller told CNN.

### Timeline of Terror

The events began around 3 p.m. Saturday when Dalton visited Southwick's, a local gun shop where he was a regular customer. Owner Jonathan Southwick reported that Dalton appeared normal, even smiling and hugging an employee, before purchasing a jacket suitable for concealing a small pistol. He left without raising any suspicion.

The first shooting occurred around 5:42 p.m. Police believe Dalton first shot Tiana Carruthers multiple times in an apartment complex parking lot, in front of her children. She is expected to survive. Four hours later, Richard Smith, 53, and his son Tyler, 17, were killed at a car dealership. Tyler's 17-year-old girlfriend witnessed the attack from their car. Public Safety Director Jeff Hadley described the incident as "cold blooded."

Dalton then drove to a Cracker Barrel restaurant, where he opened fire in the parking lot. Four women were killed in this attack: Dorothy Brown, 74; Barbara Hawthorne, 68; Mary Lou Nye, 62; and Mary Jo Nye, 60. A 14-year-old girl, Abigail Kopf, was critically wounded and remains in serious condition, though she is responding to verbal commands.

Dalton was arrested without incident in downtown Kalamazoo approximately two hours after the final shooting. Eleven rifles were found at his home.

### Witness Accounts and Background Checks

Matt Mellen, who rode with Dalton shortly before the shootings, recounted Dalton receiving a phone call and then driving erratically, side-swiping a car and running stop signs and red lights. Mellen pleaded with Dalton to stop so he could exit the vehicle, noting Dalton remained surprisingly calm. Mellen managed to escape and call 911. Another man, identified only as Derek, told WOOD-TV he hailed an Uber for safety, only to find himself in Dalton's car. He jokingly asked if Dalton was the shooter, receiving a dismissive "no" and a shake of the head. Dalton then claimed he was just tired, and they proceeded with a normal conversation.

Uber confirmed Dalton passed a background check, and police stated he had no criminal record. "For all intents and purposes, he was your average Joe," said Hadley. "This was random."

query: What circumstances led to James Harrison's 2008 arrest, and what were the outcomes of the charges against him?

documents.text: James Harrison believes he has grown since being arrested in 2008, according to Scott Brown of ESPN. Harrison, who recently signed a one-year deal with the Pittsburgh Steelers, was arrested for hitting his girlfriend and charged with simple assault and criminal mischief. Both charges were misdemeanors and were eventually dropped. "It was something that happened, an unfortunate situation, and right or wrong everybody makes mistakes," Harrison said following his first practice since re-signing with the Steelers on Tuesday. "I learned from it and grew from it and I'm where I'm at now." Before the Steelers brought Harrison back into the fold, head coach Mike Tomlin sat down and talked with Harrison on the current state of the league. The NFL has recently been rocked by scandals involving domestic violence, most notably cases including Ray Rice, Greg Hardy and Ray McDonald. Rice is under indefinite suspension and Hardy was placed on the exempt list, allowing him to be paid but not to participate with his team. McDonald is still playing for the San Francisco 49ers, who are waiting for the legal system to play out. Harrison could be active this Sunday against the Tampa Bay Buccaneers, although the amount of playing time he might receive is uncertain. Harrison won two Super Bowls in 2005 and 2008 with the Steelers, along with a Defensive Player of the Year award.

Best of the Batch Foundation is a nonprofit organization devoted to improving the lives of children and families in distressed communities by building character, self-esteem, and appreciation for education. Currently serving southwestern Pennsylvania, Best of the Batch Foundation does good works, such as scholarships to college-bound students and a yearly back-to-school BatchPack book bag distribution to kids and teachers. Best of the Batch also offers programming that couples sports with learning—such as the seven week summer basketball camp where participating boys and girls attend study halls where they read and discuss pre-selected books. Founded by NFL quarterback Charlie Batch in 1999, Best of the Batch Foundation embodies Charlie's values of dedication, leadership, and trust. Over the years, Charlie has leveraged his status as a Pittsburgh Steeler to attract and engage young people. Every program at Best of the Batch affirms the value of his personal motto--the 5 P's--in all aspects of life: proper preparation prevents poor performance. His passion is to unlock the potential of children and their families by providing resources to educate and empower them to give their best efforts in all they do...to be the best of the batch in life. He established Best of the Batch Foundation to assist local youth and hosts Project C.H.U.C.K, an annual youth basketball league in Homestead. I can vouch for the fact that Charlie loves kids.—Dan Rooney

LATROBE, Pa. -- James Harrison has sacked quarterbacks 71.5 times in his career. Never has he struck something quite like this. What started as an Instagram post over the weekend has mushroomed into a parenting discussion about whether sports participation deserves a trophy. When CNN gets involved, the story has transcended normal NFL fandom. Does sports participation deserve a trophy? Let the parental debate begin! @kellywallacetv http://t.co/DXhDMQG41y pic.twitter.com/jo6Juqum3Y — CNN Health (@cnnhealth) August 18, 2015 Like most things, there’s more to the Harrison trophy story. His sons were recipients of student-athlete awards for good grades as part of the Best of the Batch Foundation, run by Harrison’s former teammate, now-retired quarterback Charlie Batch. Upholding a certain standard in the classroom is part of the program. That’s not what drew interest. Harrison’s words on the topic were pointed, and they came at a time when the everyone-gets-a-trophy notion had bubbled to the mainstream. If Harrison hits send on this message two years ago, the reaction might have been tame. Not now. Harrison told ESPN on Sunday he's successfully returned the trophies but didn't answer follow-up questions as he entered a loud haze of autograph-seekers at training camp. Harrison is taking a stand on something to which on some level every parent can relate. There doesn’t seem to be mass outrage against Harrison’s stance, or at least I haven’t seen that. After following the story on social media, many take the stand of indifference while appreciating why Harrison might feel this way. After all, he was a former Kent State walk-on has been cut twice and played a season in NFL Europe early in his career. One fan outside Steelers’ practice Sunday yelled to Harrison, “I appreciate what you’re teaching your sons.” ESPN’s Mike Greenberg from Mike and Mike used the story line to draw attention to the need for kids’ participation in activities, whether they receive trophies or not. By snapping a picture and posting the trophies on Instagram with his own message, Harrison has stirred a debate about the concept of rewarding children in athletics and whether benchmarks should be appropriate for those rewards. There doesn’t’ seem to a right or wrong answer. This story is all about timing and resonance. It's also emblematic of how sports coverage has shifted. A story like this can draw more interest than player injuries and coaching changes because it affects people on a human level. It also elicits the sort of he-did-what reaction that social media is made for. Who knows if Harrison will go anywhere else with this story, though holding up a Lombardi in six months would be an entertaining response.

## Rethinking Participation Trophies: Rewarding True Achievement

Upon returning home, I discovered my sons had been awarded two participation trophies. While I am immensely proud of their efforts and deeply committed to nurturing their lifelong enthusiasm for activities, I have decided to return these awards. My reasoning is rooted in a fundamental belief: trophies should signify genuine achievement, not merely participation.

This decision is driven by a core principle that in all facets of life, rewards are earned through dedicated effort and accomplishment. My goal is to raise my sons without fostering a sense of entitlement. It's crucial they understand that simply trying their best, while commendable, doesn't always equate to an award. Instead, this realization should act as a powerful motivator for improvement, rather than a reason for complaint or a demand for unearned recognition. This perspective is a cornerstone of our family values.

### The Case Against Participation Trophies

Pittsburgh Steelers linebacker James Harrison recently made waves by announcing he's taking away the participation trophies from his two sons, aged 8 and 6. His reason? They hadn't "EARN[ed] a real trophy." This bold move highlights a growing debate about the prevalence of participation trophies in youth sports and their potential impact on children.

#### The Illusion of Effortless Success

For years, a prevailing notion has suggested that children should be shielded from the harsh realities of life, including disappointment and failure. This philosophy often leads to the widespread distribution of trophies and ribbons simply for showing up or trying. The idea is that constant praise and encouragement will foster a sense of self-worth. However, this approach may be doing more harm than good.

#### The Downside of Undeserved Recognition

Critics argue that awarding trophies for mere participation sends the wrong message. Instead of motivating children to improve and persevere, it can inadvertently teach them that simply showing up is enough. This can lead to a generation that expects rewards without genuine effort. As former NFL MVP Kurt Warner pointed out on Twitter, "They don't let kids pass classes 4 just showing up!"

#### Fostering True Growth Through Competition

Experts like Ashley Merryman, co-author of "Top Dog: The Science of Winning and Losing," emphasize that the true benefit of competition lies in improvement, not just winning. When children are constantly rewarded regardless of their performance, the focus shifts away from learning from mistakes and developing skills. Merryman states, "When you're constantly giving a kid a trophy for everything they’re doing, you're saying, 'I don’t care about improvement. I don’t care that you're learning from your mistakes. All we expect is that you’re always a winner.'"

#### A Disconnect from Reality

The abundance of participation trophies often found gathering dust in children's rooms suggests they hold little intrinsic value. These awards may be more about the parents' desire for affirmation and a misguided attempt to protect their children from the "cold, cruel world." However, real life is challenging, and no amount of undeserved accolades can prepare children for future disappointments.

Merryman, who has mentored Olympic athletes, wisely advises, "I like kids. I want them to be happy and do well. But I'd much rather have a 6-year-old cry because he didn’t get a medal than have a 26-year-old lose it because they realized they weren’t as special as they thought they were." Ultimately, the true rewards lie in learning the invaluable lessons of hard work, perseverance, and resilience.

James Harrison, the Steelers linebacker, has voiced strong opposition to participation trophies and the idea that "everyone is a winner." This stance has generated considerable debate. However, a closer look at the situation, particularly regarding trophies his children received from Charlie Batch's foundation, reveals a more complex picture.

### Understanding the "Best of the Batch" Trophies

James Harrison returned participation trophies given to his sons, expressing his disapproval of a culture that he believes overemphasizes participation over achievement. It's important to note that Harrison was aware of the nature of these specific trophies when he made the decision to return them. The trophies in question were inscribed with "Best of the Batch," indicating they were not simply for showing up. These awards originated from the foundation of former Steelers quarterback Charlie Batch, an organization dedicated to fostering academic achievement and effort in young people.

Charlie Batch's foundation focuses on providing opportunities for children, often emphasizing academic effort. Many of the young people supported by his foundation face significant challenges, including food insecurity. The foundation offers vital support services, such as after-school snacks, to ensure children can concentrate on their activities and studies without being hindered by hunger. The "Best of the Batch" program, which includes the Next Level Athletics Track Club, is designed to build academic development, self-esteem, and confidence. Initially, Batch's program aimed to provide constructive activities for children and keep them engaged in positive pursuits. While the program has grown to include a wider range of participants, its core values of effort and achievement remain central.

For children facing adversity, such as hunger or unstable home environments, receiving a trophy like the "Best of the Batch" represents a significant accomplishment. These awards are not merely tokens for participation; they serve as validation of hard work in both athletic and academic endeavors, offering tangible rewards. For many, these trophies can be the initial spark that inspires a lifelong commitment to effort and achievement. Rather than fostering a sense of entitlement, these awards can instill a powerful belief in a child's potential, reinforcing the message: "I'm good at school. I can be more. I can do more." Therefore, while Harrison's critique of participation trophies is valid in principle, the specific trophies his children received symbolized earned success and could be a crucial motivator for the young individuals served by Charlie Batch's foundation. For some recipients, the value of these awards could be as profound as a Super Bowl ring is to Harrison himself.

query: What did President Donald Trump say about the suspected chemical attack in Syria and whom did he blame for the situation?

documents.text:
President Donald Trump strongly condemned the suspected chemical attack in Syria, calling it an act that "cannot be ignored by the civilized world." In a statement released Tuesday, Trump linked the Syrian government's actions to what he described as the previous administration's failure to act.

Trump specifically criticized former President Obama's 2012 declaration of a "red line" against chemical weapons use, stating that "these heinous actions by the Bashar al Assad regime are a consequence of the past administration's weakness and irresolution." The Syrian government is suspected of carrying out the attack in a rebel-held area. The Syrian Observatory for Human Rights reported that at least 58 people died, making it one of the deadliest incidents in the six-year Syrian civil war.

In 2013, Obama had warned Assad about consequences for using chemical weapons. However, Obama ultimately decided against intervention, partly due to concerns that removing Assad could strengthen more extreme groups. The White House's current statement called the attack "reprehensible," but officials have not yet announced any specific actions. This position differs from Trump's own advice in 2013, when he urged Obama to "save your 'powder' for another day" and avoid military intervention.

Meanwhile, Senator John McCain, who has advocated for arming Syrian rebels, suggested that the Trump administration would share responsibility with Obama if the U.S. does not take action. McCain stated, "I want to hear him say we’re going to arm the Free Syrian Army, we’re going to dedicate ourselves to the removal of Bashar Assad." He added, "We will not sit by and watch chemical weapons being used to slaughter innocent women and children."

President Trump is flanked by Secretary of State Rex Tillerson to his left and Defense Secretary Jim Mattis, right, at the White House on Thursday. (Olivier Douliery/European Pressphoto Agency) President Trump released a statement Tuesday on the apparent use of chemical weapons that have killed dozens of people in Syria. It is full of sound and fury — at the Obama administration — and it signifies almost nothing. Here's what Trump said: Today’s chemical attack in Syria against innocent people, including women and children, is reprehensible and cannot be ignored by the civilized world. These heinous actions by the Bashar al-Assad regime are a consequence of the past administration’s weakness and irresolution. President Obama said in 2012 that he would establish a “red line” against the use of chemical weapons and then did nothing. The United States stands with our allies across the globe to condemn this intolerable attack. First off, the statement reads like something that you would put out in the heat of the campaign. Half of it is devoted to what the past administration did and didn't do. Certainly the Obama administration took heat — and most would say deservedly so — for not holding to its “red line” policy on Syrian President Bashar al-Assad using chemical weapons. But two full sentences — out of four? Second is the fact that Trump himself in 2013 urged Obama not to enforce that red line. To wit: AGAIN, TO OUR VERY FOOLISH LEADER, DO NOT ATTACK SYRIA - IF YOU DO MANY VERY BAD THINGS WILL HAPPEN & FROM THAT FIGHT THE U.S. GETS NOTHING! — Donald J. Trump (@realDonaldTrump) September 5, 2013 President Obama, do not attack Syria. There is no upside and tremendous downside. Save your "powder" for another (and more important) day! — Donald J. Trump (@realDonaldTrump) September 7, 2013 But even more conspicuous than that, the statement takes a harsh tone toward the Obama administration without saying what the Trump administration will do differently. The applicable Trump policy here, in fact, appears even less stringent than Obama's was: It's leaving Assad in power in the name of fighting the Islamic State (ISIS) first. As recently as last week, both Secretary of State Rex Tillerson and U.S. Ambassador to the United Nations Nikki Haley both signaled that Assad would be left alone. “Are we going to sit there and focus on getting him out? No,” Haley said. Tillerson said during a trip to Turkey, meanwhile, that he thought the “longer-term status of President Assad will be decided by the Syrian people.” Doctors and activists in rebel-held areas have blamed the Syrian government for a sharp increase in chemical attacks since the end of last year. (The Washington Post) Obama set a red line and then didn't live up to it; the Trump administration's policy is apparently to talk tough without dictating any specific policies — except that Obama's policies were wrong (even though Trump agreed with them in the end). The White House, of course, explains that it doesn't want to tip off the enemy before any action is taken. “I think the president has made it clear in the past, and will reiterate that today, that he is not here to telegraph what we're going to do,” press secretary Sean Spicer said earlier Tuesday. That's a great excuse for not having a real strategy, which the Trump administration doesn't seem to have.

(CNN) Survivors of a deadly airstrike in Syria have described chemical bombs being dropped from planes, in accounts that directly contradicted the Assad regime's version of a dawn attack that drew condemnation around the world. The strike, one of the deadliest of its kind since the Syrian war began six years ago, killed at least 70 people, including children. Syria denied it used chemical weapons. Russia asserted the deaths resulted from a gas released when a regime airstrike hit a "terrorist" chemical weapons factory on the ground. But survivors being treated in a hospital on the Turkish side of the border told a CNN team they saw chemical bombs dropped from the air. The World Health Organization said victims bore the signs of exposure to nerve agents, and Amnesty International said evidence pointed to an "air-launched chemical attack." International agencies are investigating the origin of the agents used in the strike. Key developments Chemical weapons expert: Russia's explanation of events is "highly implausible." US President Donald Trump said the attack changed his views on Syria and Assad. Medical experts said the attack was likely the result of a nerve agent, such as sarin gas. The UN Security Council held an emergency meeting but didn't vote on a resolution. War of words A child receives treatment Tuesday at an Idlib province hospital after a suspected chemical attack. Trump called the attack on innocent civilians an "affront to humanity," saying it had changed his views on Syria and Assad. "These heinous actions by the Assad regime cannot be tolerated," Trump said. "It crossed a lot of lines for me. When you kill innocent children, innocent babies ... with a chemical gas that is so lethal that people were shocked to hear what gas it was, that crosses many, many lines -- beyond a red line." The Russian Defense Ministry said on its Facebook page that a Syrian airstrike hit "workshops, which produced chemical warfare munitions" on the eastern outskirts of Khan Sheikhoun. It said "terrorists" had been transporting the chemical munitions from their largest arsenal to Iraq. "Nerve agents are the result of a very expensive, exotic, industrial chemical process -- these are not something you just whip up," said Kaszeta, managing director of Strongpoint Security, a security consulting firm based in London. "It's much more plausible that Assad, who's used nerve agents in the past, is using them again." Hours after the attack, several people were injured when an airstrike hit near a hospital in the same town. Survivors of the earlier attack were being treated there, the Aleppo Media Center activist group reported. The hospital was knocked out of service, said the Syrian Civil Defense rescue group, known as the White Helmets. Victim: 'I don't know if my family is dead or alive' Mazin Yusif, a 13-year-old-boy, broke down in tears at the Reyhanli Hospital in southern Turkey near the Syrian border. About 25 survivors of Tuesday's attack are being treated there, and several said they saw a plane drop chemical bombs. Mazin Yusif, 13, tells a harrowing story of being caught up in the apparent chemical attack. "At 6:30 in the morning, the plane struck. I ran up on our roof and saw that the strike was in front of my grandfather's house," Mazin told CNN. He said he ran toward his house and found his grandfather slumped over. He ran outside to call for help. "I got dizzy and then fainted in front of my grandfather's garage. I next found myself here in this hospital, naked in a bed." Aisha al-Tilawi, 55, says she lost three members of her family in Tuesday's attack. The boy's grandmother, Aisha al-Tilawi, 55, said she saw blue and yellow after the plane dropped a chemical-laden bomb. "We started choking, felt dizzy, then fainted. Mazin was trying to wake up his grandfather. Three of my family died," she said, lying in bed with an oxygen mask on her face. Ahmed Abdel Rahim, 31, says he has no idea of his family's whereabouts after the attack. Another survivor, Ahmed Abdel Rahim, 31, stared vacantly from his hospital bed while explaining he was hit with a poisonous substance carried by three rockets. "I was in my house. I had difficulty breathing, but I feel better now. But I did throw up after getting to the hospital. I don't know if my family is dead or alive. I don't know anything," he said. Worldwide condemnation WHO said some victims showed symptoms consistent with exposure to a category of chemicals that includes nerve agents. That conclusion was supported by Amnesty International, which said victims were "very likely" to have been exposed to a compound such as sarin. Photos: Suspected chemical attack in Syria Hide Caption 1 of 11 Photos: Suspected chemical attack in Syria This photo, provided Tuesday, April 4, by the activist Idlib Media Center, shows dead children after a suspected chemical attack in the rebel-held city of Khan Sheikhoun, Syria. Dozens of people were killed, according to multiple activist groups. Hide Caption 2 of 11 Photos: Suspected chemical attack in Syria A man carries a child at a makeshift hospital in Khan Sheikhoun. Activists have blamed the Syrian regime for the attack. In an apparent reference to the attack, two state-run Syrian media outlets reported that there was an explosion at a "rebel poison gas factory" in the countryside of Idlib province. The government has denied using chemical weapons in the past. Hide Caption 3 of 11 Photos: Suspected chemical attack in Syria A child's body is seen in Khan Sheikhoun. Hide Caption 4 of 11 Photos: Suspected chemical attack in Syria In this photo provided by the Idlib Media Center, doctors treat a child after the suspected attack. Hide Caption 5 of 11 Photos: Suspected chemical attack in Syria A baby's dead body is covered. Many of the casualties came as a result of asphyxiation, local doctors say. Hide Caption 6 of 11 Photos: Suspected chemical attack in Syria A dead baby is seen in the aftermath of the attack. "I've never seen anything like it, beyond description," said Fares al-Jundi, a doctor who spoke with CNN. He lives in a nearby village and rushed to the hospital after the airstrikes. Hide Caption 7 of 11 Photos: Suspected chemical attack in Syria Al-Jundi described how whole families were killed: They died of asphyxiation, and foam covered their mouths. Many died suddenly. "I believe this horrible memory will stay with me for the rest of my life," al-Jundi said. Hide Caption 8 of 11 Photos: Suspected chemical attack in Syria An unconscious Syrian child receives treatment at a hospital in Khan Sheikhoun. Hide Caption 9 of 11 Photos: Suspected chemical attack in Syria This photo provided by the Idlib Media Center shows victims of the suspected attack. Hide Caption 10 of 11 Photos: Suspected chemical attack in Syria Civil defense workers try to reduce the effects of toxic gas as they carry out search-and-rescue missions in Khan Sheikhoun. Hide Caption 11 of 11 The Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons said it was gathering evidence about the attack. Speaking at a high-level meeting in Belgium on the future of Syria, UK Foreign Secretary Boris Johnson blamed the Syrian regime. "All the evidence I have seen suggests that it was the Assad regime who did it, in full knowledge they were using illegal weapons in a barbaric attack on their own people," Johnson said. Assad's military has repeatedly denied using chemical weapons and consistently blames "terrorist" groups when chemical attacks are reported. But many of these are delivered through airstrikes, and no rebel or terrorist group in Syria is believed to have the capacity to carry out aerial bombardments. A UN investigation in August found that chemical weapons had been used in Syria, both by the national air force and ISIS militants. It found two instances where regime forces had used chlorine as a chemical weapon, and one where ISIS had used mustard gas between 2014 and 2015. The Syrian Coalition, an umbrella opposition group, compared this week's chemical attack to one in 2013 in eastern Ghouta "that the international community allowed to pass without accountability or punishment." A UN report found that sarin had been used on civilians in the earlier attack that activists said killed about 1,400 people. UN emergency meeting takes no action At an emergency meeting of the UN Security Council, Nikki Haley, the US ambassador, held up photographs of children foaming at the mouth as evidence of the attack. Syrian doctors treat a child following the suspected chemical attack Tuesday. "If Russia has the influence in Syria that it claims to have, we need to see them use it," said Haley, who is the council president. "We need to see them put an end to these horrific acts. How many more children have to die before Russia cares?" Haley hinted that the United States was open to using military action in response. "When the UN consistently fails in its duty to act collectively, there are times in the life of states that we are compelled to take our own action," she said. Russia, Syria's most powerful ally, made clear it would not support a resolution put forward by the United States, the UK and France condemning the use of chemical weapons within Syria and requiring the regime to provide flight logs from the day of the attack. Russia has used its veto power as a permanent member of the Security Council at least seven times on Syrian resolutions. On Wednesday afternoon, Security Council members left closed consultations without taking a vote on the draft resolution. No member spoke as they left the chambers. Syria's deputy ambassador to the United Nations, Mounzer Mounzer, flatly denied accusations his government is responsible, blaming "terrorist groups" for the dozens of deaths. "Syria also reaffirms that the Syrian Arab Army does not have any form or type of chemical weapons," he said. "We have never used them, and we will never use them."

## Trump Denounces Syrian Chemical Attack, Points Finger at Assad Amid Shifting U.S. Policy

President Donald Trump has strongly condemned the recent chemical weapons attack in Syria's Idlib province, calling the incident "reprehensible" and directly blaming Syrian President Bashar al-Assad. The attack, which tragically claimed numerous lives, including children, has intensified international scrutiny of U.S. policy in the region. While France has called for a more decisive American response, President Trump has not outlined specific retaliatory measures. He also criticized former President Barack Obama, suggesting that the Assad regime's continued use of chemical weapons was a consequence of the previous administration's "weakness and irresolution," specifically referencing Obama's unfulfilled "red line" warning against such actions. The Syrian military has denied any responsibility for the attack.

### Evolving U.S. Strategy and Global Reactions

The chemical attack unfolded as key U.S. figures, including Secretary of State Rex Tillerson and U.N. Ambassador Nikki Haley, signaled a shift in the administration's priorities. They indicated that the primary U.S. objective in Syria was the defeat of the Islamic State (ISIS), rather than the immediate removal of Assad. A senior administration official acknowledged that policy options were being evaluated but emphasized that these were limited and that the stated priorities remained unchanged. France's foreign minister suggested the attack might have been a deliberate test of the Trump administration's resolve and urged for greater clarity on U.S. policy concerning Assad's future. Representatives of the Syrian opposition, alongside U.S. officials, noted that crucial allies such as Saudi Arabia and Turkey also prioritized the fight against ISIS, viewing Assad's removal as a potentially destabilizing factor.

Despite the administration's stated focus on ISIS, the chemical attack has generated significant concern. A senior State Department official described the incident as "clearly a war crime" if confirmed, adding that the U.S. believes sarin nerve agent was used and that Assad's forces were "almost certainly" responsible. This official also pointed to Russia and Iran for their continued support of the Syrian regime. Middle East expert Aaron David Miller observed that President Trump's approach to Syria mirrors former President Obama's "risk aversion," underscoring the priority placed on defeating ISIS over ousting Assad. The U.S. Department of Defense has stated that it has no evidence of U.S. forces being involved in the attack.

**Syria Chemical Attack: Dozens Dead in Idlib Province, International Condemnation Mounts**

A devastating suspected chemical attack has claimed the lives of dozens of people in a rebel-held area of Syria, marking one of the most horrific incidents in the nation's six-year civil war. Activists reported that at least 83 individuals, including 25 children, perished in the assault in northern Idlib province, located southwest of Aleppo. An additional 350 people sustained injuries, according to the relief organization UOSSM, formally known as the Union of Medical Care and Relief Organizations. This non-profit coalition is dedicated to providing medical assistance to victims of the ongoing conflict in Syria.

The international community has reacted with widespread outrage and condemnation. U.S. President Donald Trump described the attack as "reprehensible and cannot be ignored by the civilized world." He directly attributed responsibility to the regime of Syrian President Bashar al-Assad, also criticizing the Obama administration's perceived "weakness and irresolution" in addressing the use of chemical weapons, which had previously been declared a "red line."

In stark contrast, the Syrian government has vehemently denied any involvement in the incident. State-run news agency SANA reported that the government asserted its compliance with the 1997 Chemical Weapons Convention, which prohibits the use of such weapons. Instead, the government pointed fingers at "armed terrorist organizations," claiming they were responsible for the attack. SANA further stated, "The Syrian Arab republic stresses that all those fabricated allegations will not prevent it from continuing its war on terrorism ... and from working for a political solution to the crisis in Syria."

The Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons (OPCW), the international body tasked with monitoring adherence to the chemical weapons treaty, has initiated a fact-finding mission to thoroughly investigate the events of Tuesday's attack. Early reports and visual evidence have emerged, adding to the gravity of the situation. The pro-opposition Idlib Media Center shared harrowing images on Facebook depicting children who appeared to be either deceased or incapacitated. The White Helmets, a volunteer rescue organization, released footage showing their members attempting to aid affected children by spraying them with water. While NBC News has not independently verified these images, government officials, including U.S. Secretary of State Rex Tillerson, have stated that the attack clearly involved the use of banned chemical weapons.

Secretary Tillerson highlighted the alarming frequency of such alleged attacks, noting that this was the third instance this month. He declared, "It is clear that this is how Bashar al-Assad operates: with brutal, unabashed barbarism." He further admonished those who support the Syrian regime, including Russia and Iran, stating, "Those who defend and support him, including Russia and Iran, should have no illusions about Assad or his intentions." Tillerson concluded with a strong statement on accountability: "Anyone who uses chemical weapons to attack his own people shows a fundamental disregard for human decency and must be held accountable."

Echoing these sentiments, British Foreign Secretary Boris Johnson issued a statement acknowledging that while certainty was not yet absolute, the attack bore "all the hallmarks of an attack by the regime which has repeatedly used chemical weapons." British Prime Minister Theresa May condemned the incident as "barbarism," and Israeli Prime Minister Binyamin Netanyahu expressed his shock and outrage via Twitter. U.N. Security Council President Nikki Haley, who also serves as the U.S. ambassador to the United Nations, announced that the Security Council would convene on Wednesday morning to discuss the attack.

Adding another layer to the complex narrative, a senior Syrian military officer, speaking to NBC News on condition of anonymity, denied the government's responsibility. "Syrian forces and its allies target terrorist groups, not civilians," the officer asserted, reaffirming the country's commitment to combating terrorism. This statement aligns with the Syrian government's consistent denial of using chemical weapons or chlorine gas, instead accusing rebel factions of employing such agents. It is important to recall that Syrian President Assad had previously agreed to dismantle his country's chemical weapons arsenal following international condemnation of a sarin nerve gas attack in the Damascus suburb of Ghouta in 2013, which resulted in hundreds of civilian casualties.

A senior U.S. State Department official, speaking on condition of anonymity, indicated that if the attack is definitively linked to the Assad government, both Russia and Iran would face significant repercussions. "Russia has said it had nothing to do with the airstrikes in Syria today, but that’s not the issue," the official explained during a conference call with reporters. "The issue is an apparent inability or unwillingness to hold the regime to its own commitments and to account."

The international response underscores the deep concern over the use of chemical weapons and the ongoing humanitarian crisis in Syria. The OPCW's investigation is crucial in establishing the facts and ensuring accountability for those responsible. The situation remains fluid, with diplomatic efforts underway to address the escalating violence and the potential for further international intervention. The tragic events in Idlib province serve as a grim reminder of the devastating consequences of the protracted conflict and the urgent need for a lasting political resolution. The international community's resolve to hold perpetrators accountable will be tested in the coming days and weeks as more information comes to light. The implications for regional stability and the future of Syria are profound, demanding a unified and decisive response.

The ongoing debate surrounding the Syrian conflict and the use of chemical weapons has been a persistent feature of international relations. Reports from organizations like the OPCW have previously documented the use of chemical weapons by various actors in Syria, including in 2015 when fighters were reported to have used them. These findings have fueled calls for greater transparency and stricter enforcement of international treaties.

Even figures who have previously advocated for different approaches to the Syrian crisis, such as U.S. Representative Tulsi Gabbard, have condemned the attack. Gabbard, who faced criticism for meeting with President Assad in January, described the incident as a "war crime" on Twitter. While she had previously urged the United States to cease its "regime change" efforts in Syria, she stated on Tuesday that whoever was responsible for the attack "must be held accountable," regardless of whether it was the Assad government. This sentiment reflects a broad consensus across the political spectrum regarding the unacceptability of chemical weapons use. The international community's commitment to justice and accountability remains paramount as the world watches for further developments in this tragic chapter of the Syrian civil war.

Turkish experts evacuate a victim of a suspected chemical weapons attacks in the Syrian city of Idlib, at a local hospital in Reyhanli, Turkey, Tuesday, April 4, 2017. A suspected chemical attack in a... (Associated Press) BEIRUT (AP) — The death toll from a suspected chemical attack on a northern Syrian town rose to 72 on Wednesday while activists and rescue workers kept finding more terrified survivors hiding in shelters near the site of the harrowing assault, one of the deadliest in years in Syria's civil war. According to a Syrian opposition group, renewed airstrikes hit the town of Khan Sheikhoun a day after the attack that the Trump administration has blamed on the government of President Bashar Assad, saying that his patrons, Russia and Iran, bore "great moral responsibility" for the deaths. The U.N. Security Council was to hold an emergency meeting On Wednesday in response to the strike and in Brussels, officials from 70 nations gathered for a major donors' conference on the future of Syria and the region. The attack on Khan Sheikhoun killed dozens of people on Tuesday, leaving residents gasping for breath and convulsing in the streets and overcrowded hospitals. Videos from the scene showed volunteer medics using fire hoses to wash the chemicals from victims' bodies. Haunting images of lifeless children piled in heaps reflected the magnitude of the attack, which was reminiscent of a 2013 chemical assault that left hundreds dead and was the worst in the country's ruinous six-year conflict. Syrian doctors said a combination of toxic gases is suspected to have been released during the airstrikes, causing the high death toll and severe symptoms. Pope Francis said Wednesday during his general audience that he was "watching with horror at the latest events in Syria," and said he "strongly deplored the unacceptable massacre." Earlier, President Donald Trump denounced the attack as a "heinous" act that "cannot be ignored by the civilized world." German Foreign Minister Sigmar Gabriel called on Russia to endorse a planned Security Council resolution condemning the attack. British Foreign Secretary Boris Johnson said "all the evidence" he had seen so far in the latest chemical weapons attack in Syria "suggests this was the Assad regime ... (that) did it in the full knowledge that they were using illegal weapons in a barbaric attack on their own people." Syria's government denied it carried out any chemical attack. But early on Wednesday, Russia, a major ally of the Syrian government, alleged a Syrian airstrike hit a rebel arsenal, releasing the toxic agents. The Russian Defense Ministry spokesman, Maj. Gen. Igor Konashenkov, said in a statement that Russian military assets registered the strike on a weapons depot and ammunition factory on the town's eastern outskirts. Konashenkov said the factory produced chemical weapons that were used in Iraq. Wednesday's renewed airstrikes hit not far from the location of the suspected chemical attack, said Ahmed al-Sheikho, of the Idlib Civil Defense team. He said the strikes did not cause any casualties because the area had been evacuated following Tuesday's attack. The Britain-based Syrian Observatory for Human Rights said 20 children and 17 women were among the 72 killed. Abu Hamdu, a senior member of the Syrian Civil Defense in Khan Sheikoun said his group has recorded 70 deaths. He said his team of rescuers was still finding survivors, including two women and a boy hiding in an underground shelter beneath their home.

## Chemical Weapons Attack in Syria Sparks International Condemnation

A devastating chemical weapons attack in northern Syria has drawn sharp criticism from world powers at the UN Security Council. Russia's assertion that civilians were poisoned by rebel weapons on the ground has been widely dismissed. Evidence presented by the UK's foreign secretary, a rebel commander, and a weapons expert points to an attack by the Syrian government, an ally of Russia. Meanwhile, international donors have pledged $6 billion in aid for Syria this year, with seventy donor nations discussing relief efforts in Brussels.

### What Happened in Khan Sheikhoun?

The Syrian Observatory for Human Rights, a UK-based monitoring group, reported that 20 children and 52 adults were killed in the chemical incident in Khan Sheikhoun, Idlib province. Footage from the aftermath shows civilians, including many children, suffering from choking and foaming at the mouth. Witnesses claim that clinics treating the injured were subsequently targeted by air strikes. Some victims received treatment across the border in Turkey. One woman described the experience: "We were affected by the gas. We couldn't stand up. I felt dizzy and sick. I suffer from shortness of breath. I couldn't breathe."

### Medical Evidence and Symptoms

The World Health Organization stated that some victims exhibited symptoms consistent with exposure to nerve agents. A team from the medical charity MSF, treating patients in Idlib, reported that symptoms were "consistent with exposure to a neurotoxic agent such as sarin gas." Sonia Khush, Syria director for Save the Children, noted that the widespread travel of victims seeking treatment made it difficult to ascertain the total number affected.

### Russia's Defense and Syrian Government's Denial

The government of Syrian President Bashar al-Assad denies its forces carried out a chemical weapons attack. Russia has acknowledged Syrian planes struck Khan Sheikhoun but claims the aircraft hit a depot manufacturing chemical weapons for militants in Iraq. Russian defense ministry spokesman Igor Konoshenkov stated that Syrian aviation struck a "large terrorist ammunition depot and a concentration of military hardware" on the outskirts of Khan Sheikhoun, adding that "on the territory of the depot there were workshops which produced chemical warfare munitions."

### UN Security Council Debates and Accusations

At the UN Security Council, the UK's ambassador, Matthew Rycroft, accused President Assad of "humiliating" Russia and undermining the peace process. He questioned Russia's plan to prevent further attacks, stating, "We had a plan and we had the support and you rejected it to protect Assad." Russia and China have previously blocked sanctions against Syria.

Russia's deputy ambassador, Vladimir Safronkov, countered by accusing the UK of being "obsessed" with overthrowing President Assad rather than pursuing peace. He argued that a new resolution was unnecessary and called for a "full, objective" international investigation, suggesting that much of the video evidence was "staged."

US Ambassador Nikki Haley warned that Syrian chemical attacks would persist if no action was taken, stating, "Time and time again Russia uses the same false narrative to deflect attention from their ally in Damascus." She hinted at potential unilateral US action, adding, "When the United Nations consistently fails in its duty to act collectively, there are times in the life of states that we are compelled to take our own action." French UN envoy Francois Delattre blamed the Assad government for the attack, calling it "destructive madness" and emphasizing the need for an "America that is seriously committed to a solution in Syria."

### Doubts Cast on Russia's Account

Hasan Haj Ali, commander of the Free Idlib Army rebel group, stated, "Everyone saw the plane while it was bombing with gas." Local journalists report no military positions within Khan Sheikhoun itself, but rather rebel groups controlling the surrounding area. Critics of Russia's explanation point out that reports of gas release emerged hours before the times cited by the Russian defense ministry.

### Expert Opinion and Historical Context

Chemical weapons expert Col Hamish de Bretton-Gordon described the Russian version of events as "pretty fanciful," deeming the idea of Sarin gas spreading after a weapons manufacturing site was bombed as "unsustainable." US President Donald Trump called the deaths a "terrible affront to humanity."

The Syrian government has faced previous accusations from Western powers of using chemical weapons, including Sarin, in Ghouta. While President Assad denied these charges, blaming rebel fighters, he later agreed to destroy Syria's chemical arsenal. Despite this, the Organisation for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons has continued to document the use of toxic chemicals in Syrian attacks.

### The Broader Syrian Conflict

Syria's civil war has resulted in over 250,000 deaths and, after more than six years, a political solution remains elusive. The UN reports that nearly five million Syrians have fled the country, with over six million internally displaced. EU foreign policy chief Federica Mogherini described the conflict as "the most complex and the most violent conflict in our times."

documents.text:
Goop, Gwyneth Paltrow's lifestyle and e-commerce site, has recently promoted coffee enemas as a method to "supercharge" an annual detox and begin the year with optimal health. Their latest guide for "deep detoxification" suggests using a device called the "Implant O'Rama" for administering coffee rectally at home. This product, priced at $135, is essentially a glass bottle with silicone tubing.

It's important to clarify that the concept of a "detox" is generally unnecessary for healthy individuals. Your body possesses natural mechanisms, primarily the kidneys and liver, to efficiently clear toxins. There is no scientific evidence to support the efficacy of DIY detoxes or cleanses in improving overall health. Conversely, there is substantial evidence highlighting the potential harms associated with coffee enemas and colon cleansing practices.

Despite this, colonic irrigation methods and the outdated notion of "auto-intoxication" (toxin buildup from food) have persisted for centuries. These practices encompass various forms, including tonics, specialized devices, and fad diets, and are often claimed to treat a wide range of ailments. These include allergies, alcoholism, asthma, constipation, mental disorders, and ulcerative colitis.

Coffee enemas, specifically, have a history dating back to the early 20th century and were once incorporated into alternative cancer therapies. The purported benefits often cited include the absorption of caffeine through the colon, stimulating the liver to eliminate toxins. However, these claims lack scientific validation.

The documented risks associated with coffee enemas are significant. There are reported cases of electrolyte imbalances leading to fatalities. Other documented harms include severe colon inflammation, burns, infections, sepsis, and rectal perforation.

In a 1997 editorial in the *Journal of Clinical Gastroenterology*, a British doctor characterized coffee enemas and similar colonics as "quackery," deeming their resurgence a "triumph of ignorance over science." This criticism has not deterred Goop, which has a history of endorsing questionable and potentially harmful health advice and products.

The manufacturer of the "Implant O'Rama," Implant O'Rama LLC, claims on its website that coffee enemas can offer relief from depression, confusion, nervous tension, allergy symptoms, and severe pain, while also lowering "serum toxins." However, these assertions are immediately followed by a disclaimer stating that such claims are "not necessarily" based on scientific evidence. The company explicitly notes that its products are not intended to "treat, diagnose, mitigate, prevent, or cure any condition or disease." ## Your Guide to a Beauty and Wellness Detox

We believe that what you apply to your body should meet the same high standards as what you consume. While any time is a good time to eliminate conventionally toxic products and embrace self-care that supports your body's natural detoxification systems, January is an ideal starting point, especially if you're following a beginning-of-the-year food plan. This guide offers everything your body needs to truly go clean, focusing on essential everyday skincare, and provides ideas to enhance your detox journey.

### Supercharge Your Detox Routine

While avoiding gluten is a common detox practice, incorporating a routine that includes sweat, stimulation, exfoliation, and deep detoxification is also crucial. We've curated a comprehensive detox package, allowing you to select the elements that best suit your needs.

### Clean Up Your Daily Routine

We prefer to avoid carcinogens, endocrine disruptors, and any potentially toxic ingredients on our skincare labels. For the products we use daily, we exclusively choose clean options, free from toxins. This section highlights top-tier clean beauty essentials for your face wash, lip balm, mascara, and more, along with valuable clean beauty resources and services.

### Perfect Your Personal Care Lineup

If we're mindful of toxins on our face, we're even more cautious about what we use on the rest of our body. This includes a personal care assortment that addresses every inch. As you stock up on favorites like Cocofloss or try a menstrual cup, consider supporting worthy nonprofits like Lava Mae, which transforms old buses into hygiene stations for the homeless in LA and SF.

### Replenish and Maintain Wellness

We are confident that a detox can leave you feeling refreshed and revitalized. To maintain this feeling of being 100 percent long after a cleanse concludes, a sustained commitment to gut health and a wellness-focused daily routine is essential. Here are ways to make this commitment easier and more enjoyable, including convenient daily vitamin packets.

The effectiveness and safety of colonics, a procedure designed to hydrate and irrigate the colon—the final five-foot section of your intestines—are subjects of ongoing discussion. Colonics involve filling the colon with warm water and repeatedly flushing it out. While proponents, like Dr. Alejandro Junger, a recognized expert in cleansing and digestive health, believe colonics are essential for waste elimination, particularly in the face of perceived modern toxin burdens, critics argue the colon is naturally equipped for self-cleansing.

**What is the colon's role in the body?**

According to Dr. Junger, the colon is the final segment of our digestive tract. Its primary functions include absorbing remaining nutrients and water into the bloodstream. Crucially, it also eliminates waste products, not just from undigested food, but also from the blood. In a process that reverses absorption, the colon transports substances from circulation for expulsion with feces. Furthermore, the colon hosts the majority of our intestinal flora, trillions of bacteria vital for detoxification, digestion, and immune system regulation, as detailed in various studies on gut microbiome health.

**What does a colonic procedure accomplish?**

In essence, a colonic assists in the elimination of waste material currently passing through the colon.

**Can colonics benefit gut health?**

Colonics can be beneficial when performed in the appropriate context by skilled practitioners, particularly during a detoxification cleanse or when an individual's colon struggles with elimination. During a cleanse, the colon may need to expel excess mucus. If this mucus is thick and sticky, it can adhere to the colon walls, obstructing waste removal and potentially leading to a blockage. In severe instances, this mucus can take on the colon's shape, resembling a thick layer of old paint. When a colonic is administered in such a situation, the expelled material, sometimes referred to as mucoid plaque, can appear tarry and black. While some believe this represents years of accumulated waste, Dr. Junger explains it is generally new material actively drawn from the bloodstream and deposited into the colon for elimination. Its stickiness can cause it to accumulate, forming a thick layer. Visual examples of mucoid plaque facilitated by colonics are widely available online.

**How often should one have a colonic, and when is it most beneficial?**

Dr. Junger cautions against the misuse of colonics. He primarily recommends them for individuals undertaking a cleanse, and only during the program, as the body may require faster expulsion of mucus-laden waste to prevent constipation. Certain individuals with specific medical conditions impeding colon elimination, often under the care of a gastroenterologist, may also benefit. Many people, however, receive colonics regularly outside of cleansing programs, often for temporary relief from bloating or constipation. This practice can be problematic, as it prevents addressing the underlying cause and may lead to reliance on colonics for waste elimination, potentially disrupting intestinal flora and negatively impacting health. Moreover, individuals with diverticula or other colon lesions, common in inflammatory diseases, risk perforation, a life-threatening emergency, from colonics. Dr. Junger personally vets colonic therapists he recommends, assessing their equipment, technique, and patient care.

**What differentiates a colonic from an enema, and are there at-home options?**

A colonic typically introduces water further into the colon than an enema, with modern colonic machines using pressure for greater reach and effectiveness. Enemas rely on gravity. In a closed-system colonic, water flows in and out continuously. With an enema, one typically fills, stops the flow for expulsion, and repeats. Some colonic methods, like the LIBBE system (an open system), function similarly to enemas with gravity-guided fluid and generally carry a lower risk of perforation. For those seeking an at-home option with the necessary knowledge, the Implantorama System is recommended.
