These statements prompted widespread fact-checking, with a consensus identifying Abu Musab Al-Zarqawi as the actual founder of ISIS. Al-Zarqawi led al Qaeda in Iraq before his death in a U.S. airstrike. The group later rebranded as ISIS under Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi, capitalizing on regional instability, missteps by both the George W. Bush and Obama administrations, the Syrian civil war, and Iraqi government failures to establish a global terror network.

By Friday morning, facing significant backlash, Trump employed a familiar strategy: a "walk-back." He tweeted, "Ratings challenged @CNN reports so seriously that I call President Obama (and Clinton) 'the founder' of ISIS, & MVP. THEY DON'T GET SARCASM?"

This explanation was met with skepticism from various figures. For instance, **Rep. Seth Moulton**, a Democrat and Iraq War veteran, stated on CNN's "New Day" that Trump "lies every single day and then the next day he just tries to claim it was sarcasm." **Former House Speaker Newt Gingrich**, an ally, commented on "Fox and Friends" that Trump's "imprecise language" is frustrating, noting he "sometimes uses three words when he needs 10."

However, the document highlights that "sarcasm" is a precisely defined term, meaning the use of irony to convey the opposite of what is literally said for rhetorical effect. Interpreting Trump's original statements as sarcasm would imply he was mocking his own assertions. Later that day, Trump continued to criticize the press, tweeting, "I love watching these poor, pathetic people (pundits) on television working so hard and so seriously to try and figure me out. They can't!"

Donald Trump has launched a campaign initiative seeking volunteer "observers" to prevent his Democratic rival Hillary Clinton from "rigging" the November presidential election. "Help Me Stop Crooked Hillary From Rigging This Election!" an apparently new page on the Republican candidate's official website says. "Please fill out this form to receive more information about becoming a volunteer Trump Election Observer." The form collects users' contact information, then directs them to a donations form. Those who sign up also receive an email promising that "we are going to do everything we are legally allowed to do to stop crooked Hillary from rigging this election."

query: What specific claims are being made in the lawsuit filed by Aaron Hernandez's estate against the NFL and the New England Patriots?

documents.text:
The NFL has responded to the news of the Aaron Hernandez lawsuit, with league spokesman Joe Lockhart addressing the matter during a media briefing. Lockhart emphasized that the discovery of Hernandez's "severe" case of Chronic Traumatic Encephalopathy (CTE) should not lead to him being viewed as a victim.

"His personal story is complex, it doesn’t lend itself to simple answers," Lockhart stated. He highlighted that Hernandez was convicted of homicide and that his documented behavioral issues predated his NFL career. "The real victims are the friends and family of those he killed, along with his young daughter," Lockhart added.

Regarding the CTE phenomenon, Lockhart stressed the need for further research. "It’s a very complicated puzzle," he said. "Every piece is important. . . There are a lot of dots here, and science just hasn’t been able to connect them."

On the subject of the lawsuit filed by Hernandez, Lockhart was firm. "We intend to contest the claim vigorously," he declared. While specific strategies were not detailed, potential arguments include the labor deal preventing such lawsuits. The league may also contend that Hernandez's rights were already addressed by the settlement of a class-action lawsuit, though it remains unclear if Hernandez was part of that class or opted out.

The National Football League and New England Patriots will fight hard to get the Aaron Hernandez estate’s concussion lawsuit dismissed, but they could have an incentive to settle if that strategy fails, according to legal experts. “The NFL will fight tooth and nail to get this case thrown out of court,” said Daniel Wallach, a prominent sports law attorney based in Florida, said Friday by phone. His comments came one day after lawyers for Hernandez’s estate filed a $20 million lawsuit against the league and the Patriots, on the grounds that both parties failed to protect Hernandez from the effects of chronic traumatic encephalopathy, or CTE, a brain condition linked to concussions and repeated blows to the head. Advertisement Wallach, who has advised top athletes and pro franchises, said the league probably will cite a number of factors in its bid to get the suit tossed, including the exclusion of any players who retired on or after July 2014 from signing onto a prior class-action concussion settlement expected to top $1 billion. Get Metro Headlines in your inbox: The 10 top local news stories from metro Boston and around New England delivered daily. Sign Up Thank you for signing up! Sign up for more newsletters here Hernandez was in jail awaiting his first murder trial in July 2014, and the league could argue he fell into the retired player category because he had “no reasonable expectation” of resuming his playing career, Wallach said. In addition, he said, the league will cite the full scope of Hernandez’s football career going back to his youth Pop Warner days, as well as his violent tendencies that predated his selection by the Patriots in the 2010 draft. The team released the once-promising tight end immediately after his June 2013 arrest for murder. Michael McCann, an attorney and founding director of the Sports and Entertainment Law Institute at the University of New Hampshire School of Law, agreed that Hernandez’s estate faces challenges. Advertisement “These are difficult cases to bring, that a finding of CTE proves liability against the NFL,” he said. At the same time, McCann added, the league might be tempted to settle if it can’t get the case dismissed, to avoid turning over documents in a protracted legal battle. “It’s still a potentially threatening lawsuit to the Patriots and the NFL,” said McCann, who is also a leading expert on the Deflategate saga. “We know the Patriots are known to be private about various issues.” So is the league, Wallach said. “Five years into this overall concussion litigation, one thing the league has been very successful at is keeping its executives off the witness stand, avoiding testimony under oath, and not having any of its so-called treasure trove of documents in the public eye,” Wallach said. Advertisement But a third expert, Andrew Brandt, said he doesn’t see a settlement on the horizon. “My sense is they would not settle, as they would have a lot of defenses around Hernandez’s lifestyle and his short career in the NFL compared to other levels of football,” said Brandt, who heads the Moorad Center for the Study of Sports Law at Villanova University. Hernandez was acquitted in April on charges of killing Safiro Furtado and Daniel de Abreu in a drive-by shooting in Boston in 2012. Five days after the acquittal, the 27-year-old Hernandez hanged himself in his prison cell at Souza-Baranowski Correctional Center. He was serving a life sentence for the June 2013 killing of Odin L. Lloyd. A judge later vacated Hernandez’s first-degree murder conviction in the Lloyd case because Hernandez had not exhausted his appeals at the time of his suicide. Bristol County prosecutors are challenging that decision. The league, citing Hernandez’s criminal history, vowed Friday to vigorously defend itself against the lawsuit. Material from The New York Times was included in this report. Ben Volin and Milton J. Valencia of the Globe Staff contributed to this report.

## Aaron Hernandez Diagnosed with Severe CTE, Boston University Study Reveals

A groundbreaking study by Boston University researchers has revealed that former New England Patriots tight end Aaron Hernandez was diagnosed with a severe form of Chronic Traumatic Encephalopathy (CTE) following his death. CTE is a progressive degenerative brain disease strongly linked to repeated head trauma, a condition frequently observed in athletes who have sustained concussions and sub-concussive impacts throughout their careers.

### Dr. Ann McKee's Examination Uncovers Significant CTE

Following his passing, Aaron Hernandez's brain was donated for scientific examination. Dr. Ann McKee, a leading neuropathologist at Boston University's CTE Center, conducted the analysis. Dr. McKee's findings confirmed that Hernandez suffered from Stage III CTE, representing a significant level of the disease on a four-stage scale. Visual evidence released by the Boston University research team highlighted early indicators of brain atrophy and a "severe deposition" of the tau protein. This abnormal accumulation of tau protein is a critical pathological hallmark of CTE.

### CTE: A Growing Concern in Contact Sports

The scientific consensus, supported by numerous studies including those from Boston University, indicates that CTE develops as a result of repeated blows to the head. This makes it a prevalent concern in high-impact sports such as American football, hockey, and boxing. Stage III CTE is often associated with the onset of cognitive decline and dementia.

The diagnosis in Hernandez's case tragically adds to an increasingly recognized pattern among former National Football League (NFL) players. Many have been diagnosed with CTE posthumously, often in conjunction with instances of suicide. Prominent examples include former NFL stars Junior Seau and Dave Duerson, both of whom died by suicide and were later found to have CTE. Research has also suggested a link between CTE and increased aggression and violent behavior. For instance, former Kansas City Chiefs linebacker Jovan Belcher, who tragically murdered his girlfriend before taking his own life, was posthumously diagnosed with CTE.

Aaron Hernandez died by suicide in prison in April 2017, while serving a life sentence for the 2013 murder of Odin Lloyd. The findings from the Boston University study provide further critical data in understanding the long-term neurological consequences of repetitive head trauma in athletes.

The brain scan came as a surprise even to researchers who for years have been studying the relationship between brain disease and deaths of professional football players. Aaron Hernandez, the former New England Patriots tight end and a convicted murderer, was 27 when he committed suicide in April. Yet a posthumous examination of his brain showed he had such a severe form of the degenerative brain disease C.T.E. that the damage was akin to that of players well into their 60s. It was, a lawyer for his family said, in announcing the findings on Thursday, “the most severe case they had ever seen in someone of Aaron’s age.” C.T.E., or chronic traumatic encephalopathy, has been found in more than 100 former N.F.L. players, some of whom committed suicide, according to researchers at Boston University.

The CTE made him do it. That’s the charge leveled Thursday in a federal lawsuit filed by the family of Aaron Hernandez. Chronic traumatic encephalopathy is what turned him into a homicidal maniac. I’m no doctor, but you don’t have to be a doctor to know CTE is a devastating mental condition. You also don’t have to be a doctor to smell a money grab. The amount here is $20 million, and the defendants are the Patriots and the NFL. The lawsuit’s filing coincided nicely with the news release from Boston University’s CTE. Center. It said Hernandez had an advanced form of CTE, which can only be diagnosed in an autopsy. It was “the most severe case they had ever seen in someone of Aaron’s age,” said attorney Jose Baez. Hernandez was 27. This is terrible news for the NFL, which was already getting scorned for not doing enough to prevent concussion-related damage. But at least most of its CTE victims were players who had long careers. Hernandez played 38 games for the Patriots. If young NFL players weren’t panicky about life-altering concussions before, they should be now. It’s a reach, however, to say Hernandez wouldn’t have broken bad if only the Patriots had been more caring. Then again, it was a reach to say Caylee Anthony drowned in the family pool and her grandfather George disposed of the body. But hey, that defense worked for Baez when he defended Casey Anthony. Sentinel file photo Casey Anthony Casey Anthony (Sentinel file photo) That tabloid-crazed trial might be upstaged by the mere sight of a court registrar telling Bill Belichick to raise his right hand and tell the truth, the whole truth and nothing but the truth. One question I’d like asked is of all the people who were posthumously diagnosed with CTE, why did only one start acting like a mob hitman? Maybe CTE aggravates a pre-existing condition. There is no doubt, however, that the condition existed long before Hernandez put on a Patriots helmet. His troubles date back to at least 2006, when his father died from complications following hernia surgery. By the time Hernandez got to Florida a year later, he was ready to detonate. “It was a rough process, and I didn't know what to do for him,” his mother, Terri, told USA Today. “He would rebel. It was very, very hard, and he was very, very angry. “He wasn't the same kid, the way he spoke to me. The shock of losing his dad, there was so much anger.” A 17-year-old Hernandez got in a bar fight and ended up busting a bouncer’s eardrum. He was also identified as the person who fired five shots into a car one night following a dispute over a gold chain necklace. The case quietly took a number of twists and turns, and the witness later recanted his testimony. Technically, the only blot on Hernandez’s record was a one-game suspension for a failed drug test. The USA Today story even referred to him as a “good kid (who) was headed down the wrong path.” Most people familiar with the routine criminality and covering up during Urban Meyer’s tenure might have a different description for Hernandez. Gary W. Green / Orlando Sentinel Florida tight end Aaron Hernandez (81) runs for a 28 yard pass completion during the second quarter of the Gators game against Charleston Southern at Ben Hill Griffin Stadium in Gainesville, FL Saturday, September 5, 2009. (Gary W. Green/Orlando Sentinel) ORG XMIT: 00124324A Florida tight end Aaron Hernandez (81) runs for a 28 yard pass completion during the second quarter of the Gators game against Charleston Southern at Ben Hill Griffin Stadium in Gainesville, FL Saturday, September 5, 2009. (Gary W. Green/Orlando Sentinel) ORG XMIT: 00124324A (Gary W. Green / Orlando Sentinel) The Boston Globe reported Hernandez failed as many as six drug tests at Florida. He left after three seasons, but NFL teams were so leery that a first-round talent wasn’t picked until the fourth round. “We stayed away,” one scout told CBSSports.com, “because we hated the people he hung out with and how trouble always seemed to find this guy.” Don’t be surprised if Baez adds Florida and the NCAA to the complaint. Heck, why not throw in Bristol Central High? The cynical answer is because there’s no money to make suing a high school. If CTE turned Hernandez into a murderer, however, who’s to say which blow to the head started the spiral? Baez will surely find an expert witness who will blame it on the Patriots and/or the Gators. But Hernandez would have been a thug if he’d never played a down after high school. Even the Casey Anthony jury could see that. David Whitley can be reached at dwhitley@orlandosentinel.com

The dots are awfully close to each other, daring you to connect them. Aaron Hernandez had Stage 3 CTE when he committed suicide, after years of inexplicable violent behavior, including at least one murder. It would be simple and easy to blame football for what happened to him. Dot: The hits to the head gave him brain damage. Dot: The brain damage made him paranoid. Dot: The paranoia made him violent. Dot: The brain damage eventually made him suicidal. Maybe all the dots really do connect in this case. Maybe just some of them do. But the truth is that we don’t know, and we may never know. There is so much—so much—we still don’t know about concussions. We know repeated head trauma can ruin a life, but not every brain reacts in the same way. We know there is a direct correlation between football and CTE. And we know that the NFL’s response to this issue, for many years, was to sweep it under the rug—and when that didn’t work, to buy bigger rugs. The CTE problem in football is real, and it is enormous. But it has also led to facts-based hysteria. We take what we know about football and CTE, which is obviously significant, and we apply it to every single player, every case of dementia, every football life gone awry. And it’s just not that simple. On the surface, the Hernandez case may seem like the clearest case of football-related head trauma causing a player to literally and figuratively lose his mind. After all, nobody went crazier than Aaron Hernandez, right? I would argue the opposite: the extreme nature of Hernandez’s transformation, and the early age when it began, makes his story even foggier than most. Hernandez’s widow is suing the NFL on behalf of his daughter. The suit claims that the NFL in general and the Patriots specifically were “fully aware of the damage that could be inflicted from repetitive impact injuries and failed to disclose, treat or protect him from the dangers of such damage.” Maybe Shayanna Jenkins and her daughter Avielle will triumph in court. I’m not going to speculate on that; my colleague Michael McCann is far more qualified and capable of examining the lawsuit than I am. I will say this, though: Pinning his suicide on the NFL makes for a good headline. It may be a winning legal strategy. But it’s a hard theory for me to buy in this case. Aaron Hernandez played 40 games in the NFL. He also played 40 games at the University of Florida, and he played a lot of football before he arrived at Florida. Even if you do pin his suicide on football, can you really isolate his NFL career as the cause? Hernandez’s behavior started to change radically when he was in high school, almost immediately after his father died. He showed signs of Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder, though it was never diagnosed. He hung out with a different crowd. At times he was distant, at others charming, and he seemed to go back and forth with frightening ease. We still don’t know the full extent of his drug use. He was investigated for a double shooting in Florida in 2007—when he was a college freshman. Character concerns caused him to fall in the NFL draft. Nothing in that last paragraph can be pinned on the NFL. Nothing. Of course, the thrust of the lawsuit is that the CTE made Hernandez suicidal, not that it made him a violent criminal. But can you really separate the two that easily? Hernandez took his own life while serving a life sentence for murder. He did it just after being acquitted in another murder trial. You can come up with a bunch of theories for Hernandez’s suicide that seem sensible on their own. He couldn’t stand to live the life ahead of him. He was filled with regret, though he didn’t show it publicly. Once he was acquitted, there were no battles left to win. He knew that dying while he was appealing his conviction would technically vacate his conviction, possibly shielding his wife and daughter from any civil suits. He had CTE. Or: We don’t know. Maybe someday we will. The link between football-related head trauma and CTE is a serious issue. The NFL deserves heavy scrutiny for its actions, both past and present. We should absolutely continue that conversation, but maybe it’s best, for now, to leave Aaron Hernandez out of it.

The tragic case of a 27-year-old former NFL player, who played 44 career games and whose brain exhibited characteristics of a 67-year-old man, compels a critical question: Will the league finally take meaningful action against Chronic Traumatic Encephalopathy (CTE)? Aaron Hernandez's attorney, Jose Baez, revealed that his deceased client, who died by suicide at 27, had the brain of a man nearing 70 with severe dementia. This revelation, while perhaps unsurprising given Hernandez's inexplicable decisions in his final years, underscores a recurring pattern: young, affluent athletes exhibiting erratic life choices often have underlying physical or neurological reasons. The urgent question remains: what concrete steps will be taken to address this?

The era of mere hand-wringing about football's role in CTE must end. While scientific understanding of CTE is still developing, and a comprehensive assessment of long-term impacts will take decades, sufficient evidence now warrants drastic measures. For over a decade, neurologists like Dr. Ann McKee at Boston University's CTE Center have diligently established a link between head impacts and the accumulation of tau proteins, the hallmark of CTE. Through extensive measurements of impact forces and meticulous examination of brain tissue, researchers have concluded that football bears significant responsibility for the premature cognitive decline observed in athletes. Even the NFL, through its Head, Neck and Spine Committee, has acknowledged this connection to some extent.

Despite widespread discussions about safer helmets and promoting intelligent tackling techniques, a fundamental issue persists: the cumulative trauma within players' helmets can transform rational individuals into those exhibiting severe behavioral changes. We are inundated with rhetoric and promises of brain study initiatives, yet decisive action to potentially save players' lives remains conspicuously absent.

In a notable development, the Canadian Football League (CFL) and its players' union recently announced the elimination of full-contact practices during the season. CFL Commissioner Randy Ambrosie stated to the CBC, "It was clear we had an opportunity to get this out and do something proactive for player safety." While this move might seem less impactful given that CFL teams already conduct a limited number of full-contact practices (comparable to the NFL's allowance of 14 per team per season), it signifies a commitment from the CFL and its players' union to proactively address the root causes of CTE. This is a message that the NFL has indicated it does not anticipate matching.

A common misconception is that CTE is primarily driven by high-impact collisions that result in players being knocked unconscious. This perception, solidified by early CTE discoveries in deceased football players and amplified by the film "Concussion," is being challenged by current research. The focus has shifted from these dramatic blows to the seemingly innocuous, repetitive head impacts that occur daily during practices and games. For instance, a study published in *Brain* by researchers at Boston University found CTE in 99% of former NFL players' brains they examined.

Observing a football practice, one might not immediately recognize the danger in players repeatedly bumping heads at half-speed or engaging in similar contact during games. However, each instance of hard plastic colliding with hard plastic can trigger trauma. While these initial injuries may be minor, they accumulate over time, potentially reaching dangerous levels. Scientists now suspect that this cumulative damage, often involving hundreds or even thousands of subconcussive hits per season, is a significant factor in CTE development.

The NFL's reluctance to fully embrace the CTE discussion is understandable, given the substantial financial implications. Litigation from former players has already cost the league close to a billion dollars in settlements. Jose Baez, Aaron Hernandez's attorney, announced on Thursday that he is filing a lawsuit against the Patriots and the NFL on behalf of Hernandez's young daughter, seeking damages for wrongful death. The league appears to operate with a defensive posture, employing extensive legal teams to counter potential lawsuits.

In some respects, the NFL's skepticism regarding the extent to which CTE affects its players is not entirely unfounded. Properly studying a disease that can only be definitively diagnosed posthumously will take years. The brains available for scientific study have often come from players like Hernandez, whose concerning behavior prompted relatives to consent to brain donation. Crucially, the brains of the thousands of former players who have lived seemingly normal lives without significant cognitive issues have not been subjected to the same level of examination. This self-selective nature of current CTE studies, as highlighted by researchers at the University of Pittsburgh, could potentially lead to misleading conclusions.

It is imperative to acknowledge that CTE cannot be the sole explanation for every negative outcome in a player's life. Sometimes, poor choices are simply poor choices. We cannot definitively state that CTE directly caused Aaron Hernandez to commit the murder of Odin Lloyd in 2013, the crime for which he received a life sentence. However, CTE is a tangible factor, and its implications are undeniably serious, as evidenced by the increasing number of former players diagnosed with the condition.

Addressing the risk of CTE will require more than simply reducing full-contact practices during the season. It may necessitate a fundamental re-evaluation of how football is played at all levels, potentially leading to the elimination of nearly all contact practices. The sport itself might need to undergo a complete reinvention. Nevertheless, the CFL's proactive step in confronting this devastating menace is a positive stride forward. While it may be a small step, it is a step nonetheless. The time has arrived to move beyond legalistic maneuvering and embrace a multitude of decisive actions to protect the health and well-being of athletes, as advocated by organizations like the Concussion Legacy Foundation.

## Aaron Hernandez and the Shadow of CTE: A Tipping Point for the NFL

The revelation that former New England Patriots tight end Aaron Hernandez suffered from severe chronic traumatic encephalopathy (CTE) at the young age of 27 has sent shockwaves through the National Football League. This finding, detailed in an autopsy of his brain, is particularly alarming because Hernandez died by suicide while incarcerated for murder, having spent his final years off the field. The severity of his CTE, described as Stage 3, with brain shrinkage and significant membrane damage, is a stark indicator of the disease's devastating impact.

### Unusually Severe CTE at a Young Age

A lawsuit filed by Hernandez's fiancée and daughter against the NFL and the Patriots highlights the unusual nature of his condition. The Boston University neuropathy report noted that Hernandez's CTE pathology was "unusually severe considering his age." This challenges the NFL's long-standing narrative, which often downplayed the link between football and CTE, especially when affecting older players whose cognitive decline could be attributed to various factors.

### The NFL's Evolving Stance on CTE

Historically, the NFL has navigated the issue of CTE by emphasizing the need for more research and pointing to other potential causes for neurological issues in former players. Even in cases like Junior Seau and Dave Duerson, who died in their 40s and 50s with brains ravaged by CTE, the league managed to deflect direct responsibility. The league's official stance, even after a study in the Journal of the American Medical Association found CTE in 110 out of 111 former NFL players, maintained that "many unanswered questions relating to the cause, incidence and prevalence of long-term effects of head trauma such as CTE" remained.

### Hernandez's Case: A New Reality for the NFL

However, the diagnosis of severe CTE in a 27-year-old like Aaron Hernandez presents a new and undeniable challenge for the NFL. The league invests significant resources in reassuring parents about the safety of youth football. But the news of Hernandez's condition will undoubtedly amplify parental fears, raising the unsettling question: if a player can develop such severe CTE before the age of 30, what is the long-term prognosis for young athletes today?

### Symptoms and the Path Forward

According to the CTE Society, individuals with Stage III CTE, like Hernandez, often experience a range of debilitating symptoms. These can include memory loss, impaired executive function, aggression, explosive behavior, difficulty concentrating, mood swings, depression, apathy, and cognitive impairment. While an NFL spokesman stated the league had not yet reviewed the lawsuit, the organization has historically attempted to delay confronting the full implications of CTE.

The NFL's past strategies of casting doubt on scientific findings and delaying research have proven insufficient. The key to safeguarding the future of football lies in understanding the root causes of CTE. This includes determining the threshold for head trauma, identifying when damage is most likely to occur, and understanding individual susceptibility. Aaron Hernandez's tragic death at such a young age may finally serve as the critical turning point for addressing CTE and repetitive head trauma. To ignore this reality would be not only deceitful but also profoundly delusional for the league.

query: What major executive actions is President Donald Trump planning to sign on his first day in office?

documents.text:
Donald Trump's presidency was set to begin with a packed agenda, but his team remained tight-lipped about the exact timing of his first-day actions. While Trump had campaigned on a detailed list of immediate priorities, his administration appeared to be adjusting the pace for some complex issues requiring negotiation with Congress or international leaders. However, other significant policy announcements were anticipated in the initial hours and days of his term.

### Trade Policy: Immediate Actions Planned

Transition spokesman Sean Spicer indicated that President Trump would soon issue two executive orders concerning trade. A key promise from Trump's campaign was to formally declare the United States' withdrawal from the Trans-Pacific Partnership (TPP) trade deal. He had strongly criticized the TPP, arguing it harmed American businesses and workers. Additionally, Trump pledged to announce his intention to renegotiate the North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA) or withdraw from the 23-year-old pact. Spicer stated, "I think you will see those happen very shortly."

### Energy and Cybersecurity Initiatives

Early action was also expected in the energy sector, with plans to roll back regulations on oil drilling and coal production. In cybersecurity, Trump had already committed to requesting a report on the nation's cyber defenses within 90 days of taking office.

### Immigration Policy: A Complex Challenge

Trump had also made broad promises to immediately reverse President Barack Obama's immigration policies. However, some of these vows presented significant implementation challenges. The president-elect viewed Monday as his administration's first major workday. He had previously mentioned that his team had planned "some pretty good signings on Monday and Tuesday and Wednesday and Thursday and Friday, and then, also the next week."

### Cabinet Nominations and Executive Orders

The real estate mogul was expected to sign some initial paperwork on Friday, including formally nominating members of his Cabinet for Senate confirmation. The transition team also indicated that executive orders, both logistical and agenda-driven, might be signed to officially launch his administration.

Vice President-elect Mike Pence, who chaired the transition team, highlighted the structured approach: "Specifically we've focused in the president-elect's direction on a Day One, Day 100 and Day 200 action plan for keeping our word to the American people and putting the president-elect's promises into practice." He added, "We are all ready to go to work. We can't wait to get to work for the American people."

### Key "Day One" Promises

Trump's initial agenda included ambitious proposals such as:

*   Proposing a constitutional amendment for congressional term limits.
*   Implementing a hiring freeze for federal workers.
*   Beginning the process of removing criminal immigrants residing in the country unlawfully.
*   "Cancel every unconstitutional executive action, memorandum and order issued by President Obama."

This last point could lead to substantial policy shifts, including the potential cancellation of the Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals (DACA) program. DACA has provided protection from deportation and work permits to approximately 750,000 young immigrants. Trump could choose to immediately end deportation protection and revoke work permits, or halt new enrollments while allowing existing permits to expire.

### Enforcement of Immigration Policies

Trump had stated his intention to prioritize immigration enforcement on criminals, estimating this group could number between 2 million and 3 million people. He pledged to "move criminal aliens out day one" through coordinated efforts with state, local, and federal authorities. However, this promise faced practical hurdles, including jurisdictional objections from some areas to assisting with federal immigration enforcement and a shortage of detention space. The government has capacity for 34,000 immigration detainees, but recent arrests at the Mexican border had pushed detentions above 40,000.

### Shifting Stance on China

Initially, Trump had planned to label China a currency manipulator on his first day. However, in a recent interview, he indicated a change of approach, stating, "I would talk to them first."

### Administrative and Judicial Priorities

The potential for a more measured pace in some areas might stem from the ongoing process of finalizing his administration. Cabinet confirmations and agency staffing were still in progress, with key positions like the chairman of the Council of Economic Advisers remaining unfilled. Instead of immediate policy unveilings, the transition period included meetings with prominent CEOs and highlighting job creation announcements. Trump's team had also been reviewing Obama's executive actions to identify those that could be quickly rescinded. A significant task awaiting the administration was the nomination of a Supreme Court justice to fill the vacancy left by the late Justice Antonin Scalia, with Trump indicating a nominee would be announced in about two weeks.

Congressional Republicans, who had seen their agenda stalled by Obama's administration, welcomed Trump's promises of swift changes. Pence and other Trump aides had shared initial plans with House leadership, signaling an intent to quickly dismantle Obama's legacy. Representative Steve King (R-Iowa) expressed the sentiment, stating, "There is a call of history and that calls us to put America back on the rails again... and we've now been delivered the tools to do this by the voters."

*Associated Press writer Alicia A. Caldwell contributed to this report.*

Donald Trump Donald John TrumpAnalyst says Trump's base will support him if he backs off wall funding demand 'Green Book' writer apologizes for Islamophobic tweet: 'I will do better' Poll finds Trump's approval rating at 44 percent amid shutdown MORE is ready to take an ax to government spending. Staffers for the Trump transition team have been meeting with career staff at the White House ahead of Friday’s presidential inauguration to outline their plans for shrinking the federal bureaucracy, The Hill has learned. ADVERTISEMENT The changes they propose are dramatic.The departments of Commerce and Energy would see major reductions in funding, with programs under their jurisdiction either being eliminated or transferred to other agencies. The departments of Transportation, Justice and State would see significant cuts and program eliminations.The Corporation for Public Broadcasting would be privatized, while the National Endowment for the Arts and National Endowment for the Humanities would be eliminated entirely.Overall, the blueprint being used by Trump’s team would reduce federal spending by $10.5 trillion over 10 years.The proposed cuts hew closely to a blueprint published last year by the conservative Heritage Foundation, a think tank that has helped staff the Trump transition.Similar proposals have in the past won support from Republicans in the House and Senate, who believe they have an opportunity to truly tackle spending after years of warnings about the rising debt.Many of the specific cuts were included in the 2017 budget adopted by the conservative Republican Study Committee (RSC), a caucus that represents a majority of House Republicans. The RSC budget plan would reduce federal spending by $8.6 trillion over the next decade.Two members of Trump’s transition team are discussing the cuts at the White House budget office: Russ Vought, a former aide to Vice President-electand the former executive director of the RSC, and John Gray, who previously worked for Pence, Sen.(R-Ky.) and Speaker(R-Wis.) when Ryan headed the House Budget Committee.Vought and Gray, who both worked for the Heritage Foundation, are laying the groundwork for the so-called skinny budget — a 175- to 200-page document that will spell out the main priorities of the incoming Trump administration, along with summary tables. That document is expected to come out within 45 days of Trump taking office.The administration’s full budget, including appropriations language, supplementary materials and long-term analysis, is expected to be released toward the end of Trump’s first 100 days in office, or by mid- to late April.Rep. Mick Mulvaney (R-S.C.), Trump’s choice to head the Office of Management and Budget, has not yet weighed in on the proposed spending reforms because he is still awaiting confirmation by the Senate.Mulvaney voted for the RSC budget offered as a more conservative alternative to the main House Republican budget in 2015. The House did not vote on the RSC budget for fiscal year 2017.The preliminary proposals from the White House budget office will be shared with federal departments and agencies soon after Trump takes the oath of office Friday, and could provoke an angry backlash.Trump’s Cabinet picks have yet to be apprised of the reforms, which would reduce resources within their agencies.The budget offices of the various departments will have the chance to review the proposals, offer feedback and appeal for changes before the president’s budget goes to Congress.It’s not clear whether Trump’s first budget will include reforms to Social Security or Medicare, two major drivers of the federal deficit.Trump vowed during the campaign not to cut Medicare and Social Security, a pledge that Rep. Tom Price (R-Ga.), his pick to head the Department of Health and Human Services, told lawmakers in testimony Wednesday has not changed.Yet it could be very difficult to reduce U.S. debt without tackling the entitlement programs. Conservative House budgets have repeatedly included reforms to Medicare and Social Security, arguing they are necessary to save the programs.The presidential budget is important in setting policy and laying out the administration’s agenda, though Congress would be responsible for approving a federal budget and appropriating funds.Moving Trump’s budget through Congress could be difficult. In 2015, with the GOP in control of the House, the RSC budget failed by a vote of 132 to 294.Moderate Republicans and Democrats on the Appropriations Committee are likely to push back at some of the cuts being considered by Trump.But they seem likely to have the support of Mulvaney, a conservative budget hawk who backed the RSC budget.“Mick Mulvaney and his colleagues at the Republican Study Committee when they crafted budgets over the years, they were serious,” said a former congressional aide. “Mulvaney didn’t take this OMB position to just mind the store.”“He wants to make significant, fundamental changes to the structure of the president’s budget, and I expect him to do that with Vought and Gray putting the meat on the bones,” the source added.The Heritage blueprint used as a basis for Trump’s proposed cuts calls for eliminating several programs that conservatives label corporate welfare programs: the Minority Business Development Agency, the Economic Development Administration, the International Trade Administration and the Manufacturing Extension Partnership. The total savings from cutting these four programs would amount to nearly $900 million in 2017.At the Department of Justice, the blueprint calls for eliminating the Office of Community Oriented Policing Services, Violence Against Women Grants and the Legal Services Corporation and for reducing funding for its Civil Rights and its Environment and Natural Resources divisions.At the Department of Energy, it would roll back funding for nuclear physics and advanced scientific computing research to 2008 levels, eliminate the Office of Electricity, eliminate the Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy and scrap the Office of Fossil Energy, which focuses on technologies to reduce carbon dioxide emissions.Under the State Department’s jurisdiction, funding for the Overseas Private Investment Corporation, the Paris Climate Change Agreement and the United Nations’ Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change are candidates for elimination.Conservatives allied with fiscal hawks such as Pence, Paul and the Heritage Foundation say the time is long past due to get serious about cutting the federal deficit.“The Trump Administration needs to reform and cut spending dramatically, and targeting waste like the National Endowment for the Arts and National Endowment for the Humanities would be a good first step in showing that the Trump Administration is serious about radically reforming the federal budget,” said Brian Darling, a former aide to Paul and a former staffer at the Heritage Foundation.

WASHINGTON (Reuters) - Donald Trump is preparing to sign executive actions on his first day in the White House on Friday to take the opening steps to crack down on immigration, build a wall on the U.S.-Mexican border and roll back outgoing President Barack Obama’s policies. President-elect Donald Trump salutes the statue of Abraham Lincoln as he and his wife Melania take part in a Make America Great Again welcome concert in Washington. REUTERS/Jonathan Ernst Trump, a Republican elected on Nov. 8 to succeed Democrat Obama, arrived in Washington on a military plane with his family a day before he will be sworn in during a ceremony at the U.S. Capitol. Aides said Trump would not wait to wield one of the most powerful tools of his office, the presidential pen, to sign several executive actions that can be implemented without the input of Congress. “He is committed to not just Day 1, but Day 2, Day 3 of enacting an agenda of real change, and I think that you’re going to see that in the days and weeks to come,” Trump spokesman Sean Spicer said on Thursday, telling reporters to expect activity on Friday, during the weekend and early next week. MORE FROM REUTERS * Trumponomics may be wrong medicine for U.S. economy today * Graphic: The presidential touch on markets * Politics Live: Latest on Trump's inauguration Trump plans on Saturday to visit the headquarters of the CIA in Langley, Virginia. He has harshly criticized the agency and its outgoing chief, first questioning the CIA’s conclusion that Russia was involved in cyber hacking during the U.S. election campaign, before later accepting the verdict. Trump also likened U.S. intelligence agencies to Nazi Germany. Trump’s advisers vetted more than 200 potential executive orders for him to consider signing on healthcare, climate policy, immigration, energy and numerous other issues, but it was not clear how many orders he would initially approve, according to a member of the Trump transition team who was not authorized to talk to the press. Signing off on orders puts Trump, who has presided over a sprawling business empire but has never before held public office, in a familiar place similar to the CEO role that made him famous, and will give him some early victories before he has to turn to the lumbering process of getting Congress to pass bills. The strategy has been used by other presidents, including Obama, in their first few weeks in office. “He wants to show he will take action and not be stifled by Washington gridlock,” said Princeton University presidential historian Julian Zelizer. Trump is expected to impose a federal hiring freeze and take steps to delay a Labor Department rule due to take effect in April that would require brokers who give retirement advice to put their clients’ best interests first. He also will give official notice he plans to withdraw from the 12-nation Trans-Pacific Partnership trade deal and renegotiate the North American Free Trade Agreement with Canada and Mexico, Spicer said. “I think you will see those happen very shortly,” Spicer said. Obama, ending eight years as president, made frequent use of his executive powers during his second term in office, when the Republican-controlled Congress stymied his efforts to overhaul immigration and environmental laws. Many of those actions are now ripe targets for Trump to reverse. BORDER WALL Trump is expected to sign an executive order in his first few days to direct the building of a wall on the southern border with Mexico, and actions to limit the entry of asylum seekers from Latin America, among several immigration-related steps his advisers have recommended. That includes rescinding Obama’s order that allowed more than 700,000 people brought into the United States illegally as children to stay in the country on a two-year authorization to work and attend college, according to several people close to the presidential transition team. It is unlikely Trump’s order will result in an immediate roundup of these immigrants, sources told Reuters. Rather, he is expected to let the authorizations expire. The issue could set up a confrontation with Obama, who told reporters on Wednesday he would weigh in if he felt the new administration was unfairly targeting those immigrants. Advisers to Trump expect him to put restrictions on people entering the United States from certain countries until a system for “extreme vetting” for Islamist extremists can be set up. During his presidential campaign, Trump proposed banning non-American Muslims from entering the United States, but his executive order regarding immigration is expected to be based on nationality rather than religion. Another proposed executive order would require all Cabinet departments to disclose and pause current work being done in connection with Obama’s initiatives to curb carbon emissions to combat climate change. Trump also is expected to extend prohibitions on future lobbying imposed on members of his transition team. ‘THE HIGHEST IQ’ Washington was turned into a virtual fortress ahead of the inauguration, with police ready to step in to separate protesters from Trump supporters at any sign of unrest. As Obama packed up to leave the White House, Trump and his family laid a wreath at the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier in Arlington National Cemetery and attended a concert at the Lincoln Memorial. Trump spoke earlier to lawmakers and Cabinet nominees at a luncheon in a ballroom at his hotel, down the street from the White House, announcing during brief remarks that he would pick Woody Johnson, owner of the New York Jets of the National Football League, as U.S. ambassador to Britain. “We have a lot of smart people. I tell you what, one thing we’ve learned, we have by far the highest IQ of any Cabinet ever assembled,” Trump said. Trump has selected all 21 members of his Cabinet, along with six other key positions requiring Senate confirmation. The Senate is expected on Friday to vote to confirm retired General James Mattis, Trump’s pick to lead the Pentagon, and retired General John Kelly, his homeland security choice. Senate Republicans had hoped to confirm as many as seven Cabinet members on Friday, but Democrats balked at the pace. Trump spokesman Spicer accused Senate Democrats of “stalling tactics.” Also in place for Monday will be 536 “beachhead team members” at government agencies, Vice President-elect Mike Pence said, a small portion of the thousands of positions Obama’s appointees will vacate. Slideshow (3 Images) Trump has asked 50 Obama staffers in critical posts to stay on until replacements can be found, including Deputy Defense Secretary Robert Work and Brett McGurk, envoy to the U.S.-led coalition fighting Islamic State. The list includes Adam Szubin, who has long served in an “acting” capacity in the Treasury Department’s top anti-terrorism job because his nomination has been held up by congressional Republicans since Obama named him to the job in April 2015. The Supreme Court said U.S. Chief Justice John Roberts, who will administer the oath of office on Friday, met with Trump on Thursday to discuss inauguration arrangements.

Donald Trump's presidency faces a crucial test: can his administration translate campaign promises into tangible policy achievements that resonate with the American public? Significant challenges lie ahead. For instance, the promised U.S.-Mexico border wall currently lacks dedicated funding. While repealing the Affordable Care Act (ACA) is a stated goal, Republicans remain divided on a replacement strategy. Similarly, withdrawing from existing trade deals is procedurally simple, but negotiating new ones on Trump's terms will require a level of diplomatic skill that has not been consistently demonstrated.

Numerous unpredictable hurdles could impede policy success. From tax reform to national security and economic uncertainties, Trump's campaign pledges have already encountered resistance from within his own party and even from his prospective cabinet members. While the first 100 days serve as an arbitrary benchmark, an assessment of the policy landscape reveals key ideas, influential leaders, and significant obstacles that will shape the new administration's early performance.

### Healthcare: A Divided Path

**Vision:** President Trump has vowed to repeal and replace the Affordable Care Act (ACA), which he has labeled a "disaster," promising "cheaper and better insurance for everybody." However, his approach differs from many congressional Republicans who aim to partially privatize Medicare. He does align with Republicans on converting Medicaid into block grants to states, a move that Democrats strongly oppose. Furthermore, Trump supports allowing government health programs to negotiate drug prices, a stance that could alienate both Republicans and the powerful pharmaceutical industry.

**Key Leaders:** Donald Trump, Vice President Mike Pence, House Speaker Paul Ryan, HHS Secretary-designate Tom Price, and CMS Administrator-designate Seema Verma.

**First 100 Days Outlook:** Trump has pledged to initiate executive actions on day one to begin dismantling the ACA. Health officials anticipate near-term measures to stabilize ACA exchanges during the transition period. Congressional action on repeal and replace legislation remains uncertain, with Trump indicating his administration would present its own plan shortly after his HHS secretary is confirmed.

**Obstacles:** Crafting a healthcare plan that satisfies conservative Republicans' desire for reduced federal involvement while securing the necessary Senate votes (at least eight Democrats) will be a significant challenge. This difficulty is compounded by the risk of alienating his base by potentially removing health coverage from millions of Americans.

### Trade: Renegotiating Global Deals

**Vision:** Trump aims to revitalize U.S. manufacturing, strengthen trade deal enforcement, and penalize companies that move jobs abroad. Key priorities include withdrawing from the Trans-Pacific Partnership (TPP) and renegotiating the North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA). His administration views trade as beneficial but emphasizes the need for stronger, preferably bilateral, agreements. With a focus on challenging China's trade practices, Trump intends to address tariffs, subsidies, and other barriers to fair trade. He also seeks to reduce the U.S. trade deficit by increasing exports and decreasing imports, and plans to impose tariffs on goods from companies relocating production overseas.

**Key Leaders:** Trade attorney Robert Lighthizer (U.S. Trade Representative), economics professor Peter Navarro (National Trade Council), and investor Wilbur Ross (Commerce Secretary). Ross is expected to be the primary architect of Trump's trade agenda, with Lighthizer traditionally leading negotiations. Jason Greenblatt will serve as "special representative for international negotiations," and Jared Kushner will also focus on trade deals.

**First 100 Days Outlook:** Trump plans to withdraw from the TPP on day one. Renegotiating NAFTA will follow, though specific proposals remain undefined. Trump has also expressed interest in a bilateral trade agreement with the United Kingdom, contingent on its exit from the EU. He may direct Treasury Secretary-designate Steven Mnuchin to label China a currency manipulator. Proposals for a "border tax" and a 35% tariff on goods from companies moving production offshore are also gaining traction.

**Obstacles:** While the executive branch possesses significant trade authority, congressional oversight and potential international retaliation against punitive tariffs pose challenges. NAFTA renegotiations will depend on Canada and Mexico's agreement, with Trump threatening to withdraw if his terms are not met.

### Tax Reform: Overhauling the Code

**Vision:** Republicans, now in control of Washington, have outlined a plan for tax code overhaul. Shared goals include lowering tax rates for individuals and corporations, boosting U.S. production, and eliminating many current tax incentives.

**Key Leaders:** Donald Trump, Treasury Secretary-designate Steven Mnuchin, NEC Director Gary Cohn, Speaker Paul Ryan, House Ways and Means Chairman Kevin Brady, and Senate Finance Chairman Orrin Hatch.

**First 100 Days Outlook:** Initial discussions suggested swift tax reform, but it is now clear that addressing healthcare will take precedence. Tax reform efforts will likely follow the ACA debate.

**Obstacles:** Disagreements have already emerged, particularly regarding the House GOP's "border adjustability" proposal, which taxes imports while exempting exports. Tax reform inherently creates winners and losers, and opposition from those negatively impacted is expected as details emerge.

### Jobs: Restoring Manufacturing

**Vision:** Trump campaigned as a champion of the working class, promising to restore manufacturing and coal jobs. His platform projected 25 million new jobs over a decade. Post-election, he has cited company announcements, such as those from United Technologies and General Motors, as evidence of job creation. He has threatened a 35% border tax on companies moving production abroad.

**Key Leaders:** Labor Secretary-designate Andrew Puzder, CEO of CKE Restaurants, who has opposed overtime rule expansions and has been hesitant to raise the minimum wage.

**First 100 Days Outlook:** Trump is likely to prioritize tax cuts and deregulation, which have strong GOP support. Discussions are also underway regarding maternity leave and childcare proposals.

**Obstacles:** Puzder's confirmation faces opposition from labor and women's groups. Trump's anti-trade and immigration policies may also face resistance from some congressional Republicans.

### Immigration: Securing the Border

**Vision:** Trump's signature promises include building a U.S.-Mexico border wall and deporting millions of undocumented immigrants. These are among his most challenging pledges. The border wall carries a multi-billion dollar price tag and faces Republican opposition. Deporting up to 3 million immigrants with criminal records is also a significant undertaking. The future of President Obama's Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals (DACA) program, which protects over 752,000 young undocumented immigrants, remains uncertain.

**Key Leaders:** Speaker Paul Ryan, Homeland Security Secretary-designate John Kelly, and Attorney General-designate Jeff Sessions. Kelly has adopted less extreme positions on border security and deportations than Trump's past statements.

**First 100 Days Outlook:** Trump could leverage the 2006 Secure Fence Act to fund border barriers. He may also reverse Obama-era enforcement policies that prioritized serious criminals, potentially leading to mass arrests and detentions of undocumented immigrants. The fate of DACA is also an immediate decision.

**Obstacles:** While increased border security and deportations could begin quickly, addressing broader immigration issues legislatively requires a comprehensive vision beyond walls and deportations, which Trump has not publicly articulated.

### National Security: A Stronger Military

**Vision:** Trump advocates for "peace through strength" with a significant military buildup, including expanding the Army, Navy, Air Force, and Marines, and modernizing the nuclear arsenal. He has called for a 30-day plan to defeat ISIS and suggested closer cooperation with Russia on counter-terrorism.

**Key Leaders:** National Security Adviser-designate Michael Flynn, Defense Secretary-designate James Mattis, and Secretary of State-designate Rex Tillerson. Mattis has expressed differing views from Trump on Russia and the Iran nuclear deal. Tillerson's lack of government experience and ties to Russia have drawn scrutiny.

**First 100 Days Outlook:** The administration will likely submit a supplemental defense budget request and its fiscal year 2018 budget proposal. Numerous high-level national security positions still need to be filled.

**Obstacles:** Pentagon spending caps imposed by the 2011 Budget Control Act are a direct impediment to Trump's buildup plans, requiring 60 Senate votes to repeal. Any reset in relations with Russia will face intense bipartisan scrutiny.

### Climate: Skepticism and Rollbacks

**Vision:** Trump has expressed skepticism about climate change science, calling it a "hoax." His administration is expected to roll back Obama-era environmental regulations.

**Key Leaders:** EPA Administrator-designate Scott Pruitt, Secretary of State-designate Rex Tillerson, and Energy Secretary-designate Rick Perry. Pruitt has a history of challenging EPA regulations. Tillerson's role will be crucial in determining U.S. participation in the Paris Agreement. Perry has previously advocated for dismantling the Energy Department.

**First 100 Days Outlook:** The EPA could begin withdrawing carbon rules for power plants. Conservatives are pushing for immediate withdrawal from global climate pacts like the Paris Agreement. The administration may also alter methods for calculating the economic benefits of greenhouse gas reductions.

**Obstacles:** There appears to be no consensus within Trump's inner circle on climate change. The administrative process to dismantle major EPA carbon rules could take years. Despite past statements, Trump has expressed an "open mind" on the Paris deal post-election.

### Infrastructure: Unlocking Investment

**Vision:** Trump and his advisors have proposed a legislative package to unlock $1 trillion in infrastructure investments, focusing on American-made products.

**Key Leaders:** Transportation Secretary-designate Elaine Chao, House Transportation Chairman Bill Shuster, House Ways and Means Chairman Kevin Brady, Senate Environment and Public Works Chairman John Barrasso, and Senate Finance Chairman Orrin Hatch.

**First 100 Days Outlook:** Despite Trump's emphasis on

Story highlights Trump has promised to sign executive orders on Obamacare Washington (CNN) President-elect Donald Trump is ready to "get the show going." Less than 24 hours before he is inaugurated as the 45th President of the United States, Trump told supporters at a luncheon at his Washington hotel Thursday that he is preparing to use the presidential pen to sign some "very meaningful" documents. "We will be signing some papers that will be very meaningful tomorrow right after the speech to get the show going," Trump said, according to cell phone video of his remarks during a closed press portion of the luncheon obtained by CNN. The remarks came hours after the President-elect's incoming White House Press Secretary Sean Spicer said Trump is planning to sign a series of executive orders beginning Friday, though Spicer said Trump is "still working through which ones he wants to deal with tomorrow versus Monday or Tuesday." "The President-elect is continuing to get briefed on some of the orders he wants to do and the sequencing thereof," Spicer said. "I think you'll see some activity on both tomorrow, over the weekend and then Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday." Read More

Donald Trump's presidency, commencing with his inauguration as the 45th U.S. president, immediately faces scrutiny regarding his first 100 days in office. This period is often seen as a crucial test of a new administration's ability to enact its agenda.

During his campaign, when trailing Hillary Clinton in the polls, Trump unveiled his "Donald Trump's Contract With the American Voter." This plan outlined key objectives for his initial months, including:

*   **Constitutional Amendment:** Imposing term limits on members of Congress.
*   **Trade Policy:** Withdrawing the United States from the Trans-Pacific Partnership (TPP).
*   **Executive Actions:** Rescinding any executive orders from President Barack Obama deemed unconstitutional.

While achieving all these ambitious goals within the first three months presents significant logistical challenges, Trump's clear intention is to disrupt the established order in Washington and reverse policies enacted by the Obama administration. Here's a closer look at pivotal issues to monitor in the nascent stages of the new administration:

### 'Obamacare'

Trump consistently expressed his desire to repeal the Affordable Care Act (ACA). His 100-day plan proposed its repeal and replacement with health savings accounts. This objective aligns with the Republican-controlled Congress. On January 12, the Senate advanced a measure enabling ACA repeal legislation to pass with a simple 51-vote majority, a departure from the usual 60-vote requirement for significant bills. The Republican party holds 52 seats in the Senate.

*   **Potential Repeal Timeline:** While repeal might occur within the first 100 days, the duration of existing ACA provisions and the specifics of any replacement remain uncertain.
*   **Replacement Uncertainty:** Trump recently indicated to The Washington Post a desire for the replacement law to ensure universal coverage, though concrete details were not provided.
*   **Impact of Repeal:** The Congressional Budget Office estimates that 18 million individuals could lose their health insurance if the ACA is repealed. A recent ABC News/Washington Post poll revealed that 47 percent of Americans oppose repeal, while 46 percent support it.

### Border Security

Trump identified border security as a top priority, intending to sign an executive order on the matter on his "first day." He stated, "My country, we will get from my first day in office on very secure borders." He further elaborated, "One of my first decrees, which I will sign on the first day, so on Monday — not Friday or Saturday, since I do not want to make it between the whole festivities — this decree will turn around safeguarding our borders."

*   **Border Wall Funding:** While the specifics of any border security order are undisclosed, securing the U.S.-Mexico border was a central campaign promise. The construction of a wall necessitates congressional approval and appropriations, which will inevitably extend the timeline.
*   **Congressional Strategy:** House Republicans and Trump's transition team are reportedly considering linking funding for the wall to a broader government-funding measure in April. This tactic could potentially make it more difficult for Democrats to oppose allocating funds for the wall, according to a source familiar with the discussions.

### Trade

Trump pledged to withdraw the U.S. from the Trans-Pacific Partnership (TPP), characterizing it as "a potential disaster for our country." He indicated an intent to issue a formal notification of withdrawal early in his presidency.

*   **Bilateral Agreements:** Trump stated, "Instead, we will negotiate fair, bilateral trade deals that bring jobs and industry back onto American shores."
*   **TPP Context:** The TPP, a trade agreement supported by Obama and designed to foster investment and trade among 12 Pacific Rim nations, was a rare point of bipartisan opposition during the campaign, with both Trump and Clinton expressing reservations.
*   **U.K. Trade Deal:** Following the Brexit vote, which Obama had warned could place the U.K. "back of the queue" for a U.S. trade deal, Trump praised Brexit as "a great thing" and signaled his intention to prioritize a trade agreement with Britain, anticipating its swift finalization. He remarked, "We will work very hard to get that fast and reasonable — good for both sides."

### Energy

Trump committed to rolling back what he termed "job killing" energy regulations and positioned himself as an advocate for clean coal. He declared, "On energy, I will cancel job-killing restrictions on the production of American energy, including shale energy and clean coal, creating many millions of high-paying jobs."

*   **Regulatory Uncertainty:** The specific regulations targeted for cancellation remain unclear. Jonathan Adler, director of the Center for Business Law and Regulation at Case Western Reserve University School of Law, noted that dismantling many energy industry rules within the first 100 days could prove challenging.
*   **Process for Deregulation:** Adler explained that the administration would likely need to either:
    *   Secure congressional legislation to eliminate these restrictions.
    *   Engage in a lengthy administrative process to reverse the regulations, potentially requiring agencies to repeat the procedures used for their initial adoption.

### Infrastructure

Trump's 100-day agenda includes the passage of the American Energy and Infrastructure Act, envisioned as a decade-long, $1 trillion infrastructure investment program utilizing public-private partnerships and private investments facilitated by tax incentives. Vice President-elect Mike Pence affirmed that an infrastructure bill would be a priority for the administration, stating, "Tell them we are going to do an infrastructure bill and it's gonna be big." This initiative holds potential for bipartisan support, with Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer telling ABC News, "We're not going to oppose something simply because it has the name Trump on it, but we will certainly not sacrifice our principles just to get something done."

WASHINGTON — On the morning of Jan. 21, 2017, his first full day in office, President Donald J. Trump will take a minute to settle behind the 19th-century Resolute desk, first used in the Oval Office by John F. Kennedy. Then he will get very busy — if he follows through on his campaign promises for what he will do on his first day in office. On Day 1, Mr. Trump has promised, he will redirect immigration enforcement, alter trade relations with China and other nations, relax restrictions on energy production, impose new rules on lobbyists, halt efforts to combat global warming, lift curbs on guns, push for congressional term limits and demand a new strategy for defeating the Islamic State. He may face some legal and procedural hurdles, but most of his Day 1 pledges involve issuing presidential directives, executive orders or memorandums that do not need legislative approval. Although Mr. Trump and his top advisers have appeared to moderate some of his broader campaign pledges — they have suggested he might keep parts of the Affordable Care Act, delay building a wall along the border with Mexico and not appoint a special prosecutor to investigate Hillary Clinton’s emails — Mr. Trump has said nothing to indicate that he will not make good on his explicit Day 1 promises, many of which he delivered in his “Contract With the American Voter” during a speech in late October in Gettysburg, Pa.

Donald Trump's planned visit to the CIA headquarters in Langley, Virginia, on Saturday marks a significant moment, potentially serving as his first engagement with a government agency as president. This visit is contingent on the Senate confirming Rep. Mike Pompeo as Director of the CIA on Friday. Should Pompeo's confirmation proceed as expected, Trump would attend his swearing-in ceremony.

This trip to the CIA's Virginia campus follows a period of tension between the president-elect and parts of the intelligence community. These tensions stemmed from assessments that Russia interfered in the recent election. A transition source indicated that Pompeo had expressed a desire for Trump to address the CIA workforce. However, if the visit occurs on Saturday, a substantial portion of the staff may not be present.

Despite this, the visit could be interpreted as a conciliatory gesture from the incoming president. Trump has faced criticism from many intelligence officers due to a series of critical remarks.

Key details regarding the planned visit include:

*   **Timing:** Saturday, following Trump's oath of office on Friday.
*   **Purpose:** To attend the swearing-in of Rep. Mike Pompeo as CIA Director, assuming Senate confirmation.
*   **Location:** CIA headquarters in Langley, Virginia.
*   **Context:** Occurs after weeks of friction between Trump and the intelligence community over election interference assessments.

Trump's spokesman, Sean Spicer, declined to confirm the visit on Thursday, stating to reporters, "I am sure that at some point, shortly, he will visit not just the CIA but a lot of the departments."
