If NASA approves it, New Horizons will sail past and study another icy world within the next five years. The spacecraft carries enough fuel for that encounter (it’ll use the propellent to adjust its course), and will still be able to send data back to Earth, even though that body is nearly a billion miles farther out. Ultimately, it will continue heading out of the solar system, much as NASA’s Voyager I and II spacecraft are doing. (Learn more about New Horizon's Mission ). 8. Is this our only chance, or will any other spacecraft be going to Pluto? There are no plans at this point to send another spacecraft to Pluto, but it sure would be nice to learn even more about that enigmatic little world and its cousins on the fringe of the observable solar system. 9. Is it carrying a message for aliens? Not yet. There is a project, called the One Earth Message, that aims to upload a digital message to the spacecraft once it’s done collecting data and sending it all to Earth. That could be years from now, if New Horizons flies by another body in the Kuiper Belt. The message would be something like a new version of the Voyager Golden Record , which carried the sights and sounds of Planet Earth into the cosmos aboard the Voyager I and II spacecraft. 10. What did New Horizons do during the nine years it took to get to Pluto? It spent a lot of that time hibernating, but did occasionally wake up for system tests. And, of course, it woke up to take a look at Jupiter as it flew by the giant planet in 2007. No sense missing out on that view! Later, when it crossed Neptune’s orbit in 2014, New Horizons woke up again to snap a picture of the icy blue giant—but Neptune was so far away it looked like a faint dot. (Learn about the spacecraft first awakening after a multi-year nap ).

This collaborative project is an extension of the 2016 End of Term project, intended to document the federal government's web presence by archiving government websites and data. As part of this preservation effort, URLs supplied from partner institutions, as well as nominated by the public, will be crawled regularly to provide an on-going view of federal agencies' web and social media presence. Key partners on this effort are the Environmental Data & Governance Initiative and the Data Refuge project. This collection is a continuation of the 2016 End of Term web archiving and, as such, is deduplicated against that collection. It allows for the ongoing archiving of publicly nominated websites beyond the "official" end of the End of Term project. Interested members of the public, particularly government information specialists, are invited to submit selected web sites to be included in the collection using the public nomination tool. For more information on partner institutions, web crawling and past End of Term projects, please visit the End of Term Archive.

query: What were the key targets of the ISIS attacks in Iran, and what was the impact on casualties?

documents.text:
## ISIS Attacks Iran: Understanding the Motives Behind the Unprecedented Strikes

On Wednesday, Iran experienced coordinated assaults on its parliament and the mausoleum of Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini, resulting in 12 fatalities. These attacks are believed to be the first carried out by ISIS on Iranian soil. Following a series of ISIS-claimed attacks in Western nations, many are questioning the group's sudden shift in focus towards Iran. To gain insight, The World spoke with William McCants, author of "The ISIS Apocalypse" and director of the Project on US Relations with the Islamic World at the Brookings Institution.

### Religious and Strategic Rivalry

William McCants explains that ISIS views the Islamic Republic of Iran as a significant religious competitor. Iran is predominantly Shiite Muslim, a sect that ISIS considers a rival. Beyond religious differences, there's a strategic competition for influence in Iraq. ISIS has been in conflict with Iranian forces intermittently since its founding in 2006. While ISIS has long desired to strike within Iran, al-Qaeda initially prevented it. After splitting from al-Qaeda in 2014, ISIS may have lacked the necessary resources to execute such an attack.

The religious animosity runs deep. Sunni jihadists, including those in ISIS, generally hold a very negative view of Shiites, often labeling them as apostates. ISIS, in particular, considers all Shiites to be apostates, arguing that ignorance of their faith is no excuse. They believe Shiites have deliberately strayed from true Islam, making them, in ISIS's eyes, worse than non-Muslims like Jews and Christians.

### Strategic Calculations Amidst Territorial Losses

Despite losing territory in Iraq and Syria, including its former stronghold of Raqqa, ISIS's decision to attack Iran now might seem counterintuitive. However, McCants outlines several strategic reasons:

*   **Punishing Iranian Involvement:** The attacks could be a direct response to Iran's perceived interference in Iraq, where Iranian forces have been instrumental in combating ISIS.
*   **Escalating Sectarian Conflict:** ISIS may aim to force Iran to commit more resources to Iraq, thereby intensifying the conflict along sectarian lines. This could potentially garner more support from Sunni locals who fear Iranian influence. For instance, ISIS has previously sought to exploit sectarian grievances in areas like Mosul.
*   **Provoking Domestic Crackdowns:** Another objective could be to pressure the Iranian government into cracking down on its Sunni population. ISIS might hope this would alienate Iranian Sunnis, driving them to support ISIS's cause. This tactic has been observed in other regions where ISIS operates.

### Recruitment and Competition

The attacks also serve ISIS's recruitment and competitive agenda with al-Qaeda. Having split from al-Qaeda, ISIS is vying for influence among global Sunni jihadists. Facing recruitment challenges due to increased security and territorial losses, ISIS seeks to maintain momentum and project strength. A strike in Iran, targeting two major symbols of power, would be a significant statement in the jihadist world.

This attack is also part of a broader pattern of ISIS-inspired or directed assaults occurring globally, intended to demonstrate the organization's continued operational capacity and ability to recruit. The timing, during the Muslim fasting month of Ramadan, mirrors a similar high-profile series of attacks last year, likely aimed at attracting new recruits. In 2016, for example, ISIS claimed responsibility for attacks in Orlando, Florida, and Nice, France, during Ramadan.

### A Long-Held Ambition

Internal ISIS and al-Qaeda documents reveal that ISIS has harbored a long-standing desire to attack Iran. Al-Qaeda had previously discouraged such plans due to its logistical hub and personnel held within Iran. However, after the split, ISIS was free to pursue this ambition, though it required building the necessary capabilities. It's possible that Iranian Sunnis who have fought for ISIS in Iraq, potentially numbering in the hundreds, may have been involved in these recent attacks. The lack of capability, rather than any other factor, likely explains why such attacks were not seen before ISIS's split from al-Qaeda in 2014.

### Symbolic Targets

The choice of targets in Tehran – the national parliament and the mausoleum of Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini – holds significant symbolic weight. The parliament represents the legislative heart of the Iranian government, while Khomeini's shrine is the spiritual center of Iran's revolutionary leadership. Khomeini, the founder of the republic, was even revered by some as having messianic qualities. Thus, the attacks struck at both the political and spiritual core of Iran's governing establishment.

### Implications for the United States

In the aftermath of such attacks, the United States' stance is crucial. Demonstrating solidarity with nations that have experienced attacks on innocent civilians is paramount. While the US terrorist list is largely comprised of Sunni extremist groups, it is not exclusively so. Historically, the US has designated groups that threatened Iran domestically but did not pose a direct threat to the US. Upholding this principle remains important, as noted by experts at the Council on Foreign Relations.

Armed assailants, including some disguised as women, stunned Iran on Wednesday with brazen attacks on the Parliament building and the tomb of its revolutionary founder, the worst terrorist strike to hit the Islamic republic in years. At least 12 people were killed and 46 were wounded in the near-simultaneous assaults, which lasted for hours, clearly took Iran’s elite security forces by surprise and shattered the self-proclaimed image of calm in a turbulent region. The six known attackers also were killed, official news media said, and five suspects were reported detained. Their identities were not made clear. “We will avenge the blood of those martyred in today’s terrorism attacks,” said Brig. Gen. Hossein Salami, deputy commander of the Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps, the country’s powerful paramilitary force.

The recent assaults on significant symbols of Iranian ideology, claimed by ISIS, are poised to escalate tensions with Saudi Arabia, other Gulf states, and the United States under President Trump. The repercussions of these attacks in Tehran will undoubtedly resonate globally.

By targeting the Iranian parliament and the revered tomb of Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini, the founder of the Islamic Republic, the terrorists who unleashed a deadly rampage in Tehran on Wednesday struck at the very heart of the 1979 revolution. For the average citizens of Tehran, who have largely been spared such horrific violence in recent years, these attacks represent a profound and deeply unsettling shock. The seemingly coordinated nature of these assaults has led Iran's hardline Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) to point fingers at Saudi Arabia. Regardless of the veracity of this accusation, it is highly probable that these events will trigger significant political fallout, extending far beyond Iran's borders and intensifying existing tensions with the United States, Saudi Arabia, and the Sunni Muslim monarchies of the Persian Gulf.

Should the Islamic State's claim of responsibility prove accurate, it would signify a critical turning point: the militant group would have successfully infiltrated the core of its primary Shia Muslim adversary, bringing its divisive brand of indiscriminate terror directly to Iranian soil. The mausoleum where Ayatollah Khomeini was laid to rest nearly three decades ago, on June 6, 1989, stands as a colossal complex that dominates the southern Tehran skyline. This site, treated as a sacred shrine, draws tens of thousands of Iranian visitors annually. In stark contrast to the heavily fortified Majlis (parliament) building situated in the city center, security at the mausoleum was notably less stringent. However, this reality is set to change dramatically in the wake of the attack.

For Iranians, the assault on Khomeini's tomb carries a symbolic weight akin to an attempt to detonate a bomb at the Lincoln Memorial in Washington D.C. If ISIS's claim of responsibility is substantiated, this would mark the group's first successful strike within Iran's interior, despite reports of previous failed attempts. ISIS and Iran are engaged in a multifaceted conflict, both ideologically and militarily, with Iranian forces and their allies actively confronting the group in Syria and Iraq. A video released by ISIS in March explicitly declared its intention to "conquer Iran and restore it to the Sunni Muslim nation as it was before." The extremist group asserts that Iran's clerical leadership, and indeed its historical Persian rulers, have systematically persecuted Sunnis for centuries.

ISIS finds fertile ground for recruitment and support among militant factions operating within Iran's volatile southeastern Sistan-Baluchistan province, a region with a substantial Sunni population. This historical context is underscored by the 2010 attack where Sunni extremists, affiliated with the Jundallah network, killed 39 people at a mosque in the province. Furthermore, minority Kurdish groups in the northwest and ethnic Arab populations in the southwest have a documented history of engaging in smaller-scale attacks.

Wednesday's horrific acts unfold against the backdrop of the anticipated collapse of ISIS's self-proclaimed caliphate. As Iranian-backed Iraqi and Syrian army forces, alongside US and British-supported Kurdish militias, tighten their grip on ISIS strongholds in Mosul and Raqqa, the group is under immense pressure. In response to this escalating military pressure, ISIS has issued a direct call to its followers worldwide, urging them to carry out attacks against their perceived enemies wherever they reside. The recent spate of terrorist incidents in Manchester, Kabul, Baghdad, Marawi in the southern Philippines, and London can be seen as fitting this emerging pattern of displaced and retaliatory activity by ISIS operatives.

Conservative Iranian leaders and commentators are highly likely to echo the IRGC's stance, interpreting Wednesday's attacks as orchestrated by Saudi Arabia and, by extension, implicating the administration of U.S. President Donald Trump. The well-documented covert operations undertaken by George W. Bush's administration in the 2000s, aimed at destabilizing Iran through the funding of internal opposition groups, remain a vivid memory in Tehran. Similarly, the unofficial and intermittent American support for the Mujahedin-e-Khalq (MEK), also known as the People's Mujahadin of Iran, a group previously backed by Saddam Hussein and responsible for numerous armed assaults within Iran, is not forgotten.

The crucial question now is whether President Trump's overt hostility towards Iran is inadvertently encouraging a resurgence of past destabilization efforts, whether directly or indirectly. Departing from the previous U.S. president Barack Obama's policy of measured engagement, Trump launched a scathing critique of Iran last month. During a visit to Riyadh, he vehemently called for Iran's international isolation, accusing Tehran of being the world's primary exporter of "the fires of sectarian conflict and terror." His host, King Salman, has similarly accused Iran of "spearheading global terrorism." Furthermore, with encouragement from Israel, Trump has threatened to abrogate the landmark 2015 nuclear deal negotiated under the Obama administration. He also trumpeted a significant arms deal with Saudi Arabia. This week, he publicly endorsed Riyadh's diplomatic and commercial ostracism of Qatar, a nation that, almost uniquely among Gulf Arab states, has strived to maintain amicable relations with Iran. These actions have prompted regional politicians to issue warnings of impending conflict.

Iran's leadership unequivocally rejects Trump's accusations. They contend that Riyadh is ISIS's most significant source of inspiration and support, and they highlight the fact that Iran and the United States share a common objective in combating ISIS in Iraq. In contrast to the autocratic regimes prevalent in the Gulf, Iran possesses a quasi-democratic political system, complete with an elected parliament and president. This democratic structure fundamentally challenges the narrative that Iran is a "sponsor of terrorism," an inconvenient truth that appears to provoke equal measures of outrage in President Trump, the Saudi leadership, and ISIS itself.

The video shows a bloody body on the floor next to a desk. An Iranian member of Parliament said the images in the video were from inside his office, and he said the victims were his “colleagues.” A voice on the video is heard in Arabic saying: “Do you think we will leave? We will remain, God willing.”

LONDON (Reuters) - Suicide bombers and gunmen attacked the Iranian parliament and Ayatollah Khomeini’s mausoleum in Tehran on Wednesday, killing at least 13 people in an unprecedented assault that Iran’s Revolutionary Guards blamed on regional rival Saudi Arabia. Islamic State claimed responsibility and threatened more attacks against Iran’s majority Shi’ite population, seen by the hardline Sunni militants as heretics. Iranian Foreign Minister Javad Zarif tweeted: “Terror-sponsoring despots threaten to bring the fight to our homeland. Proxies attack what their masters despise most: the seat of democracy.” He did not explicitly blame any country but the tweet appeared to refer to comments made by Saudi Arabia’s deputy crown prince, Mohammed bin Salman al-Saud, in May, saying that that Riyadh would bring “the battle” for regional influence to Iran. Sunni Saudi Arabia denied any involvement in the Tehran attacks, but the assault further fuels tensions between Riyadh and Tehran as they vie for control of the Gulf and influence in the wider Islamic world. It comes days after Riyadh and other Sunni Muslim powers cut ties with Qatar, accusing it of backing Tehran and militant groups. They were the first attacks claimed by Islamic State inside the tightly controlled Shi’ite Muslim country, one of the powers leading the fight against IS forces in nearby Iraq and Syria. The deputy head of Iran’s National Security Council, Reza Seifollhai, told state TV late on Wednesday that the attackers were people from Iran who had joined Islamic State. Iranian police said they had arrested five suspects Supreme leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei said: “These fireworks have no effect on Iran. They will soon be eliminated.” “They are too small to affect the will of the Iranian nation and its officials,” state TV quoted him saying. The powerful Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps accused Riyadh of being behind the attacks and vowed to seek revenge. “This terrorist attack happened only a week after the meeting between the U.S. president (Donald Trump) and the (Saudi) backward leaders who support terrorists. The fact that Islamic State has claimed responsibility proves that they were involved in the brutal attack,” a Guards statement said. Trump said in a statement that he prayed for the victims of the attacks but added that “states that sponsor terrorism risk falling victim to the evil they promote.” The U.S. State Department and U.N. Secretary-General Antonio Guterres both condemned the attacks. Saudi Foreign Minister Adel Al-Jubeir said he did not know who was responsible for the attacks and said there was no evidence Saudi extremists were involved. DRESSED AS WOMEN Attackers dressed as women burst through parliament’s main entrance, deputy interior minister Mohammad Hossein Zolfaghari said, according to the Tasnim news agency. One of them detonated a suicide vest, he said. On a video released by the IS news agency Amaq a man purportedly inside the parliament says in Arabic: “Oh God, thank you. [Gunshots]. Do you think we will leave? No! We will remain, God willing.” Police helicopters circled over parliament, with snipers on its rooftop. Within five hours, four attackers were dead and the incident was over, Iranian media said. “I was inside the parliament when shooting happened. Everyone was shocked and scared. I saw two men shooting randomly,” said one journalist at the scene. Soon after the assault on parliament began, a bomber detonated a suicide vest near the shrine of the Islamic Republic’s revered founder, Ayatollah Khomeini, a few kilometers south of the city, Zolfaghari said. A second attacker was shot dead, he said. The shrine is a main destination for tourists and religious pilgrims. “The terrorists had explosives strapped to them and suddenly started to shoot around,” said the shrine’s overseer, Mohammadali Ansari. By late evening, deputy interior minister Zolfaghari put the death toll at 13, with 43 wounded. Members of Iranian forces take cover during an attack on the Iranian parliament in central Tehran, Iran, June 7, 2017. Tasnim News Agency/Handout via REUTERS The Intelligence Ministry said security forces had arrested another “terrorist team” planning a third attack. The National Security Council’s Seifollhai said Iran had foiled 58 similar attacks, without specifying a time period. REGIONAL ANIMOSITY The attacks follow several weeks of heightened rhetorical animosity between Riyadh and Tehran. In unusually blunt remarks on May 2, Prince Mohammed bin Salman, who is Saudi defense minister and a son of King Salman, said he would protect his country from what he called Iranian efforts to dominate the Muslim world. Any struggle for influence between the Sunni Muslim kingdom and the revolutionary Shi’ite theocracy ought to take place “inside Iran, not in Saudi Arabia,” he said without elaborating. The next day Iran accused Saudi Arabia of seeking tension in the region, saying the prince had made “destructive” comments and it was proof that Riyadh supported terrorism. The attacks could also exacerbate tensions in Iran between newly re-elected President Hassan Rouhani, who positions himself as a reformer, and his rivals among hardline clergy and the Revolutionary Guards. But Rouhani said Iran would be more united and more determined in the fight against regional terrorism and violence. “We will prove once again that we will crush the enemies’ plots with more unity and more strength,” he said. In an appeal for unity, Rouhani’s chief of staff, Hamid Aboutalebi, took to Twitter to praise the security services. “Applause to the power and firmness of our revolutionary guards, Basij (volunteer militia), police and security forces,” he wrote. However, two senior government officials, who asked not to be named, said the attacks might prompt a blame game. Slideshow (23 Images) “They (hardliners) are very angry and will use every opportunity to grow in strength to isolate Rouhani,” said one. The other said the attacks would push Iran toward “a harsher regional policy”. Militant attacks are rare in Tehran and other major cities although two Sunni militant groups, Jaish al-Adl and Jundallah, have been waging a deadly insurgency, mostly in remote areas, for almost a decade. Iran’s Sistan and Baluchestan province, in the southeast on the borders with Pakistan and Afghanistan, is home to the Balouch minority and has long been a hotbed of Sunni insurgents fighting the Shi’ite-led republic. Last year Iranian authorities said they had foiled a plot by Sunni militants to bomb targets in Tehran and other cities during the Muslim holy month of Ramadan.

Saudi Arabia's Foreign Minister Adel Jubeir has called for Iran to be held accountable for its regional interference and its support of terrorist groups, including Al-Qaeda. These statements were reported by Al Arabiya News Channel on Wednesday.

### Iran's Role in Regional Interference

Speaking from Paris, where he began his visit on Tuesday, Jubeir stated that Iran offers refuge to certain Al-Qaeda leaders and other figures from various terrorist organizations. He stressed that Iran must cease its interference in other nations' affairs, labeling Tehran as the world's leading supporter of terrorism. Jubeir asserted that for Iran to be considered a "normal state," it must comply with international law. He also declared that the Iranian regime's "political ideas" are completely unacceptable. The minister further highlighted that the Iranian regime has targeted over 12 embassies in the past 37 years.

### Saudi Arabia's Stance on Qatar

In a related development, Saudi Arabia, alongside seven other nations including Egypt, the UAE, Bahrain, Yemen, Maldives, Mauritius, and Mauritania, recently severed diplomatic ties with Qatar. Jubeir explained that Riyadh's decision to cut ties with Doha was due to Qatari policies that negatively affected the region, particularly the Gulf states. He also noted that Qatar had failed to honor previous anti-terrorism agreements, continuing instead to support terrorist groups.

Jubeir urged Qatar to "change their policies" and stop supporting "extremist groups." He clarified that Saudi Arabia's aim was not to harm Qatar but to make a necessary decision. "Qatar has to stop its support of groups such as Hamas and the Muslim Brotherhood," Jubeir stated on Tuesday in Paris. "We decided to make it clear that enough is enough," he added. "We want Qatar to be an ally in finding peace and stability in the region." These remarks were made as Kuwait began mediation efforts to de-escalate tensions between Qatar and several of its Gulf neighbors.

*This report was last updated on Wednesday, June 7, 2017, at 05:08 KSA time (02:08 GMT).*

Here's a revised version of the document, aiming to meet your quality targets:

## Twin Terror Attacks Strike Tehran, Leaving 12 Dead

Tehran, Iran – A series of coordinated assaults rocked the Iranian capital on Wednesday, June 7, 2017, targeting both the Parliament building and the mausoleum of Ayatollah Khomeini. The attacks, which involved armed militants, some disguised as women and reportedly wearing suicide vests, resulted in 12 fatalities and over 40 injuries.

### Parliament Under Siege

The first attack commenced around 10:30 a.m. at the Parliament building. Several assailants stormed the complex, engaging in a violent spree that included firing on people and taking hostages.

### Khomeini Mausoleum Targeted

Approximately thirty minutes later, a second group of attackers launched an assault on the mausoleum of Ayatollah Khomeini, the late founder of the Iranian revolution. These militants also engaged in violence, including detonating explosives.

### ISIS Claims Responsibility, Iran Accuses Saudi Arabia

ISIS immediately claimed responsibility for the attacks, releasing a video allegedly filmed by one of the perpetrators. If confirmed, this would mark ISIS's first attack within Iran. The extremist group adheres to a radical interpretation of Sunni Islam and considers Shiite Muslims, Iran's majority, to be apostates.

However, Iran's Revolutionary Guard pointed fingers at Saudi Arabia, suggesting the nation was behind the assault. A statement from the Revolutionary Guard linked the attack to a recent meeting between U.S. President Trump and "backward leaders who support terrorists," implying Saudi Arabia's involvement. The statement also suggested that ISIS's claim of responsibility was proof of Saudi Arabia's connection to the Sunni extremist group.

### Regional Tensions Escalate

These attacks occurred amidst a period of heightened regional tension. Saudi Arabia and other Sunni-majority nations had recently severed diplomatic ties with Qatar, citing its alleged support for radical groups like the Muslim Brotherhood and ISIS. Qatar's engagement with Iran was also seen as a factor in this decision.

President Trump, during his visit to Saudi Arabia, had emphasized a shared goal with Sunni allies to isolate Iran. While the Saudi foreign minister denied any involvement of Saudi extremists, the attacks, coupled with Trump's strong stance against Iran, have intensified the volatile regional rivalry.

### Internal Political Ramifications

The attacks may also exacerbate internal political divisions within Iran. Analysts suggest it could fuel tensions between more moderate forces, who supported President Hassan Rouhani's recent re-election, and conservative hard-liners.

Iran's intelligence ministry announced the arrest of another group plotting a third attack, according to Reuters.

Following ISIS's first-ever attacks on Iranian soil, President Donald Trump suggested Iran shared some blame for "falling victim to the evil they promote." This statement came after a coordinated assault by suicide bombers and gunmen on the Iranian parliament and the Mausoleum of Ayatollah Khomeini in Tehran. The twin attacks resulted in the deaths of at least 12 individuals.

**Key Details of the Attacks and Reactions:**

*   **The Assault:** Suicide bombers and gunmen executed a coordinated attack on two significant sites in Tehran.
*   **Fatalities:** The twin attacks claimed the lives of at least 12 people.
*   **Responsibility Claims:**
    *   Iran's Revolutionary Guards pointed fingers at regional rival Saudi Arabia.
    *   ISIS claimed responsibility for the violence, releasing a video that purportedly showed gunmen inside the parliament.

President Trump expressed condolences, stating, "We grieve and pray for the innocent victims of the terrorist attacks in Iran, and for the Iranian people, who are going through such challenging times." He then added, "We underscore that states that sponsor terrorism risk falling victim to the evil they promote."

The United States officially designates Iran as a state sponsor of terrorism. This designation is based on Iran's documented actions, including:

*   Providing arms to designated terrorist groups.
*   Funding terrorist organizations, notably the Shia group Hezbollah.
*   Actively expanding its regional influence across the Middle East.
*   Maintaining its alliance with Syrian President Bashar al-Assad, who faces accusations of war crimes from organizations like the United Nations Human Rights Council.

President Trump has consistently adopted a firm stance against Iran, frequently criticizing the Obama-era Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action (JCPOA), a nuclear deal aimed at curbing Iran's nuclear program. These ISIS attacks occurred amid escalating regional tensions. For instance, Saudi Arabia and other Sunni powers had severed ties with Qatar just days prior, accusing it of supporting both Tehran and various militant organizations. ISIS has a documented history of threatening Iran, a nation actively engaged in combating ISIS forces in both Iraq and Syria, according to reports from the U.S. Department of Defense.

Iran has long been a primary target for the Islamic State (IS). Despite its proximity to territory controlled by the jihadist group, Iran had, until recent attacks, successfully prevented IS from striking within its borders. This success was attributed to a robust counter-terrorism program.

### Preventing Attacks

In May 2016, Iranian authorities announced the dismantling of 20 terrorist cells and the foiling of a plot targeting 50 locations across the country. These operations were part of an extensive counter-terrorism strategy. Iran also implemented an anti-IS messaging campaign, worked to contain conflicts within Syria and Iraq to prevent them from reaching its borders, engaged with various political and religious groups, and conducted extensive intelligence operations. The country's strong and stable state, coupled with effective security services, also played a crucial role in these preventative measures. The overarching goal was to undermine IS and its ideology while preventing attacks on Iranian soil. However, given Iran's prominent position as a target, it was understood that complete immunity from attacks was unlikely.

### IS's Shifting Tactics

Currently, IS militants are facing significant setbacks in Iraq and are retreating in Syria. This has led to an increased focus on carrying out attacks outside these two countries. In recent months, the group has intensified its anti-Iran propaganda and has attempted to recruit from the country's Sunni minority.

### Impact of the Attacks

Wednesday's attacks, the first to occur in Iran, will significantly boost IS morale. This is particularly true as Iran is the region's largest Shia country and is considered relatively stable. The attacks are likely to fuel calls for revenge, especially among hardliners, which could complicate President Hassan Rouhani's administration's efforts to reduce Iran's involvement in Iraq and Syria.

Furthermore, the attacks are expected to increase public support for the Revolutionary Guards, who are seen as the nation's protectors, and for Iran's anti-IS initiatives. This mirrors the public reaction seen in 2014 when the jihadist group seized large areas of Iraq. However, Iran is already heavily involved in both Iraq and Syria and has limited resources. Its involvement in Syria, in particular, is unpopular, viewed as a costly endeavor with little benefit and significant Iranian casualties.

*Dina Esfandiary is a Fellow at the Centre for Science and Security Studies (CSSS) in the War Studies Department at King's College London, and an Adjunct Fellow (non-resident) in the Middle East Programme at the Centre for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS).*

query: What is the expected share price range for Snap's initial public offering (IPO)?

documents.text:
Snapchat's parent company, Snap Inc., is preparing for its highly anticipated Initial Public Offering (IPO), with trading expected to begin as early as Wednesday, March 1st. This event is drawing considerable attention, with many anticipating it to be the most significant IPO in recent memory. Snap Inc. is aiming to price its shares between $14 and $16, a range that would value the company at approximately $20 billion. For perspective, this valuation significantly outpaces Twitter's current market capitalization of around $11 billion, and dwarfs Facebook's impressive market capitalization nearing $400 billion.

The road to Snap's public debut has encountered some turbulence. Initial investor reactions to Snap's prospectus, a mandatory filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) before a company can go public, revealed some concerns. A key point of apprehension was the observed slowdown in Snap's user growth. This deceleration has coincided with the introduction of features on Facebook's widely used Instagram platform that bear a strong resemblance to Snapchat's offerings. As expected, Snap also disclosed that it is not currently profitable and acknowledged the possibility that it may never achieve profitability.

Despite these challenges, investor sentiment appears to be improving as the IPO date nears. Snap is viewed in multiple ways. While users primarily interact with it as a messaging application, Snap Inc. itself defines its core identity as a "camera company." Increasingly, industry observers are beginning to recognize Snap as a significant media entity with the potential to capture advertising revenue traditionally directed towards television. If Snap can successfully secure substantial advertising income, its growth trajectory could potentially mirror that of Facebook. For instance, Facebook's stock has seen remarkable growth, increasing by over 250% since its IPO in May 2012, according to financial data from sources like FactSet.

However, a persistent concern among some analysts, including those at Morgan Stanley, is that Snap might follow a less successful path, similar to Twitter. Twitter has experienced a decline in engagement from users, advertisers, and investors, leading its stock to trade considerably below its initial IPO price. The ultimate success of Snap's IPO will depend on its ability to address these investor concerns and articulate a clear strategy for sustained growth and profitability within the competitive digital landscape. The company's unique communication approach and its evolving identity as a media player will be under intense scrutiny. Investors, such as those at Fidelity Investments, will be seeking evidence of a robust business model capable of withstanding competitive pressures and capitalizing on emerging advertising trends. The $20 billion valuation reflects high expectations, and Snap will need to deliver on its promises to justify this significant market debut. The company's capacity for innovation and platform adaptation will be crucial for its long-term success, as highlighted in analyses by firms like Goldman Sachs.

Snap Inc.'s highly anticipated $3.2 billion Initial Public Offering (IPO) underscores a significant generational divide between its core young user base and traditional investors. While Snapchat enjoys immense popularity among teens and young adults, many seasoned investors find it challenging to comprehend the app's unique features and its underlying business model. This generational gap presents a notable hurdle for Snap's IPO and could influence its long-term stock performance.

### Bridging the Generational Chasm

Snapchat's primary demographic skews young, with the majority of its users falling between 18 and 34 years old, and those under 25 being the most active. This demographic profile starkly contrasts with the investment community, where many professionals do not actively use or fully grasp the appeal of ephemeral messaging. This lack of comprehension, coupled with Snap's reported financial losses and its decision to issue non-voting shares, is fostering investor hesitation. This dissonance could potentially limit participation in the IPO and impact sustained stock demand post-listing.

Snap is expected to price its offering between $14 and $16 per share, potentially valuing the company at approximately $18.5 billion. Reports from sources like Renaissance Capital indicate the offering is already oversubscribed at the higher end of the price range, signaling strong initial interest. However, the critical question remains: will there be sustained investor demand after the stock begins trading on the New York Stock Exchange?

### Investor Apprehension and Market Dynamics

Industry experts, including **Rob Sanderson of MKM Partners**, suggest that traditional investors' reluctance and confusion could exert downward pressure on Snap's stock. Investors who do participate may be inclined to sell quickly if the company encounters financial difficulties. Sanderson has characterized Snap's IPO as "controversial," highlighting that older investors may struggle to understand its use case compared to more established platforms like Uber or Airbnb. He anticipates this will be a persistent challenge for the stock.

**Paul Nolte of Kingsview Asset Management** finds it difficult to reconcile Snap's substantial valuation with its net losses and low revenue per user. Nolte, who is not a Snapchat user himself, admits, "I'm old and they're older," making it challenging to perceive a compelling user-growth narrative. He plans to "steer clear until we see the dust settle," preferring to wait until after the IPO lockup period to assess long-term prospects, although he notes some clients have expressed interest in investing.

**David Menlow of SecondaryRatings.com** attributes current investor enthusiasm to a scarcity of tech IPOs and the general buzz surrounding Snap, rather than a deep conviction in its long-term future. He suggests that older investors are largely following the opinions of their younger counterparts, stating, "The investors are just looking around to say ‘I guess it sounds good because everyone’s telling me it is.’"

To address this disconnect, Snap's roadshow reportedly included detailed tutorials on app functionality, and its prospectus elaborated on app features to clarify them for unfamiliar investors. However, understanding the app's mechanics does not automatically translate into a favorable investment opinion.

**Eric Schiffer of Patriarch Organization** believes that if investors were to actively use Snapchat, they might better recognize the competitive threat posed by Facebook's Instagram Stories, which has emulated Snapchat's ephemeral content model. He commented, "I think that if they sat down at the table and had a conversation with their kids, they’d realize how flawed the investment is."

Snap has publicly acknowledged a slowdown in daily active user growth, citing "increased competition" from Instagram Stories. Menlow posits that investors lacking a thorough understanding of the app may face difficulties if user growth continues to decelerate. If they fail to grasp Snapchat's user engagement dynamics or the inherent cyclical nature of social media trends, they might be inclined to sell their shares. Menlow suggests that with a more comprehensive understanding, they would likely view such slowdowns as a "blip in a positive long-term profile."

Conversely, some analysts believe investors can recognize the advertising potential of social media companies without needing to be direct app users. However, this broader understanding doesn't always equip them to identify Snapchat as a clear market leader. **Kim Forrest of Fort Pitt Capital Group** observed, "I think that shows how social media has really identified itself to investors regardless of age." Despite this, Forrest personally intends to avoid the offering due to Snap's lack of capital return to shareholders and its decision not to grant voting rights.

Snap's upcoming Initial Public Offering (IPO) is poised to allocate a significant portion of its new shares to long-term investors, potentially as many as 25% of those sold. These investors will be bound by a one-year lockup period, as detailed in a regulatory filing submitted on Monday. This strategy aims to bolster the success of Snap's ambitious fundraising goal, which could reach $3.2 billion this week through the sale of 200 million shares priced between $14 and $16 each. Specifically, the filing indicates that approximately 50 million shares are anticipated to be subject to this lockup agreement.

This approach of reserving substantial share blocks for institutional investors is a recognized method for ensuring the success of large stock offerings. For instance, major mutual funds or corporate entities might agree to hold onto their shares for a specified period in exchange for a guaranteed allocation. However, Snap's filing also notes that the company retains the discretion to waive these lockup requirements.

The anticipation surrounding Snap's IPO is considerable. Business Insider reported that the offering was already oversubscribed by at least $6.8 billion prior to the weekend. Despite this strong demand, Snap's Monday filing clarified that no binding agreements regarding the lockup have been finalized yet. The company is expected to determine its final share price on March 1, with trading scheduled to commence on March 2. Snap also has the flexibility to adjust its share price or increase the number of shares offered before Wednesday.

The filing explicitly states: "We expect approximately 50 million shares of our Class A common stock purchased by investors in this offering will be subject to a separate lock up agreement with us providing for a restricted period of one year following the date of this prospectus. These agreements will reduce the number of our shares available for sale in the public market during their term. We may, in our sole discretion, waive any of these lock up agreements before the restricted period expires." A representative for Snap declined to provide further comment on the matter.

Snap Inc., the company behind the ephemeral messaging app Snapchat, is poised for a significant initial public offering this week, potentially marking the largest tech debut in years. Unlike many Silicon Valley counterparts that champion open collaboration and expansive office spaces, Snap operates with a distinct, almost "unsocial" ethos. Its app features messages that vanish, and its internal structure diverges from typical tech giants like Facebook and Google.

Snapchat co-founders Evan Spiegel and Bobby Murphy are set to retain substantial control, holding over 90% of the company's voting rights post-IPO. This unique share structure, as examined by The Wall Street Journal's Shelby Holliday, sets Snap apart from other tech firms.

Former employees describe a leadership style where CEO Evan Spiegel is often elusive. His presence was sometimes only noted by the arrival of his chauffeured SUV, and he reportedly prefers one-on-one or small-group communication over company-wide meetings. In contrast to the sprawling, collaborative campuses of Facebook and Alphabet Inc.'s Google, Snap lacks a central headquarters, with its main offices dispersed across Venice, California. This fragmentation, according to former employees, can hinder communication and create silos. Snap itself acknowledged this in its IPO filing, listing the absence of a headquarters as a risk factor that could impact morale, oversight, and talent acquisition.

Snapchat has successfully captured a large teenage user base, drawn to the impermanence of shared content and the ability to keep their parents out of their digital lives. This appeal has also attracted advertisers eager to reach a young demographic, paving the way for its upcoming IPO. The New York Stock Exchange listing is anticipated to value the company at up to $22 billion, potentially making it the largest U.S. tech IPO in recent memory. A Snap spokeswoman declined to comment due to the company's quiet period.

The core question remains: can Spiegel's management style and emphasis on privacy enable Snap to compete effectively against established television networks and the formidable presence of Facebook? Some former employees suggest Spiegel's approach has occasionally left staff uninformed about key initiatives. Furthermore, this has led Snap to resist offering advertisers the granular user-targeting capabilities that have fueled immense profits for companies like Facebook and Google.

Hemant Taneja, managing director at venture-capital firm General Catalyst and an early Snap investor, notes that Spiegel "doesn't talk much," which can lead to confusion, particularly when Spiegel deviates from conventional descriptions. For instance, he surprised potential investors by rebranding Snap as a "camera company" rather than a social media platform, though Taneja clarifies this concept wasn't entirely new.

Snap's rapid expansion, with its full-time workforce tripling to 1,859 employees last year, intensifies competition. The company is directly challenging global social media giants, notably Facebook's Instagram, which has successfully emulated Snap's features, such as its "Stories" function. Snap attributes its slowing user growth, in part, to this increased competition. Michael Pachter, an analyst at Wedbush Securities, suggests that user loyalty often prevents switching platforms if current usage is satisfactory. Snap also competes with traditional television networks for the attention of young viewers and advertisers, as younger demographics increasingly shift their viewing habits to smartphones.

Spiegel, a Stanford alumnus, diverges from many tech executives. Friends recall him as more social and outgoing during his college years. He has expressed a desire to avoid the "bubblelike culture of Silicon Valley," preferring to be away from its perceived insularity. He views Facebook's "Like" button, introduced during his freshman year at Stanford, as a source of social pressure, fostering the creation of idealized online personas. Spiegel envisioned Snapchat as a space for spontaneous, authentic interactions, akin to real-life conversations, where self-expression, not perfection, is paramount.

Snapchat's design is inherently mobile-first, featuring vertical content optimized for smartphone screens, popular location-based filters, and easily swipeable bite-sized content. This mobile-centric approach allowed Snapchat to gain an early advantage over Facebook, which was still transitioning to mobile in 2012. In 2013, Spiegel famously turned down a $3 billion acquisition offer from Mark Zuckerberg.

Eric Schmidt, executive chairman of Alphabet, praised Spiegel's foresight and business acumen, stating in an email that Spiegel "operates with much more wisdom than anyone else I have seen" and is a "very, very clear thinker" who "gets it right every time" on critical decisions. Snap currently has a five-year, $2 billion cloud data storage contract with Google.

Snap generates revenue through short video advertisements, location-based "geofilters," and interactive "Lenses," such as a Taco Bell promotion that transformed users' faces into tacos. Over two dozen media and entertainment outlets, including The Wall Street Journal, also contribute content to the app, with advertisements placed within their publications. In the past year, Snap's revenue surged over 500% to $404.5 million, though its net loss widened to $514.6 million. User growth in the fourth quarter rose 48% to 158 million. This revenue, however, is a fraction of Facebook's $27.6 billion in revenue and $10.2 billion profit last year.

Spiegel has historically expressed concern about intrusive advertising. Early on, he preferred that potential advertisers learn about the app organically rather than through presentations. When Snapchat's first ads debuted in October 2014, Spiegel emphasized in a blog post that users could opt out and that ads would not be placed in personal messages, aiming for advertising that felt less "creepy and targeted."

While Spiegel has resisted extensive user data collection for ad targeting, he has recently made concessions. In January, Snap partnered with Oracle Corp. to leverage offline purchase data for more targeted advertising. Nevertheless, Snap's approach remains less aggressive than that of Facebook and Google, whose precise ad targeting has yielded billions in profits, enabling them to outspend Snap on talent and product development, including augmented reality hardware.

Snap's advertising philosophy emphasizes broader appeal and high production values, akin to traditional television spots. Examples include Super Bowl-themed filters for team helmets or Gatorade. Snap imposes stringent requirements on its content partners, with potential removal from the app if content underperforms. Initially, advertising revenue primarily came from direct brand deals, with a slower adoption by major ad agencies. Snap has since hired Viacom's Jeff Lucas to bolster its efforts in securing larger deals. As the IPO approaches, Snap has engaged with Oracle Data Cloud and Nielsen to enhance its use of metrics and provide greater transparency into advertising effectiveness. The stock listing will mandate more regular disclosures on user and engagement data.

Post-IPO, Spiegel and co-founder Bobby Murphy will maintain significant control, as Snap is issuing only non-voting shares to the public, with over 90% of voting shares remaining in their hands.

Snapchat's financial performance presents a complex picture for investors, characterized by exceptional revenue growth alongside persistent profitability hurdles. In the past year, the company achieved a remarkable 589% increase in revenue, underscoring its expanding market presence. However, this impressive top-line growth has not yet translated into consistent profitability. Snapchat's daily active user (DAU) base has expanded significantly, reaching 158 million in the most recent reported quarter, a substantial rise from 107 million a year prior. Despite this upward trend, the pace of user growth has shown signs of deceleration in recent quarters, a concern highlighted by financial commentator Jim Cramer.

Cramer suggests that the introduction of Instagram Stories by Facebook's Instagram last summer, a feature widely seen as a direct imitation of Snapchat's core functionality, has contributed to this slowdown. He attributes the decelerated user growth in the latter half of 2016, where Snapchat's DAU increased by only 10% compared to a more robust 33% in the preceding six months, largely to this competitive pressure. Furthermore, performance issues encountered on Android devices have also played a role in tempering user acquisition and engagement.

Beyond user growth dynamics, Cramer has identified another significant factor impacting Snapchat's financial outlook: substantial cloud-hosting expenditures. The company anticipates spending approximately $400 million annually on Google Cloud services and an additional $200 million annually on Amazon Web Services over the next five years. This projected outlay of $600 million per year for cloud infrastructure is particularly noteworthy when contrasted with Snapchat's revenue expectations. The company forecasts generating approximately $1 billion in advertising revenue this year. This implies that a substantial portion, at least 60%, of its anticipated ad revenue will be consumed by these hosting costs alone.

Despite these financial headwinds, Cramer maintains an optimistic outlook, asserting that "the good absolutely outweighs the bad here, at least for the near- and intermediate-term future." He emphasizes that while user growth has slowed, Snapchat still possesses considerable untapped potential for international expansion. Cramer posits that any company achieving such immense revenue growth must be executing its strategy effectively.

Adding to the positive sentiment, Goldman Sachs, one of the underwriters for Snapchat's upcoming Initial Public Offering (IPO), projects that the company could achieve revenues of approximately $2 billion by 2018. The potential for Snapchat to become a highly attractive investment is further amplified by the prospect of utilizing the substantial capital raised through its IPO to venture into new business lines. Should these new ventures prove successful, they could significantly bolster the company's overall valuation and long-term prospects.

From a trading perspective, Cramer anticipates that Snapchat will represent a "phenomenal trade" with its stock poised for a significant surge upon its public debut. He notes that Snapchat is entering the public market approximately a year earlier in its growth cycle than Twitter did, offering investors an opportunity to capitalize on its early-stage growth trajectory. Cramer advises a cautious approach for investors on the day of the IPO, suggesting that if the stock experiences a sharp initial spike, it might be prudent to wait for a price pullback before investing, or to accept that the opportunity may have been missed if the stock doubles at the opening.

The anticipated pricing for Snapchat's IPO is set between $17 and $18 per share, which would establish a market capitalization of $20 billion. At this IPO price, Snapchat's stock would trade at 20.3 times its projected sales for the current year. While this valuation might initially appear steep to Cramer, he contextualizes it by comparing it to Facebook's valuation of 19.4 times sales at the time of its IPO, suggesting that it is not an unprecedented valuation in the tech landscape. Cramer foresees a strong possibility of the stock doubling, driven by large institutional investors who acquired a significant stake in the IPO and are likely to increase their holdings in the open market, thereby benefiting from a superior cost basis relative to the closing price.

For those seeking further insights from Jim Cramer, contact information for "Mad Money" and his social media channels are provided, including a dedicated email for website feedback.

Snap Inc. has successfully priced its initial public offering (IPO) above its initial target range, raising $3.4 billion. This significant fundraising comes as investors have shown strong interest in the company, overlooking concerns about its lack of profitability and the absence of voting rights for shareholders. The parent company of the popular disappearing-messaging app, Snapchat, is now valued at approximately $24 billion at $17 per share. This valuation is more than double that of rival Twitter and represents the highest valuation for a U.S. tech IPO since Facebook in 2012.

### IPO Details and Investor Appetite

Snap had initially targeted a valuation between $19.5 billion and $22.3 billion. The offering was heavily oversubscribed, with demand exceeding the available shares by more than ten times. Sources indicate that Snap could have priced its IPO as high as $19 per share. However, the company opted for a slightly lower price to prioritize securing mutual funds as long-term investors, rather than hedge funds potentially looking for quick sales.

This share sale marks a crucial test of investor enthusiasm for a social media platform primarily used by teenagers and young adults. While the app is popular for its creative filters and engaging features, the challenge lies in translating this "cool factor" into substantial revenue. Despite a nearly seven-fold increase in revenue, Snap's net loss widened by 38 percent last year. The company also faces stiff competition from established players like Facebook and is experiencing a slowdown in user growth.

Snap priced 200 million shares at $17 each, exceeding its initial range of $14 to $16 per share. The timing of the IPO proved advantageous. The market for technology IPOs experienced a significant downturn in 2016, making it the slowest year for such launches since 2008. Investors are actively seeking new opportunities in the tech sector. This successful debut could pave the way for other "unicorns" – tech startups with private valuations of $1 billion or more – to go public.

### Unprecedented Share Structure

Investors have purchased shares despite them carrying no voting power, a notable departure from typical IPO structures. This comes at a time when fund managers have expressed increasing concerns about corporate governance and their desire for influence over company executives. Snap is scheduled to commence trading on the New York Stock Exchange on Thursday under the ticker symbol SNAP.

### Valuing Potential Over Profitability

Snap is entering the public market at a relatively early stage of its development compared to companies like Twitter or Facebook at their IPOs. However, its current valuation of nearly 60 times revenue is more than double the 27 times revenue Facebook achieved in 2012. To justify this valuation and address concerns about decelerating user growth, Snap has highlighted the app's importance to its user base, the time users spend on the platform, and the growing trend of young people preferring video communication over text.

While Snap has been somewhat vague about its specific plans for monetization, it has encouraged investors to trust the vision of its co-founder, Evan Spiegel. Introduced during the investor roadshow as a "once-in-a-generation founder," the 26-year-old is expected to hold a stake valued at approximately $4.05 billion. Spiegel and co-founder Bobby Murphy are each selling 16 million shares in the IPO, which will net them $272 million apiece. Many other Snap investors are also poised to become millionaires.

Spiegel and Murphy will retain significant control over Snap's stock through a unique three-tier share class system. This structure grants them 10 votes per share, while existing investors will have one vote per share, and new investors will have no voting rights.

The drone gives a glimpse into what kind of future products Snap may be considering, which would affect the company’s growth. How the company plans to grow is on the minds of investors as Snap goes public this week. Snap priced its initial public offering on Wednesday and the stock is expected to trade on the New York Stock Exchange on Thursday, in what is set to be the biggest technology I.P.O. since the Chinese e-commerce company Alibaba in 2014. Snap set its I.P.O. pricing at $17 a share — higher than the expected range of $14 to $16 — valuing the company at $24 billion. Getting more visual data such as photographs and video is important to Snap because it helps bolster people’s interactions with Snapchat. Communicating on Snapchat is a highly visual process, intended to create an intense engagement among people with the app. Snap said in its public offering prospectus that the average Snapchat user opened the app more than 18 times a day, and that more than 2.5 billion messages and images were sent each day with the app. Evan Spiegel, Snap’s chief executive, has been vocal about his company’s ambitions around cameras. In a video for investors about Snap’s public offering this month, Mr. Spiegel said cameras augmented the way a person communicates, rather than a person’s memory. “We’re at the beginning of what cameras can do,” Mr. Spiegel told viewers of the video. When Spectacles appeared, Snap faced questions from critics about why it would enter the realm of hardware products. The product is designed to make taking videos a fun and seamless part of everyday life, which dovetails with the company’s goal of getting users to feed Snapchat a steady stream of images and videos.

Snap, the company behind Snapchat, is expected to price its initial public offering today and start trading on the New York Stock Exchange tomorrow. Ahead of Snap’s IPO, Recode Senior Editor Kurt Wagner answered some of what are no doubt the questions you’re dying to ask. What does an IPO actually mean? IPO stands for Initial Public Offering, and it's the first time that a private company starts selling its stock on the public stock market. That means you (yes, you!) are now able to buy a piece of Snap. Does this mean we’re all getting rich? Sorry, you're probably not gonna get rich, but you never know. People invested in Facebook at $20-$30 a share. It's now worth over $100, so you can make some money. Someone is getting rich off this IPO. Who? Snapchat's co-founders are going to get very rich. Evan Spiegel and Bobby Murphy are both going to be worth billions of dollars by the time this whole thing is done. Why does Snap want to go public? Companies go public for a number of reasons: First, to pay back their investors that have given them money along the way to keep the business going. It's a good way for founders and executives to actually cash out some of the stock that they've earned during their time working there. It's also a way for the company to raise money from people like you who may invest. They can use that money to do things like acquisitions, international expansion and new product growth, things that Snap would love to do. Does Snap even make money? Believe it or not, Snapchat is a real business. The company brought in over $400 million in revenue last year, the vast majority of which came from advertising. So, even though the app is free, the company is still making money by putting ads alongside all the content that you're creating inside the app. Doesn’t Snap make money in other ways? Snap also makes hardware, called Spectacles. These are video-recording sunglasses that, until recently, you had to buy out of a vending machine. Snap says those sunglasses don't create meaningful revenue for the company. So while they may be cool, they're not actually benefitting the company's bottom line. What could possibly go wrong with Snap going public? When you're a publicly traded company, you have to share a lot of information and you have to do it regularly. When you're private, you can keep things like revenue or user growth to yourself, but now, as a publicly traded entity, Snap's going to have to report that number publicly to investors. That means if things aren't going well, all of us are going to know about it.

query: What was CNN's coverage of the Steubenville Rape Case verdict criticized for focusing on?

documents.text: CNN's unconscionable coverage of the Steubenville Rape Case verdict is pissing everyone off. Newscaster Candy Crowley, general correspondent Poppy Harlow, and legal expert Paul Callan all did their very best to focus solely on the guilty verdict's repercussions on the two rapists. There's next to no coverage of the girl who was brutally raped; instead, they talk almost exclusively of the rapists— the two teenagers who had such bright futures, and now their lives are completely ruined from this one little indiscretion. Isn't it a shame how they suffer? Let's not forget that these boys committed truly despicable acts and to all appearances showed no remorse or regret until they were faced with real-world consequences. But still, wasn't it so sad when they broke down crying upon learning they'll spend a few years in juvie? It was so sad that CNN forgot to talk about the sixteen-year-old girl whose life will be spent dealing with the ramifications of rape. After all, she left the house with a vagina; she KNEW the consequences. Here's what actually happened on CNN: When Crowley said she couldn't imagine how emotional it was in the courtroom, Harlow — who was there and had been inside the courtroom — responds: I've never experienced anything like it, Candy. It was incredibly emotional, incredibly difficult, even for an outsider like me, to watch what happened as these two young men that had such promising futures — star football players, very good students — literally watched as they believed their lives fell apart...when that sentence came down, [Ma'lik] collapsed in the arms of his attorney...He said to him, 'My life is over. No one is going to want me now.' Very serious crime here, both found guilty of raping the sixteen-year-old girl at a series of parties back in August. Alcohol fueled parties; alcohol is a huge part of this. Advertisement Then, after the verdict is read, we watch Trent Mays and Ma'lik Richmond speak. Mays says "I would truly like to apologize to [the girl], her family, my family, and community. Those pictures shouldn't have been sent around, let alone taken." "I had no intentions of doing anything like that, and I'm sorry I put you guys through it. I'm sorry," said Richmond. He was held and comforted; his back was pat. Advertisement On top of all that, Harlow makes a big deal out of Ma'lik Richmond's lawyer — this guy — telling her that today was the first day Ma'lik's dad told him he loved him — doesn't your heart just bleed for him? Because without a father's love, a boy just has to rape. CNN legal contributor Paul Callan then enters the chat. Crowley inquires: You know, Paul, a sixteen-year-old now just sobbing in court, regardless of what big football players they are, the other one just seventeen, a sixteen year old victim, they still sound like sixteen-year-olds... The Steubenville rape trial verdict sparked intense online debate, revealing troubling perspectives on sexual assault and justice. CNN's coverage, in particular, drew criticism for its tone and focus, with some viewers feeling it inadvertently sympathized with the convicted teenagers.

During a report from the Steubenville courtroom, anchor Candy Crowley and correspondent Poppy Harlow discussed the guilty verdicts for Trent Mays and Ma'lik Richmond. Harlow expressed a complex emotional response, stating it was "incredibly difficult... to watch what happened as these two young men that had such promising futures, star football players, very good students, literally watched as they believed their lives fell apart." Legal expert Paul Callan added that the conviction meant the defendants would become registered sex offenders, a status that "will haunt them for the rest of their lives."

While these statements were factually accurate, their delivery and overall tone were perceived as problematic. The segment, in its attempt to acknowledge the defendants' backgrounds, inadvertently seemed to elicit sympathy. This was poorly received, especially when juxtaposed with overtly offensive online comments from individuals defending the perpetrators.

Online reactions often shifted blame to the victim. One sarcastic tweet read: "The Steubenville story is all too familiar. Be responsible for your actions ladies before your drunken decisions ruin innocent lives." Another, more disturbingly sincere tweet, stated: "So you got drunk at a party and two people take advantage of you, that's not rape you're just a loose drunk slut."

Media outlets also faced scrutiny for their focus on the defendants. Gawker's Mallory Ortberg criticized CNN for focusing on the impact on the rapists' "promising futures." Ortberg argued that their dreams were not crushed by an impersonal system, but by their own actions. A preview piece on *Good Morning America* detailed the backgrounds of Mays and Richmond, lamenting that the two teens "face incarceration... and the almost-certain demise of their dreams of playing football."

Cases like Steubenville highlight distorted perceptions of justice. Critics pointed out that such reporting often humanizes the accused while leaving victims as anonymous figures, a valid concern given the victim's testimony was not prominently featured until late in the trial. Ohio Attorney General Mike DeWine commented after the verdict: "Any rape is a tragedy. But, it is even more of a tragedy when that victim is continually re-victimized in the social media." The hope was that mainstream media outlets would adopt a more balanced approach moving forward. An update revealed that CNN was not the only network to face scrutiny, as MSNBC and Fox also allowed the victim's name to be broadcast.

CNN on Sunday aggressively covered the breaking news of the verdict in the Steubenville rape case. Star football players Trent Mays, 17, and Ma’lik Richmond, 16, were found guilty of raping a 16-year-old girl. Propelled by social media and mainstream media, the Steubenville case has long held a high profile. Defense attorney Walter Madison, right, holds his client, 16-year-old Ma’lik Richmond, second from right, while defense attorney Adam Nemann, left, sits with his client Trent Mays, foreground, 17, as the judge reads the decision. (Keith Srakocic/Associated Press) The scrutiny that comes with its profile descended on CNN. In repeated segments yesterday, CNN featured reports from reporter Poppy Harlow, who was in Ohio for the verdict. Here’s the transcript of some of the coverage at the 11 a.m. hour: ANNOUNCER: This is CNN breaking news. CANDY CROWLEY, CNN ANCHOR: I’m Candy Crowley in Washington. RELIABLE SOURCES is just ahead. But, first, a breaking story we’re following. Two star football players in Steubenville, Ohio, have been found guilty of raping a West Virginia teenager. The story has attracted national attention. The judge just ruled a few minutes ago. Listen in. (BEGIN VIDEO CLIP) JUDGE THOMAS LIPPS, HAMILTON COUNTY FAMILY COURT: In this case, you know, regarding the charges of rape, both defendants Ma’lik Richmond and Trent Mays are committed to the Department of Youth Services for a minimum of one year and a maximum period until you’re 21. (END VIDEO CLIP) CROWLEY: Again, this case was played out in juvenile court, that is why there was a judge, no jury. He decided on the verdict, as well as, you heard there, talking about the sentence. We want to go now to CNN’s Poppy Harlow. She is in Steubenville, and has been covering this trial. I cannot imagine having just watched this on the feed coming in. How emotional that must have been sitting in the courtroom. POPPY HARLOW, CNN CORRESPONDENT: I’ve never experienced anything like it, Candy. It was incredibly emotional — incredibly difficult even for an outsider like me to watch what happened as these two young men that had such promising futures, star football players, very good students, literally watched as they believe their life fell apart. One of — one of the young men, Ma’lik Richmond, when that sentence came down, he collapsed. He collapsed in the arms of his attorney, Walter Madison. He said to me, “My life is over. No one is going to want me now.” Very serious crime here. Both found guilty of raping this 16- year-old girl at a series of parties back in August, alcohol-fueled parties. Alcohol is a huge part in this. […] I want to bring in Paul Callan, our CNN legal contributor. You know, Paul, a 16-year-old now just sobbing in court, regardless of what big football players they are, still sound like 16 year olds. The other one, 17. A 16-year-old victim. The thing is, when you listen to it and you realize that they could stay until they’re 21, they are going to get credit for time served. What’s the lasting effect, though, on two young men being found guilty in juvenile court of rape, essentially? PAUL CALLAN, CNN LEGAL CONTRIBUTOR: Well, you know, Candy, we’ve seen here a courtroom drenched in tears and tragedy and, you know, Poppy’s description, I think, you know, sums it all up. But across America scenes like this happen all the time. I know as a prosecutor and defense attorney, when that verdict is handed down, usually it’s just the family and families of the defendants and the victims, there’s always that moment of just lives are destroyed. And lives have already been destroyed by the crime. And we got a chance to see that. But in terms of what happens now, yes, the most severe thing with these young men is being labeled as registered sex offenders. That label is now placed on them by Ohio law and, by the way, the laws in most other states now require such a designation in the face of such a serious crime. That will haunt them for the rest of their lives. Employers, when looking up their background, will see they’re registered sex offender. When they move into a new neighborhood and somebody goes on the Internet where these things are posted. Neighbors will know they’re a registered sex offender. It’s really something that will have a lasting impact. Much more of a lasting impact than going to a juvenile facility for one or two years. CROWLEY: Paul, thanks. I want to bring Poppy back in — because, Poppy, there’s — you know, the 16-year-old victim, her life, never the same, again. And I understand you have been talking to some of the families involved. HARLOW: Her life never the same again. Absolutely, Candy. The last thing she wanted to do was sit on that stand and testify. She didn’t want to bring these charges. She said it was up to her parents. But I want to tell our viewers about a statement that her mother just made, just made in the court after the sentencing. Her mother just said that she has pity on the two young boys that did this. She said human compassion is not taught by teachers or coaches. It’s a God-given gift, saying that you displayed a lack of compassion, a lack of moral code, saying that you were your own accuser throughout this for posting about this all over social media. And she said she takes pity on them.

Journalists on Sunday sharply criticized media coverage of the Steubenville, Ohio, rape case, particularly after the convictions of two teenage boys. Prominent figures like Lauren Wolfe and Xeni Jardin specifically called out CNN's Poppy Harlow and Paul Callan, accusing them of showing sympathy for the convicted boys and inappropriately highlighting that the victim was "allegedly drunk." The following day, Fox News, CNN, and MSNBC aired the woman's name, a decision Think Progress labeled "an act of serious journalistic negligence."

Lauren Wolfe, director of the Women's Media Center's Women Under Siege project, expressed her strong disapproval. "What I’m so furious about, after the act perpetrated on this young woman, is our media’s take," Wolfe stated. "Mainstream media, of course, reflects society — so in this case, they reflect rape culture. But shouldn’t we expect more from the media? Aren’t there such things as news judgment and context and analysis?"

Wolfe questioned the media's focus, pointing out several critical oversights. She asked why so few outlets investigated the underlying issues of masculinity, and why there was such an emphasis on those defending a football team over a violated young woman. Wolfe also challenged the media's focus on the victim's alleged intoxication, countering, "How could the media possibly be putting the emphasis on [the fact that] the girl drank? Did the boys not drink?"

Furthermore, Wolfe criticized ABC's Nightline for framing the Steubenville case as a "cautionary tale for teenagers living in today’s digital world." She argued this framing was misleading, stating, "As if the problem was what the boys did online, not that they raped a 16-year-old girl and then bragged about it publicly." CNN's Poppy Harlow had commented that the charges meant the two young men "literally watched as their life fell apart." However, Wolfe noted Harlow failed to discuss the severe traumatic effects rape can inflict on young women.

This incident echoes past criticisms of news organizations for omitting crucial context and using victim-blaming language. For instance, The New York Times faced similar backlash in 2011 for its coverage of a gang rape in Cleveland, Texas. When the Steubenville case first emerged, initial media coverage offered minimal information about the young woman, protecting her identity. As she had not spoken out, the focus remained predominantly on the accused boys. In January, Matt Lauer interviewed the attorney and former guardians of Ma’lik Richmond, one of the convicted teenagers. Richmond's former guardians presented him positively, sharing childhood photos and expressing support. This coverage left many questioning the well-being of the young woman and whether anyone was advocating for her.

The media also extensively discussed the teenage boys' roles as football players, often referring to them as "glorified athlete suspects." In January, BuzzFeed's Katie Heaney wrote about how an accuser's athletic achievements could be perceived as a "legitimate alibi absolving them of wrongdoing." Salon's Irin Carmon echoed these concerns, critiquing CNN's coverage, which Gawker also found problematic. Carmon explained via email that networks were limited in showing footage of the victim and her family, while they could show the boys’ emotional breakdowns. She noted that despite the seriousness of the crimes, coverage devolved into a "sideshow about whether a father told his son he loved him." Carmon also observed, "We rarely see such compassion evinced for young offenders when the crime isn’t rape, or when they lack the social status of football players."

Both Wolfe and Carmon highlighted Yahoo Sports' Dan Wetzel as a journalist who handled the Steubenville news effectively. Wolfe praised his column for "going beyond the obvious" and "smashing victim-blaming." Wetzel's reporting delved into the complexities of the town, describing how "a culture of extreme arrogance collapse[d] in two tearful rape convictions." The local football team faced scrutiny, with critics suggesting its prominence had allowed players to act with impunity for years. Social media posts, videos, photos, and text messages revealed a disturbing picture of a high school team seemingly out of control. Wetzel also provided essential context on the nature of rape, explaining that experts consider it a crime of power and control rather than solely sexual gratification. He emphasized that arrogance often underlies such acts, and in Steubenville, it reached extreme levels. Reporting on rape is undeniably challenging, requiring significant time and resources to explore the crime's nuances, its prevalence, and all perspectives. However, this in-depth and contextualized reporting is precisely what is most needed, as demonstrated by Wetzel's work.
